玄怪录 - Records of Mysterious Anomalies

    《玄怪录》 唐 牛僧孺
    　　卷一
    　　○杜子春
    　　杜子春者，周、隋间人。少落魄，不事家产，然以心气闲纵，嗜酒邪游。资 产荡尽，投于亲故，皆以不事事之故见弃。方冬，衣破腹空，徒行长安中，日晚 未食，彷徨不知所往，于东市西门，饥寒之色可掬，仰天长吁。有一老人策杖于 前，问曰：“君子何叹？”子春言其心，且愤其亲戚疏薄也。感激之气，发于颜 色。老人曰：“几缗则丰用？”子春曰：“三五万则可以活矣。”老人曰：“未 也，更言之。”“十万。”曰：“未也。”乃言：“百万。”曰：“未也。”曰： “三百万。”乃曰：“可矣。”于是袖出一缗，曰：“给子今夕，明日午时俟子 于西市波斯邸，慎无后期。”及时，子春往，老人果与钱三百万，不告姓名而去。 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少落魄，不事家产 - Describes someone who is dissolute and neglects family affairs.
    • 心气闲纵 - Refers to a relaxed and unrestrained temperament.
    • 嗜酒邪游 - Indicates a fondness for alcohol and wandering in a morally questionable manner.
    • 资财荡尽 - Means that all assets have been exhausted or depleted.
    • 投于亲故 - Refers to seeking help from relatives and friends.
    • 见弃 - Means to be abandoned or rejected.
    • 饥寒之色可掬 - A vivid description of extreme hunger and cold, suggesting a pitiful appearance.
    • 仰天长吁 - Describes looking up to the sky and sighing deeply.
    • 几缗则丰用 - Asks how much money would be sufficient for a comfortable life.
    • 三五万则可以活矣 - States that 30,000 to 50,000 coins would be enough to survive.
    • 十万 - Refers to 100,000 coins.
    • 百万 - Refers to 1,000,000 coins.
    • 三百万 - Refers to 3,000,000 coins.
    • 袖出一缗 - Describes taking out a string of coins from one's sleeve.
    • 给子今夕 - Means to provide for the person for the evening.
    • 明日午时俟子于西市波斯邸 - Instructs the person to wait at a Persian inn in the West Market the next day at noon.
    • 慎无后期 - Warns against being late.
    • 不告姓名而去 - Indicates that the elder did not reveal his name before leaving.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《玄怪录》 - The title of the collection, which records strange and supernatural events.
    • 唐 牛僧孺 - The author, a prominent figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 周、隋间人 - Refers to someone living during the transition between the Zhou and Sui dynasties, indicating a long lifespan or a legendary status.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Tang Dynasty, known for its grandeur and cultural significance.
    • 东市西门 - A specific location in Chang'an, the West Gate of the East Market.
    • 波斯邸 - A Persian inn, reflecting the cosmopolitan nature of Chang'an with its diverse foreign communities.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《玄怪录》 - The title of the collection.
    • 牛僧孺 - The author of the collection.
    • 杜子春 - The protagonist of the story.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 东市西门 - A specific location in Chang'an.
    • 波斯邸 - A Persian inn in Chang'an.

    The Xuan Guai Lu by Niu Sengru of the Tang Dynasty
    Volume One
    ○ Du Zichun
    Du Zichun was a man from the Zhou and Sui dynasties. In his youth, he was dissolute and neglected his family affairs, yet he had a relaxed and unrestrained temperament, enjoying alcohol and wandering in morally questionable ways. His assets were depleted, and he turned to relatives and friends for help, but they all abandoned him due to his lack of diligence. In the winter, he walked through Chang'an in tattered clothes, empty-handed, and without food, wandering aimlessly and unsure of where to go. At the West Gate of the East Market, his face bore the visible signs of extreme hunger and cold, and he looked up to the sky and sighed deeply. An old man came forward with a walking stick and asked, "Why are you sighing, sir?" Du Zichun explained his situation and expressed his frustration at the indifference of his relatives. His feelings of indignation were evident on his face. The old man asked, "How much would be enough for a comfortable life?" Du Zichun replied, "Thirty to fifty thousand coins would be sufficient." The old man said, "Not enough, tell me more." "One hundred thousand," Du Zichun said. "Not enough," the old man replied. "One million," Du Zichun said. "Not enough," the old man said. "Three million," Du Zichun said. "That will do," the old man replied. He then took out a string of coins from his sleeve and said, "Here is some money for tonight. Meet me at the Persian inn in the West Market tomorrow at noon. Be careful not to be late." When the time came, Du Zichun went to the inn, and the old man indeed gave him three million coins without revealing his name and then left. [1]

    子春既富，荡心复炽。自以为终身不复羁旅也，乘肥衣轻，会酒徒，徵丝竹 歌舞于倡楼，不复以治生为意。一二年间，稍稍而尽。衣服车马，易贵从贱，去 马而驴，去驴而徒，倏忽如初。既而复无计，自叹于市门。发声而老人到，握其 手曰：“君复如此，奇哉！吾将复济子，几缗方可？”子春惭不对，老人因逼之， 子春愧谢而已。老人曰：“明日午时，来前期处。”子春忍愧而往，得钱一千万。 未受之初，愤发以为从此谋生，石季伦、猗顿小竖耳。钱既入手，心又翻然，纵 适之情，又却如故。不三四年间，贫过旧日。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 子春既富，荡心复炽 - 子春 became wealthy, but his desire to spend extravagantly flared up again. '荡心' (reckless heart) and '炽' (flaring up) describe a sudden and intense desire for luxury.
    • 乘肥衣轻 - Riding in luxurious carriages and wearing fine clothes, a common idiom in Classical Chinese to describe living a life of luxury.
    • 会酒徒，徵丝竹歌舞于倡楼 - Gathered with drinking companions, he hired musicians and dancers from brothels. '倡楼' refers to brothels or entertainment venues.
    • 治生 - Making a living or earning a livelihood, a term often used in Classical Chinese to describe the pursuit of wealth or business.
    • 倏忽如初 - Suddenly, he was back to his original state. '倏忽' means 'suddenly' or 'in a flash.'
    • 自叹于市门 - Lamented by the city gate, a phrase that suggests a public or visible expression of regret or frustration.
    • 握其手曰 - Grasped his hand and said, a formal way of introducing direct speech.
    • 君复如此，奇哉 - You are back to this, how extraordinary. '奇哉' expresses surprise or astonishment.
    • 几缗方可 - How many strings of coins are enough? '缗' refers to a unit of currency (1000 coins).
    • 愧谢而已 - Only apologized in embarrassment, a phrase that conveys a sense of shame and regret.
    • 明日午时，来前期处 - Come to the same place tomorrow at noon. '前期处' refers to the previous meeting place.
    • 得钱一千万 - Received one million coins, where '一千万' is a large sum of money.
    • 石季伦、猗顿小竖耳 - Stone Jilun and Yi Dun are just insignificant figures. '小竖' is a derogatory term for insignificant people.
    • 纵适之情，又却如故 - His desire for pleasure returned to its former state. '纵适' means 'indulging in pleasure.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 子春 - A fictional or historical figure, no specific historical figure is referenced here.
    • 石季伦 - Shi Chong (石崇), a wealthy and extravagant official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 猗顿 - Yi Dun (猗頓), a famous merchant during the Warring States period known for his wealth.
    • 倡楼 - Refers to brothels or entertainment venues, where musicians and dancers were hired for entertainment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 子春 - A fictional or historical figure, no specific translation required.
    • 石季伦 - Shi Chong (石崇), a wealthy and extravagant official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 猗顿 - Yi Dun (猗頓), a famous merchant during the Warring States period known for his wealth.
    • 倡楼 - No specific translation required, as it refers to a general type of entertainment venue.

    After becoming wealthy, Zi Chun's desire to indulge in extravagance flared up once more. He believed he would never again be a wanderer, and so he rode in luxurious carriages and wore fine clothes, gathering with drinking companions and hiring musicians and dancers from brothels. He no longer cared about making a living. Within one or two years, his wealth was gradually spent. His clothes and vehicles, once luxurious, became cheap, and he went from riding horses to riding donkeys and then to walking, suddenly returning to his original state. Soon after, he was at a loss again and lamented by the city gate. As soon as he spoke, an old man appeared, grasped his hand and said, "You are back to this, how extraordinary! I will help you again, but how many strings of coins are enough?" Zi Chun was ashamed and could not respond, and the old man pressed him further, to which Zi Chun could only apologize in embarrassment. The old man said, "Come to the same place tomorrow at noon." Reluctantly, Zi Chun went, and received one million coins. At first, he was determined to use the money to make a living, thinking that figures like Shi Chong and Yi Dun were insignificant. However, as soon as the money was in his hands, his heart turned again, and his desire for pleasure returned to its former state. Within three or four years, he was poorer than before. [2]

    复遇老人于故处，子春不胜其愧， 掩面而走，老人牵裾止之，曰：“嗟乎！拙谋也。”因与三千万，曰：“此而不 痊，则子贫在膏肓矣。”子春曰：“吾落魄邪游，生涯罄尽。亲戚豪族，无相顾 者，独此叟三给我，我何以当之？”因谓老人曰“吾得此，人间之事可以立，孤 孀可以衣食，于名教复圆矣。感叟深惠，立事之后，唯叟所使。”老人曰：“吾 心也。子治生毕，来岁中元，见我于老君双桧下。”子春以孤孀多寓淮南，遂转 资扬州，买良田百顷，郭中起甲第，要路置邸百余间，悉召孤孀分居第中，婚嫁 甥侄，迁祔旅榇，恩者煦之，仇者复之。既毕事，及期而往。 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 复遇老人于故处 - Encountered the old man again at the same place; describes a recurring encounter.
    • 不胜其愧 - Unable to bear the shame; conveys a deep sense of guilt or embarrassment.
    • 掩面而走 - Covered his face and ran away; describes a person feeling so ashamed that they hide their face and flee.
    • 牵裾止之 - Pulled his sleeve to stop him; a polite and respectful way to detain someone.
    • 嗟乎！拙谋也 - Alas! It was a poor plan; expresses regret and acknowledges a mistake.
    • 因与三千万 - Then gave him three million; describes a significant financial transaction.
    • 此而不痊 - If this does not heal; indicates a potential outcome.
    • 则子贫在膏肓矣 - Then you will be poor beyond help; suggests that the situation is hopeless or irreparable.
    • 吾落魄邪游 - I am a wandering, disreputable person; reflects a state of being aimless and without a fixed social position.
    • 生涯罄尽 - My life's resources are exhausted; indicates that all resources have been used up.
    • 亲戚豪族，无相顾者 - Neither relatives nor wealthy families care for me; highlights social isolation.
    • 唯叟三给我 - Only this old man has given me three times; emphasizes the repeated generosity of the old man.
    • 我何以当之 - How can I repay such kindness?; a rhetorical question expressing gratitude and a sense of duty.
    • 感叟深惠 - Grateful for the old man's deep kindness; conveys deep appreciation.
    • 立事之后 - After establishing myself; refers to achieving success or stability.
    • 唯叟所使 - At the old man's command; indicates a willingness to serve or follow the old man's instructions.
    • 吾心也 - It is my heart; conveys sincerity and genuine intention.
    • 子治生毕 - When you have finished managing your affairs; refers to completing one's business or responsibilities.
    • 来岁中元 - Next year during the Zhongyuan Festival; specifies a particular time in the lunar calendar.
    • 见我于老君双桧下 - Meet me under the two cypress trees of Laojun; specifies a location for the meeting.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中元 - The Zhongyuan Festival, also known as the Ghost Festival, is a traditional Chinese festival held on the 15th day of the seventh month of the lunar calendar. It is a time when the spirits of the dead are believed to return to the world of the living.
    • 老君 - Laojun, or Laozi, is a revered figure in Taoism, often associated with the founder of Taoism and the author of the *Tao Te Ching*.
    • 双桧 - Two cypress trees, which are often associated with longevity and immortality in Chinese culture. Cypress trees are also commonly found near temples and are considered sacred.

    Proper Nouns

    • 中元 - The Zhongyuan Festival.
    • 老君 - Laojun, or Laozi, a revered figure in Taoism.
    • 双桧 - Two cypress trees, often associated with longevity and immortality.

    He encountered the old man again at the same place, and Zi Chun could not bear his shame, covering his face and running away. The old man pulled his sleeve to stop him, saying, "Alas! It was a poor plan." Then he gave him three million, saying, "If this does not heal, then you will be poor beyond help." Zi Chun said, "I am a wandering, disreputable person, and my life's resources are exhausted. Neither relatives nor wealthy families care for me. Only this old man has given me three times. How can I repay such kindness?" He then told the old man, "With this, I can establish myself in the world, provide for widows and orphans, and restore my reputation in the teachings. Grateful for the old man's deep kindness, after I have established myself, I will follow your commands." The old man said, "It is my heart. When you have finished managing your affairs, next year during the Zhongyuan Festival, meet me under the two cypress trees of Laojun." Zi Chun, knowing that many widows and orphans lived in Huainan, transferred his resources to Yangzhou, bought a hundred acres of good land, built a grand mansion in the city, and set up over a hundred houses on main roads. He summoned all the widows and orphans and divided them among the houses, married off his nephews and nieces, arranged the burial of the deceased, and treated those who were kind to him with warmth and those who were hostile with forgiveness. After completing all these tasks, he went to meet the old man as agreed. [3]

    老人者方啸于二桧之阴，遂与登华山云台峰。入四十里余，见一居处，室屋 严洁，非常人居。彩云遥覆，鸾鹤飞翔，其上有正堂，中有药炉，高九尺余，紫 焰光发，灼焕窗户。玉女九人环炉而立，青龙白虎，分据前后。其时日将暮，老 人者不复俗衣，乃黄冠绛帔士也。持白石三丸，酒一卮遗子春，令速食之讫。取 一虎皮铺于内西壁，东向而坐，戒曰：“慎勿语，虽尊神、恶鬼、夜叉、猛兽、 地狱，及君之亲属为所囚缚，万苦皆非真实，但当不动不语耳，安心莫惧，终无 所苦。当一心念吾所言。”言讫而去。子春视庭，唯一巨瓮，满中贮水而已。 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 方啸 - A poetic way of saying 'was singing' or 'was chanting.'
    • 遂与 - Indicates a sequence of actions.
    • 入 - Means 'to enter' or 'to proceed into.'
    • 严洁 - Emphasizes cleanliness and orderliness.
    • 遥覆 - Suggests a distant and expansive coverage.
    • 鸾鹤 - Refers to mythical birds often associated with immortals.
    • 紫焰 - Describes a purple flame.
    • 灼焕 - Means 'to shine brightly.'
    • 环炉而立 - Describes the arrangement of the jade maidens.
    • 分据 - Means 'to stand in positions.'
    • 慎勿 - A strong admonition.
    • 虽 - Introduces a concessive clause.
    • 及 - Means 'even if' or 'and.'
    • 万苦 - Means 'all kinds of suffering.'
    • 但当 - Means 'just' or 'only.'
    • 安心 - Means 'be at peace.'
    • 终 - Means 'ultimately' or 'finally.'
    • 一心念 - Means 'to concentrate on.'
    • 言讫 - Means 'after speaking.'
    • 而已 - Means 'only' or 'nothing more.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老人者 - Refers to an old man, possibly an immortal or a wise figure.
    • 二桧之阴 - Refers to the shade of two cypress trees, which are often symbolic of longevity and wisdom in Chinese culture.
    • 华山云台峰 - Refers to the Yuntai Peak of Mount Hua, a sacred mountain in Chinese mythology.
    • 玉女 - Refers to jade maidens, often associated with immortals or deities.
    • 青龙白虎 - Refers to the green dragon and white tiger, which are mythological creatures often associated with the four cardinal directions and the elements of water and metal.
    • 夜叉 - Refers to yakshas, mythological beings often depicted as fierce and fearsome.
    • 猛兽 - Refers to fierce beasts, often symbolic of danger and chaos.
    • 地狱 - Refers to hell, a place of punishment in Chinese Buddhist and Taoist cosmology.
    • 亲属 - Refers to relatives, emphasizing the personal and familial aspects of the experience.

    Proper Nouns

    • 华山云台峰 - The Yuntai Peak of Mount Hua.
    • 玉女 - Jade maidens.
    • 青龙白虎 - Green dragon and white tiger.
    • 夜叉 - Yakshas.
    • 地狱 - Hell.

    The old man was chanting in the shade of two cypress trees, and then he ascended with him to the Yuntai Peak of Mount Hua. After traveling more than forty miles, they came upon a dwelling, the rooms and houses of which were clean and tidy, not inhabited by ordinary people. Colorful clouds covered from afar, and lunatic phoenixes and cranes were flying. Above was a main hall, and in the middle was a furnace, nine feet high, emitting purple flames that shone brightly, illuminating the windows. Nine jade maidens stood around the furnace, and the green dragon and white tiger stood respectively in front and behind. By then, it was evening, and the old man no longer wore secular clothing; instead, he was a scholar with a yellow crown and a red cape. He held three white stones and a cup of wine and gave them to Zichun, instructing him to eat them quickly. He then took a tiger skin and laid it on the west wall inside, facing east, and warned, "Be cautious not to speak. Even revered deities, evil spirits, yakshas, fierce beasts, and hells, and even if your relatives are bound by them, all kinds of suffering are not real. Just remain still and silent. Be at ease and do not fear; ultimately there will be no suffering. Concentrate on what I have said." After speaking, he left. Zichun looked at the courtyard and saw only a large jar, full of water. [4]

    道士适去，而旌旗戈甲，千乘万骑，遍满崖谷来，呵叱之声动天，有一人称 大将军，身长丈余，人马皆着金甲，光芒射人。亲卫数百人，拔剑张弓，直入堂 前，呵曰：“汝是何人，敢不避大将军！”左右竦剑而前，逼问姓名，又问作何 物，皆不对。问者大怒，催斩，争射之，声如雷，竟不应。将军者拗怒而去。俄 而猛虎、毒龙、狻猊、狮子、腹蛇万计，哮吼拿攫而争前，欲搏噬，或跳过其上。 子春神色不动。有顷而散。既而大雨滂澍，雷电晦暝，火轮走其左右，电光掣其 前后，目不得开。须臾，庭际水深丈余，流电吼雷，势若山川开破，不可制止， 瞬息之间，波及坐下。子春端坐不顾。未顷而散。 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 道士適去 - The Taoist just left, indicating a recent departure.
    • 旌旗戈甲，千乘万骑 - Flags, spears, and armor, a thousand chariots and ten thousand horses, a vivid description of a grand military procession.
    • 遍满崖谷来 - Filling the ravines and valleys, emphasizing the vastness and intensity of the scene.
    • 呵叱之声动天 - The shouts and commands shook the heavens, a hyperbolic expression of the loudness and authority of the commands.
    • 身长丈余 - Over ten feet tall, a superlative description of the general's height.
    • 人马皆着金甲 - Both men and horses were clad in golden armor, highlighting the grandeur and wealth of the procession.
    • 光芒射人 - Shining brightly, describing the reflective quality of the golden armor.
    • 亲卫数百人 - Hundreds of personal guards, indicating a large and loyal entourage.
    • 拔剑张弓 - Drawing swords and stringing bows, a gesture of readiness and aggression.
    • 直入堂前 - Straight into the hall, emphasizing the direct and forceful entry.
    • 汝是何人，敢不避大将军 - Who are you, and dare you not avoid the Great General, a confrontational question.
    • 左右竦剑而前 - The attendants drew their swords and advanced, indicating a threatening posture.
    • 逼问姓名 - Forced to ask for names, suggesting a coercive interrogation.
    • 作何物 - What are you doing, a question about the individual's actions.
    • 皆不对 - All answered not, indicating a refusal to respond.
    • 问者大怒，催斩，争射之 - The questioner grew furious, urging execution, and shooting arrows, a description of escalating violence.
    • 声如雷 - The sound was like thunder, emphasizing the intensity of the attack.
    • 竟不应 - Yet still no response, indicating a continued silence.
    • 拗怒而去 - Left in anger, describing the departure of the general.
    • 猛虎、毒龙、狻猊、狮子、腹蛇万计 - Thousands of tigers, venomous dragons, lion-like beasts, lions, and vipers, a list of dangerous animals.
    • 哮吼拿攫而争前 - Roaring, snarling, and clawing, they rushed forward, describing the aggressive behavior of the animals.
    • 欲搏噬 - Wishing to seize and devour, indicating the animals' intent to attack.
    • 或跳过其上 - Or leaping over them, describing the animals' movements.
    • 子春神色不动 - Zi Chun remained unperturbed, indicating calmness in the face of danger.
    • 有顷而散 - After a while, they dispersed, describing the end of the disturbance.
    • 大雨滂澍 - Heavy rain poured down, a vivid description of the weather.
    • 雷电晦暝 - Thunder and lightning darkened the sky, emphasizing the storm's intensity.
    • 火轮走其左右 - Fire wheels moved around them, a metaphorical description of the storm's effects.
    • 电光掣其前后 - Lightning flashed before and behind them, adding to the dramatic imagery.
    • 目不得开 - Unable to open their eyes, indicating the blinding effect of the storm.
    • 庭际水深丈余 - The water in the courtyard was over ten feet deep, emphasizing the severity of the flooding.
    • 流电吼雷 - Flowing lightning and roaring thunder, a vivid description of the storm.
    • 势若山川开破，不可制止 - The force was like mountains and rivers being torn apart, unstoppable, a hyperbolic description of the storm's power.
    • 瞬息之间，波及坐下 - In an instant, the waves reached under their feet, indicating the rapid spread of the flood.
    • 端坐不顾 - Sitting calmly without looking up, describing Zi Chun's composure.
    • 未顷而散 - After a short while, it dispersed, indicating the end of the storm.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest or practitioner, indicating a spiritual or mystical context.
    • 大将军 - A high-ranking military title, suggesting a powerful and authoritative figure.
    • 金甲 - Golden armor, symbolizing wealth, power, and military might.
    • 亲卫 - Personal guards, indicating a close and loyal entourage.
    • 猛虎、毒龙、狻猊、狮子、腹蛇 - These are mythological and real animals, often used in Chinese literature to represent danger and chaos.
    • 大雨滂澍 - Heavy rain, often associated with natural disasters or divine intervention.
    • 雷电晦暝 - Thunder and lightning, often used to create a dramatic and ominous atmosphere.
    • 火轮 - Fire wheels, a metaphorical description of the storm's effects.
    • 电光 - Lightning, a common element in Chinese literature to create a dramatic and intense scene.
    • 庭际水深丈余 - Deep water in the courtyard, indicating a severe natural disaster.
    • 流电吼雷 - Flowing lightning and roaring thunder, a vivid description of a powerful storm.
    • 势若山川开破，不可制止 - The force of the storm is compared to the tearing apart of mountains and rivers, emphasizing its unstoppable nature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 子春 - The name of the subject, likely a historical or literary figure.

    The Taoist had just departed when flags, spears, and armor, a thousand chariots and ten thousand horses, filled the ravines and valleys, advancing with shouts and commands that shook the heavens. A man called himself the Great General, over ten feet tall, with both men and horses clad in golden armor, shining brightly. Hundreds of personal guards drew their swords and stringed their bows, entering the hall directly and shouting, "Who are you, and dare you not avoid the Great General!" The attendants drew their swords and advanced, forcing the question of names and what the individual was doing, but all answered not. The questioner grew furious, urging execution and shooting arrows, the sound like thunder, yet still no response. The general left in anger. Soon, thousands of tigers, venomous dragons, lion-like beasts, lions, and vipers roared, snarling, and clawing, rushing forward, wishing to seize and devour, or leaping over them. Zi Chun remained unperturbed. After a while, they dispersed. Then, heavy rain poured down, thunder and lightning darkened the sky, fire wheels moved around them, and lightning flashed before and behind them, blinding their eyes. In an instant, the water in the courtyard was over ten feet deep, flowing lightning and roaring thunder, the force like mountains and rivers being torn apart, unstoppable. In an instant, the waves reached under their feet. Zi Chun sat calmly without looking up. After a short while, it dispersed. [5]

    将军者复来，引牛头狱卒，奇 貌鬼神，将大镬汤而置子春前，长枪刃叉，四面周匝，传命曰：“肯言姓名即放， 不肯言，即当心叉取置之镬中。”又不应。因执其妻来，捽于阶下，指曰：“言 姓名免之。”又不应。乃鞭捶流血，或射或斫，或煮或烧，苦不可忍。其妻号哭 曰：“诚为陋拙，有辱君子。然幸得执巾栉，奉事十余年矣，今为尊鬼所执，不 胜其苦。不敢望君匍匐拜乞，望君一言，即全性命矣。人谁无情，君乃忍惜一言。” 雨泪庭中，且咒且骂，子春终不顾。将军曰：“吾不能毒汝妻耶？”令取锉碓， 从脚寸寸坐刂之。妻叫哭愈急，竟不顾之。将军曰：“此贼妖术已成，不可使久 在世间。”敕左右斩之。 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 引牛头狱卒 - Vivid description of a terrifying executioner, literally 'dragging a head of a bull as a jailer.'
    • 奇貌鬼神 - Describes the executioner's appearance as bizarre and supernatural.
    • 大镬汤 - Refers to a large cauldron of boiling water used for punishment.
    • 将大镬汤而置子春前 - Dramatic action of placing the cauldron in front of Zi Chun.
    • 长枪刃叉 - Refers to long spears and forks used as instruments of torture.
    • 四面周匝 - Means 'all around' or 'surrounding.'
    • 肯言姓名即放 - Means 'if you say your name, you will be released.'
    • 不肯言，即当心叉取置之镬中 - Means 'if you don't speak, we will impale you and throw you into the cauldron.'
    • 捽于阶下 - Means 'dragged down the steps.'
    • 指曰 - Means 'pointing and saying.'
    • 言姓名免之 - Means 'say your name to save yourself.'
    • 鞭捶流血 - Describes the use of whips and beatings causing bloodshed.
    • 或射或斫 - Means 'or shooting or chopping.'
    • 或煮或烧 - Means 'or boiling or burning.'
    • 苦不可忍 - Means 'the suffering is unbearable.'
    • 号哭 - Means 'weeping and crying.'
    • 诚为陋拙 - Means 'truly, I am unworthy and clumsy.'
    • 有辱君子 - Means 'a disgrace to a gentleman.'
    • 幸得执巾栉 - Means 'fortunately, I have served you for many years.'
    • 奉事十余年矣 - Means 'serving you for over ten years.'
    • 今为尊鬼所执 - Means 'now I am held by a powerful spirit.'
    • 不胜其苦 - Means 'unable to bear the suffering.'
    • 不敢望君匍匐拜乞 - Means 'I dare not hope that you will crawl and beg.'
    • 望君一言 - Means 'hope for a word from you.'
    • 即全性命矣 - Means 'to save my life.'
    • 人谁无情 - Means 'who among people has no feelings?'
    • 君乃忍惜一言 - Means 'you are willing to spare just one word.'
    • 雨泪庭中 - Means 'weeping tears in the courtyard.'
    • 且咒且骂 - Means 'cursing and scolding.'
    • 子春终不顾 - Means 'Zi Chun remained unmoved.'
    • 吾不能毒汝妻耶 - Means 'I cannot harm your wife?'
    • 令取锉碓 - Means 'ordered to fetch a millstone.'
    • 从脚寸寸坐刂之 - Means 'cutting her from the feet upwards.'
    • 叫哭愈急 - Means 'crying more desperately.'
    • 此贼妖术已成 - Means 'this demon's magic is complete.'
    • 不可使久在世间 - Means 'cannot allow him to stay in the world for long.'
    • 敕左右斩之 - Means 'ordered the attendants to behead him.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 将军 - Refers to a high-ranking military officer.
    • 狱卒 - Refers to prison guards or executioners.
    • 镬汤 - A large cauldron of boiling water used for punishment.
    • 长枪刃叉 - Traditional weapons used in executions and torture.
    • 鞭捶 - Traditional methods of corporal punishment.
    • 射或斫 - Methods of execution involving shooting or chopping.
    • 煮或烧 - Methods of execution involving boiling or burning.
    • 锉碓 - A tool used for grinding or crushing.
    • 妖术 - Refers to magic or sorcery.
    • 斩之 - Refers to beheading as a method of execution.

    Proper Nouns

    • 子春 - The name of the person being tortured.
    • 将军 - A high-ranking military officer.
    • 狱卒 - Prison guards or executioners.
    • 尊鬼 - A powerful spirit or demon.
    • 锉碓 - A tool used for grinding or crushing.

    The general returned, leading a bull-headed jailer with an appearance so bizarre and supernatural that it seemed like a ghost. He placed a large cauldron of boiling water in front of Zi Chun, surrounded by long spears and forks. He commanded, "If you reveal your name, you will be set free. If you refuse, we will impale you and cast you into the cauldron." Zi Chun remained silent. The general then dragged Zi Chun's wife down the steps and pointed at her, saying, "Speak your name to save yourself." Still, she did not respond. The general ordered whips and beatings, causing blood to flow, or shooting and chopping, or boiling and burning, all forms of torture that were unbearable. The wife wept and cried, saying, "Truly, I am unworthy and clumsy, a disgrace to a gentleman. Fortunately, I have served you for many years. Now I am held by a powerful spirit, unable to bear the suffering. I dare not hope that you will crawl and beg, but I hope for a word from you to save my life. Who among people has no feelings? You are willing to spare just one word." She wept tears in the courtyard, cursing and scolding, but Zi Chun remained unmoved. The general said, "I cannot harm your wife?" and ordered to fetch a millstone, cutting her from the feet upwards. She cried more desperately. The general said, "This demon's magic is complete. Cannot allow him to stay in the world for long," and ordered the attendants to behead him. [6]

    斩讫，魂魄被领见阎罗王，王曰：“此乃云台峰妖民乎？”促付狱中，于是 熔铜、铁杖、碓捣、硙磨、火坑、镬汤、刀山、剑林之苦，无不备尝。然心念道 士之言，亦似可忍，竟不呻吟。狱卒告受罪毕，王曰：“此人阴贼，不合得作男 身，宜令作女人。”配生宋州单父县丞王勤家，生而多病，针灸医药之苦，略无 停日。亦尝坠火堕床，痛苦不济，终不失声。俄而长大，容色绝代，而口无声， 其家目为哑女，亲戚相狎，侮之万端，终不能对。 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 魂魄被领见阎罗王 - Literally means 'the soul and spirit are led to see the King of Hell.' Common idiom in Chinese literature to describe the journey of a deceased person to the afterlife.
    • 此乃云台峰妖民乎 - Rhetorical question asking if the person is an 'evil person from Yuntai Peak.' '妖民' refers to a person with supernatural or malevolent qualities.
    • 熔铜、铁杖、碓捣、硙磨、火坑、镬汤、刀山、剑林之苦 - List of tortures in hell, including molten copper, iron rods, pestles and mortars, fire pits, boiling cauldrons, knife mountains, and sword forests. Symbolic representations of severe punishments in Buddhist and Taoist cosmology.
    • 亦似可忍 - Means 'it seems bearable' or 'it can be endured.'
    • 竟不呻吟 - Means 'he/she never groaned' or 'he/she remained silent.'
    • 阴贼 - Refers to someone who is evil or treacherous.
    • 宜令作女人 - Means 'it is appropriate to make him/her a woman.'
    • 配生宋州单父县丞王勤家 - Means 'destined to be reborn in the family of Wang Qin, the county magistrate of Shanfu, Song Prefecture.'
    • 容色绝代 - Means 'beauty unparalleled' or 'unmatched beauty.'
    • 亲戚相狎，侮之万端 - Means 'relatives and friends mock and tease her in countless ways.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 阎罗王 - The King of Hell, a figure in Chinese Buddhism and Taoism who judges the souls of the dead.
    • 云台峰 - A mountain peak, often associated with supernatural or mystical significance in Chinese literature.
    • 妖民 - A person with supernatural or evil qualities, often used in stories of the supernatural.
    • 熔铜、铁杖、碓捣、硙磨、火坑、镬汤、刀山、剑林 - Specific tortures in hell, each representing a different form of punishment. Symbolic and not literal descriptions of physical torture.
    • 宋州单父县丞王勤家 - Refers to a specific family in a specific location, indicating the rebirth of the soul in a particular context.
    • 亲戚相狎，侮之万端 - Reflects the social dynamics and relationships within a family, where the reborn soul is subjected to ridicule and mistreatment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 阎罗王 - The King of Hell.
    • 云台峰 - Yuntai Peak.
    • 宋州单父县丞王勤家 - The family of Wang Qin, the county magistrate of Shanfu, Song Prefecture.

    After the execution, the soul was led to the King of Hell, who asked, "Is this the evil person from Yuntai Peak?" The soul was then sent to prison, where it endured all manner of tortures: molten copper, iron rods, pestles and mortars, fire pits, boiling cauldrons, knife mountains, and sword forests. Despite these torments, the soul remembered the words of the Taoist and found them somewhat bearable, never groaning even once. When the jailer reported that the soul had finished its punishment, the King of Hell said, "This person is evil and treacherous; it is not fitting for them to be reborn as a man. Let them be reborn as a woman." The soul was reborn into the family of Wang Qin, the county magistrate of Shanfu, Song Prefecture. She was born with many illnesses, enduring the pain of acupuncture and medicine without respite. She also fell into fires and fell off beds, suffering greatly but never crying out. As she grew up, she was beautiful beyond compare, yet she remained mute. Her family called her a deaf-mute girl, and relatives and friends mocked and teased her in countless ways, but she could not respond. [7]

    同乡有进士卢珪者，闻者容而 慕之，因媒氏求焉。其家以哑辞之，卢曰：“苟为妻而贤，何用言矣，亦足以戒 长舌之妇。”乃许之。卢生备礼亲迎为妻，数年，恩情甚笃，生一男，仅二岁， 聪慧无敌。卢抱儿与之言，不应。多方引之，终无辞。卢大怒曰：“昔贾大夫之 妻鄙其夫才不笑尔。然观其射雉，尚释其憾。今吾陋不及贾，而文艺非徒射雉也， 而竟不言。大丈夫为妻所鄙，安用其子！”乃持两足，以头扑于石上，应手而卒， 血溅数步。子春爱生于心，忽忘其约，不觉失声云：“噫！” [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 聞者容而慕之 - Hearing of her beauty and virtue, he admired her. Combines admiration for both physical and moral qualities.
    • 因媒氏求焉 - Through a matchmaker, he sought her hand. Refers to the traditional practice of using a matchmaker to arrange marriages.
    • 其家以哑辞之 - Her family declined because she was mute. Indicates a specific reason for the initial refusal.
    • 苟为妻而贤，何用言矣 - If she is a good wife, why does it matter if she cannot speak? Rhetorical question emphasizing the value of character over speech.
    • 亦足以戒长舌之妇 - It is also sufficient to warn against the gossipy women. Suggests that the story serves as a cautionary tale.
    • 生一男，仅二岁，聪慧无敌 - She bore a son, who was only two years old but incredibly intelligent. Highlights the exceptional nature of the child.
    • 昔贾大夫之妻鄙其夫才不笑尔 - In the past, the wife of Duke Jia despised her husband because he lacked wit. Refers to a historical anecdote about a wife who looked down on her husband's lack of intelligence.
    • 然观其射雉，尚释其憾 - However, when she saw him shoot pheasants, she still let go of her resentment. Shows a moment of reconciliation.
    • 今吾陋不及贾，而文艺非徒射雉也 - Now, I am inferior to Duke Jia, and my literary and artistic talents are not just for shooting pheasants. Expresses the speaker's self-deprecation and comparison.
    • 大丈夫为妻所鄙，安用其子 - A man who is despised by his wife, what use is his son? Rhetorical question underscoring the speaker's despair.
    • 乃持两足，以头扑于石上，应手而卒 - He then grabbed the child's feet and struck his head against a rock, killing him instantly. Describes a violent act.
    • 子春爱生于心，忽忘其约，不觉失声云 - Zi Chun, filled with love for the child, forgot his vow and unconsciously exclaimed. Shows a moment of emotional conflict.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 卢珪 - A name, likely a fictional character or a historical figure.
    • 贾大夫 - Refers to Duke Jia, a historical figure known for his wife's disdain for his lack of wit.
    • 子春 - A name, likely a fictional character or a historical figure.
    • 长舌之妇 - Refers to gossipy women, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 射雉 - Refers to the act of shooting pheasants, often used as a metaphor for hunting or skill.

    Proper Nouns

    • 卢珪 - A name, likely a fictional character or a historical figure.
    • 贾大夫 - A historical figure.
    • 子春 - A name, likely a fictional character or a historical figure.

    A Fellow Village Scholar Named Lu Gui

    There was a scholar named Lu Gui from the same village who, upon hearing of her beauty and virtue, admired her greatly and sought her hand through a matchmaker. Her family declined because she was mute. Lu said, "If she is a good wife, why does it matter if she cannot speak? It is also sufficient to warn against the gossipy women." Thus, he accepted her. Lu prepared the necessary rituals and married her. After several years, they were deeply affectionate, and she gave birth to a son who was only two years old but incredibly intelligent. When Lu held the child and spoke to him, the child did not respond. Despite many attempts to engage him, the child remained silent. Enraged, Lu said, "In the past, the wife of Duke Jia despised her husband because he lacked wit. However, when she saw him shoot pheasants, she still let go of her resentment. Now, I am inferior to Duke Jia, and my literary and artistic talents are not just for shooting pheasants, yet he remains silent. A man who is despised by his wife, what use is his son?" He then grabbed the child's feet and struck his head against a rock, killing him instantly. Blood splattered several steps. Zi Chun, filled with love for the child, forgot his vow and unconsciously exclaimed, "Ah!" [8]

    “噫”声未息，身坐故处，道士者亦在其前，初五更矣。其紫焰穿屋上天， 火起四舍，屋室俱焚。道士叹曰：“措大误余乃如是！”因提其髻投水瓮中。未 顷火息。道士前曰：“出。吾子之心，喜怒哀惧恶欲，皆能忘也。所未臻者，爱 而已。向使子无‘噫’声，吾之药成，子亦上仙矣。嗟乎，仙才之难得也！吾药 可重炼，而子之身犹为世界所容矣。勉之哉！”遥指路使归。子春强登基观焉， 其炉已坏，中有铁柱大如臂，长数尺。道士脱衣，以刀子削之。
    　　子春既归，愧其忘誓，复自效以谢其过，行至云台峰，绝无人迹，叹恨而归。 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 噫 - An interjection expressing surprise, disappointment, or exclamation, often translated as 'Ah!' or 'Alas!'
    • 措大 - A term for a scholar or student, often used in a derogatory sense.
    • 紫焰穿屋上天 - A vivid description of a purple flame shooting through the house and into the sky, possibly symbolizing supernatural or divine intervention.
    • 火起四舍 - Means 'fire broke out in all four quarters,' indicating a widespread fire.
    • 屋室俱焚 - Means 'all houses and rooms were burned down.'
    • 吾子之心，喜怒哀惧恶欲，皆能忘也 - A statement about the person's heart being free from the five emotions (joy, anger, sorrow, fear, desire).
    • 所未臻者，爱而已 - Means 'the only thing that has not been achieved is love.'
    • 向使子无‘噫’声 - Means 'if you had not made that ‘Ah!’ sound.'
    • 吾之药成，子亦上仙矣 - Means 'my medicine would have been successful, and you would have become an immortal.'
    • 嗟乎，仙才之难得也 - Means 'Alas, it is difficult to find someone with the talent to become an immortal.'
    • 吾药可重炼，而子之身犹为世界所容矣 - Means 'my medicine can be refined again, and your body is still part of the world.'
    • 勉之哉 - Means 'Be diligent!' or 'Strive!'
    • 遥指路使归 - Means 'pointed to the road to send you back.'
    • 子春强登基观焉 - Means 'Zi Chun forced his way up to look.'
    • 炉已坏 - Means 'the furnace was already ruined.'
    • 中有铁柱大如臂，长数尺 - Means 'there was an iron pillar as thick as an arm and several feet long.'
    • 道士脱衣，以刀子削之 - Means 'the Taoist priest took off his clothes and used a knife to shave it.'
    • 愧其忘誓 - Means 'feeling ashamed for breaking his vow.'
    • 自效以谢其过 - Means 'to make amends for his mistake.'
    • 云台峰 - A mountain peak, possibly referring to a specific location in the story.
    • 绝无人迹 - Means 'no human tracks,' indicating a deserted place.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest or practitioner.
    • 紫焰 - Purple flame, often associated with divine or supernatural phenomena.
    • 五更 - The fifth watch of the night, roughly corresponding to 3-5 AM.
    • 上仙 - Refers to an immortal or a deity.
    • 仙才 - Refers to the talent or potential to become an immortal.
    • 炉 - Refers to a furnace, often used in alchemy.
    • 铁柱 - An iron pillar, possibly a symbol or a magical object.
    • 云台峰 - A specific mountain peak, possibly a significant location in the story.

    Proper Nouns

    • 子春 - The name of the person in the story.
    • 道士 - The name of the Taoist priest, not explicitly mentioned but implied.
    • 云台峰 - A specific mountain peak.

    Ah! The sound had not yet died down when the person sat in the same place, and the Taoist priest was also in front of him. It was the fifth watch of the night. The purple flame shot through the house and into the sky, and a fire broke out in all four quarters, burning down all the houses and rooms. The Taoist priest sighed and said, "You scholar have caused me so much trouble!" He then pulled the person's hair and threw him into a water jar. Soon the fire was extinguished. The Taoist priest stepped forward and said, "Come out. Your heart, free from joy, anger, sorrow, fear, and desire, can all be forgotten. The only thing that has not been achieved is love. If you had not made that 'Ah!' sound, my medicine would have been successful, and you would have become an immortal. Alas, it is difficult to find someone with the talent to become an immortal! My medicine can be refined again, and your body is still part of the world. Be diligent!" He pointed to the road to send the person back. Zi Chun forced his way up to look, and the furnace was already ruined, with an iron pillar as thick as an arm and several feet long inside. The Taoist priest took off his clothes and used a knife to shave it.

    Zi Chun returned home, feeling ashamed for breaking his vow, and tried to make amends for his mistake. He walked to Yun Tai Peak, where there were no human tracks, and returned home in regret. [9]

    ○裴谌
    　　裴谌、王敬伯、梁芳约为方外之友。隋大业中，相与入白鹿山学道，谓黄白 可成，不死之药可致，云飞羽化，无非积学。辛勤采炼，手足胼胝，十数年间。 无何，梁芳死，敬伯谓谌曰：“吾所以去国忘家，耳绝丝竹，口厌肥豢，目弃奇 色，去华屋而乐茅斋，贱欢娱而贵寂寞者，岂非觊乘云驾鹤，游戏蓬壶？纵其不 成，亦望长生，寿毕天地耳。今仙海无涯，长生未致，辛勤于云山之外，不免就 死。敬伯所乐，将下山乘肥衣轻，听歌玩色，游于京洛，意足然后求达，垂功立 事，以荣耀人寰，纵不能憩三山，饮瑶池，骖龙衣霞，歌鸾飞凤，与仙翁为侣， 且腰金拖紫，图影凌烟，厕卿大夫之间，何如哉？子盍归乎？无空死深山。”谌 曰；“吾乃梦醒者，不复低迷。”敬伯遂归，谌留之不得。时唐贞观初，以旧籍 调授左武卫骑曹参军，大将军赵朏妻之以女。数年间，迁大理廷评，衣绯，奉使 淮南，舟行过高邮。 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 方外之友 - Friends who are outside the conventional social and moral norms, often implying a spiritual or philosophical connection.
    • 黄白 - Refers to gold and silver, often used metaphorically in alchemical texts.
    • 不死之药 - The elixir of immortality, a common theme in Chinese alchemy and Daoist texts.
    • 云飞羽化 - Metaphorical expressions for ascending to heaven or achieving immortality, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 胼胝 - Calluses on the hands and feet, indicating hard physical labor.
    • 耳绝丝竹 - Cut off from music, meaning to renounce worldly pleasures.
    • 口厌肥豢 - Tired of rich food, meaning to reject luxurious living.
    • 目弃奇色 - Abandon beautiful colors, meaning to forsake physical beauty.
    • 华屋 - Grand houses or mansions, symbolizing wealth and luxury.
    • 茅斋 - A simple, humble hut, often associated with ascetic living.
    • 垂功立事 - Establishing achievements and accomplishments.
    • 人寰 - The human world or realm.
    • 憩三山 - Resting in the Three Mountains, a metaphor for achieving immortality.
    • 饮瑶池 - Drinking from the Jade Pool, another metaphor for immortality.
    • 骖龙衣霞 - Riding dragons and wearing clouds, a metaphor for ascending to heaven.
    • 歌鸾飞凤 - Singing with phoenixes and flying with cranes, a metaphor for achieving spiritual elevation.
    • 腰金拖紫 - Wearing gold and purple, symbols of high rank and wealth.
    • 图影凌烟 - Having one's image inscribed on the Hall of Heroic Deeds, a metaphor for achieving great honor.
    • 卿大夫之间 - The ranks of high officials in ancient China.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋大业中 - The period of Emperor Yang of Sui (569-618), known for his ambitious but ultimately unsuccessful reign.
    • 白鹿山 - A specific mountain, likely chosen for its natural beauty and seclusion, where the characters seek to practice alchemy and Daoism.
    • 黄白可成 - The alchemical process of transmuting base metals into gold and silver.
    • 不死之药可致 - The pursuit of the elixir of immortality, a central theme in Chinese alchemy and Daoism.
    • 云飞羽化 - Common metaphors in Daoist texts for achieving spiritual transformation and ascending to heaven.
    • 垂功立事 - Leaving a lasting legacy through achievements, a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 人寰 - The human world, often contrasted with the spiritual or heavenly realms.
    • 憩三山 - The Three Mountains (蓬莱, 方丈, 瀛洲) are mythical islands in the East Sea, often associated with immortality in Chinese mythology.
    • 饮瑶池 - The Jade Pool is a mythical place in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality.
    • 骖龙衣霞 - Riding dragons and wearing clouds are metaphors for achieving spiritual elevation and immortality.
    • 歌鸾飞凤 - Singing with phoenixes and flying with cranes are metaphors for achieving spiritual harmony and elevation.
    • 腰金拖紫 - Wearing gold and purple are symbols of high rank and wealth in ancient China.
    • 图影凌烟 - The Hall of Heroic Deeds (凌烟阁) was a place in the Tang dynasty where the images of famous generals and statesmen were inscribed, symbolizing great honor.
    • 卿大夫之间 - Refers to the ranks of high officials in ancient China, indicating a position of power and influence.

    Proper Nouns

    • 裴谌 - Pei Chen, one of the main characters.
    • 王敬伯 - Wang Jingbo, another main character.
    • 梁芳 - Liang Fang, a third main character.
    • 隋大业 - The era of Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 白鹿山 - A specific mountain where the characters practice alchemy and Daoism.
    • 唐贞观 - The era of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 左武卫骑曹参军 - A military rank in the Tang dynasty.
    • 赵朏 - Zhao Fei, a high-ranking official who marries Wang Jingbo's daughter.
    • 大理廷评 - A judicial position in the Tang dynasty.
    • 淮南 - A region in southern China.
    • 高邮 - A city in the region of Jiangsu.

    Pei Chen, Wang Jingbo, and Liang Fang were friends who sought to transcend conventional social and moral norms. During the era of Emperor Yang of Sui, they entered the White Deer Mountain together to practice Daoism, believing that gold and silver could be transmuted, the elixir of immortality could be attained, and that spiritual transformation and ascension to heaven were possible through diligent study. They worked tirelessly, their hands and feet covered in calluses, for more than a decade. Soon, Liang Fang died, and Wang Jingbo said to Pei Chen, "I have forsaken my country and family, cut myself off from music, grown tired of rich food, abandoned beautiful colors, left grand houses for a humble hut, and valued solitude over pleasure. Is it not to hope to ride clouds and ride on cranes, to play in the mythical Isles of the Immortals? Even if I do not achieve immortality, I still hope for long life, to live as long as the heavens and earth. Now, the sea of immortality is boundless, and I have not yet attained eternal life. My hard work in the mountains has not saved me from death. What I enjoy is to descend from the mountains, ride in fine vehicles and wear light clothing, listen to songs and admire beautiful women, travel to Luoyang, and only then seek to achieve something, to establish accomplishments and leave a legacy, to honor the human world. Even if I cannot rest in the Three Mountains, drink from the Jade Pool, ride dragons and wear clouds, sing with phoenixes and fly with cranes, and be companions with immortals, I can still wear gold and purple, have my image inscribed on the Hall of Heroic Deeds, and mingle with the ranks of high officials. What do you think? Why don't you return? Don't die in vain in the mountains." Pei Chen replied, "I am now awake from my dream; I will not be disheartened again." Wang Jingbo then returned, but Pei Chen could not persuade him to stay. At the beginning of the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, Wang Jingbo was appointed as a Left Guard Cavalry Officer based on his previous credentials, and the general Zhao Fei married him to his daughter. Over the next few years, Wang Jingbo was promoted to a judge in the Supreme Court, wore red robes, and was sent as an envoy to Huainan, where he sailed past Gaoyou. [10]

    制使之行，呵叱风生，行船不敢动。时天微雨，忽有一渔舟突过，中有老人， 衣蓑戴笠，鼓棹而去，其疾如风。敬伯以为吾乃制使，威振远近，此渔父敢突过 我。试视之，乃谌也。遽令追之，因请维舟，延之坐内，握手慰之曰：“兄久居 深山，抛掷名宦而无成，到此极也。夫风不可系，影不可捕，古人倦夜长，尚秉 烛游，况少年白昼而掷之乎？敬伯自出山数年，今廷尉评事矣。昨者推狱平允， 乃天锡命服。 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 制使之行，呵叱風生 - Describes the imposing presence and authority of the envoy, literally 'the envoy's journey, with shouts and commands like a gust of wind.'
    • 行船不敢動 - Means 'boats dare not move,' emphasizing the fear and respect inspired by the envoy.
    • 忽有一漁舟突過 - Means 'suddenly a fishing boat passed through,' describing an unexpected event.
    • 中有老人，衣蓑戴笠，鼓棹而去，其疾如風 - Describes an elderly man dressed in a straw cloak and hat, rowing his boat away swiftly, like the wind.
    • 敬伯以為吾乃制使，威振遠近 - Means 'Jingbo thought I am the envoy, with my authority resonating far and wide,' indicating Jingbo's initial assumption about the envoy's identity and power.
    • 此漁父敢突過我 - Means 'this fisherman dared to pass me by,' expressing Jingbo's surprise at the fisherman's boldness.
    • 試視之，乃谌也 - Means 'I looked at him, and it was Chen,' revealing the identity of the fisherman.
    • 遽令追之，因請維舟，延之坐內 - Means 'I immediately ordered to follow him, and then asked him to tie his boat, inviting him to sit inside,' describing Jingbo's actions to catch up with and invite the fisherman.
    • 握手慰之曰 - Means 'he grasped my hand and comforted me, saying,' indicating a gesture of reassurance.
    • 兄久居深山，拋擲名宦而無成，到此極也 - Means 'Brother, you have been living deep in the mountains for a long time, abandoning officialdom without achieving anything, reaching this extreme,' expressing concern for the fisherman's situation.
    • 夫風不可系，影不可捕 - Means 'the wind cannot be tied, the shadow cannot be caught,' using metaphors to illustrate the futility of trying to control certain things.
    • 古人倦夜長，尚秉燭遊 - Means 'ancient people, tired of long nights, still held candles to play,' suggesting that even in difficult times, there is still enjoyment to be found.
    • 況少年白晝而擲之乎 - Means 'how much more so for young people to throw away their daytime,' emphasizing the waste of potential.
    • 敬伯自出山數年，今廷尉評事矣 - Means 'Jingbo has been out of the mountains for several years, and now he is an official in the Imperial Censorate,' providing background on Jingbo's current status.
    • 昨者推獄平允，乃天賜命服 - Means 'yesterday, when I adjudicated a case fairly, it was a gift from heaven to be appointed,' indicating Jingbo's recent success and recognition.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 制使 - Refers to an envoy or representative of the imperial court, often with significant authority.
    • 廷尉 - Refers to the highest judicial official in ancient China, responsible for major legal cases.
    • 評事 - Refers to an official position in the Imperial Censorate, involved in reviewing and adjudicating cases.
    • 風生 - Metaphorically describes the powerful and commanding presence of the envoy.
    • 漁舟 - Refers to a fishing boat, often used in literature to symbolize a simple, rural life.
    • 蓑戴笠 - Describes the traditional attire of a fisherman, with a straw cloak and hat.
    • 鼓棹 - Means to row or paddle a boat, often used to describe swift movement.

    Proper Nouns

    • 敬伯 - Jingbo, the narrator of the story.
    • 谌 - Chen, the fisherman.

    The envoy's journey was marked by his commanding presence, with shouts and commands like a gust of wind, causing boats to dare not move. It was slightly raining at the time. Suddenly, a fishing boat passed through, with an elderly man inside, dressed in a straw cloak and hat, rowing his boat away swiftly, like the wind. Jingbo thought he was the envoy, with his authority resonating far and wide, and wondered how this fisherman dared to pass him by. He looked at him, and it was Chen. He immediately ordered to follow him, and then asked him to tie his boat, inviting him to sit inside. He grasped his hand and comforted him, saying, "Brother, you have been living deep in the mountains for a long time, abandoning officialdom without achieving anything, reaching this extreme. The wind cannot be tied, the shadow cannot be caught. Ancient people, tired of long nights, still held candles to play. How much more so for young people to throw away their daytime?" Jingbo has been out of the mountains for several years, and now he is an official in the Imperial Censorate. Yesterday, when he adjudicated a case fairly, it was a gift from heaven to be appointed. [11]

    淮南疑狱，今氵献于有司，上择详明吏覆讯之，敬伯预其选，故有 是行。虽未可言官达，比之山叟，自谓差胜。兄甘劳苦，竟如曩日，奇哉！奇哉！ 今何所须，当以奉给。”谌曰：“吾侪野人，心近云鹤，未可以腐鼠吓也。吾沉 子浮，鱼鸟各适，何必矜炫也。夫人世之所须者，吾当给尔，子何以赠我？吾与 山中之友，或市药于广陵，亦有息肩之地。青园桥东，有数里樱桃园，园北车门， 即吾宅也。子公事少隙，当寻我于此。”遂倏然而去。 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 淮南疑獄 - Refers to a case of doubt or uncertainty in the Huainan region, likely involving a legal matter.
    • 今氵獻於有司 - "氵獻" is a rare character, possibly a variant or corruption of "獻" (to present or offer). "有司" refers to officials or authorities. The phrase means "to present the case to the officials."
    • 上擇詳明吏覆訊之 - "上" refers to the emperor or high authority. "擇" means to select, "詳明" means thorough and clear, "吏" refers to officials, and "覆訊之" means to re-examine or investigate again. The phrase means "the emperor selected thorough and clear officials to re-examine the case."
    • 敬伯預其選 - "敬伯" is likely a proper name, possibly referring to a person named Jinglei. "預其選" means "was selected for the task."
    • 雖未可言官達 - "雖未可言" means "although it cannot be said," "官達" refers to officials or high-ranking officials. The phrase means "although it cannot be said that he reached high office."
    • 比之山叟 - "比之" means "compared to," "山叟" refers to a mountain hermit or old man living in the mountains. The phrase means "compared to a mountain hermit."
    • 自謂差勝 - "自謂" means "to consider oneself," "差勝" means "slightly better." The phrase means "considered himself slightly better."
    • 兄甘勞苦 - "兄" refers to a brother, "甘" means to endure willingly, "勞苦" means hard work or toil. The phrase means "the brother willingly endured hard work."
    • 竟如曩日 - "竟" means "still," "如" means "like," "曩日" means "as before." The phrase means "still as before."
    • 奇哉！奇哉！ - An exclamation meaning "how strange!" or "how remarkable!"
    • 今何所須，當以奉給 - "今" means "now," "何所須" means "what is needed," "當以奉給" means "I will provide it." The phrase means "what do you need now, I will provide it."
    • 吾儕野人 - "吾儕" means "we," "野人" refers to people from the countryside or rustic people. The phrase means "we rustic people."
    • 心近雲鶴 - "心近" means "close to the heart," "雲鶴" refers to clouds and cranes, symbolizing a reclusive or lofty lifestyle. The phrase means "our hearts are close to the clouds and cranes."
    • 未可以腐鼠嚇也 - "未可以" means "cannot be," "腐鼠" refers to a rotten mouse, symbolizing something insignificant, "嚇" means to frighten. The phrase means "cannot be frightened by something as insignificant as a rotten mouse."
    • 吾沉子浮 - "吾" means "I," "沉" means to sink, "子" means "you," "浮" means to float. The phrase means "I sink, you float."
    • 魚鳥各適 - "魚鳥" refers to fish and birds, "各適" means "each finds its own place." The phrase means "fish and birds each find their own place."
    • 何必矜炫也 - "何必" means "why must," "矜炫" means to boast or show off. The phrase means "why must you boast or show off?"
    • 夫人世之所須者，吾當給爾 - "夫人世" means "what the world needs," "所須者" means "what is needed," "吾當給爾" means "I will provide it for you." The phrase means "what the world needs, I will provide for you."
    • 子何以贈我 - "子" means "you," "何以" means "what with," "贈我" means "to give me." The phrase means "what can you give me?"
    • 吾與山中之友 - "吾" means "I," "與" means "with," "山中之友" means "friends in the mountains." The phrase means "I with my friends in the mountains."
    • 或市藥於廣陵 - "或" means "sometimes," "市藥" means to buy medicine, "於廣陵" means "in Guangling." The phrase means "sometimes buy medicine in Guangling."
    • 亦有息肩之地 - "亦" means "also," "有" means "have," "息肩之地" means a place to rest one's burden. The phrase means "also have a place to rest one's burden."
    • 青園橋東 - "青園橋" refers to Qingyuan Bridge, "東" means east. The phrase means "east of Qingyuan Bridge."
    • 有數里櫻桃園 - "有" means "have," "數里" means several miles, "櫻桃園" means cherry orchard. The phrase means "have a cherry orchard several miles long."
    • 園北車門 - "園北" means north of the garden, "車門" means carriage gate. The phrase means "the carriage gate north of the garden."
    • 即吾宅也 - "即" means "is," "吾宅" means my house. The phrase means "is my house."
    • 子公事少隙，當尋我於此 - "子" means "you," "公事" means official duties, "少隙" means a little free time, "當尋我於此" means "you should find me here." The phrase means "if you have a little free time from your official duties, you should find me here."
    • 遂倏然而去 - "遂" means "then," "倏然" means suddenly, "去" means to leave. The phrase means "then suddenly left."

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 淮南 - Refers to the Huainan region, a historical area in southern China.
    • 疑獄 - Refers to a case of doubt or uncertainty, likely involving a legal matter.
    • 有司 - Refers to officials or authorities responsible for handling the case.
    • 敬伯 - Likely a proper name, possibly referring to a person named Jinglei, who was selected for the task.
    • 山叟 - Refers to a mountain hermit or old man living in the mountains, symbolizing a reclusive lifestyle.
    • 雲鶴 - Symbolizes a reclusive or lofty lifestyle, often associated with hermits or sages.
    • 腐鼠 - Symbolizes something insignificant, often used to describe trivial or unworthy things.
    • 魚鳥各適 - A metaphor for finding one's own place or niche in life, often used to describe contentment with one's situation.
    • 廣陵 - Refers to Guangling, a city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 青園橋 - Refers to Qingyuan Bridge, a specific location in the narrative.

    A Case of Doubt in Huainan

    A case of doubt in Huainan was presented to the authorities, and the emperor selected thorough and clear officials to re-examine it. Jinglei was chosen for this task, hence his journey. Although he did not reach high office, compared to a mountain hermit, he considered himself slightly better. His brother willingly endured hard work, still as before, how remarkable! How remarkable! What do you need now, I will provide it. Chen replied, "We rustic people, our hearts are close to the clouds and cranes, cannot be frightened by something as insignificant as a rotten mouse. I sink, you float, fish and birds each find their own place, why must you boast or show off? What the world needs, I will provide for you, what can you give me? I with my friends in the mountains, sometimes buy medicine in Guangling, also have a place to rest one's burden. East of Qingyuan Bridge, there is a cherry orchard several miles long, the carriage gate north of the garden, is my house. If you have a little free time from your official duties, you should find me here." Then he suddenly left. [12]

    敬伯到广陵十余日，事少闲，思谌言，因出寻之。果有车门，试问之，乃裴 宅也。人引以入，初尚荒凉，移步愈佳。行数百步，方及大门，楼阁重复，花木 鲜秀，似非人境。烟翠葱茏，景色妍媚，不可形状。香风飒来，神清气爽，飘飘 然有凌云之意，不复以使车为重，视其身若腐鼠，视其徒若蝼蚁。既而稍闻剑佩 之声，二青衣出曰：“阿郎来。”俄有一人，衣冠伟然，仪貌奇丽，敬伯前拜， 视之乃谌也。裴慰之曰：“尘界仕官，久食腥膻，愁欲之火焰于心中，负之而行， 固甚劳困。”遂揖以入，坐于中堂，窗户栋梁，饰以录宝，屏帐皆画云鹤。有顷， 四青衣捧碧玉台盘而至，器物珍异，皆非人世所有，香醪嘉馔，目所未窥。既而 日将暮，命其促席，燃九光之灯，光华满座。女乐二十人，皆绝代之色，列坐其 前。 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 敬伯到广陵十余日 - Literally means 'Jingbo arrived in Guangling for more than ten days,' indicating a period of time spent in a place.
    • 事少闲 - Means 'affairs were few and leisurely,' suggesting a period of relative calm or inactivity.
    • 因出寻之 - Means 'therefore went out to look for it,' indicating an action taken after a period of inactivity.
    • 果有车门 - Means 'indeed there was a carriage door,' describing a specific feature encountered.
    • 试问之 - Means 'tried to ask about it,' indicating an attempt to inquire.
    • 乃裴宅也 - Means 'it was indeed Pei's residence,' confirming the identity of the place.
    • 人引以入 - Means 'people led him in,' indicating an action taken by others.
    • 初尚荒凉 - Means 'at first it was still desolate,' describing the initial impression.
    • 移步愈佳 - Means 'as he moved further, it became better,' indicating a gradual improvement in the environment.
    • 行数百步 - Means 'he walked several hundred steps,' indicating a significant distance.
    • 方及大门 - Means 'finally reached the main gate,' indicating the destination.
    • 楼阁重复 - Means 'towers and pavilions were repeated,' describing the architectural layout.
    • 花木鲜秀 - Means 'flowers and trees were fresh and beautiful,' describing the natural scenery.
    • 似非人境 - Means 'seemed not of this world,' indicating a supernatural or otherworldly quality.
    • 烟翠葱茏 - Means 'smoke and greenery were lush,' describing the dense vegetation.
    • 景色妍媚 - Means 'the scenery was beautiful and charming,' describing the aesthetic appeal.
    • 不可形状 - Means 'cannot be described,' indicating the difficulty of capturing the beauty in words.
    • 香风飒来 - Means 'fragrant wind came swiftly,' describing the sudden arrival of a pleasant breeze.
    • 神清气爽 - Means 'spirit was clear and refreshed,' indicating a feeling of mental and physical well-being.
    • 飘飘然有凌云之意 - Means 'felt light and airy as if he could touch the clouds,' indicating a sense of transcendence.
    • 不复以使车为重 - Means 'no longer considered his carriage important,' indicating a shift in perspective.
    • 视其身若腐鼠 - Means 'saw himself as a rotten mouse,' indicating a devaluation of one's own status.
    • 视其徒若蝼蚁 - Means 'saw his followers as ants,' indicating a devaluation of one's followers.
    • 既而稍闻剑佩之声 - Means 'soon he heard the sound of swords and belts,' indicating the presence of others.
    • 二青衣出曰 - Means 'two young men came out and said,' indicating the appearance of attendants.
    • 阿郎来 - Means 'Master is coming,' indicating the arrival of a higher-ranking person.
    • 俄有一人 - Means 'soon there was a person,' indicating the arrival of another individual.
    • 衣冠伟然 - Means 'wearing splendid attire,' describing the appearance of the person.
    • 仪貌奇丽 - Means 'with extraordinary beauty and grace,' describing the person's appearance.
    • 敬伯前拜 - Means 'Jingbo bowed before him,' indicating a gesture of respect.
    • 视之乃谌也 - Means 'he saw that it was Chen,' identifying the person.
    • 尘界仕官 - Refers to officials of the mundane world, contrasting with the supernatural or otherworldly realm.
    • 久食腥膻 - Refers to long-term exposure to the stench of meat, indicating a negative association with worldly life.
    • 愁欲之火焰于心中 - Refers to inner turmoil, indicating a negative emotional state.
    • 负之而行 - Refers to carrying a burden, indicating a negative emotional state.
    • 固甚劳困 - Refers to extreme tiredness and exhaustion, indicating a negative emotional state.
    • 遂揖以入 - Refers to a gesture of welcome, indicating a positive interaction.
    • 坐于中堂 - Refers to the main hall, indicating a formal setting.
    • 窗户栋梁，饰以录宝 - Refers to the interior decoration, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 屏帐皆画云鹤 - Refers to the decorative theme, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 有顷 - Refers to the passage of time, indicating a transition.
    • 四青衣捧碧玉台盘而至 - Refers to the arrival of food, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 器物珍异 - Refers to precious and rare utensils, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 皆非人世所有 - Refers to the otherworldly nature of the items, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 香醪嘉馔 - Refers to fragrant wine and delicious food, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 目所未窥 - Refers to things never seen before, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 既而日将暮 - Refers to the passage of time, indicating a transition.
    • 命其促席 - Refers to a change in the arrangement, indicating a formal setting.
    • 燃九光之灯 - Refers to the lighting of special lamps, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 光华满座 - Refers to the brightness of the room, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 女乐二十人 - Refers to the number of musicians, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 皆绝代之色 - Refers to extraordinary beauty, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 列坐其前 - Refers to the arrangement of the musicians, indicating a formal setting.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 敬伯 - A name, likely referring to a historical or fictional figure.
    • 广陵 - A place name, likely referring to a city in ancient China.
    • 裴 - A surname, likely referring to a historical or fictional person.
    • 谌 - A name, likely referring to a historical or fictional figure.
    • 尘界 - Refers to the mundane world, contrasting with the supernatural or otherworldly realm.
    • 衣冠伟然 - Refers to splendid attire, indicating a high social status.
    • 仪貌奇丽 - Refers to extraordinary beauty and grace, indicating a high social status.
    • 尘界仕官 - Refers to officials of the mundane world, contrasting with the supernatural or otherworldly realm.
    • 久食腥膻 - Refers to long-term exposure to the stench of meat, indicating a negative association with worldly life.
    • 愁欲之火焰于心中 - Refers to inner turmoil, indicating a negative emotional state.
    • 负之而行 - Refers to carrying a burden, indicating a negative emotional state.
    • 固甚劳困 - Refers to extreme tiredness and exhaustion, indicating a negative emotional state.
    • 遂揖以入 - Refers to a gesture of welcome, indicating a positive interaction.
    • 坐于中堂 - Refers to the main hall, indicating a formal setting.
    • 窗户栋梁，饰以录宝 - Refers to the interior decoration, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 屏帐皆画云鹤 - Refers to the decorative theme, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 有顷 - Refers to the passage of time, indicating a transition.
    • 四青衣捧碧玉台盘而至 - Refers to the arrival of food, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 器物珍异 - Refers to precious and rare utensils, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 皆非人世所有 - Refers to the otherworldly nature of the items, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 香醪嘉馔 - Refers to fragrant wine and delicious food, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 目所未窥 - Refers to things never seen before, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 既而日将暮 - Refers to the passage of time, indicating a transition.
    • 命其促席 - Refers to a change in the arrangement, indicating a formal setting.
    • 燃九光之灯 - Refers to the lighting of special lamps, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 光华满座 - Refers to the brightness of the room, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 女乐二十人 - Refers to the number of musicians, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 皆绝代之色 - Refers to extraordinary beauty, indicating a luxurious setting.
    • 列坐其前 - Refers to the arrangement of the musicians, indicating a formal setting.

    Proper Nouns

    • 敬伯 - A name, likely referring to a historical or fictional figure.
    • 广陵 - A place name, likely referring to a city in ancient China.
    • 裴 - A surname, likely referring to a historical or fictional person.
    • 谌 - A name, likely referring to a historical or fictional figure.

    Jingbo arrived in Guangling for more than ten days, with few affairs and leisure. Thinking of Chen's words, he went out to look for him. Indeed, there was a carriage door. He tried to ask about it, and it turned out to be Pei's residence. People led him in. At first, it was still desolate, but as he moved further, it became better. After walking several hundred steps, he finally reached the main gate. Towers and pavilions were repeated, and flowers and trees were fresh and beautiful, seeming not of this world. Smoke and greenery were lush, and the scenery was beautiful and charming, beyond description. A fragrant wind came swiftly, refreshing his spirit and clearing his mind. He felt light and airy as if he could touch the clouds, no longer considering his carriage important, seeing himself as a rotten mouse and his followers as ants. Soon, he heard the sound of swords and belts. Two young men came out and said, "Master is coming." Soon, there was a person, wearing splendid attire, with extraordinary beauty and grace. Jingbo bowed before him, and he saw that it was Chen. Pei comforted him, saying, "Officials of the mundane world, long accustomed to the stench of meat, the flames of desire and sorrow burned in their hearts, carrying it with them, indeed very tiring and exhausting." He then invited him in and sat in the main hall. Windows, doors, and beams were decorated with precious materials, and screens and curtains were all painted with clouds and cranes. After a while, four young men carried a jade table and plate, with precious and rare utensils, all not found in the mundane world. Fragrant wine and delicious food, things never seen before. Soon, the day was ending, and he ordered the seating to be moved closer, lighting lamps with nine lights, the light filling the room. Twenty female musicians, all of extraordinary beauty, sat in front of him. [13]

    裴顾小黄头曰：“王评事昔吾山中之友，道情不固，弃吾下山，别近十年， 才为廷尉属。今俗心已就，须俗妓以乐之。顾伶家女无足召者，当召士大夫之女 已适人者。如近无姝丽，五千里内皆可择之。”小黄头唯唯而去。诸妓调碧玉筝， 调未谐而黄头已复命，引一妓自西阶登，拜裴席前。裴指曰：“参评事。”敬伯 答拜，细视之，乃敬伯妻赵氏也。敬伯惊讶不敢言，妻亦甚骇，目之不已。遂令 坐玉阶下，一青衣捧玳瑁筝授之，赵素所善也，因令与妓合曲以送酒。 [14]

    Pei instructed the low-ranking servant, saying, "Wang Pingshi, once my friend in the mountains, was not steadfast in his Daoist beliefs, abandoned me, and went down the mountain. We have been separated for nearly ten years, and he has only recently become a subordinate of the Imperial Censor. Now, he has embraced worldly desires and needs a secular courtesan to entertain him. However, none of the courtesans from the entertainment quarters are worthy enough to be summoned; instead, I should summon a woman who has married a scholar-official. If there are no beautiful women nearby, then she can be chosen from within five thousand li." The low-ranking servant bowed and departed. The courtesans began to tune their jade zithers, but before the tuning was complete, the low-ranking servant returned and led a courtesan up the western steps to bow before Pei. Pei pointed and said, "Present yourself to Pingshi." Jingbo bowed in response and looked at her closely, realizing that she was his wife Zhao. Jingbo was astonished and dared not speak, and his wife was also greatly surprised, staring at him without ceasing. Pei then ordered her to sit on the jade steps, and a maid presented a tortoiseshell zither to her. Zhao was skilled at playing it, so Pei ordered her to play a duet with the courtesan to serve the wine. [14]

    敬伯坐间 取一殷色朱李投之，赵顾敬伯，潜系于衣带。妓奏之曲，赵皆不能逐。裴乃令随 赵所奏，时时停之，以呈其曲。其歌舞虽非云韶九奏之乐，而清沉宛转，酬献极 欢。天将晓，裴召前黄头曰：“送赵氏夫人。”且谓曰：“此堂乃九天画堂，常 人不到。吾昔与王为方外之交，怜其为俗所迷，自投汤火，以智自烧，以明自贼， 将沉浮于生死海中，求岸不得，故命于此，一以醒之。今日之会，诚难再得，亦 夫人之宿命，乃得暂游，云山万重，往复劳苦，无辞也。”赵拜而去。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 敬伯坐間 - Phrase indicating that the action occurs during a gathering or meeting, likely a banquet or social event.
    • 殷色朱李 - Refers to dark red plums, possibly a specific type of plum with a rich color.
    • 潛系於衣帶 - Literally means 'secretly tied to the belt,' suggesting that the plum was hidden in the belt.
    • 妓奏之曲 - Refers to the music played by the courtesans or entertainers.
    • 不能逐 - Means 'unable to follow' or 'unable to keep up,' implying that Zhao could not keep pace with the music.
    • 方外之交 - Refers to a friendship outside the conventional bounds, often implying a spiritual or philosophical connection.
    • 俗所迷 - Means 'lost in worldly concerns' or 'ensnared by the world.'
    • 自投湯火 - A metaphor for throwing oneself into danger or trouble.
    • 以智自燒 - Means 'burning oneself with wisdom,' suggesting that excessive wisdom can be self-destructive.
    • 以明自贼 - Means 'destroying oneself with clarity,' implying that too much clarity can lead to self-harm.
    • 沉浮於生死海中 - A metaphor for being caught in the cycle of birth and death, often used in Buddhist contexts.
    • 求岸不得 - Means 'unable to find a shore,' suggesting that Zhao is unable to escape the cycle of rebirth.
    • 一以醒之 - Means 'to wake him up' or 'to enlighten him,' indicating that the experience is intended to bring Zhao to a state of awareness.
    • 云山万重 - A metaphor for the many obstacles or difficulties that Zhao must overcome.
    • 无辞也 - Means 'no regrets' or 'no complaints,' suggesting that Zhao accepts the journey without hesitation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 敬伯 - Likely a proper name, possibly referring to a person of high social status or a nobleman.
    • 赵 - Likely a proper name, possibly referring to Zhao, a common Chinese surname.
    • 裴 - Likely a proper name, possibly referring to Pei, another common Chinese surname.
    • 黄头 - Refers to a servant or attendant, often depicted as a low-ranking individual.
    • 九天画堂 - Refers to a grand and divine hall, often used in mythological or religious contexts to denote a place of great significance.
    • 方外之交 - Implies a spiritual or philosophical connection, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist traditions.
    • 云韶九奏之乐 - Refers to the highest and most refined music, often associated with the celestial realm or divine music.
    • 生死海 - A metaphor for the cycle of birth and death, often used in Buddhist contexts.
    • 宿命 - Refers to predestined fate or karma, suggesting that Zhao's presence at the gathering is part of a larger, predetermined plan.

    Proper Nouns

    • 敬伯 - A proper name, likely referring to a person of high social status.
    • 赵 - A proper name, likely referring to Zhao, a common Chinese surname.
    • 裴 - A proper name, likely referring to Pei, another common Chinese surname.
    • 黄头 - A term for a servant or attendant.
    • 九天画堂 - A term for a grand and divine hall.
    • 方外之交 - A term for a spiritual or philosophical connection.
    • 云韶九奏之乐 - A term for the highest and most refined music.
    • 生死海 - A term for the cycle of birth and death.
    • 宿命 - A term for predestined fate or karma.

    During the gathering, Jinger took a dark red plum and threw it to Zhao, who glanced at Jinger and secretly tied it to his belt. The courtesans played music, but Zhao could not keep up with the melody. Pei then instructed the musicians to pause at the moments Zhao played, highlighting his performance. Although the music and dance were not the divine music of the Ninefold Chimes, they were still clear, deep, and graceful, bringing great joy to the audience. As dawn approached, Pei summoned a servant and said, "Escort Lady Zhao." He also addressed her, saying, "This hall is the Nine Heavens' Painted Hall, a place rarely visited by ordinary people. In the past, I had a spiritual friendship with Wang, who was lost in worldly concerns, throwing himself into danger, burning himself with wisdom, and destroying himself with clarity, caught in the cycle of birth and death, unable to find a shore. I arranged for him to come here to wake him up. Today's gathering is truly a rare opportunity, also part of your predestined fate, allowing you to briefly visit, facing countless mountains and hardships, with no regrets." Zhao bowed and departed. [15]

    裴谓敬伯曰：“评公使车留此一宿，得无惊群将乎？宜且就馆，未赴阙闲时， 访我可也。尘路遐远，万愁攻人，努力自爱。”敬伯拜谢而去。后五日，将还， 潜诣取别，其门不复有宅，乃荒凉之地，烟草极目，惆怅而返。
    　　及京奏事毕，得归私第，诸赵竞怒曰：“女子诚陋拙，不足以奉事君子。然 已辱厚礼，亦宜敬之。夫上以承祖先，下以继后事，岂苟而已哉。奈何以妖术致 之万里而娱人之视听乎？朱李尚在，其筵足徵，何讳乎？”敬伯尽言之，且曰： “当此之时，敬伯亦自不测。此盖裴之道成矣，以此相炫也。”其妻亦记得裴言， 遂不复责。 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 裴谓敬伯曰 - A formal way of introducing direct speech, meaning 'Pei told Jingbo.'
    • 评公使车留此一宿 - Literal meaning 'the envoy's carriage staying here for one night.' '评公使车' refers to the official carriage used by an envoy.
    • 得无惊群将乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'would it not disturb the troops?' '得无' is an archaic way of expressing doubt or a rhetorical question.
    • 宜且就馆 - Means 'it is best to temporarily stay in the guesthouse.' '宜' suggests advisability, and '就馆' means to stay in a guesthouse.
    • 未赴阙闲时 - Means 'before the time of reporting to the imperial court.' '阙' refers to the imperial court or the emperor's palace.
    • 访我可也 - Means 'you may visit me.' '访' means to visit, and '可也' is an archaic way of saying 'you may.'
    • 尘路遐远 - Literally means 'dusty road far away,' referring to a long and arduous journey.
    • 万愁攻人 - Means 'a thousand worries assail one,' expressing deep anxiety or distress.
    • 努力自爱 - Means 'take care of yourself,' a common farewell expression.
    • 后五日 - Means 'five days later.'
    • 潜诣取别 - Means 'to secretly go to bid farewell.' '潜' means secretly, and '诣' means to go to.
    • 其门不复有宅 - Means 'the door no longer had a house,' indicating that the place had changed or disappeared.
    • 乃荒凉之地 - Means 'it became a desolate place,' describing the area as barren and uninhabited.
    • 烟草极目 - Means 'grass and tobacco plants stretch to the horizon,' describing a desolate and empty landscape.
    • 惆怅而返 - Means 'returned with a sense of sadness and longing.'
    • 及京奏事毕 - Means 'after finishing the report in the capital.' '及京' refers to the capital city.
    • 得归私第 - Means 'to return to one's private residence.' '私第' refers to one's own home.
    • 诸赵竞怒曰 - Means 'the various Zhao (family members or associates) angrily said.' '诸赵' likely refers to family members or associates.
    • 女子诚陋拙 - Means 'the woman is indeed ugly and clumsy.' '诚' means indeed, and '陋拙' means ugly and clumsy.
    • 不足以奉事君子 - Means 'is not fit to serve a gentleman.' '奉事' means to serve, and '君子' refers to a noble or virtuous person.
    • 已辱厚礼 - Means 'having already received great respect.' '辱' is a humble term meaning to receive or accept.
    • 亦宜敬之 - Means 'it is also proper to respect her.' '宜' means proper or appropriate.
    • 上以承祖先 - Means 'to honor the ancestors above.' '承' means to inherit or honor.
    • 下以继后事 - Means 'to continue the affairs of the future below.' '继' means to continue.
    • 岂苟而已哉 - Means 'how could it be just a casual matter?' '岂' is a rhetorical particle, and '苟' means casual or hasty.
    • 奈何以妖术致之万里而娱人之视听乎 - Means 'how could one use magic to bring her from afar to entertain people?' '妖术' means magic or sorcery, and '娱人之视听' means to entertain people's sight and hearing.
    • 朱李尚在 - Means 'the red and black (colors) are still there,' possibly referring to signs or symbols.
    • 其筵足徵 - Means 'the banquet is sufficient proof.' '筵' refers to a banquet, and '足徵' means sufficient proof.
    • 何讳乎 - Means 'why hide it?' '讳' means to hide or conceal.
    • 敬伯尽言之 - Means 'Jingbo spoke everything.' '尽言' means to speak everything.
    • 当此之时 - Means 'at that time.'
    • 敬伯亦自不测 - Means 'Jingbo was also uncertain.' '不测' means uncertain or unpredictable.
    • 此盖裴之道成矣 - Means 'this is because Pei's method has succeeded.' '道成' means the method has succeeded.
    • 以此相炫也 - Means 'to show off with this.' '相炫' means to show off.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 裴 - Likely a historical figure or a person of high status.
    • 敬伯 - Likely a subordinate or associate.
    • 阙 - Refers to the imperial court or the emperor's palace, indicating a high-level official context.
    • 私第 - Refers to one's own home, indicating a personal or familial context.
    • 诸赵 - Likely refers to family members or associates, possibly of high status.
    • 朱李 - Likely refers to colors or symbols, possibly indicating a sign or emblem.

    Proper Nouns

    • 裴 - Likely a historical figure or a person of high status.
    • 敬伯 - Likely a subordinate or associate.
    • 阙 - Refers to the imperial court or the emperor's palace.
    • 私第 - Refers to one's own home.
    • 诸赵 - Likely refers to family members or associates.

    Pei told Jingbo, "If the envoy's carriage stays here for one night, would it not disturb the troops? It is best to temporarily stay in the guesthouse, and you may visit me before the time of reporting to the imperial court. The dusty road is far away, and a thousand worries assail one; take care of yourself." Jingbo bowed and thanked him before departing. Five days later, as he was about to return, he secretly went to bid farewell. The door no longer had a house; it had become a desolate place, with grass and tobacco plants stretching to the horizon. He returned with a sense of sadness and longing.

    After finishing the report in the capital and returning to his private residence, the various Zhao angrily said, "The woman is indeed ugly and clumsy, not fit to serve a gentleman. Yet, having already received great respect, it is also proper to respect her. To honor the ancestors above and continue the affairs of the future below, how could it be just a casual matter? How could one use magic to bring her from afar to entertain people? The red and black (colors) are still there; the banquet is sufficient proof. Why hide it?" Jingbo spoke everything, and added, "At that time, Jingbo was also uncertain. This is because Pei's method has succeeded; he is showing off with this." His wife also remembered Pei's words and did not rebuke him further. [16]

    吁！神仙之变化，诚如此乎？将幻者鬻术以致惑乎？固非常智之所及。且夫 雀为蛤，雉为蜃，人为虎，腐草为萤，蜣螂为蝉，鲲为鹏，万物之变化，书传之 记者，不可以智达，况耳目之外乎！
    　　○韦氏
    　　京兆韦氏女者，既笄二年，母告之曰：“有秀才裴爽者，欲聘汝。”女笑曰： “非吾夫也。”母记之，虽媒媪日来，盛陈裴之才，其家甚慕之，然终不谐。又 一年，母曰：“有王悟者，前参京兆军事，其府之司录张审约者，汝之老舅也， 为王媒之，将聘汝矣。”女亦曰：“非也。”母又曰：“张亦熟我，又为王之媒 介也，其辞不虚矣。”亦终不谐。 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 吁！ - An exclamation expressing surprise or wonder.
