    • 神仙之變化 - Refers to the transformations of immortals or deities.
    • 誠如此乎 - Means 'is it truly like this?'
    • 將幻者鬻術以致惑乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'is it that the deceiver sells his tricks to cause confusion?'
    • 固非常智之所及 - Means 'it is certainly beyond the reach of ordinary wisdom.'
    • 雀為蛤，雉為蜃 - Refers to the transformation of sparrows into clams and pheasants into sea dragons, a common metaphor in Chinese literature.
    • 人為虎 - Means 'people turn into tigers.'
    • 腐草為萤 - Refers to the transformation of decaying grass into fireflies.
    • 蜣螂為蝉 - Means 'beetles turn into cicadas.'
    • 鲲為鹏 - Refers to the transformation of a giant fish into a giant bird, a famous allusion from the *Zhuangzi*.
    • 万物之變化 - Means 'the transformations of all things.'
    • 書傳之記者 - Refers to what is recorded in books and traditions.
    • 不可以智達 - Means 'cannot be understood by intelligence.'
    • 況耳目之外乎 - Means 'how much more so beyond what we can see and hear?'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神仙之變化 - A common theme in Chinese mythology and literature, often symbolizing the supernatural and the unpredictable nature of the world.
    • 雀為蛤，雉為蜃，人為虎，腐草為萤，蜣螂為蝉，鲲為鹏 - These transformations are metaphors found in classical Chinese literature, often used to illustrate the fluidity and unpredictability of nature and human affairs.
    • 万物之變化，書傳之記者 - Refers to the idea that many transformations are recorded in ancient texts, emphasizing the importance of tradition and history.
    • 京兆韋氏女 - The Wei family of Jingzhao was a prominent family in ancient China, known for their cultural and social status.
    • 裴爽 - A scholar, likely from a respected family, who is considered a suitable match.
    • 王悟 - Another potential suitor, with a background in military service, indicating a different social and professional background.

    Proper Nouns

    • 韋氏 - The Wei family.
    • 裴爽 - A scholar.
    • 王悟 - A man with a military background.
    • 張審約 - Zhang Shenyue, the recorder of the military office and the young woman's uncle.

    Oh! The transformations of immortals, are they truly like this? Or is it that the deceiver sells his tricks to cause confusion? It is certainly beyond the reach of ordinary wisdom. Let alone the transformations of sparrows into clams, pheasants into sea dragons, people into tigers, decaying grass into fireflies, beetles into cicadas, and the giant fish into the giant bird. The transformations of all things, as recorded in books and traditions, cannot be understood by intelligence, how much more so beyond what we can see and hear!

    The daughter of the Wei family of Jingzhao, two years after she reached the age of marriage, her mother told her: "There is a scholar named Pei Shuang who wishes to marry you." The girl laughed and said: "He is not my husband." Her mother remembered this, even though matchmakers came every day, greatly praising Pei's talents, his family greatly admired her, but it still did not work out. Another year later, her mother said: "There is a man named Wang Wu, who previously served in the military of Jingzhao. His recorder, Zhang Shenyue, is your uncle, and he is the matchmaker for Wang. He will marry you." The girl also said: "No." Her mother then said: "Zhang also knows me well, and he is the matchmaker for Wang. His words are not false." But it still did not work out. [17]

    又二年，进士张楚金求之。母以告之，女笑曰：“吾之夫乃此人也。”母许 之，遂择吉焉。既成礼讫，因其母徐问之，对曰：“吾此乃梦徵矣。然此生之事 皆见矣，岂独适楚金之先知乎！某既笄，梦年二十适清河楚金，以尚书节制广陵， 在镇七年，而楚金伏法。阖门皆死，惟某与新妇一人，生入掖庭，蔬食而役者十 八年，蒙诏放出。自午承命，日暮方出宫关，与新妇渡水，迨暗及滩，四顾将昏 然，不知所往，因与新妇相于滩于掩泣，相勉曰：‘此不可久立，宜速渡。’遂 南行。及岸数百步，有坏坊焉。 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 又二年 - Indicates a time lapse of two more years.
    • 进士 - A scholar-official who passed the imperial examination, indicating a high social status.
    • 母以告之 - The mother is a significant figure in the story, providing crucial information.
    • 吾之夫乃此人也 - The woman's identity as a wife is central to the narrative.
    • 母许之 - The mother's agreement is a key decision in the story.
    • 遂择吉焉 - Choosing an auspicious date is a traditional practice in Chinese weddings.
    • 既成礼讫 - The completion of the wedding ceremony marks a significant event.
    • 因其母徐问之 - The gentle questioning by the mother adds a personal touch to the narrative.
    • 对曰 - The woman's response is a pivotal moment in the story.
    • 吾此乃梦徵矣 - The dream is a supernatural element that guides the narrative.
    • 然此生之事皆见矣 - The woman's foresight into her future is a key plot point.
    • 岂独适楚金之先知乎 - The woman's knowledge extends beyond her immediate situation.
    • 某既笄 - The coming-of-age ceremony marks a transition in the woman's life.
    • 梦年二十适清河楚金 - The dream sets the stage for the woman's future.
    • 以尚书节制广陵 - Zhang Chujin's high position as a Shangshu (Minister of State) adds historical context.
    • 在镇七年 - The duration of Zhang Chujin's governance provides a timeline.
    • 而楚金伏法 - The execution of Zhang Chujin is a tragic event.
    • 阖门皆死 - The death of the entire family adds a dramatic element.
    • 惟某与新妇一人 - The survival of the woman and the new bride is a turning point.
    • 生入掖庭 - Entry into the imperial harem indicates a change in the woman's status.
    • 蔬食而役者十八年 - The long period of service in the harem adds depth to the narrative.
    • 蒙诏放出 - The imperial decree to release the woman is a significant event.
    • 自午承命 - The timing of the order adds a temporal dimension.
    • 日暮方出宫关 - The late departure from the palace gates adds a sense of urgency.
    • 与新妇渡水 - Crossing the water together is a symbolic act.
    • 迨暗及滩 - The darkness and the shoal add a mysterious and tense atmosphere.
    • 四顾将昏然 - The dusk setting adds a poetic touch.
    • 不知所往 - The uncertainty of their destination adds suspense.
    • 因与新妇相于滩上掩泣 - The weeping on the shoal adds an emotional depth.
    • 相勉曰 - The encouragement between the woman and the new bride adds a human element.
    • 此不可久立 - The realization that they cannot stay adds a sense of urgency.
    • 宜速渡 - The need to cross quickly adds a sense of action.
    • 遂南行 - The direction of travel adds a geographical context.
    • 及岸数百步 - The distance walked to the shore adds a sense of effort.
    • 有坏坊焉 - The ruined building adds a symbolic element.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 进士 - A scholar-official who passed the imperial examination, indicating a high social status.
    • 母以告之 - The mother is a significant figure in the story, providing crucial information.
    • 吾之夫乃此人也 - The woman's identity as a wife is central to the narrative.
    • 母许之 - The mother's agreement is a key decision in the story.
    • 遂择吉焉 - Choosing an auspicious date is a traditional practice in Chinese weddings.
    • 既成礼讫 - The completion of the wedding ceremony marks a significant event.
    • 因其母徐问之 - The gentle questioning by the mother adds a personal touch to the narrative.
    • 对曰 - The woman's response is a pivotal moment in the story.
    • 吾此乃梦徵矣 - The dream is a supernatural element that guides the narrative.
    • 然此生之事皆见矣 - The woman's foresight into her future is a key plot point.
    • 岂独适楚金之先知乎 - The woman's knowledge extends beyond her immediate situation.
    • 某既笄 - The coming-of-age ceremony marks a transition in the woman's life.
    • 梦年二十适清河楚金 - The dream sets the stage for the woman's future.
    • 以尚书节制广陵 - Zhang Chujin's high position as a Shangshu (Minister of State) adds historical context.
    • 在镇七年 - The duration of Zhang Chujin's governance provides a timeline.
    • 而楚金伏法 - The execution of Zhang Chujin is a tragic event.
    • 阖门皆死 - The death of the entire family adds a dramatic element.
    • 惟某与新妇一人 - The survival of the woman and the new bride is a turning point.
    • 生入掖庭 - Entry into the imperial harem indicates a change in the woman's status.
    • 蔬食而役者十八年 - The long period of service in the harem adds depth to the narrative.
    • 蒙诏放出 - The imperial decree to release the woman is a significant event.
    • 自午承命 - The timing of the order adds a temporal dimension.
    • 日暮方出宫关 - The late departure from the palace gates adds a sense of urgency.
    • 与新妇渡水 - Crossing the water together is a symbolic act.
    • 迨暗及滩 - The darkness and the shoal add a mysterious and tense atmosphere.
    • 四顾将昏然 - The dusk setting adds a poetic touch.
    • 不知所往 - The uncertainty of their destination adds suspense.
    • 因与新妇相于滩上掩泣 - The weeping on the shoal adds an emotional depth.
    • 相勉曰 - The encouragement between the woman and the new bride adds a human element.
    • 此不可久立 - The realization that they cannot stay adds a sense of urgency.
    • 宜速渡 - The need to cross quickly adds a sense of action.
    • 遂南行 - The direction of travel adds a geographical context.
    • 及岸数百步 - The distance walked to the shore adds a sense of effort.
    • 有坏坊焉 - The ruined building adds a symbolic element.

    After another two years, the Jinshi Zhang Chujin sought her. His mother told him about it, and the woman smiled and said, "My husband is this person." His mother agreed, and he then chose an auspicious date. After the ceremony was completed, he gently asked his mother, and she replied, "This is a sign from my dream. However, all the events of this life were seen, how could it be only the knowledge of being married to Zhang Chujin first? I had already reached the age of笄 (a coming-of-age ceremony for girls), and in my dream, I was twenty years old and married to Zhang Chujin of Qinghe. As a Shangshu (Minister of State) controlling Guangling, he governed for seven years, and then Zhang Chujin was executed. The entire family died, only I and the new bride survived, and we survived and entered the imperial harem, where we served for eighteen years, eating vegetarian food. We received an imperial decree to be released. From noon when I received the order, it was evening when I left the palace gates, and together with the new bride, we crossed the water. When it became dark and we reached the shoal, looking around, it was already dusk, and we did not know where to go. So we wept together on the shoal, encouraging each other, 'We cannot stay here for long, we should cross quickly.' We then went south. After walking several hundred steps to the shore, there was a ruined building there. [18]

    自入西门，随垣而北，其东大门屋，因造焉，又 无人而大开，遂入。及坏戟门，亦开，又入。逾屏回廊四合，有堂既扃。阶前有 四大樱桃树林，花发正茂。及月色满庭，似无人居，不知所告。因与新妇对卧阶 下。未几，有老人来诟逐，告以前情，遂去。又闻西廊步必履之声，有一少年郎 来诟，且呼老人令遂之。苦告之，少年郎低首而走。徐乃白衫素履，哭拜阶下曰： ‘某尚书之侄也。’乃恸哭曰：‘无处问耗，不知阿母与阿嫂至，乃自天降也。 此即旧宅，堂中所锁，无非旧物。’恸哭开户，宛如故居之地，居之九年前从化 （本句疑有脱误）。”其母大奇之。且人之荣悴，无非前定，素闻之矣，岂梦中 之信，又如此乎？乃心记之。 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 因造焉 - A somewhat archaic way of indicating construction or creation.
    • 似无人居 - It seemed as if no one lived there; a perception of the environment.
    • 乃恸哭曰 - And then he wept bitterly, saying; an expression of deep emotion.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 西门 - Refers to a western gate, likely part of a temple or compound.
    • 垣 - Refers to a wall, often used in religious or ceremonial contexts.
    • 戟门 - Refers to a gate adorned with halberds, a symbol of authority and protection.
    • 屏回廊四合 - Refers to a screen and a four-sided corridor, typical of traditional Chinese architecture.
    • 堂 - Refers to a hall, often a central space in a building.
    • 樱桃树林 - Refers to cherry tree groves, which may have symbolic significance in Chinese culture.
    • 月色满庭 - Refers to the moonlight filling a courtyard, a common theme in Chinese poetry and literature.
    • 新妇 - Refers to a newly married wife, indicating a personal relationship.
    • 尚书 - Refers to a high-ranking official, specifically the Minister of Works.
    • 阿母 - Refers to a mother, often used in a respectful or affectionate context.
    • 阿嫂 - Refers to a sister-in-law, indicating a familial relationship.
    • 旧宅 - Refers to an old house, likely a family home.
    • 旧物 - Refers to old possessions, often with sentimental value.

    Proper Nouns

    • 西门 - The western gate.
    • 垣 - The wall.
    • 戟门 - The gate adorned with halberds.
    • 屏回廊四合 - The screen and four-sided corridor.
    • 堂 - The hall.
    • 樱桃树林 - The cherry tree groves.
    • 新妇 - The newly married wife.
    • 尚书 - The Minister of Works.
    • 阿母 - The mother.
    • 阿嫂 - The sister-in-law.
    • 旧宅 - The old house.
    • 旧物 - The old possessions.

    Entering from the western gate, he followed the wall to the north. The eastern main gate, which was constructed there, was wide open and unoccupied, so he entered. When he reached the broken gate of the halberd, it was also open, and he entered again. Crossing the screen and the four-sided corridor, he found a hall that was already locked. In front of the steps, there were four large cherry tree groves, and the flowers were in full bloom. When the moonlight filled the courtyard, it seemed as if no one lived there, and he did not know what to report. So he lay down with his new wife at the base of the steps. Not long after, an old man came to scold and chase them away, and he told him of the previous situation before leaving. Then he heard the sound of footsteps and shoes in the western corridor, and a young man came to scold, calling out to the old man to follow. He pleaded with him, and the young man lowered his head and walked away. Slowly, he appeared in a white shirt and plain shoes, crying and bowing at the base of the steps, saying, "I am the nephew of a certain Minister of Works." And then he wept bitterly, saying, "There was nowhere to inquire about news, not knowing that my mother and sister-in-law had arrived, as if they had descended from heaven. This is the old house, and what was locked inside the hall were all old possessions." He opened the door and cried, finding it just as he remembered from nine years ago (this sentence may have an omission). His mother was greatly surprised. Moreover, the prosperity and decline of people are all predestined, and I have always heard this. How could the dream be so true, and yet so real? He then remembered it in his heart. [19]

    俄而楚金授钺广陵，神龙中以徐敬业有兴复之谋，连坐伏法，惟妻与妇□死， 配役掖庭十八年，则天因降诞日，大纵籍役者，得□例焉。午后受诏，及行，总 监绯阉走留食，候之。食毕，实将暮矣。其褰裳涉水而哭，及宅所在，无差梦焉。
    　　噫！梦信徵也，则前所叙扶风公之见，又何以偕焉。
    　　○元无有
    　　宝应中，有元无有，尝以仲春末独行维扬郊野。值日晚，风雨大至。时兵荒 后，人户逃窜，入路旁空庄。须臾霁止。斜月自出。无有憩北轩，忽闻西廊有人 行声，未几至堂中。有四人，衣冠皆异，相与谈谐，吟咏甚畅，乃云：“今夕如 秋，风月如此，吾党岂不为文，以纪平生之事？”其文即曰口号联句也。吟咏既 朗，无有听之甚悉。其一衣冠长人曰： [20]

    “齐纨鲁缟如霜雪，寥亮高声为子发。”
    　　其二黑衣冠短陋人曰：
    　　“嘉宾良会清夜时，辉煌灯烛我能持。”
    　　其三故弊黄衣冠人，亦短陋，诗曰：
    　　“清冷之泉俟朝汲，桑绠相牵常出入。”
    　　其四黑衣冠，身亦短陋，诗曰：
    　　“爨薪贮水常煎熬，充他口腹我为劳。”
    　　无有亦不以四人为异，四人亦不虞无有之在堂隍也，递相褒赏，羡其自负， 虽阮嗣宗《咏怀》亦不能加耳。四人迟明方归旧所，无有就寻之，堂中惟有故杵、 烛台、水桶、破铛，乃知四人即此物所为也。
    　　○郭代公
    　　代国公郭元振，开元中下第，自晋之汾，夜行阴晦失道。久而绝远有灯火之 光，以为人居也，迳往投之。八九里有宅，门宇甚峻。既入门，廊下及堂下灯烛 辉煌，牢馔罗列，若嫁女之家，而悄无人。公系马西廊前，历阶而升，徘徊堂上， 不知其何处也。俄闻堂中东阁有女子哭声，呜咽不已。 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 齐纨鲁缟如霜雪 - Metaphor comparing fine silk from Qi and Lu to snow and frost, emphasizing its purity and whiteness.
    • 寥亮高声为子发 - "寥亮" means clear and resonant, and "高声" means loud voice. Together, it describes a clear and loud voice, likely referring to a musical or poetic performance.
    • 嘉宾良会清夜时 - "嘉宾" means distinguished guests, "良会" means a good gathering, and "清夜时" means a clear night. It describes a gathering of distinguished guests during a clear night.
    • 辉煌灯烛我能持 - "辉煌" means brilliant, and "灯烛" means lamps and candles. It describes the ability to hold or manage brilliant lamps and candles.
    • 清冷之泉俟朝汲 - "清冷" means cool and clear, "泉" means spring, "俟" means wait, and "朝汲" means morning drawing. It describes a cool and clear spring waiting to be drawn in the morning.
    • 桑绠相牵常出入 - "桑绠" means mulberry rope, "相牵" means to be connected, and "常出入" means frequently entering and exiting. It describes a mulberry rope frequently being used to draw water from a well.
    • 爨薪贮水常煎熬 - "爨薪" means burning firewood, "贮水" means storing water, and "常煎熬" means frequently boiling. It describes the frequent boiling of water using firewood.
    • 充他口腹我为劳 - "充他口腹" means to fill others' stomachs, and "我为劳" means I am laboring. It describes the act of laboring to fill others' stomachs.
    • 递相褒赏 - "递相" means to reciprocally, and "褒赏" means to praise and reward. It describes mutual praise and reward.
    • 羡其自负 - "羡" means envy, and "自负" means self-pride. It describes envying their self-pride.
    • 虽阮嗣宗《咏怀》亦不能加耳 - "虽" means even if, "阮嗣宗" refers to Ruan Ji, a famous poet, "《咏怀》" is the title of his poem, and "亦不能加耳" means cannot surpass it. It suggests that even Ruan Ji's "Elegies" cannot surpass this.
    • 迟明方归旧所 - "迟明" means late morning, and "方归旧所" means finally return to the old place. It describes returning to the old place late in the morning.
    • 堂中惟有故杵、烛台、水桶、破铛 - "堂中" means in the hall, "惟有" means only have, "故杵" means old pestle, "烛台" means candle holder, "水桶" means water bucket, and "破铛" means broken pot. It describes the only items left in the hall being an old pestle, candle holder, water bucket, and broken pot.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 齐纨鲁缟 - Fine silk produced in Qi and Lu, known for its quality and purity.
    • 阮嗣宗《咏怀》 - Ruan Ji, a famous poet of the Wei-Jin period, and his work "Elegies," which is renowned for its depth and emotional resonance.
    • 郭代公 - Duke Guo, a historical figure, likely a reference to a noble or official.
    • 开元中 - The reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for its cultural and economic prosperity.
    • 晋之汾 - A reference to the area around the Fen River in Shanxi province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 齐纨鲁缟 - Fine silk from Qi and Lu.
    • 阮嗣宗 - Ruan Ji, a famous poet of the Wei-Jin period.
    • 《咏怀》 - The title of Ruan Ji's famous poem.
    • 郭代公 - Duke Guo, a historical figure.
    • 开元中 - The reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 晋之汾 - A reference to the area around the Fen River in Shanxi province.

    The fine silk from Qi and Lu is as pure as frost and snow, and a clear and resonant voice is raised for you.

    The second person, dressed in black and short and unremarkable, said:
    "At a gathering of distinguished guests during a clear night, I can manage brilliant lamps and candles."

    The third person, dressed in an old yellow robe and also short and unremarkable, recited:
    "A cool and clear spring waits to be drawn in the morning, with a mulberry rope frequently being used to draw water."

    The fourth person, also dressed in black and short and unremarkable, recited:
    "I burn firewood to store water and frequently boil it, laboring to fill others' stomachs."

    The host did not find these four people strange, and the four people did not suspect that the host was in the hall. They praised each other, envying their self-pride, even though Ruan Ji's "Elegies" could not surpass it. The four people returned to their old place late in the morning, and the host searched for them, finding only an old pestle, candle holder, water bucket, and broken pot in the hall. Thus, it was known that the four people were these objects.

    ○ Duke Guo
    Duke Guo Yuanzhen, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, failed the imperial examination and traveled from Jin to Fen. He was walking at night in the dark and lost his way. After a long time, he saw a distant light of lamps, thinking it was a human dwelling, and went straight to it. After eight or nine miles, he reached a mansion with a very imposing gate. After entering, the corridors and hall were brightly lit with lamps and candles, and the food was arranged as if it were a wedding feast, yet there was no one around. The duke tied his horse in front of the western corridor, climbed the steps, and wandered around in the hall, not knowing where he was. Soon, he heard the sound of a woman crying in the east pavilion, sobbing endlessly. [21]

    公问曰：“堂中泣者，人 耶，鬼耶？何陈设如此，无人而独泣？”曰：“妾此乡之祠有乌将军者，能祸福 人，每岁求偶于乡人，乡人必择处女之美者而嫁焉。妾虽陋拙，父利乡人之五百 缗，潜以应选。今夕，乡人之女并为游宴者，到是，醉妾此室，共锁而去，以适 于将军者也。今父母弃之就死，而令惴惴哀惧。君诚人耶，能相救免，毕身为扫 除之妇，以奉指使。”公愤曰：“其来当何时？”曰：“二更。”公曰：“吾忝 为大丈夫也，必力救之。如不得，当杀身以徇汝，终不使汝枉死于淫鬼之手也。” 女泣少止，于是坐于西阶上，移其马于堂北，令一仆侍立于前，若为宾而待之。 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 公问曰 - Respectful title for an official or gentleman, 'asked, saying.'
    • 堂中泣者，人耶，鬼耶？ - In the hall, the weeping one, is it a human or a ghost?
    • 何陈设如此，无人而独泣？ - Why is it set up like this, no one is here but she is weeping alone.
    • 妾此乡之祠有乌将军者 - In this village's shrine, there is a deity named '乌将军.'
    • 能祸福人 - Can bring misfortune or blessings to people.
    • 每岁求偶于乡人 - Every year, seek a spouse from the villagers.
    • 乡人必择处女之美者而嫁焉 - The villagers must choose the most beautiful unmarried girls and marry them.
    • 妾虽陋拙 - Though I am ugly and clumsy.
    • 父利乡人之五百缗 - Father profits from the villagers' five hundred strings of cash.
    • 潜以应选 - Secretly to meet the selection.
    • 今夕，乡人之女并为游宴者 - Tonight, the villagers' daughters are all participating in a feast.
    • 到是，醉妾此室，共锁而去，以适于将军者也 - When they arrived, they got me drunk in this room, locked me up and left, to take me to the general.
    • 今父母弃之就死 - Now, parents abandon me to die.
    • 而令惴惴哀惧 - And make me fearful and grieving.
    • 君诚人耶，能相救免 - You are truly a human, can save me.
    • 毕身为扫除之妇 - All my life as a cleaning woman.
    • 以奉指使 - To serve commands.
    • 吾忝为大丈夫也 - I, unworthy, am a man of virtue.
    • 必力救之 - Must rescue her with all my might.
    • 如不得，当杀身以徇汝 - If I fail, will kill myself to save you.
    • 终不使汝枉死于淫鬼之手也 - Will never let you die in vain at the hands of a lustful ghost.
    • 女泣少止 - Her weeping stopped a little.
    • 于是坐于西阶上 - Then sat on the western steps.
    • 移其马于堂北 - Moved his horse to the north of the hall.
    • 令一仆侍立于前 - Made a servant stand in front.
    • 若为宾而待之 - As if treating him as a guest.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 乌将军 - Deity worshipped in a village shrine, capable of bringing misfortune or blessings.
    • 五百缗 - Large sum of money, equivalent to five hundred strings of cash, indicating a significant dowry.
    • 游宴 - Festive gathering, often involving drinking and entertainment.
    • 淫鬼 - Lustful ghost, often associated with malevolent spirits in Chinese folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 乌将军 - Deity worshipped in a village shrine.
    • 五百缗 - Large sum of money, equivalent to five hundred strings of cash.
    • 淫鬼 - Lustful ghost, often associated with malevolent spirits in Chinese folklore.

    The gentleman asked, "Who is weeping in the hall? Is it a human or a ghost? Why is it set up like this, with no one here but she is weeping alone?" She replied, "In this village's shrine, there is a deity known as the Black General, who can bring misfortune or blessings to people. Every year, he seeks a spouse from the villagers, and the villagers must choose the most beautiful unmarried girls and marry them. Although I am ugly and clumsy, my father profited from the villagers' offer of five hundred strings of cash and secretly agreed to my being chosen. Tonight, the villagers' daughters, who were all participating in a feast, came to this room, got me drunk, locked me up, and left to take me to the Black General. Now, my parents have abandoned me to die, and I am filled with fear and grief. If you are truly a human, can you save me? I will serve as a cleaning woman for the rest of my life and obey your commands." The gentleman angrily said, "When will he come?" She replied, "At the second watch." He said, "I am unworthy to be a man of virtue, but I will do my utmost to save you. If I fail, I will kill myself to save you, and I will never let you die in vain at the hands of a lustful ghost." The woman's weeping stopped a little. Then, he sat on the western steps, moved his horse to the north of the hall, and had a servant stand in front of him, treating him as a guest. [22]

    未几，火光照耀，车马骈阗，二紫衣吏入而复出，曰：“相公在此。”逡巡， 二黄衣吏入而出，亦曰：“相公在此。”公私心独喜：“吾当为宰相，必胜此鬼 矣。”既而将军渐下，导吏复告之。将军曰：“入。”有戈剑弓矢翼引以入，即 东阶下，公使仆前曰：“郭秀才见。”遂行揖。将军曰：“秀才安得到此？”曰： “闻将军今夕嘉礼，愿为小相耳。”将军者喜而延坐，与对食，言笑极欢。公于 囊中有利刀，思取刺之，乃问曰：“将军曾食鹿腊乎？”曰：“此地难遇。 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 未幾 - Temporal expression meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly thereafter,' indicating a brief passage of time.
    • 車馬骈阗 - Vivid expression meaning 'carriages and horses crowded together,' used to describe a bustling or crowded scene.
    • 逡巡 - Adverb meaning 'hesitantly' or 'with hesitation,' describing a cautious or uncertain action.
    • 私心獨喜 - Phrase meaning 'secretly delighted' or 'privately rejoiced,' indicating a personal, hidden emotion.
    • 吾當為宰相 - Statement meaning 'I shall become the prime minister,' expressing a confident expectation.
    • 必勝此鬼矣 - Phrase meaning 'I will surely overcome this ghost,' indicating determination and resolve.
    • 既而 - Temporal expression meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly thereafter,' similar to '未幾.'
    • 導吏復告之 - Phrase meaning 'the guide officials reported to him again,' describing a repeated action.
    • 秀才安得到此 - Question meaning 'how did you come to be here?' expressing curiosity or suspicion.
    • 願為小相耳 - Phrase meaning 'I merely wish to be a minor official,' indicating a modest request or aspiration.
    • 言笑极欢 - Phrase meaning 'they spoke and laughed very joyfully,' describing a lively and pleasant interaction.
    • 遂行揖 - Phrase meaning 'he then bowed,' describing a formal gesture of respect.
    • 無不利於事也 - Phrase meaning 'nothing is unhelpful in matters,' suggesting that all actions or pursuits are valuable.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宰相 - Refers to the prime minister or chief minister of the state, a high-ranking official in the Chinese government.
    • 鬼 - Refers to a ghost or spirit, often associated with supernatural or otherworldly entities.
    • 嘉礼 - Refers to a joyous or festive occasion, often associated with celebrations or ceremonies.
    • 戈剑弓矢 - Refers to weapons such as spears, swords, bows, and arrows, indicating a martial or defensive context.
    • 秀才 - Refers to a scholar or student, often associated with the lower ranks of the imperial examination system.
    • 小相 - Refers to a minor official or assistant, often associated with administrative or clerical duties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宰相 - The title of the prime minister or chief minister of the state.
    • 鬼 - The term for a ghost or spirit.
    • 嘉礼 - The term for a joyous or festive occasion.
    • 戈剑弓矢 - The terms for weapons such as spears, swords, bows, and arrows.
    • 秀才 - The term for a scholar or student.
    • 小相 - The term for a minor official or assistant.

    Shortly thereafter, the light of the fire shone brightly, and carriages and horses were crowded together. Two officials in purple robes entered and then exited, saying, "The prime minister is here." Hesitantly, two officials in yellow robes entered and then exited, also saying, "The prime minister is here." The scholar secretly delighted, thinking, "I shall become the prime minister and will surely overcome this ghost." Soon after, the general gradually descended, and the guide officials reported to him again. The general said, "Enter." With spears, swords, bows, and arrows as accompaniment, the scholar then bowed and said, "Deacon Guo is here." He proceeded to pay his respects. The general asked, "How did you come to be here?" The scholar replied, "I heard that you are having a joyous occasion tonight and merely wish to be a minor official." The general was pleased and invited him to sit, and they ate together, speaking and laughing very joyfully. The scholar had a sharp knife in his bag and thought to kill him, so he asked, "Have you ever eaten deer jerky?" The general replied, "It is hard to find here." [23]

    ”公 曰：“某有少须珍者，得自御厨，愿削以献。”将军者大悦。公乃起，取鹿腊并 小刀，因削之，置一小器，令自取。将军喜，引手取之，不疑其他。公伺其无机， 乃投其脯，捉其腕而断之。将军失声而走，导从之吏，一时惊散。公执其手，脱 衣缠之，令仆夫出望之，寂无所见，乃启门谓泣者曰：“将军之腕已在于此矣。 寻其血踪，死亦不久。汝既获免，可出就食。”泣者乃出，年可十七八，而甚佳 丽，拜于公前，曰：“誓为仆妾。”公勉谕焉。天方曙，开视其手，则猪蹄也。 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 某有少须珍者 - Polite expression meaning 'I have something rare and precious that I wish to offer.'
    • 得自御厨 - Indicates that the item comes from the imperial kitchen, emphasizing its exclusivity.
    • 愿削以献 - A humble expression meaning 'I would like to carve and present it.'
    • 将军者大悦 - A formal expression indicating that the general is very pleased.
    • 公乃起，取鹿腊并小刀 - Describes the process: 'The official then rose, took the deer jerky and a small knife.'
    • 因削之，置一小器 - Describes the process of carving and placing it in a small container.
    • 令自取 - A polite expression meaning 'allow him to take it himself.'
    • 将军喜，引手取之 - Describes the general's reaction: 'The general was pleased and reached out to take it.'
    • 不疑其他 - Indicates that the general had no suspicion of any other intention.
    • 公伺其无机，乃投其脯，捉其腕而断之 - Describes the official's action: 'The official waited for the right moment, then threw the jerky, grabbed his wrist, and broke it.'
    • 将军失声而走 - Describes the general's reaction: 'The general cried out and ran away.'
    • 导从之吏，一时惊散 - Describes the reaction of the general's attendants: 'The officials accompanying him were suddenly scattered in alarm.'
    • 公执其手，脱衣缠之 - Describes the official's action: 'The official held his hand, removed his clothes, and wrapped them around it.'
    • 令仆夫出望之，寂无所见 - Describes the official's action: 'He ordered the driver to look outside, but saw nothing.'
    • 乃启门谓泣者曰 - Describes the official's action: 'He then opened the door and said to the weeping person.'
    • 将军之腕已在于此矣 - A formal expression meaning 'The general's wrist is now here.'
    • 寻其血踪，死亦不久 - A formal expression meaning 'Following the blood trail, death will not be far off.'
    • 汝既获免，可出就食 - A formal expression meaning 'Since you have been spared, you may go out to eat.'
    • 誓为仆妾 - A formal expression meaning 'I swear to be your servant and concubine.'
    • 公勉谕焉 - A formal expression meaning 'The official encouraged and advised her.'
    • 天方曙 - A formal expression meaning 'It was just dawn.'
    • 开视其手，则猪蹄也 - Describes the final observation: 'When he opened his hand, it was a pig's paw.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 御厨 - Refers to the imperial kitchen, indicating the high status and exclusivity of the item.
    • 鹿腊 - Refers to deer jerky, a delicacy often associated with nobility and feasts.
    • 小刀 - A small knife, likely used for carving or slicing.
    • 将军 - Refers to a high-ranking military officer, indicating the social and political context.
    • 公 - Refers to an official or noble, indicating the hierarchical structure of the society.
    • 仆夫 - Refers to a driver or servant, indicating the presence of a retinue.
    • 泣者 - Refers to a weeping person, likely a servant or concubine.
    • 猪蹄 - Refers to a pig's paw, a common food item, often used in cooking.

    Proper Nouns

    • 御厨 - The imperial kitchen.
    • 鹿腊 - Deer jerky.
    • 小刀 - A small knife.
    • 将军 - A high-ranking military officer.
    • 公 - An official or noble.
    • 仆夫 - A driver or servant.
    • 泣者 - A weeping person, likely a servant or concubine.
    • 猪蹄 - A pig's paw.

    The official said, "I have something rare and precious, obtained from the imperial kitchen, and I wish to carve and present it." The general was very pleased. The official then rose, took the deer jerky and a small knife, carved it, placed it in a small container, and allowed the general to take it himself. The general was pleased and reached out to take it, with no suspicion of any other intention. The official waited for the right moment, then threw the jerky, grabbed the general's wrist, and broke it. The general cried out and ran away, and the officials accompanying him were suddenly scattered in alarm. The official held his hand, removed his clothes, and wrapped them around it. He ordered the driver to look outside, but saw nothing. He then opened the door and said to the weeping person, "The general's wrist is now here. Following the blood trail, death will not be far off. Since you have been spared, you may go out to eat." The weeping person then came out, about seventeen or eighteen years old, and very beautiful. She knelt before the official and said, "I swear to be your servant and concubine." The official encouraged and advised her. It was just dawn, and when he opened his hand, it was a pig's paw. [24]

    俄闻哭泣之声渐近，乃女之父母兄弟及乡中耆老，相与舁榇而来，将收其尸 以备殡殓。见公及女，乃生人也。咸惊以问之，公具告焉。乡老共怒残其神，曰： “乌将军，此乡镇神，乡人奉之久矣，岁配以女，才无他虞。此礼少迟，即风雨 雷雹为虐。奈何失路之客，而伤我明神，致暴于人，此乡何负？当杀公以祭乌将 军，不尔，亦缚送本县。”挥少年将令执公，公谕之曰：“尔徒老于年，未老于 事。 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 俄闻哭泣之声渐近 - 俄 means 'soon' or 'in a short while'; 闻 means 'to hear'; 哭泣之声 means 'the sound of crying'; 渐近 means 'gradually approaching'. This phrase conveys the idea that the sound of crying was heard getting closer over time.
    • 乃女之父母兄弟及乡中耆老 - 乃 means 'is' or 'are'; 女之父母兄弟 means 'the girl's parents and siblings'; 乡中耆老 means 'elders of the village'. This phrase identifies the group of people approaching.
    • 相与舁榇而来 - 相与 means 'together' or 'in unison'; 舁 means 'to carry on a pole'; 榇 means 'coffin'. This phrase describes the group carrying the coffin together.
    • 将收其尸以备殡殓 - 将 means 'will' or 'going to'; 收其尸 means 'collect the body'; 以备 means 'to prepare'; 殡殓 means 'funeral rites'. This phrase indicates their intention to perform the funeral rites.
    • 咸惊以问之 - 咸 means 'all' or 'everyone'; 惊 means 'surprised' or 'astonished'; 以问之 means 'to ask about it'. This phrase describes the reaction of the villagers upon seeing the living individuals.
    • 公具告焉 - 公 means 'the gentleman' or 'the man'; 具告 means 'to fully inform'; 焉 is a particle that can mean 'there' or 'about it'. This phrase indicates that the man provided a detailed explanation.
    • 乡老共怒残其神 - 乡老 means 'village elders'; 共 means 'all' or 'together'; 怒 means 'angry'; 残 means 'to harm' or 'to damage'; 其神 means 'their god'. This phrase describes the elders' anger towards the god.
    • 乌将军，此乡镇神 - 乌将军 is a proper name; 此 means 'this'; 镇 means 'village'; 神 means 'god'. This phrase identifies the god as the village deity.
    • 乡人奉之久矣 - 乡人 means 'villagers'; 奉 means 'to worship' or 'to honor'; 之 is a particle that can mean 'it' or 'him'; 久矣 means 'for a long time'. This phrase indicates that the villagers have been worshipping the god for a long time.
    • 岁配以女，才无他虞 - 岁 means 'yearly' or 'annually'; 配 means 'to marry' or 'to provide'; 以女 means 'with a girl'; 才 means 'only' or 'just'; 无他虞 means 'no other worry'. This phrase describes the annual practice of providing a girl to the god to avoid other calamities.
    • 此礼少迟，即风雨雷雹为虐 - 此礼 means 'this ritual'; 少迟 means 'slightly delayed'; 即 means 'if' or 'when'; 风雨雷雹 means 'wind, rain, thunder, and hail'; 为虐 means 'to cause disaster'. This phrase warns that a slight delay in the ritual can lead to natural disasters.
    • 奈何失路之客，而伤我明神，致暴于人 - 奈何 means 'what to do with' or 'how to deal with'; 失路之客 means 'a lost traveler'; 而 is a conjunction that can mean 'but' or 'and'; 伤 means 'to harm'; 我明神 means 'our clear god'; 致暴于人 means 'to bring violence upon people'. This phrase expresses the villagers' frustration with the traveler causing harm to their god and bringing violence upon them.
    • 此乡何负 - 此乡 means 'this village'; 何 means 'what'; 负 means 'debt' or 'wrong'. This phrase asks what the village has done wrong.
    • 当杀公以祭乌将军 - 当 means 'should' or 'must'; 杀 means 'to kill'; 公 means 'the gentleman' or 'the man'; 以 means 'to'; 祭乌将军 means 'to sacrifice to the General of the Crows'. This phrase suggests that the man should be killed as a sacrifice to the god.
    • 不尔，亦缚送本县 - 不尔 means 'if not'; 亦 means 'also' or 'even'; 缚 means 'to bind'; 送 means 'to send'; 本县 means 'our county'. This phrase suggests that if the man is not killed, he should be bound and sent to the county.
    • 挥少年将令执公 - 挥 means 'to wave' or 'to gesture'; 少年 means 'young men'; 将 means 'to command'; 令 means 'to order'; 执公 means 'to seize the gentleman'. This phrase describes the elders gesturing to the young men to seize the man.
    • 公谕之曰 - 公 means 'the gentleman' or 'the man'; 谕 means 'to instruct' or 'to explain'; 之 is a particle that can mean 'them' or 'to them'. This phrase indicates that the man explained something to the villagers.
    • 尔徒老于年，未老于事 - 尔 means 'you'; 徒 means 'only' or 'just'; 老于年 means 'old in age'; 未 means 'not yet' or 'not'; 老于事 means 'experienced in affairs'. This phrase suggests that while the villagers are old in age, they are not experienced in handling such matters.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 乌将军 - A proper name for the village deity, often associated with crows in Chinese folklore.
    • 镇神 - Refers to the village deity, a local god worshipped by the villagers.
    • 岁配以女 - An annual ritual where a girl is provided to the god, a common practice in ancient Chinese folk religion to appease deities and prevent natural disasters.
    • 风雨雷雹 - Natural elements often associated with divine wrath or natural disasters in Chinese mythology.
    • 失路之客 - Refers to a traveler who has lost his way, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 乌将军 - The name of the village deity.
    • 镇神 - The village deity.
    • 本县 - The county to which the man would be sent if not killed.

    Soon, the sound of weeping grew closer, and it turned out to be the girl's parents, brothers, and the elders of the village, all carrying the coffin together, intending to collect the body for burial. Seeing the gentleman and the girl, they realized they were still alive. Everyone was surprised and asked about it, and the gentleman explained in detail. The village elders, enraged by the desecration of their god, said, "General of the Crows, this is our village deity. The villagers have worshipped him for a long time. We provide him with a girl each year, and only then do we avoid other calamities. If this ritual is slightly delayed, wind, rain, thunder, and hail will wreak havoc. How can a lost traveler harm our clear god and bring violence upon our people? What has this village done wrong? We should kill you to sacrifice to General of the Crows. If not, we will bind you and send you to our county." They gestured to the young men to seize the gentleman, but he explained to them, "You may be old in age, but you are not experienced in handling such matters." [25]

    我天下之达理者，尔众听吾言。夫神，承天而为镇也，不若诸侯受命于天子 而疆理天下乎？”曰：“然。”公曰：“使诸侯渔色于中国，天子不怒乎？残虐 于人，天子不伐乎？诚使尔呼将军者，真神明也，神固无猪蹄，天岂使淫妖之兽 乎？且淫妖之兽，天地之罪畜也，吾执正以诛之，岂不可乎！尔曹无正人，使尔 少女年年横死于妖畜，积罪动天。安知天不使吾雪焉？从吾言，当为尔除之，永 无聘礼之患，如何？”乡人悟而喜曰：“愿从公命。” [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 我天下之达理者 - Formal address meaning 'I, who understand the principles of the world.'
    • 尔众听吾言 - 'you all listen to my words,' a formal way of addressing an audience.
    • 夫神，承天而为镇也 - Classical phrase meaning 'the deity, who upholds the heavens and stabilizes the earth.'
    • 不若诸侯受命于天子而疆理天下乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is it not better for the feudal lords to receive their mandate from the Son of Heaven and govern the realm?'
    • 使诸侯渔色于中国 - 'if the feudal lords indulge in lust within the central lands.'
    • 天子不怒乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'would the Son of Heaven not be angry?'
    • 残虐于人 - 'to oppress and harm people.'
    • 天子不伐乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'would the Son of Heaven not punish?'
    • 诚使尔呼将军者，真神明也 - Conditional phrase meaning 'if indeed the one you call 'General' is truly a deity.'
    • 神固无猪蹄 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'a deity would not have pig's feet.'
    • 天岂使淫妖之兽乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'would Heaven send a lustful and monstrous beast?'
    • 且淫妖之兽，天地之罪畜也 - Judgmental phrase meaning 'moreover, a lustful and monstrous beast is the guilty creature of heaven and earth.'
    • 吾执正以诛之 - 'I will uphold righteousness and punish it.'
    • 尔曹无正人 - 'you people have no righteous ones.'
    • 使尔少女年年横死于妖畜 - 'if your young girls die year after year at the hands of this monstrous beast.'
    • 积罪动天 - 'accumulated sins move Heaven.'
    • 安知天不使吾雪焉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how do you know that Heaven does not intend for me to avenge this?'
    • 从吾言，当为尔除之 - 'if you follow my words, I will eliminate it for you.'
    • 永无聘礼之患 - 'you will never again suffer from the burden of bride prices.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天子 - Refers to the Son of Heaven, the emperor, who is the supreme ruler in the Mandate of Heaven system.
    • 诸侯 - Refers to feudal lords, who were vassals of the emperor and governed their own territories.
    • 神 - Refers to a deity, often associated with divine authority in Chinese cosmology.
    • 淫妖之兽 - A metaphor for a monstrous and lustful entity, possibly a demon or a supernatural being.
    • 聘礼 - Refers to the bride price, a traditional custom in ancient China where the groom's family paid a sum to the bride's family.

    Proper Nouns

    • 天子 - The Son of Heaven, the emperor.
    • 诸侯 - Feudal lords.
    • 神 - Deity.
    • 淫妖之兽 - Lustful and monstrous beast.
    • 聘礼 - Bride price.

    I, who understand the principles of the world, address you all. The deity, who upholds the heavens and stabilizes the earth, is not as worthy as the feudal lords who receive their mandate from the Son of Heaven and govern the realm, is he not? The people responded affirmatively. I said, "If the feudal lords indulge in lust within the central lands, would the Son of Heaven not be angry? If they oppress and harm people, would the Son of Heaven not punish? If indeed the one you call 'General' is truly a deity, a deity would not have pig's feet. Would Heaven send a lustful and monstrous beast? Moreover, a lustful and monstrous beast is the guilty creature of heaven and earth. I will uphold righteousness and punish it, is this not possible! You people have no righteous ones. If your young girls die year after year at the hands of this monstrous beast, accumulated sins move Heaven. How do you know that Heaven does not intend for me to avenge this? If you follow my words, I will eliminate it for you, and you will never again suffer from the burden of bride prices. What do you say?" The villagers understood and were pleased, saying, "We wish to follow your command." [26]

    乃令数百人，执弓矢刀枪锹之属，环而自随，寻血而行。才二十里，血入 大冢穴中。因围而属刂之，应手渐大如瓮口，公令束薪燃火投入照之。其中若大 室，见一大猪，无前左蹄，血卧其地，突烟走出，毙于围中。
    　　乡人翻共相庆，会钱以酬公。公不受，曰：“吾为人除害，非鬻猎者。”得 免之女辞其父母亲族曰：“多幸为人，托质血属，闺闱未出，固无可杀之罪。今 者贪钱五十万，以嫁妖兽，忍锁而去，岂人所宜！若非郭公之仁勇，宁有今日？ 是妾死于父母而生于郭公也。请从郭公，不复以旧乡为念矣。”泣拜而从公，公 多歧援谕，止之不获，遂纳为侧室，生子数人。 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 乃令数百人，执弓矢刀枪锹之属，环而自随 - Command to gather a large group of people armed with various weapons and tools, forming a circle around themselves.
    • 寻血而行 - Follow the trail of blood.
    • 应手渐大如瓮口 - Gradually becomes larger like the mouth of a jar.
    • 束薪燃火投入照之 - Bundle firewood, light it, and throw it in to illuminate.
    • 无前左蹄 - Without a left front hoof.
    • 突烟走出 - Bursting out of the smoke.
    • 毙于围中 - Died within the enclosure.
    • 翻共相庆 - They all rejoiced together.
    • 会钱以酬公 - Gathered money to reward the official.
    • 吾为人除害，非鬻猎者 - Eliminate harm for people, not to sell hunting.
    • 多幸为人，托质血属，闺闱未出，固无可杀之罪 - Fortunate to be human, entrusted with blood relations, and have not yet left the inner chambers, so there is no fault in killing.
    • 贪钱五十万，以嫁妖兽，忍锁而去，岂人所宜 - Greedy for fifty thousand coins, to marry an evil beast, endure the lock and leave, is this suitable for a person?
    • 若非郭公之仁勇，宁有今日 - If not for the benevolence and courage of Official Guo, how could I have this day?
    • 请从郭公，不复以旧乡为念矣 - I will follow Official Guo, no longer thinking of my old hometown.
    • 多歧援谕，止之不获 - Many attempts to persuade and stop her were unsuccessful.
    • 遂纳为侧室，生子数人 - Eventually took her as a concubine and they had several children.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郭公 - Refers to an official named Guo, likely a local magistrate or a prominent figure in the community.
    • 妖兽 - Refers to a mythical or evil beast, often used in folklore to represent a dangerous or supernatural entity.
    • 侧室 - Refers to a concubine or secondary wife in ancient Chinese society.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sequence of actions must be read as a continuous process: ordering a large group of people to form a circle around themselves with various weapons and tools, and then following the trail of blood.
    • This phrase describes the distance traveled and the location where the blood leads: only twenty li, and the blood leads into a large tomb's cave.
    • This phrase describes the action of surrounding and digging, with the result being a gradual enlargement: surround and dig, immediately becomes larger like the mouth of a jar.
    • This phrase describes the action of lighting a fire and throwing it in to illuminate: the official ordered bundled firewood to be lit and thrown in to illuminate it.
    • This phrase describes the discovery of a large pig inside the tomb, its condition, and its death: inside it is like a large room, a large pig is seen, without a left front hoof, lying on the ground in blood, bursting out of the smoke, died within the enclosure.
    • This phrase describes the reaction of the villagers and their reward for the official: the villagers rejoiced together, gathered money to reward the official.
    • This phrase explains the official's motivation: I am here to eliminate harm for people, not to sell hunting.
    • This phrase explains the woman's background and her innocence: fortunate to be human, entrusted with blood relations, have not yet left the inner chambers, so there is no fault in killing.
    • This phrase describes the woman's decision to marry the beast: greedy for fifty thousand coins, to marry an evil beast, endure the lock and leave, is this suitable for a person?
    • This phrase expresses gratitude to the official: if not for the benevolence and courage of Official Guo, how could I have this day?
    • This phrase expresses the woman's decision to follow the official: I will follow Official Guo, no longer thinking of my old hometown.
    • This phrase describes the woman's decision to marry the beast: many attempts to persuade and stop her were unsuccessful.
    • This phrase describes the woman's decision to marry the beast: eventually took her as a concubine and they had several children.

    The official ordered hundreds of people, armed with bows, arrows, swords, spears, shovels, and other tools, to form a circle around themselves and follow the trail of blood. After traveling only twenty li, the blood led them to a large tomb's cave. They surrounded the area and began digging, and as they dug, the opening gradually widened like the mouth of a jar. The official then ordered bundled firewood to be lit and thrown into the opening to illuminate it. Inside, they found a large room containing a pig with no left front hoof, lying on the ground in blood. The pig burst out of the smoke and died within the enclosure.

    The villagers rejoiced together and gathered money to reward the official. The official declined, saying, "I am here to eliminate harm for people, not to sell hunting." The woman who had been spared, addressing her parents and relatives, said, "I am fortunate to be human, entrusted with blood relations, and have not yet left the inner chambers, so there is no fault in killing. Greedy for fifty thousand coins, I chose to marry an evil beast, enduring the lock and leaving, is this suitable for a person? If not for the benevolence and courage of Official Guo, how could I have this day? I have died to my parents and been reborn through Official Guo. I will follow Official Guo, no longer thinking of my old hometown." She wept and bowed, following the official. Despite many attempts to persuade and stop her, the official eventually took her as a concubine, and they had several children. [27]

    公之贵也，皆任大官之位。事已前定，虽生远地，而至于鬼神终不能害，明 矣。
    　　○来君绰
    　　隋炀帝征辽，十二军尽没，总管来护坐法受戮，炀帝尽欲诛其诸子。君绰忧 惧连诛，因与秀才罗巡、罗逖、李万进结为奔走之友，共亡命至海州。
    　　夜黑迷路，路旁有灯火，因与共投之。扣门数下，有一苍头迎拜君绰，君绰 因问：“此是谁家？”答曰：“科斗郎君，姓威，即当府秀才也。”遂启门，又 自闭，敲中门，曰：“蜗儿，外有四五个客。”蜗儿即又一苍头也。遂开门，秉 烛引客就馆客位，床榻茵褥甚备。俄有二小童持烛自中门出，曰：“六郎子出来。” 君绰等降阶见主人。主人辞彩朗然，文辩纷错，自通姓名曰“威污蠖”。叙寒温 讫，揖客由阼阶，坐曰：“污蠖忝以本州乡赋，得与足下同声，清宵良会，殊是 所愿。”即命酒合坐。渐至酣畅，谈谑交至，众所不能对。 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 公之贵也，皆任大官之位 - Formal statement indicating that the person's rise to prominence was marked by holding high-ranking official positions.
    • 事已前定 - Means 'the matter was predetermined,' suggesting that the person's fate was decided in advance.
    • 雖生遠地，而至於鬼神終不能害 - Conditional phrase meaning 'even if born far away, the gods and spirits could not harm him,' emphasizing the inevitability of the person's success.
    • 君绰忧惧连诛 - Means 'Junchuo was fearful of being implicated in the execution,' indicating his anxiety about potential repercussions.
    • 结为奔走之友 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to form a close friendship,' suggesting a bond of mutual support.
    • 夜黑迷路 - Literally 'lost in the dark night,' a common literary device to set a scene of confusion or danger.
    • 扣门数下 - Means 'knocked on the door several times,' a simple but vivid action.
    • 苍头迎拜 - Means 'an old servant greeted with a bow,' indicating a respectful reception.
    • 科斗郎君，姓威 - Playful or formal introduction, where '科斗' is a term for a young scholar, and '威' is the surname.
    • 即当府秀才也 - Means 'he is a scholar from the local prefecture,' indicating the social status of the host.
    • 自通姓名曰“威污蠖” - Means 'introduced himself as 'Wei Wuhuo',' providing the host's name.
    • 叙寒温讫 - Means 'after exchanging pleasantries,' a common ritual in social interactions.
    • 揖客由阼阶，坐曰 - Means 'invited the guests to sit on the main seat,' indicating a formal and respectful greeting.
    • 污蠖忝以本州乡赋，得与足下同声 - Formal statement of humility and respect, meaning 'I, Wuhuo, am honored to share the same state with you.'
    • 清宵良会，殊是所愿 - Means 'a pleasant meeting on a clear night, which is a great wish,' expressing the host's pleasure.
    • 命酒合坐 - Means 'ordered wine and sat together,' indicating a social gathering.
    • 渐至酣畅，谈谑交至 - Means 'gradually becoming merry, jokes and teasing alternated,' describing a lively and enjoyable atmosphere.
    • 众所不能对 - Means 'nobody could respond,' indicating the host's wit and eloquence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 公 - Refers to a high-ranking official or noble.
    • 来君绰 - A historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 隋炀帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui, the second emperor of the Sui dynasty.
    • 征辽 - Refers to the Sui dynasty's military campaign against the Goguryeo kingdom.
    • 十二军尽没 - Indicates the complete defeat of the twelve armies.
    • 总管来护坐法受戮 - Means 'General Lai Hu was executed for legal reasons,' indicating a specific historical event.
    • 秀才 - Refers to a scholar or a person who has passed the lowest level of the imperial examinations.
    • 科斗郎君 - A term for a young scholar, often used in a playful or formal context.
    • 蜗儿 - A term for a young servant, often used in a playful or formal context.
    • 六郎子 - A term of address for a young man, often used in a playful or formal context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 公 - A high-ranking official or noble.
    • 来君绰 - A historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 隋炀帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 来护 - A historical figure, likely a general.
    • 罗巡、罗逖、李万进 - Historical figures, likely scholars or officials.
    • 海州 - A historical place, likely a city or region.
    • 威污蠖 - The name of the host.

    The person's rise to prominence was marked by holding high-ranking official positions. The matter was predetermined, and even if born far away, the gods and spirits could not harm him, clearly showing the inevitability of his success.

    ○ Junchuo

    During the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui, when he launched a campaign against Goguryeo, all twelve armies were defeated. General Lai Hu was executed for legal reasons, and Emperor Yang wanted to execute all of his sons. Junchuo, fearing that he would be implicated in the execution, formed a close friendship with the scholars Luo Xun, Luo Ti, and Li Wanjin, and they fled together to Haizhou.

    At night, they were lost and disoriented. They saw lights along the road and decided to seek refuge there. They knocked on the door several times, and an old servant greeted Junchuo with a bow. Junchuo asked, "Who lives here?" The servant replied, "This is a young scholar named Wei, surnamed Wei, a local scholar from the prefecture." They then opened the door and closed it again, knocking on the inner door and saying, "Wotou, there are four or five guests outside." Wotou was another young servant. They opened the door and lit candles to guide the guests to the guest room, where the beds and cushions were well-prepared. Soon, two young boys carrying candles came out from the inner door and said, "Sixth Master, come out." Junchuo and the others descended the steps to meet the host. The host spoke eloquently and persuasively, introducing himself as "Wei Wuhuo." After exchanging pleasantries, he invited the guests to sit on the main seat and said, "Wuhuo is honored to share the same state with you, and a pleasant meeting on a clear night is a great wish." He then ordered wine and sat down with them. Gradually, they became merry, and jokes and teasing alternated, leaving everyone unable to respond. [28]

    君绰颇不能平，欲以 理挫之，无计，因举觞曰：“君绰请起一令，以坐中姓名双声者，犯罚如律。” 君绰曰：“威污蠖。”实讥其姓。众皆抚手大笑，以为得言。及至污蠖，改令曰： “以坐中人姓为歌声，自二字至五字。”令曰：“罗李，罗来李，罗李罗来，罗 李罗李来。”众皆惭其辩捷。罗巡又问：“君风雅之士，足得自比云龙，何玉名 之自贬子耶？”污蠖曰：“仆久从宾贡，多为主司见屈。以仆后于群士，何异尺 蠖于污池乎？”巡又问：“公华宗，氏族何为不载？”污蠖曰：“我本田氏，出 于齐威王，亦犹桓丁之类，何足下之不学耶？”既而蜗儿举方丈盘至，珍羞水陆， 充溢其间。君绰及仆者无不饱饫。夜阑彻烛，连榻而寝。迟明叙别，恨恨俱不自 胜。 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 颇不能平 - Means 'quite unable to be at peace' or 'quite dissatisfied.'
    • 欲以理挫之 - Means 'wants to use reason to defeat him/her.'
    • 无计 - Means 'has no plan' or 'is at a loss.'
    • 因举觞曰 - Means 'then raises his cup and says.'
    • 君绰请起一令 - Means 'Jun Chuo proposes to start a rule.'
    • 以坐中姓名双声者 - Means 'those whose names in the company have homophones.'
    • 犯罚如律 - Means 'punished according to the law.'
    • 君绰曰：威污蠖 - Jun Chuo says, 'Wei Wuhuo.' This is a pun on the name of the person being criticized.
    • 众皆抚手大笑 - Means 'everyone clapped and laughed loudly.'
    • 以为得言 - Means 'thinking they had hit the mark.'
    • 及至污蠖 - Means 'when it comes to Wuhuo.'
    • 改令曰 - Means 'changes the rule and says.'
    • 以坐中人姓为歌声 - Means 'to make the surnames of the people in the company into songs.'
    • 自二字至五字 - Means 'from two characters to five characters.'
    • 罗巡又问 - Means 'Luo Xun then asks.'
    • 君风雅之士 - Means 'a gentleman of refined tastes.'
    • 足得自比云龙 - Means 'worthy to compare oneself to a dragon.'
    • 何玉名之自贬子耶 - Means 'why do you belittle yourself with such a name?'
    • 污蠖曰 - Wuhuo says.
    • 仆久从宾贡 - Means 'I have been a guest scholar for a long time.'
    • 多为主司见屈 - Means 'often being humiliated by the examiners.'
    • 何异尺蠖于污池乎 - Means 'what difference is there between a small caterpillar in a dirty pool?'
    • 公华宗 - Means 'you are of noble lineage.'
    • 氏族何为不载 - Means 'why is your clan not recorded?'
    • 我本田氏 - Means 'I am originally from the Tian family.'
    • 出于齐威王 - Means 'descended from Duke Wei of Qi.'
    • 亦犹桓丁之类 - Means 'also like the Huan and Ding families.'
    • 何足下之不学耶 - Means 'how is it that you are not well-read?'
    • 既而蜗儿举方丈盘至 - Means 'soon after, a large tray was brought.'
    • 珍羞水陆 - Means 'delicacies from both land and sea.'
    • 充溢其间 - Means 'abundant in the tray.'
    • 君绰及仆者无不饱饫 - Means 'Jun Chuo and the servants all ate their fill.'
    • 夜阑彻烛 - Means 'as night deepened and the candles were extinguished.'
    • 连榻而寝 - Means 'they slept on the same couch.'
    • 迟明叙别 - Means 'at dawn, they parted ways.'
    • 恨恨俱不自胜 - Means 'both were deeply saddened and could not bear it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宾贡 - Refers to a system where scholars from the south were sent to the north for examinations.
    • 主司 - Refers to the examiners or officials in charge of the exams.
    • 尺蠖 - A small caterpillar, used metaphorically to describe someone in a lowly position.
    • 华宗 - Refers to a noble or prestigious family.
    • 齐威王 - Duke Wei of Qi, a historical figure known for his wisdom and military prowess.
    • 桓丁 - Refers to families of high status, often used to denote nobility.
    • 方丈盘 - A large tray, often used for serving food in banquets.
    • 珍羞水陆 - Delicacies from both land and sea, indicating a sumptuous feast.

    Proper Nouns

    • 君绰 - Jun Chuo, a character in the text.
    • 污蠖 - Wuhuo, another character in the text.
    • 罗巡 - Luo Xun, a character in the text.
    • 齐威王 - Duke Wei of Qi, a historical figure.
    • 蜗儿 - A servant or attendant in the text.

    Jun Chuo was quite unable to be at peace, wanting to use reason to defeat him/her, but having no plan, he then raised his cup and said, "Jun Chuo proposes to start a rule: those whose names in the company have homophones will be punished according to the law." Jun Chuo said, "Wei Wuhuo," which was actually a pun on the person's name. Everyone clapped and laughed loudly, thinking they had hit the mark. When it came to Wuhuo, he changed the rule and said, "To make the surnames of the people in the company into songs, from two characters to five characters." The rule was, "Luo Li, Luo Lai Li, Luo Li Luo Lai, Luo Li Luo Li Lai." Everyone was ashamed of their quick wit. Luo Xun then asked, "You are a gentleman of refined tastes, worthy to compare oneself to a dragon. Why do you belittle yourself with such a name?" Wuhuo said, "I have been a guest scholar for a long time, often being humiliated by the examiners. What difference is there between a small caterpillar in a dirty pool?" Luo Xun asked again, "You are of noble lineage, why is your clan not recorded?" Wuhuo said, "I am originally from the Tian family, descended from Duke Wei of Qi, also like the Huan and Ding families. How is it that you are not well-read?" Soon after, a large tray was brought, filled with delicacies from both land and sea. Jun Chuo and the servants all ate their fill. As night deepened and the candles were extinguished, they slept on the same couch. At dawn, they parted ways, both deeply saddened and could not bear it. [29]

    君绰等行数里，犹念污蠖，复来，见昨所会之处，了无人居，唯污池，池边 有大螾，长数尺。又有蜗螺丁子，皆大常者数倍，方知污蠖及二竖皆此物也。 遂共恶昨宵所食，各吐出青泥及污水数升。
    　　
    　　卷二
    　　○崔环
    　　安平崔环者，司戎郎宣之子。元和五年夏五月，遇疾于荥阳别业。忽见黄衫 吏二人，执帖来追，遂行数百步，入城。城中有街两畔，官林相对，绝无人家， 直北数里到门，题曰“判官院”。见二吏迤逦向北，亦有林木，袴靴秣头，佩刀 头，执弓矢者，散立者，各数百人。同到之人数千，或杻，或系，或缚，或囊 盛耳头，或连其项，或衣服俨然，或簪裙济济，各有惧色，或泣或叹。其黄衫人 一留伴环，一入告。 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 犹念污蠖 - Thinking about the creature '污蠖'; indicates lingering thoughts or concern.
    • 遂共恶昨宵所食 - Becoming collectively disgusted with what they had eaten the previous night; indicates a shared reaction.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 污蠖 - A type of creature, possibly a worm or a similar insect; culturally significant in classical Chinese literature.
    • 二竖 - Two small devils or spirits; often used in classical Chinese literature to describe mischievous or malevolent entities.
    • 青泥及污水 - Green mud and dirty water; symbolic of the creature's nature or the disgust it caused.

    Proper Nouns

    • 君绰 - A proper name, likely referring to a specific person or character.

    Jun Chuo and his companions walked several miles, still thinking about the "污蠖" (a type of creature), and then returned. They saw the place where they had met the previous day, but there was no sign of human habitation, only a dirty pond. By the pond's edge, there was a large earthworm, several feet long. There were also snails and something called "丁子," all of which were several times larger than usual. They then realized that the "污蠖" and the two little devils were all the same kind of creature. As a result, they all became disgusted with what they had eaten the previous night and each vomited out several liters of green mud and dirty water.

    Volume Two
    ○ Cui Huan
    Cui Huan of Anping was the son of Cui Xuan, a military officer. In the fifth year of the Yuanhe era, during the summer of May, he fell ill at his estate in Zhengyang. Suddenly, he saw two officials in yellow robes, holding a summons, who came to escort him. They walked several hundred steps and entered the city. The city had streets on both sides, with government forests facing each other, and there were no houses at all. They walked straight north for several miles to a gate, which was inscribed with the words "Judicial Office." They saw two officials walking north, and there were also trees, men wearing trousers and boots, and those wearing headgear, carrying swords, and holding bows and arrows, scattered among them, numbering several hundred people. There were thousands of people who had come with them, some bound, some tied, some bound, some in bags with their heads, some with their necks connected, some dressed neatly, some with hairpins and skirts, all showing signs of fear, some crying and some sighing. One of the officials in yellow robes stayed to accompany Cui Huan, while the other entered to report. [30]

    俄闻决人四下声，既而告者出曰：“判官传语：何故不抚幼 小，不务成家，广破庄园，但恣酒色！又虑尔小累无掌，且为宽恕，轻杖放归， 宜即洗心，勿复贰过。若踵前非，固无容舍。”乃敕伴者令送归。环曰：“判官 谓谁？”曰：“司戎郎也。”环泣曰：“弃背多年，号天莫及。幸蒙追到，慈颜 不遥，乞一拜见，死且无恨。”二吏曰：“明晦各殊，去留有隔，不合见也。” 环曰：“向者传语云已见责。此身不入，何以受刑？”吏曰：“入则不得归矣。 凡人有三魂，一魂在家，二魂受杖耳。不信，看郎胫合有杖痕。”遂褰衣自视， 其两胫各有杖痕四，痛苦不济，匍匐而行，举足甚艰。同到之人，叹羡之声，喧 于歧路。 [31]

    Soon, Huan heard the sound of a decisive crowd below. Shortly after, an announcer emerged and said, "The magistrate has conveyed a message: Why do you not cherish the young, not work towards establishing a family, destroy estates widely, but indulge in alcohol and women? Furthermore, considering that you have small burdens and no one to take care of you, and for this reason, we are lenient and will release you with a light flogging. You should immediately reform your heart and do not repeat your mistakes. If you follow the same mistakes, there will be no place for you to stay." Then, the official ordered an escort to send Huan back.

    Huan asked, "Who is the magistrate?" The official replied, "The Military Officer."

    Huan wept and said, "Abandoned for many years, calling to heaven is futile. Fortunately, you have come to find me, your kind face is not far away. I beg to see you once, even if I die, I will have no regrets."

    The two officials said, "The light and darkness are different, there is a separation between going and staying, it is not appropriate to see each other."

    Huan said, "Earlier, the message said I have been reprimanded. If I do not enter, how can I receive punishment?"

    The official said, "If you enter, you will not be able to return. All people have three souls, one soul stays at home, two souls receive the flogging. If you don't believe, look at the marks on your legs." Huan then lifted his clothes to look, and he saw four marks of flogging on each of his legs. He was in great pain, struggling to walk, and found it extremely difficult to lift his feet. Those who accompanied him expressed their admiration and pity, and their voices echoed along the path. [31]

    南行百余步，街东有大林。二吏前曰：“某等日夜事判官，为日虽久，幽冥 小吏，例不免贫。各有许惠资财，竟无暇取，不因送郎阴路，无因得往求之。请 即暂止林下，某等偕去，俄顷即来。诸处皆是恶鬼曹司，不合往，乞郎不移足相 待。”言讫各去，久而不来。环闷，试诣街西行，一署门题曰“人矿院”，门亦 甚净。环素有胆，且恃其父为判官，身又蒙放，遂入其中。过屏障，见一大石， 周回数里。有一军将坐于石北厅上，据案而坐，铺人各绕石及石上，有数十大鬼， 形貌不同，以大铁椎椎人为矿石。东有杻械枷锁者数千人，悲啼恐惧，不可名 状。点名拽来，投来石上，遂椎之，既碎，唱其名。军将判之，一吏于案后读之 云：“傅某狱讫。”鬼亦捧云。其中有傅硙狱者，付火狱者，傅汤狱者。 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 南行百余步 - Literally 'southeast for over a hundred steps,' a phrase used to describe a journey or movement in a specific direction.
    • 幽冥小吏 - Refers to minor officials in the underworld, often associated with the realm of the dead.
    • 许惠资财 - Means 'gifts or favors in the form of wealth,' indicating something valuable or beneficial.
    • 恶鬼曹司 - Refers to the offices or departments of evil spirits, suggesting places of torment or punishment.
    • 请即暂止林下 - A polite request to stop temporarily under the tree, indicating a pause in the journey.
    • 某等偕去 - Means 'we will go together,' showing a group action.
    • 诸处皆是恶鬼曹司 - Indicates that all places are filled with evil spirits' offices, emphasizing the dangerous nature of the surroundings.
    • 请郎不移足相待 - A polite request to stay where you are, indicating a need for the person to remain stationary.
    • 身又蒙放 - Means 'I have also been granted release,' indicating a form of liberation or permission.
    • 据案而坐 - Means 'sitting at a desk,' indicating a formal or official position.
    • 铺人各绕石及石上 - Describes people gathering around the stone, indicating a scene of activity or observation.
    • 以大铁椎椎人为矿石 - Means 'using a large iron hammer to turn people into ore,' a vivid description of a brutal process.
    • 杻械枷锁者数千人 - Refers to thousands of people bound with shackles and chains, indicating a large number of prisoners.
    • 悲啼恐惧，不可名状 - Means 'crying and fearing, beyond description,' indicating extreme distress and fear.
    • 点名拽来 - Means 'calling out names and dragging them,' indicating a process of summoning and forcing people.
    • 投来石上 - Means 'throwing them onto the stone,' indicating the action of placing people on the stone.
    • 遂椎之 - Means 'then hammering them,' indicating the continuation of the brutal process.
    • 既碎 - Means 'once broken,' indicating the completion of the process.
    • 唱其名 - Means 'calling out their names,' indicating a formal or ritualistic action.
    • 军将判之 - Means 'the military officer judges them,' indicating a formal decision or ruling.
    • 一吏于案后读之云 - Means 'one clerk reads it from behind the desk,' indicating a formal reading of a document.
    • 傅某狱讫 - Means 'Fou (a name) has completed his sentence,' indicating the end of a legal process.
    • 鬼亦捧云 - Means 'the spirits also confirm it,' indicating a supernatural acknowledgment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南行 - Indicates movement to the south, a common direction in Chinese geography and literature.
    • 幽冥 - Refers to the underworld or the realm of the dead, a significant concept in Chinese mythology and literature.
    • 小吏 - Refers to minor officials, often associated with the bureaucratic system in both the living and the dead.
    • 恶鬼曹司 - Refers to the offices of evil spirits, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.
    • 判官 - Refers to a judge or official in the underworld, a figure often depicted in Chinese ghost stories.
    • 军将 - Refers to a military officer, indicating a position of authority.
    • 杻械枷锁 - Refers to shackles and chains, symbols of imprisonment and punishment.
    • 悲啼恐惧 - Describes the emotional state of those being punished, indicating extreme distress and fear.
    • 傅某狱讫 - Indicates a specific legal case, suggesting a real or fictional event.
    • 鬼亦捧云 - Indicates a supernatural element, suggesting the involvement of spirits in the process.

    Proper Nouns

    • 傅某 - A name, likely referring to a specific individual involved in a legal case.
    • 人矿院 - A name, likely referring to a specific institution or place.
    • 判官 - A title, referring to a judge or official in the underworld.
    • 军将 - A title, referring to a military officer.
    • 大石 - A name, likely referring to a specific stone or monument.
    • 杻械枷锁 - Terms referring to shackles and chains, used to describe the prisoners.
    • 傅某狱讫 - A legal term, indicating the completion of a sentence.

    Walking southeast for over a hundred steps, they reached a large forest on the eastern side of the street. Two officials approached and said, "We serve the judge in the underworld day and night, and though we have done so for a long time, as minor officials in the underworld, we are inevitably poor. Each of us has been offered gifts and wealth, but we have no time to take them. Without sending you on your way through the underworld, we cannot go to seek them. Please stop temporarily under the tree, and we will go together, returning shortly. All places are offices of evil spirits, and it is not appropriate to go there. Please do not move from your spot." After saying this, they departed, and did not return for a long time. Feeling restless, the person tried to walk west along the street and came to a gate with a sign that read "Human Ore Institute." The gate was also very clean. The person, who had always been brave and relied on their father being a judge in the underworld, having also been granted release, entered the place. Passing through the barriers, they saw a large stone, several miles around. A military officer was seated at the northern hall of the stone, sitting at a desk, with people gathered around the stone and on it. There were dozens of large spirits with different appearances, using large iron hammers to turn people into ore. To the east, there were thousands of people bound with shackles and chains, crying and fearing, beyond description. They were called out by name, dragged to the stone, and then hammered, once broken, their names were called. The military officer judged them, and one clerk read from behind the desk, "Fou (a name) has completed his sentence." The spirits also confirmed it. Among them were those who were to be sent to the furnace, those to be sent to the fire, and those to be sent to the soup. [32]

    环直逼 石前看之，军将指之云：“曹司法严，不合妄入，彼是何人，敢来闲看！”人吏 竞来传问，环恃不对。军将怒曰：“看既无端，问又不对，傍观岂如身试之审乎？” 敕一吏拽来锻之。环一魂尚立，见其石上别有一身，被拽扑卧石上，大锤锤之， 痛苦之极，实不可忍。须臾，骨肉皆碎，仅欲成泥。二吏者走来，槌胸曰：“郎 君，再三乞不闲行，何故来此？”遂告军将曰：“此是判官郎君，阳禄未终，追 来却放，暂来入者。无间地狱，入不须臾。遂道如斯。何计得令复旧？”军将者 亦惧曰：“初问不言，忿而处置，如何？”因问诸鬼曰：“何计得令复旧？”皆 曰：“唯濮阳霞一人耳。”曰；“远近？”曰：“去此万里。昨者北海王与化形 出游，为海人所愪。其王请出，今亦未回。”乃令一鬼召之。 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 環直逼 - A phrase indicating someone approaching directly or boldly.
    • 石前看之 - Literally means 'looking at it before the stone,' but in context, it refers to examining something closely.
    • 曹司法嚴 - Refers to the strictness of the law under Cao's jurisdiction.
    • 不合妄入 - Means 'should not enter without cause' or 'should not intrude recklessly.'
    • 彼是何人 - A question meaning 'who is that person?'
    • 敢來閒看 - A rhetorical question expressing disapproval, meaning 'how dare you come to look casually?'
    • 人吏競來傳問 - Means 'officials and commoners came to ask and relay information.'
    • 環恃不對 - Indicates that the person (Huan) stood silently without responding.
    • 忿而处置 - Means 'to handle angrily' or 'to punish out of anger.'
    • 骨肉皆碎 - A vivid description of being broken into pieces, likely referring to a form of torture.
    • 僅欲成泥 - Means 'almost reduced to mud,' emphasizing the severity of the punishment.
    • 再三乞不闲行 - Means 'repeatedly begged not to wander around,' indicating a plea to avoid trouble.
    • 追来却放 - Means 'was called back but then released,' suggesting a reversal of fate.
    • 暂来入者 - Means 'one who came in temporarily,' referring to Huan's brief entry.
    • 无间地狱 - Refers to the concept of 'no-gap hell' or 'hell without respite,' a place of eternal punishment in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 何计得令复旧 - Means 'what can be done to restore the original state,' referring to how to return to life.
    • 远近 - Means 'how far' or 'the distance.'
    • 去此万里 - Literally means 'a thousand miles away,' but in context, it refers to a very distant place.
    • 昨者北海王与化形出游 - Refers to a mythical figure (Beihai King) who transformed into another form and went out to play.
    • 为海人所愪 - Means 'was captured by sea people,' indicating that the Beihai King was taken captive.
    • 其王请出 - Means 'the king requested his release,' indicating a plea for the Beihai King's freedom.
    • 今亦未回 - Means 'he has not returned yet,' indicating that the Beihai King is still in captivity.
    • 乃令一鬼召之 - Means 'he then ordered a ghost to summon him,' indicating the action taken to bring the Beihai King back.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 曹司法嚴 - Refers to the strict legal system under Cao, likely a historical or literary figure known for his harsh laws.
    • 无间地狱 - A concept from Buddhist cosmology, referring to a place of eternal punishment without respite.
    • 濮阳霞 - Likely a historical or literary figure known for his ability to help in supernatural matters.
    • 北海王 - Refers to a mythical or historical figure associated with the Beihai region, known for his magical abilities.
    • 化形出游 - Refers to the ability to transform into another form and go out to play, a common theme in Chinese mythology.
    • 海人 - Refers to mythical or supernatural beings associated with the sea.
    • 其王请出 - Refers to the act of pleading for the release of a captured king, a common theme in Chinese literature and mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 环 - The name of the person being tortured.
    • 曹司法嚴 - A reference to the strict legal system under Cao.
    • 无间地狱 - A concept from Buddhist cosmology.
    • 濮阳霞 - A historical or literary figure known for his ability to help in supernatural matters.
    • 北海王 - A mythical or historical figure associated with the Beihai region.
    • 海人 - Mythical or supernatural beings associated with the sea.

    Huan approached directly and examined it before the stone. The military officer pointed to it and said, "Under Cao's strict legal system, one should not enter without cause. Who is that person, and how dare you come to look casually?" Officials and commoners came to ask and relay information, but Huan stood silently without responding. The military officer angrily said, "Since you looked without reason and did not answer when questioned, how can mere observation be as thorough as experiencing it firsthand?" He then ordered an official to drag Huan and torture him. Huan's soul still stood, and he saw another body on the stone. The body was dragged and fell on the stone, and he was beaten with a large hammer. He suffered greatly, and the pain was truly unbearable. In a moment, his bones and flesh were all broken, and he was almost reduced to mud. Two officials came and pounded their chests and said, "Lord, you repeatedly begged not to wander around, why did you come here?" They then told the military officer, "This is the judge's lord, whose earthly life has not ended. He was called back but then released, one who came in temporarily. In the no-gap hell, one enters without a moment's delay. He has suffered like this. What can be done to restore the original state?" The military officer also feared and said, "At first, he did not speak, but then handled it angrily. What should be done?" He then asked the ghosts, "What can be done to restore the original state?" They all said, "Only Pu Yang Xia is the one." He asked, "How far is he?" They replied, "A thousand miles away. Yesterday, the Beihai King transformed and went out to play, was captured by sea people. The king requested his release, but he has not returned yet." He then ordered a ghost to summon him. [33]

    有顷而到，乃一髯眇目翁也，应急而来，喘犹未定。军将指环曰：“何计？” 霞曰：“易耳。”遂解衣缠腰，取怀中药末，糁于矿上团扑，一翻一糁，扁槎其 矿为头顶及身手足，剜刻五脏，通为肠胃，雕为九窍，逡巡成形，以手承其项曰： “起！”遂起来，与立合为一，遂能行。大为二吏所贵。相与复南行。将去，濮 阳霞抚肩曰：“措大，人矿中搜得活，然而去不许一钱。”环许钱三十万。霞笑 曰：“老吏身忙，当使小鬼枭儿往取，见即分付。” [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有顷而到 - After a short while, arrived, indicating a brief passage of time.
    • 乃一髯眇目翁也 - Turned out to be an old man with a beard and one blind eye, a descriptive phrase that combines physical traits.
    • 应急而来，喘犹未定 - Came in a hurry, still breathing heavily, indicating urgency and physical exertion.
    • 易耳 - It's easy, a colloquial expression of simplicity.
    • 遂解衣缠腰，取怀中药末，糁于矿上团扑 - Then he took off his clothes, tied them around his waist, took out medicine powder from his chest, and sprinkled it on the ore to form a shape, a detailed description of actions.
    • 一翻一糁，扁槎其矿为头顶及身手足 - Flipping and sprinkling, shaping the ore into a head and limbs, a series of actions to form a shape.
    • 剜刻五脏，通为肠胃，雕为九窍 - Carved out the internal organs, connected them as the digestive system, and carved the nine orifices, a detailed description of anatomical features.
    • 逡巡成形 - Quickly took shape, indicating a rapid transformation.
    • 以手承其项曰：“起！” - He placed his hand on its neck and said, 'Rise!', a command to bring the shape to life.
    • 遂起来，与立合为一，遂能行 - It rose up, merged with him, and could walk, indicating the successful creation of a living entity.
    • 大为二吏所贵 - Greatly valued by the two officials, indicating high regard.
    • 相与复南行 - They then continued to travel south together, indicating a continuation of their journey.
    • 抚肩曰：“措大，人矿中搜得活，然而去不许一钱。” - Patted his shoulder and said, 'Scholar, you were found alive in the mine, but you can't leave without paying a penny,' a mix of affection and a demand.
    • 环许钱三十万 - Ring agreed to pay thirty million coins, indicating a large sum of money.
    • 老吏身忙，当使小鬼枭儿往取，见即分付 - The old official is busy, so I'll send the little demon 'Xiao'er' to fetch it, and he'll hand it over when he sees you, a humorous and indirect way of assigning a task.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 髯眇目翁 - Old man with a beard and one blind eye, a physical description that may carry cultural significance.
    • 矿 - Mine, referring to a place where minerals are extracted, which could be a metaphor for a dangerous or hidden place.
    • 措大 - Scholar, a term used to refer to a learned person, often with a connotation of being somewhat naive or out of place.
    • 老吏 - Old official, a term for an experienced and possibly authoritative figure.
    • 小鬼枭儿 - Little demon 'Xiao'er', a playful or humorous reference to a subordinate or assistant.

    Proper Nouns

    • 环 - Ring, a proper name.
    • 濮阳霞 - Pu Yang Xia, a proper name.

    After a short while, he arrived, and it turned out to be an old man with a beard and one blind eye. He came in a hurry, still breathing heavily. The military officer pointed to the ring and asked, "What's the plan?" Pu Yang Xia said, "It's easy." Then he took off his clothes, tied them around his waist, took out medicine powder from his chest, and sprinkled it on the ore to form a shape. Flipping and sprinkling, he shaped the ore into a head and limbs, carved out the internal organs, connected them as the digestive system, and carved the nine orifices. It quickly took shape. He placed his hand on its neck and said, "Rise!" It rose up, merged with him, and could walk. The two officials greatly valued this. They then continued to travel south together. As they were about to leave, Pu Yang Xia patted his shoulder and said, "Scholar, you were found alive in the mine, but you can't leave without paying a penny." Ring agreed to pay thirty million coins. Pu Yang Xia smiled and said, "The old official is busy, so I'll send the little demon 'Xiao'er' to fetch it, and he'll hand it over when he sees you." [34]

    行及城门，见一吏南走，曰：“黄河欲分一枝，前者天令三丁取一，计功不 计，今请二丁取一。”二吏以私行有矿环之过，恐宣之怒环而召也，谓环曰： “彼见若问，但言欲观地狱之法，以为儆戒，故在此耳。”吏见果问，环答之如 言。遂别去复行。
    　　须臾，至荥阳，二吏曰：“还生必矣。某将有所取，能一观乎？”环曰： “固所愿也。”共入县郭，到一人家中堂，一吏以怀中绳系床上女人头，尽力拽 之，一吏以豹皮囊徐收其气，气尽乃拽下，皆缚之。同送环家，入门，二吏大呼 曰：“崔环！”误筑门扇，遂寤。其家泣候之，已七日矣。后数日，有枭鸣于庭， 环曰：“濮阳翁之子来矣。”遂令家人刻纸钱焚之，乃去。疾平，潜寻所见妇人 家，乃县纠郭霈妻也。其时尚未有分河之议，后数日，河中节度使司徒薛公平议 奏分河一枝，冀减冲城之势。初奏三丁取一，既虑不足，复奏二丁役一，竟如环 阴司所见也。 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation
