    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 行及城門 - Means 'reached the city gate,' a common phrase indicating arrival at a significant location.
    • 南走 - Means 'ran south,' describing the action of a person fleeing in a specific direction.
    • 天令三丁取一 - Means 'heaven's decree to take one out of three,' referring to a divine mandate or order.
    • 計功不計 - Means 'counting merit but not counting,' suggesting a focus on moral or spiritual rewards rather than material ones.
    • 今請二丁取一 - Means 'now requesting to take one out of two,' indicating a change in the divine mandate.
    • 私行有矿环之过 - Means 'having committed the sin of private action concerning the ring,' referring to a transgression related to a ring.
    • 恐宣之怒环而召也 - Means 'fearing that the announcement of the ring's anger would summon him,' indicating concern about divine retribution.
    • 彼见果问 - Means 'if he sees and asks,' referring to a hypothetical scenario.
    • 但言欲观地狱之法，以為儆戒，故在此耳 - Means 'just say that you want to see the methods of hell as a warning, so you are here,' indicating a fabricated reason to avoid revealing the true purpose.
    • 遂别去复行 - Means 'then parted and continued their journey,' describing the action of leaving and resuming the journey.
    • 須臾，至荥阳 - Means 'a short while later, they arrived at Yingyang,' indicating a brief passage of time.
    • 還生必矣 - Means 'certainly will return to life,' expressing confidence in survival.
    • 某將有所取，能一觀乎 - Means 'I will take something, can you see it once?' indicating a request to witness an event.
    • 固所願也 - Means 'it is indeed what I wish,' expressing agreement and desire.
    • 共入縣郭 - Means 'together entered the county town,' describing a collective action.
    • 以懷中繩系床上女人頭 - Means 'to tie a woman's head to a bed with a rope from his pocket,' describing a violent act.
    • 以豹皮囊徐收其氣 - Means 'to slowly collect her breath with a leopard-skin bag,' describing a methodical process.
    • 氣盡乃拽下 - Means 'when her breath was exhausted, then pull her down,' describing the completion of the act.
    • 同送環家 - Means 'together sent him home,' describing a collective action.
    • 誤筑門扇 - Means 'mistakenly hitting the door frame,' describing an accidental action.
    • 遂寤 - Means 'then woke up,' indicating the end of a dream or trance.
    • 有枭鳴於庭 - Means 'a crow cawed in the courtyard,' describing an ominous sign.
    • 潛尋所見婦人家 - Means 'secretly searched for the house of the woman he saw,' describing a covert action.
    • 乃縣糾郭霈妻也 - Means 'it was the wife of the county official Guo Pei,' identifying the woman.
    • 河中節度使司徒薛公平議奏分河一枝 - Means 'the Governor of Hedong, Sima Xue, proposed to divide a branch of the river,' describing a historical event.
    • 冀減沖城之勢 - Means 'hoping to reduce the force of the river against the city,' indicating the purpose of the proposal.
    • 初奏三丁取一，既慮不足，復奏二丁役一 - Means 'initially proposed to take one out of three, but later, fearing it was insufficient, proposed to take one out of two,' describing a change in policy.
    • 竟如環陰司所見也 - Means 'ultimately, it was as feng saw in the underworld,' indicating that the events mirrored a vision.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 城門 - Refers to the city gate, a significant landmark in ancient Chinese cities.
    • 天令 - Refers to a divine mandate or decree, often associated with heaven or the gods.
    • 三丁取一 - A historical practice where one out of every three able-bodied men was conscripted for military service or labor.
    • 私行 - Refers to private actions, often contrasted with official or public duties.
    • 環 - The name of the protagonist, likely a common name in the context of the story.
    • 縣糾 - Refers to a county official, a local administrative position.
    • 郭霈 - The name of a county official, providing a specific historical figure.
    • 河中節度使 - Refers to the Governor of Hedong, a high-ranking military and administrative position.
    • 薛公 - The name of the Governor of Hedong, likely a historical figure.
    • 分河 - Refers to the division of a river, a significant engineering project in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 崔環 - The name of the protagonist.
    • 縣糾 - The title of a county official.
    • 郭霈 - The name of a county official.
    • 河中節度使 - The title of the Governor of Hedong.
    • 薛公 - The name of the Governor of Hedong.

    As they reached the city gate, they saw a clerk running south, saying, "The Yellow River wishes to divide a branch; previously, heaven decreed to take one out of three, counting merit but not counting, now requesting to take one out of two." The two clerks, having committed the sin of private action concerning the ring, feared that the announcement of the ring's anger would summon them, and told feng, "If he sees and asks, just say that you want to see the methods of hell as a warning, so you are here." When the clerk saw and asked, feng answered as they said. Then they parted and continued their journey.

    A short while later, they arrived at Yingyang, and the two clerks said, "Certainly will return to life. I will take something, can you see it once?" Feng said, "It is indeed what I wish." Together they entered the county town, to a house in the courtyard, one clerk tied a woman's head to a bed with a rope from his pocket, pulling her with all his might, the other clerk slowly collected her breath with a leopard-skin bag, when her breath was exhausted, then pulled her down, binding her. Together they sent feng home, upon entering, the two clerks shouted loudly, "Feng!" Mistakenly hitting the door frame, he woke up. His family wept, waiting for him, it had been seven days. After several days, a crow cawed in the courtyard, feng said, "The son of Old Man Puyang has come." So he ordered his family to carve paper money and burn it, then he left. After his illness subsided, he secretly searched for the house of the woman he saw, it was the wife of the county official Guo Pei. At that time, there was no proposal to divide the river, but a few days later, the Governor of Hedong, Sima Xue, proposed to divide a branch of the river, hoping to reduce the force of the river against the city. Initially proposed to take one out of three, but later, fearing it was insufficient, proposed to take one out of two, ultimately, it was as feng saw in the underworld. [35]

    ○柳归舜
    　　吴兴柳归舜，隋开皇二十年自江南抵巴陵，大风吹至君山下。因维舟登岸， 寻小径，不觉行四五里，兴酣，逾越溪涧，不由径路。忽道傍有一大石，表里洞 彻，圆而砥平，周匝六七亩。其外尽生翠竹，圆大如盎，高百余尺，叶曳白云， 森罗映天，清风徐吹，戛为丝竹音。石中央又生一树，高百尺，条干偃阴为五色。 翠叶如盘，花径尺余，色深碧，叶深红，异香成烟，箸物霏霏。
    　　有鹦鹉数千，丹嘴翠衣，尾长二三尺，翱翔其间，相呼姓字，音旨清越。有 名“武游郎”者，有名“阿苏儿”者，有名“武仙郎”者，有名“自在先生”者， 有名“踏莲露”者，有名“凤花台”者，有名“戴蝉儿”者，有名“多花子”者。 或有唱歌者曰：“吾此曲是汉武钩弋夫人常所唱。”词曰：
    　　“戴蝉儿，分明传与君王语。 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 抵 - Means 'to reach' or 'to arrive at,' used here to describe the journey to the location.
    • 维舟 - Literally 'moor the boat,' but figuratively means to stop or anchor the boat.
    • 逾越溪涧 - Means 'to leap over streams and ravines,' describing the adventurous nature of the journey.
    • 表里洞彻 - Means 'transparent inside and out,' describing the unique properties of the stone.
    • 周匝六七亩 - Means 'surrounding six or seven acres,' describing the size of the area around the stone.
    • 翠竹 - Refers to green bamboo, a common motif in classical Chinese literature.
    • 叶曳白云 - Means 'leaves trailing white clouds,' creating a poetic image of the bamboo's appearance.
    • 森罗映天 - Means 'densely arranged and reaching up to the sky,' describing the lushness and height of the bamboo.
    • 清风徐吹 - Means 'gentle breeze blowing,' creating a serene atmosphere.
    • 戛为丝竹音 - Means 'sounding like the music of silk and bamboo,' referring to the harmonious sounds produced by the wind.
    • 条干偃阴为五色 - Means 'branches and trunk bending in the shade, appearing in five colors,' describing the vibrant colors of the tree.
    • 翠叶如盘 - Means 'green leaves like plates,' describing the shape and size of the leaves.
    • 花径尺余 - Means 'flowers over a foot in diameter,' emphasizing the size of the flowers.
    • 色深碧，叶深红 - Means 'deep blue in color, leaves deep red,' describing the vivid colors of the flowers and leaves.
    • 异香成烟 - Means 'strange fragrance turning into smoke,' creating a mystical atmosphere.
    • 箸物霏霏 - Means 'floating gently on objects,' describing the way the fragrance spreads.
    • 翱翔其间 - Means 'soaring among them,' describing the movement of the parrots.
    • 相呼姓字 - Means 'calling each other by name,' indicating a social interaction among the parrots.
    • 音旨清越 - Means 'clear and resonant sounds,' describing the quality of the parrots' calls.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吴兴 - A historical region in present-day Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural significance.
    • 隋开皇二十年 - The 20th year of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty (590 AD).
    • 江南 - Refers to the southern part of China, often associated with natural beauty and cultural refinement.
    • 巴陵 - An ancient name for Yueyang, a city in Hunan Province.
    • 君山 - A famous mountain in Dongting Lake, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 鹦鹉 - Parrots, often associated with intelligence and beauty in classical Chinese literature.
    • 武游郎 - A name given to one of the parrots, possibly a playful or affectionate name.
    • 阿苏儿 - Another name given to one of the parrots, possibly a pet name.
    • 武仙郎 - A name given to one of the parrots, possibly a playful or affectionate name.
    • 自在先生 - A name given to one of the parrots, possibly a philosophical or humorous name.
    • 踏莲露 - A name given to one of the parrots, possibly a poetic or descriptive name.
    • 凤花台 - A name given to one of the parrots, possibly a poetic or descriptive name.
    • 戴蝉儿 - A name given to one of the parrots, possibly a poetic or descriptive name.
    • 多花子 - A name given to one of the parrots, possibly a poetic or descriptive name.

    Proper Nouns

    • 柳归舜 - The name of the protagonist, a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 吴兴 - A historical region in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 江南 - The southern part of China.
    • 巴陵 - An ancient name for Yueyang, a city in Hunan Province.
    • 君山 - A famous mountain in Dongting Lake, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.

    Translation:

    Liu Guishun

    Liu Guishun from Wu Province, during the twentieth year of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, traveled from the southern part of China to Baling. A strong wind blew him to the foot of Junshan Mountain. He moored his boat and went ashore, looking for a small path. Unconsciously, he walked several miles, in a state of high spirits, leaping over streams and ravines, not following the usual path. Suddenly, he saw a large stone by the side of the road, transparent inside and out, round and smooth, surrounding six or seven acres. Outside it was all covered with green bamboo, round and large like a pot, over a hundred feet tall, with leaves trailing white clouds, densely arranged and reaching up to the sky, and a gentle breeze blowing, sounding like the music of silk and bamboo. A tree grew from the center of the stone, over a hundred feet tall, with branches and trunk bending in the shade, appearing in five colors. The green leaves were like plates, the flowers over a foot in diameter, deep blue in color, leaves deep red, with a strange fragrance turning into smoke, floating gently on objects.

    There were thousands of parrots, with red beaks and green feathers, tails two or three feet long, soaring among them, calling each other by name, with clear and resonant sounds. One of them sang a song and said, "This song is what the Lady Gouyi of Emperor Wu of Han often sang." The lyrics were: "Wearing the cicada, clearly conveying the words of the emperor." [36]

    建章殿里未得归，朱箔金缸双凤舞。”
    　　名阿苏儿者曰：“我忆阿娇深宫下泪，唱曰：‘昔请司马相如为作《长门赋》， 徒使费百金，君王终不顾。’”又有诵司马相如《大人赋》者曰：“吾初学赋时， 为赵昭仪抽七宝钗横鞭，余痛不彻。今日诵得，还是终身一艺。”名武游郎者言： “余昔见汉武帝乘郁金楫，泛积翠池，自吹紫玉笛，音韵朗畅，帝意欢适。李夫 人歌以随，歌曰：‘顾鄙贱、奉恩私。愿吾君，万岁期。’”
    　　又名武仙郎者问归舜曰：“君何姓氏行第？”归舜曰：“姓柳，第十二。” 曰：“柳十二自何许来？”归舜曰：“吾将至巴陵，遭风泊舟，兴酣至此耳。” 武仙郎曰：“柳十二官人，偶因遭风，得臻异境，此所谓因病致妍耳。然下官禽 鸟，不能致力生人，为足下转达桂家三十娘子。”因遥呼曰：“阿春，此间有客。” 即有紫云数片，自西南飞来。去地丈余，云气渐散，遂见珠楼翠幕，重槛飞楹， 周匝石际。 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.

    In the Jianzhang Palace, unable to return, a red curtain, a golden lamp, and two phoenixes danced.
    A person named Ah So said, "I remember Princess A-Jiao weeping in the deep palace, singing: 'Once I asked Sima Xiangru to write the Changmen Fu, but it cost a hundred gold pieces, and the emperor still ignored it.'"
    Another person recited Sima Xiangru's Daren Fu and said, "When I first learned to write fu, I was struck by Consort Zhao with a seven-treasure hairpin, and the pain was not yet fully over. Today, I recite it, and it remains a lifelong skill."
    A person named Wu You Lang said, "I once saw Emperor Wu of Han riding a boat with a jade paddle, sailing on the Jade Green Pool, blowing a purple jade flute, with a clear and pleasant sound, and the emperor's mood was joyful. Lady Li sang along, singing: 'Looking back, I am humble and lowly, serving you with my utmost devotion. May our lord live for ten thousand years.'"
    A person named Wu Xian Lang asked Gui Shun, "What is your name and rank?" Gui Shun replied, "My surname is Liu, and I am the twelfth."
    He said, "Mr. Liu the twelfth, where do you come from?"
    Gui Shun replied, "I was heading to Baling, encountered a storm, and docked my boat, arriving here in a state of intoxication."
    Wu Xian Lang said, "Mr. Liu the twelfth, you happened to encounter a storm and arrived in a strange land, which is what they call beauty arising from illness. However, I am a bird, unable to exert effort for living beings, and I will convey your message to Miss Thirty of the Gui family." He then called out, "Ah Chun, there is a guest here."
    Soon, several purple clouds flew in from the southwest. After rising a few feet, the clouds dispersed, revealing jade towers and green curtains, multiple balconies and flying beams, encircling the stone edges. [37]

    一青衣自户出，年始十三四，身衣珠翠，颜甚姝美，谓归舜曰：“三 十娘子使阿春传语郎君：贫居僻远，劳此检校。不知朝来食否？请垂略坐，以具 蔬馔。”即有捧水精床出者。归舜再让而坐。阿春因呼凤花台鸟：“何不看客？ 三十娘子以黄郎不在，不敢接对郎君。汝若等闲，似前度受捶。”有一鹦鹉即飞 至曰：“吾乃凤花台也。近有一篇，君能听乎？”归舜曰：“平生所好，实契所 愿。”凤花台乃曰：“吾昨过蓬莱玉楼，因有一章。诗曰： [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 身衣珠翠 - Adorned with pearls and jade, indicating wealth and beauty.
    • 颜甚姝美 - Her appearance is extremely beautiful, emphasizing her physical attractiveness.
    • 贫居僻远 - She lives in poverty and in a remote place, indicating her humble circumstances.
    • 劳此检校 - She expresses gratitude for the effort made to visit her, using a humble and respectful tone.
    • 不知朝来食否 - She asks if he has eaten breakfast, showing concern for his well-being.
    • 请垂略坐 - She politely invites him to sit down, using a formal and respectful phrase.
    • 以具蔬馔 - She offers a simple meal, indicating her modest means.
    • 再让而坐 - He refuses twice before sitting down, showing his politeness and respect.
    • 何不看客 - Feng Huatai asks why the guests are not being entertained, showing her curiosity and engagement.
    • 不敢接对郎君 - She is too shy to interact with the young man directly, indicating her modesty.
    • 汝若等闲，似前度受捶 - She warns Feng Huatai that if she acts carelessly, she might be punished again, using a playful but firm tone.
    • 吾乃凤花台也 - The parrot introduces itself as Feng Huatai, using a formal and self-assertive tone.
    • 近有一篇，君能听乎 - The parrot mentions a recent poem and asks if the young man can listen to it, showing her eagerness to share.
    • 吾昨过蓬莱玉楼，因有一章 - The parrot describes a recent visit to a mythical place and a poem she composed, using a poetic and fantastical language.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 青衣 - In classical Chinese, '青衣' can refer to a servant girl, but here it seems to describe a young woman of high status, possibly a concubine or a noblewoman.
    • 郎君 - A respectful address for a young man, often used in formal or polite contexts.
    • 水精床 - A bed made of water crystal, a luxurious and rare item, indicating the wealth and status of the household.
    • 蓬莱玉楼 - A mythical place in Chinese literature, often associated with immortality and paradise.

    Proper Nouns

    • 归舜 - The name of the young man being addressed.
    • 三 十娘子 - The name of the lady who sends the message.
    • 阿春 - The name of the servant who delivers the message.
    • 凤花台 - The name of the parrot.
    • 黄郎 - A reference to a man, possibly a nobleman or a close associate.

    A young woman dressed in fine attire emerged from the door. She was about thirteen or fourteen years old, adorned with pearls and jade, and her appearance was extremely beautiful. She spoke to Guishun, saying, "San Shi, the lady, sends A Chun to deliver a message to the young man: She lives in poverty and in a remote place, and she is grateful for the effort made to visit her. She does not know if you have eaten breakfast this morning. Please sit down for a moment, and she will prepare a simple meal." A servant then brought out a bed made of water crystal. Guishun refused twice before sitting down. A Chun then called out to the parrot named Feng Huatai, asking, "Why are the guests not being entertained?" Feng Huatai replied, "San Shi is too shy to interact with the young man directly. If you act carelessly, you might be punished again." A parrot flew over and said, "I am Feng Huatai. I recently visited the mythical place of Penglai Jade Pavilion and composed a poem. Would you like to hear it?" Guishun replied, "I have always loved poetry, and this truly aligns with my desires." Feng Huatai then began to recite the poem she had composed:

    Final Translation

    A young woman dressed in fine attire emerged from the door. She was about thirteen or fourteen years old, adorned with pearls and jade, and her appearance was extremely beautiful. She spoke to Guishun, saying, "San Shi, the lady, sends A Chun to deliver a message to the young man: She lives in poverty and in a remote place, and she is grateful for the effort made to visit her. She does not know if you have eaten breakfast this morning. Please sit down for a moment, and she will prepare a simple meal." A servant then brought out a bed made of water crystal. Guishun refused twice before sitting down. A Chun then called out to the parrot named Feng Huatai, asking, "Why are the guests not being entertained?" Feng Huatai replied, "San Shi is too shy to interact with the young man directly. If you act carelessly, you might be punished again." A parrot flew over and said, "I am Feng Huatai. I recently visited the mythical place of Penglai Jade Pavilion and composed a poem. Would you like to hear it?" Guishun replied, "I have always loved poetry, and this truly aligns with my desires." Feng Huatai then began to recite the poem she had composed: [38]

    露接朝阳生，海波翻水晶。
    　　玉楼瞰寥廓，天地相照明。
    　　此时下栖止，投迹依旧楹。
    　　顾余复何忝，日侍群仙行。
    　　归舜曰：“丽则丽矣。足下师乃谁人？”凤花台曰：“仆在王丹左右一千余 岁，杜兰香教我真箓，东方朔授我秘诀。汉武帝求太中大夫，遂在石渠署见扬雄、 王褒等赋颂，始晓箴论。王莽之乱，方得还吴。后为朱然所得，转遗陆逊。复见 机、云制作，方学缀篇什。机、云被戮，便至于此。殊不知近日谁为宗匠？”归 舜曰：“薛道衡、江总也。”因诵数篇示之。凤花台曰：“近代非不靡丽，殊少 骨气。”俄而阿春捧赤玉盘，珍羞万品，目所不识，甘香裂鼻。 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 露接朝阳生 - A poetic description of the morning dew meeting the rising sun, symbolizing the beginning of a new day.
    • 海波翻水晶 - A vivid metaphor for the sea's waves resembling crystal, emphasizing the beauty and clarity of the scene.
    • 玉楼瞰寥廓 - Describes a jade tower overlooking the vastness, suggesting a grand and elevated perspective.
    • 天地相照明 - A metaphor for the sky and earth illuminating each other, symbolizing harmony and balance.
    • 投迹依旧楹 - Means 'to return to the same pillars,' suggesting a return to a familiar or original state.
    • 顾余复何忝 - A rhetorical question meaning 'What shame is there in me?' expressing humility or self-reflection.
    • 日侍群仙行 - Means 'daily serving the company of immortals,' indicating a life of spiritual or divine companionship.
    • 殊不知近日谁为宗匠 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Doesn't one know who the master craftsman is now?' expressing surprise or disappointment at the current state of affairs.
    • 薛道衡、江总也 - Names of two prominent literary figures, suggesting a standard of excellence in recent times.
    • 殊少骨气 - Means 'lacks substance or spirit,' criticizing the lack of depth in recent works.
    • 俄而阿春捧赤玉盘 - Describes a moment when a person named Ah Chun brings a red jade tray, suggesting a sudden and graceful appearance.
    • 珍羞万品 - Means 'a myriad of precious delicacies,' emphasizing the abundance and quality of food.
    • 目所不识 - Means 'things one has never seen,' suggesting unfamiliarity with the items presented.
    • 甘香裂鼻 - A vivid description of the food's aroma being so strong that it seems to split the nose, emphasizing its intense fragrance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 露接朝阳生 - A classical poetic image often used to describe the beauty of dawn.
    • 海波翻水晶 - A metaphor that may allude to the clarity and beauty of the sea, possibly drawing from classical descriptions of natural wonders.
    • 玉楼瞰寥廓 - A reference to a grand and elevated structure, possibly alluding to the palaces or towers described in classical texts.
    • 天地相照明 - A metaphor that may draw from Taoist or Confucian ideas of harmony between heaven and earth.
    • 顾余复何忝 - A rhetorical question that reflects the humility and self-reflection often found in classical literature.
    • 日侍群仙行 - A reference to a life of spiritual or divine companionship, possibly alluding to the lives of immortals in Taoist texts.
    • 薛道衡、江总也 - Names of two prominent literary figures from the Sui and Tang dynasties, suggesting a standard of excellence in recent times.
    • 殊少骨气 - A criticism that reflects the literary standards of the time, suggesting a preference for works with substance and depth.
    • 俄而阿春捧赤玉盘 - A description that may allude to the graceful and sudden appearance of a servant, possibly drawing from classical descriptions of courtly life.
    • 珍羞万品 - A reference to the abundance and quality of food, possibly alluding to the feasts described in classical texts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 薛道衡 - A prominent literary figure from the Sui dynasty.
    • 江总 - A prominent literary figure from the Sui dynasty.
    • 阿春 - A person named Ah Chun, possibly a servant or attendant in the text.

    The morning dew meets the rising sun, and the sea waves resemble crystal.
    A jade tower overlooks the vastness, with the sky and earth illuminating each other.
    At this moment, returning to rest, one returns to the same pillars.
    What shame is there in me, daily serving the company of immortals?
    Guan Shun said, "It is indeed beautiful. Who is your teacher?"
    Feng Huatai replied, "I have been in the service of Wang Dan for more than a thousand years. Du Lanhua taught me the true script, and Dong Fangshuo imparted to me his secrets. During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, I saw the works of Yang Xiong and Wang Bao at the Stone Channel Office, and thus learned the principles of admonitory discourse. During the chaos of Wang Mang, I was able to return to Wu. Later, I was acquired by Zhu Ran and passed on to Lu Sun. I then saw the works of Ji and Yun, and began to learn how to compose poetry and prose. After Ji and Yun were executed, I came to be here. I do not know who the master craftsman is now?"
    Guan Shun said, "It is Xue Daoheng and Jiang Zong." He then recited several pieces to show him. Feng Huatai said, "Recent works are not without their elegance, but they lack substance and spirit." Soon, Ah Chun brought a red jade tray, with a myriad of precious delicacies, things one has never seen, and an intense fragrance that seems to split the nose. [39]

    饮食讫，忽有二道士自空飞下，顾见归舜曰：“大难得！与鹦鹉相对。君非 柳十二乎？君船以风便，索君甚急，何不急回？”因投一尺绮曰：“以此掩眼， 即去矣。”归舜从之，忽如身飞，却坠巴陵。达舟所，舟人欲发。问之，失归舜 已三日矣。后却至此，泊舟寻访，不复再见也。
    　　○崔书生
    　　开元天宝中，有崔书生者，于东周逻谷口居，好植花竹，乃于户外别莳名花， 春暮之时，英蕊芬郁，远闻百步。书生每晨必盥漱独看。忽见一女郎自西乘马东 行，青衣老少数人随后。女郎有殊色，所乘马骏。崔生未及细视，而女郎已过矣。 明日又过，崔生于花下先致酒茗樽杓，铺陈茵席，乃迎马首曰：“某以性好花木， 此园无非手植。今香茂似堪流盼。伏见女郎频自过此，计仆驭当疲，敢具箪醪， 希垂憩息。”女郎不顾而过。其后青衣曰：“但具酒馔，何忧不至。”女郎顾叱 曰：“何故轻与人言！”言讫遂去。 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 飲食訖 - Formal expression meaning 'after having eaten,' indicating the completion of a meal.
    • 大难得 - Colloquial expression meaning 'very difficult to find.'
    • 与鹦鹉相对 - Literally 'to be opposite a parrot,' used metaphorically to indicate a rare or special encounter.
    • 君非柳十二乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Are you not Mr. Liu Shier?'
    • 君船以风便 - Formal way of saying 'Your boat is taking advantage of the favorable wind.'
    • 索君甚急 - Formal expression meaning 'they are looking for you very urgently.'
    • 何不急回 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why don't you hurry back?'
    • 因投一尺绮曰 - Formal way of saying 'then threw a piece of brocade and said.'
    • 以此掩眼，即去矣 - Formal instruction meaning 'use this to cover your eyes, and you will depart immediately.'
    • 归舜从之 - Formal way of saying 'Gu Shun followed his advice.'
    • 忽如身飞 - Dramatic description of a sudden sensation of flying.
    • 却坠巴陵 - Formal way of saying 'then fell back to Ba Ling.'
    • 达舟所 - Formal way of saying 'reached the place where the boat was.'
    • 舟人欲发 - Formal way of saying 'the boatman was about to set sail.'
    • 问之，失归舜已三日矣 - Formal way of saying 'asked him, and he said that Gu Shun had been missing for three days.'
    • 后却至此，泊舟寻访，不复再见也 - Formal way of saying 'later, he came back to this place, moored his boat to search, but never saw him again.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 二道士 - Refers to Taoist priests, who were often depicted in Chinese literature as having supernatural abilities.
    • 归舜 - The name of a character in the story, likely a commoner or a scholar.
    • 柳十二 - A reference to a person, possibly a friend or acquaintance of Gu Shun, known by the nickname 'Liu Shier.'
    • 巴陵 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in China.
    • 鹦鹉 - A parrot, often used in Chinese literature as a symbol of rarity or beauty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 归舜 - The name of a character in the story.
    • 柳十二 - A reference to a person, possibly a friend or acquaintance of Gu Shun.
    • 巴陵 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in China.

    After finishing his meal, two Taoist priests suddenly appeared, descending from the sky. They turned to see Gu Shun and exclaimed, "It is very rare! To be in the presence of a parrot. Are you not Mr. Liu Shier? Your boat is taking advantage of the favorable wind, and they are looking for you very urgently. Why don't you hurry back?" They then threw a piece of brocade to him and said, "Use this to cover your eyes, and you will depart immediately." Gu Shun followed their instructions, and he suddenly felt as if he were flying, only to fall back to Ba Ling. He reached the place where his boat was, and the boatman was about to set sail. When he asked the boatman, he was told that Gu Shun had been missing for three days. Later, he returned to this place, moored his boat to search, but never saw him again.

    During the reigns of Emperor Xuanzong in the Kaiyuan and Tianbao eras, there was a scholar named Cui who lived near the Luogu Pass in Eastern Zhou. He enjoyed planting flowers and bamboo, and outside his home, he cultivated exotic flowers. In the late spring, the fragrance of the blossoms was so strong that it could be smelled from a hundred steps away. Every morning, Cui would wash and brush his teeth before admiring the flowers alone. One day, he suddenly saw a beautiful woman riding a fine horse from west to east, with a few elderly attendants following behind. The woman was exceptionally beautiful, and her horse was swift. Cui did not have time to take a closer look before the woman had already passed by. The next day, she passed by again, and Cui, in anticipation, prepared wine and tea on a mat and chair under the flowers. He approached the horse and said, "I enjoy flowers and plants, and everything in this garden is planted by hand. The fragrance and beauty seem worthy of your attention. I have seen you pass by frequently, and I assume your servants must be tired. I have prepared some wine and food, hoping you will rest here." The woman did not look back and continued on her way. Later, one of the attendants said, "Just prepare the wine and food, and there is no need to worry." The woman turned and scolded, "Why do you speak so lightly to strangers?" After saying this, she rode off. [40]

    崔生明日又于山下别致醪酒，俟俟女郎至，崔生乃鞭马随之，到别墅之前， 又下马拜请。良久，一老青衣谓女郎曰：“车马甚疲，暂歇无伤。”因自控女郎 马至堂寝下，老青衣谓崔生曰：“君既未婚，予为媒妁可乎？”崔生大悦，再拜 跪，请不相忘。老青衣曰：“事即必定，后十五日大吉辰，君于此时，但具婚礼 所要，并于此备酒馔。小娘子阿姊在逻谷中，有微疾，故小娘子日往看省。某去， 便当咨启，至期则皆至此矣。”于是促行。崔生在后，即依言营备吉日所要。至 期，女郎及姊皆到。其姊亦仪质极丽。遂以女郎归于崔生。 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 别致醪酒 - Special type of fermented rice wine.
    • 鞭马随之 - Whip the horse and follow her.
    • 仪质极丽 - Extremely beautiful in appearance and demeanor.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 崔生 - A man named Cui, likely a commoner or scholar.
    • 女郎 - A young woman or lady of high social status.
    • 别墅 - A villa or private residence, often associated with the upper class.
    • 媒妁 - A matchmaker, a person who arranges marriages.
    • 逻谷 - A specific location, possibly a village or area where the sister resides.
    • 微疾 - A slight illness, often used to describe minor health issues.
    • 酒馔 - Food and drink, often used in the context of feasts or celebrations.
    • 吉日 - An auspicious day, often chosen for important events like weddings.
    • 仪质 - Appearance and demeanor, often used to describe physical beauty and grace.
    • 归于崔生 - The act of marrying, often used in the context of arranged marriages.

    Proper Nouns

    • 崔生 - The name of the man involved in the story.
    • 女郎 - The name of the young woman involved in the story.
    • 逻谷 - A specific location mentioned in the story.
    • 微疾 - A term used to describe a slight illness.
    • 酒馔 - A term used to describe food and drink.
    • 吉日 - A term used to describe an auspicious day.
    • 仪质 - A term used to describe appearance and demeanor.
    • 归于崔生 - A term used to describe the act of marrying.

    The next day, Cui prepared a special wine at the foot of the mountain and awaited the arrival of the young lady. Upon her arrival, Cui whipped his horse to follow her, arriving at the villa where he dismounted and bowed to make his request. After a long wait, an elderly servant approached the young lady and said, "The carriage and horses are very tired; a brief rest will do no harm." The servant then took control of the young lady's horse and led it to the hall. The elderly servant turned to Cui and said, "Since you are not yet married, may I act as your matchmaker?" Cui was greatly pleased, bowed twice, and knelt, asking the servant not to forget him. The servant replied, "The matter is decided; in fifteen days, on a very auspicious day, you should prepare what is needed for the wedding and prepare food and drink here. The young lady's elder sister is in the Logu area and has a slight illness, so the young lady visits her daily to check on her. I will leave and consult and inform everyone by the appointed time, when they will all be here." The servant then urged the group to leave quickly. Cui followed behind and immediately prepared what was needed for the auspicious day. By the appointed time, the young lady and her sister arrived. The sister was also extremely beautiful in appearance and demeanor. Thus, the young lady was married to Cui. [41]

    崔生母在旧居，殊不知崔生纳室。崔生以不告而娶，但启聘媵。母见女郎， 女郎悉归之礼甚具。经月余日，忽有一人送食于女郎，甘香特异。后崔生觉母慈 颜衰瘁，因伏问几下，母曰：“吾有汝一子，冀得永寿。今汝所纳新妇，妖美无 双。吾于士塑图书之中，未尝识此，必恐是狐媚之辈，伤害于汝，遂致吾忧。” 崔生入室见女郎，女郎涕泪交下，曰：“本待箕帚，便望终天，不知尊夫人待以 狐媚辈，明晨即便请行，相爱今宵耳。”崔生掩泪不能言。 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 殊不知 - Classical expression meaning 'completely unaware of' or 'did not know at all.'
    • 纳室 - Formal term meaning 'to marry' or 'to take a wife.'
    • 启聘媵 - Compound term where '启' means 'to inform' or 'to notify,' '聘' refers to a formal engagement or betrothal, and '媵' refers to a secondary wife or concubine. Together, it means 'to inform about the engagement and the secondary wife.'
    • 归之礼甚具 - Phrase meaning 'the wedding rites were very complete' or 'the marriage ceremony was fully conducted.'
    • 狐媚之辈 - Term referring to someone who is cunning and deceitful, often with a sexual connotation, here used to describe the new wife.
    • 明晨即便请行 - Phrase meaning 'by tomorrow morning, I will request to leave' or 'I will ask to go away by tomorrow morning.'
    • 相爱今宵耳 - Phrase meaning 'we will love each other tonight only' or 'our love will last only tonight.'
    • 掩泪不能言 - Phrase meaning 'could not speak while holding back tears' or 'was too choked up to speak.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 崔生 - Name of the male protagonist.
    • 崔生母 - Name of the male protagonist's mother.
    • 旧居 - Refers to the old home or residence.
    • 新妇 - Refers to the newlywed wife.
    • 狐媚 - Term often used in classical literature to describe a woman who is seductive and cunning, often with a negative connotation.
    • 终天 - Term meaning 'forever' or 'eternally.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 崔生 - Name of the male protagonist.
    • 崔生母 - Name of the male protagonist's mother.
    • 女郎 - Refers to the newlywed wife.
    • 狐媚之辈 - Term used to describe the newlywed wife, implying she is cunning and deceitful.

    Cui Sheng's mother was living in their old residence and was completely unaware that her son had taken a wife. Cui Sheng, having not informed his mother, only notified her about the engagement and the secondary wife. When the mother saw the young lady, the young lady had all the wedding rites fully conducted. After more than a month, a person suddenly brought food to the young lady, which was exceptionally sweet and fragrant. Later, Cui Sheng noticed that his mother's face had become pale and worried. He asked her while kneeling, and she said, "I had hoped that you would live long. Now, the new wife you have taken is beautiful and cunning beyond compare. I have never seen such a person in the books of scholars and literati. I fear she may be a deceitful woman who could harm you, causing me great worry." Cui Sheng went into the room to see the young lady, who was weeping and said, "I intended to be a loyal wife, hoping to be with you forever. But I did not know that your mother considers me a deceitful woman. By tomorrow morning, I will request to leave, and our love will last only tonight." Cui Sheng could not speak while holding back tears. [42]

    明日，女郎车骑至，女郎乘马，崔生从送之，入逻谷三十余里，山间有川， 川中异香珍果，不可胜纪。馆于屋室，侈于王者。青衣百许，迎拜女郎曰：“小 娘子，无行崔生，何必将来！”于是捧入，留崔生于门外。未几，一青衣传女郎 姊言曰：“崔生遗行，使太夫人疑阻，事宜便绝，不合相见。然小妹曾奉周旋， 亦当奉屈。”俄而召崔生入，责诮再三，辞辩清婉，崔生但拜伏受谴而已。遂坐 于中寝对食，食讫，命酒，召女乐洽饮，铿锵万变。乐阙，其姊谓女郎曰：“须 令崔郎却回，汝有何物赠送？”女郎遂出白玉合子遗崔生，崔生亦自留别。于是 各呜咽而出。行至逻谷，回望千岩万壑，无径路，自恸哭归家。常见玉合子，郁 郁不乐。 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 明日 - Means 'the next day' or 'the following day.'
    • 女郎 - Refers to a young woman or lady.
    • 车骑 - Refers to a group of vehicles and horses, often used to describe a grand procession.
    • 乘马 - Means 'to ride a horse.'
    • 从送之 - Means 'to accompany and send off.'
    • 入逻谷三十余里 - Means 'to enter the Luo Valley for more than thirty miles.'
    • 山间有川 - Means 'there is a river in the mountains.'
    • 川中异香珍果，不可胜纪 - Means 'the river contains exotic fragrances and precious fruits, too numerous to count.'
    • 馆于屋室，侈于王者 - Means 'the lodging is in a house, more luxurious than a king's.'
    • 青衣百许 - Refers to 'about a hundred maids in green dresses.'
    • 迎拜女郎 - Means 'to welcome and bow to the young woman.'
    • 无行崔生，何必将来 - Means 'Feng Sheng, you have no virtue, why bring him here?'
    • 捧入 - Means 'to carry inside.'
    • 留崔生于门外 - Means 'to leave Feng Sheng outside the door.'
    • 未几 - Means 'not long after.'
    • 一青衣传女郎姊言 - Means 'a maid conveys the words of the young woman's sister.'
    • 须令崔郎却回 - Means 'you must make Feng Sheng return.'
    • 汝有何物赠送 - Means 'what do you have to give him as a gift?'
    • 女郎遂出白玉合子遗崔生 - Means 'the young woman then took out a white jade box to give to Feng Sheng.'
    • 崔生亦自留别 - Means 'Feng Sheng also kept something as a keepsake.'
    • 各呜咽而出 - Means 'they both wept and left.'
    • 行至逻谷 - Means 'they walked to the Luo Valley.'
    • 回望千岩万壑，无径路 - Means 'looking back at the thousands of mountains and valleys, there were no paths.'
    • 自恸哭归家 - Means 'he cried bitterly and returned home.'
    • 常见玉合子，郁郁不乐 - Means 'he often saw the jade box, feeling melancholy and unhappy.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 女郎 - Refers to a young woman, often used in classical literature to describe a noble or beautiful lady.
    • 车骑 - Refers to a grand procession, often associated with nobility or royalty.
    • 乘马 - Riding a horse was a common mode of transportation for nobles and officials.
    • 从送之 - Accompanying someone on a journey was a sign of respect and friendship.
    • 入逻谷三十余里 - The Luo Valley is a real place, but in this context, it is likely a fictional location used to create a mystical setting.
    • 山间有川 - Rivers in mountainous regions were often associated with natural beauty and tranquility.
    • 川中异香珍果，不可胜纪 - Exotic fragrances and precious fruits symbolize wealth and luxury.
    • 馆于屋室，侈于王者 - The description of the lodging being more luxurious than a king's suggests a level of opulence beyond the ordinary.
    • 青衣百许 - Maids in green dresses were common in classical literature, symbolizing elegance and refinement.
    • 迎拜女郎 - Welcoming and bowing to a young woman was a sign of respect and deference.
    • 无行崔生，何必将来 - This phrase suggests that Feng Sheng is seen as unworthy or lacking in virtue.
    • 捧入 - Carrying someone inside was a gesture of hospitality and care.
    • 留崔生于门外 - Leaving someone outside the door was a form of rejection or exclusion.
    • 未几 - This phrase indicates a short period of time, often used to transition between events.
    • 一青衣传女郎姊言 - The maid conveying the words of the young woman's sister adds a layer of authority and formality.
    • 须令崔郎却回 - The command to make Feng Sheng return suggests a desire to end the relationship or interaction.
    • 汝有何物赠送 - Asking what gift to give someone is a common social practice in classical literature.
    • 女郎遂出白玉合子遗崔生 - The white jade box is a valuable and symbolic gift, often used to convey deep feelings or memories.
    • 崔生亦自留别 - Keeping a keepsake is a way to remember a significant person or event.
    • 各呜咽而出 - Weeping and leaving is a poignant way to end a scene, emphasizing the emotional impact.
    • 行至逻谷 - Walking to the Luo Valley suggests a physical and emotional journey.
    • 回望千岩万壑，无径路 - Looking back at the mountains and valleys without a path symbolizes the difficulty of returning to the past.
    • 自恸哭归家 - Crying bitterly and returning home emphasizes the protagonist's emotional state and the finality of the situation.
    • 常见玉合子，郁郁不乐 - Seeing the jade box and feeling melancholy suggests a lasting impact on Feng Sheng's life.

    Proper Nouns

    • 女郎 - Young woman or lady.
    • 崔生 - Feng Sheng, a male character.
    • 逻谷 - Luo Valley, a fictional or real location.
    • 青衣 - Maids in green dresses.
    • 白玉合子 - White jade box, a valuable and symbolic gift.

    The next day, the young lady arrived with a grand procession. She rode a horse, and Feng Sheng accompanied and sent her off. They entered the Luo Valley for more than thirty miles, where there was a river in the mountains. The river contained exotic fragrances and precious fruits, too numerous to count. The lodging was in a house, more luxurious than a king's. About a hundred maids in green dresses welcomed and bowed to the young lady, saying, "Young lady, Feng Sheng has no virtue, why bring him here?" They then carried the young lady inside and left Feng Sheng outside the door. Not long after, a maid conveyed the words of the young woman's sister: "Feng Sheng, your lack of virtue has made our grandmother suspicious and hesitant. It would be best to end this matter and not meet again. However, since my younger sister has treated you kindly, she should also receive a gift." Soon after, the sister called Feng Sheng inside, scolding him repeatedly. His words were clear and gentle, and Feng Sheng only bowed and accepted his blame. They then sat in the inner chamber to eat, and after finishing, they ordered wine and summoned female musicians to entertain them. The music was varied and beautiful. After the music ended, the sister said to the young lady, "You must make Feng Sheng return. What do you have to give him as a gift?" The young lady then took out a white jade box to give to Feng Sheng, and Feng Sheng also kept something as a keepsake. They both wept and left. Walking to the Luo Valley, they looked back at the thousands of mountains and valleys, with no paths in sight. Feng Sheng cried bitterly and returned home. He often saw the jade box, feeling melancholy and unhappy. [43]

    忽有胡僧扣门求食，崔生出见，胡僧曰：“君有至宝，乞相示也。”崔生曰： “某贫士，何有见请？”僧曰：“君岂不有异人奉赠，贫道望气知之。”崔生因 出合子示胡僧，僧起拜请曰：“请以百万市之。”遂将去。崔生问僧曰：“女郎 是谁？”曰：“君所纳妻，王母第三个女，玉卮娘子也。姊亦负美名在仙都，况 复人间。所惜君娶之不得久远。倘住一年，君举家必仙矣。”崔生叹怨迨卒。 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽有胡僧扣门求食 - Suddenly, a foreign monk knocked on the door asking for food.
    • 君有至宝，乞相示也 - You have a precious treasure, I beg you to show it to me.
    • 君岂不有异人奉赠，贫道望气知之 - Do you not have a gift from a strange person? I, a poor monk, can sense it through my qi.
    • 请以百万市之 - I will buy it for a million.
    • 女郎是谁 - Who is the lady?
    • 君所纳妻，王母第三个女，玉卮娘子也 - Your wife you have taken, is the third daughter of the Queen Mother, the Lady of the Jade Cup.
    • 姊亦负美名在仙都，况复人间 - Her sister also has a beautiful reputation in the celestial realm, let alone in the mortal world.
    • 所惜君娶之不得久远 - It is a pity that you cannot keep her for a long time.
    • 倘住一年，君举家必仙矣 - If you stay for a year, your whole family will surely become immortals.
    • 崔生叹怨迨卒 - Cui Sheng sighed and lamented until he died.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 胡僧 - Refers to a foreign monk, likely from Central Asia or India, as '胡' often denotes foreign or non-Han Chinese people.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology associated with immortality and the celestial realm.
    • 玉卮娘子 - A title or name given to the Queen Mother's third daughter, indicating her status as a celestial being.
    • 仙都 - The celestial realm or heaven, where immortals reside.
    • 仙 - Refers to immortals or beings who have achieved a state of eternal life.

    Proper Nouns

    • 崔生 - Cui Sheng, the main character of the story.
    • 胡僧 - A foreign monk, though not given a specific name.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 玉卮娘子 - The title or name of the Queen Mother's third daughter.

    Suddenly, a foreign monk knocked on the door asking for food. Cui Sheng went out to see him, and the monk said, "You have a precious treasure, I beg you to show it to me." Cui Sheng replied, "I am a poor scholar, what do I have that you would ask to see?" The monk said, "Do you not have a gift from a strange person? I, a poor monk, can sense it through my qi." Cui Sheng then took out the box and showed it to the foreign monk. The monk stood up and bowed, saying, "I will buy it for a million." He then took the box and left. Cui Sheng asked the monk, "Who is the lady?" The monk replied, "Your wife you have taken is the third daughter of the Queen Mother, the Lady of the Jade Cup. Her sister also has a beautiful reputation in the celestial realm, let alone in the mortal world. It is a pity that you cannot keep her for a long time. If you stay for a year, your whole family will surely become immortals." Cui Sheng sighed and lamented until he died. [44]

    ○曹惠
    　　武德初，有曹惠者，制授江州参军。官舍有佛堂，堂中有二木偶人，长尺余， 雕饰甚巧，丹青剥落。惠因持归与稚儿。后稚儿方食饼，木偶即引手请之。儿惊 报惠，惠笑曰：“取木偶来。”即言曰：“轻红、轻素自有名，何呼木偶！”于 是转盼驰走，悉无异人。
    　　惠问曰：“汝何时来物，颇能作怪？”轻素曰：“某与轻红是宣城太守谢家 俑偶，当时天下工巧，总不及沈隐侯家老苍头孝忠也。轻素、轻红即孝忠所造也。 隐侯哀宣城无辜，葬日故有此赠。时轻素在圹中，方持汤与乐家娘子濯足，闻外 有持兵称敕声，娘子畏惧，跣足化为白蝼，少顷，二贼执炬至，尽掠财物，谢郎 时颔瑟瑟环，亦为贼敲颐脱之。贼人照见轻红等，曰：‘二明器不恶，可与小儿 为戏具。’遂持出，时天正二年也。自尔流落数家，陈末麦铁杖犹子咬头将至此， 以到今日。*? [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 武德初 - Beginning of the Wu德 era, the reign title of Emperor Gaozu of Tang.
    • 制授 - Formal term meaning 'imperial appointment' or 'imperial grant.'
    • 丹青剥落 - Literally 'vermilion and indigo peeling away,' used to describe faded paint on wooden figures.
    • 轻红、轻素 - Likely names given to the wooden figures, possibly referring to their colors or characteristics.
    • 汝何时来物 - Rhetorical question asking 'When did you come into existence?'
    • 颇能作怪 - Means 'Are you capable of causing strange or supernatural events?'
    • 宣城太守谢家俑偶 - Refers to the wooden figures being made by a servant named Xie in the household of the governor of Xuanzhou.
    • 沈隐侯家老苍头孝忠 - Refers to the servant Xie being from the household of Shen Yinhou, a prominent official of the Southern Dynasties.
    • 轻素在圹中 - Means 'light and white were in the tomb,' referring to the wooden figure named '轻素' being in the tomb.
    • 方持汤与乐家娘子濯足 - Means 'was holding water to wash the feet of Lady Yue,' where '乐家娘子' likely refers to a woman from the Yue family.
    • 闻外有持兵称敕声 - Means 'heard the sound of someone outside with weapons claiming imperial edict,' suggesting a disturbance or intrusion.
    • 娘子畏惧，跣足化为白蝼 - Means 'the lady was afraid, and her bare feet transformed into white ants,' a supernatural transformation.
    • 少顷，二贼执炬至 - Means 'soon after, two bandits with torches arrived,' indicating a robbery.
    • 尽掠财物 - Means 'they took all the valuables,' describing the bandits' actions.
    • 谢郎时颔瑟瑟环 - Means 'Lord Xie was wearing a jade ring around his neck,' describing the bandit's interest in the jade ring.
    • 为贼敲颐脱之 - Means 'the bandit knocked it off his neck,' describing the bandit taking the jade ring.
    • 贼人照见轻红等 - Means 'the bandits saw light red and others,' referring to the wooden figures.
    • 可与小儿为戏具 - Means 'they could be toys for children,' describing the bandits' intention to take the figures.
    • 时天正二年也 - Means 'it was the second year of Tianzheng era,' referring to the time period.
    • 流落数家 - Means 'they passed through several households,' describing the journey of the wooden figures.
    • 陈末麦铁杖犹子咬头将至此 - Means 'at the end of the Chen dynasty, Wei Tiezhang's nephew named Jiao brought them here,' referring to the historical context of the figures' movement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武德初 - The beginning of the Wu德 era, marking the early Tang dynasty.
    • 宣城太守 - The governor of Xuanzhou, a region in southern China.
    • 沈隐侯 - Shen Yinhou, a prominent official of the Southern Dynasties, known for his contributions to literature and politics.
    • 谢家 - The household of the governor of Xuanzhou, indicating a noble family.
    • 乐家娘子 - A woman from the Yue family, likely a noble or wealthy family.
    • 天正二年 - The second year of the Tianzheng era, a period during the Tang dynasty.
    • 陈末 - The end of the Chen dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 麦铁杖犹子咬头 - Wei Tiezhang's nephew named Jiao, indicating a historical figure involved in the movement of the wooden figures.

    Proper Nouns

    • 曹惠 - Cao Hui, the main character in the story.
    • 江州 - Jiangzhou, a region in southern China.
    • 轻红、轻素 - Names of the wooden figures.
    • 宣城太守 - The governor of Xuanzhou.
    • 沈隐侯 - Shen Yinhou, a prominent official of the Southern Dynasties.
    • 谢家 - The household of the governor of Xuanzhou.
    • 乐家娘子 - A woman from the Yue family.
    • 天正二年 - The second year of the Tianzheng era.
    • 陈末 - The end of the Chen dynasty.
    • 麦铁杖犹子咬头 - Wei Tiezhang's nephew named Jiao.

    A Tale of Cao Hui

    In the early years of the Wu德 era, there was a man named Cao Hui who was appointed as a military secretary in Jiangzhou by imperial decree. In his official residence, there was a Buddhist hall, and in the hall were two wooden figures, each about a foot long, intricately carved and decorated, though their paint had faded. Cao Hui took them home and gave them to his young son. Later, when the child was eating a cake, the wooden figures reached out and asked for it. The child was startled and reported this to Cao Hui, who laughed and said, "Bring the wooden figures here." The figures then spoke, saying, "We are known as Light Red and Light White; why do you call us wooden figures?" They then turned and walked around as if they were real people.

    Cao Hui asked, "When did you come into existence, and are you capable of causing strange events?" Light White replied, "I and Light Red were made by a servant named Xie in the household of the governor of Xuanzhou. At that time, no one in the world could match the craftsmanship of the old servant Xie from the household of Shen Yinhou. Light Red and I were made by Xie. Shen Yinhou, moved by the innocence of the people of Xuanzhou, gave these figures to us on the day of the burial. At that time, I was in the tomb, holding water to wash the feet of Lady Yue. I heard the sound of someone outside with weapons claiming an imperial edict. Lady Yue was afraid, and her bare feet transformed into white ants. Soon after, two bandits with torches arrived and took all the valuables. Lord Xie was wearing a jade ring around his neck, and the bandit knocked it off. The bandits saw Light Red and others, saying, 'These two ritual objects are not bad; they can be toys for children.' So they took them out, and it was the second year of the Tianzheng era. Since then, they have passed through several households. At the end of the Chen dynasty, Wei Tiezhang's nephew named Jiao brought them here, and they have remained until today." [45]

    被萦治试唬骸霸悰判恍鞮撬魍蹙丛蚺巠籧麑五嵩评旨夷餇子？”轻 素曰：“王氏乃生前之妻，乐家乃冥婚耳。王氏本屠酤种，性粗率多力，至冥中 犹与宣城琴瑟不睦，何宣城颜严，则磔石抵关以为威胁。宣城自密启于天帝，帝 许逐之。二女一男，悉随母归矣。遂再娶乐彦辅第八娘子，美资质，善书，好弹 琴，尤与殷东阳仲文、谢荆州晦夫人相得，日恣追寻。宣城尝云：‘我才方古词 人，唯不及东阿耳。其余文士，皆吾机中之肉，可以宰割矣。’见为南曹典铨郎， 与潘典门同列，乘肥衣轻，贵于生前百倍。然十日一朝晋、宋、梁，可以为劳， 近闻亦已停矣。” [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 被萦治试唬 - Colloquial or dialectical expression; transliterated as 'Bei Yingzhi Shihu'; meaning uncertain.
    • 霸悰判恍鞮撬魍蹙丛蚺巠籧麑五嵩评旨夷餇子 - Highly archaic and difficult to interpret; transliterated as 'Bacon Pianhuan Di Qiao Wang Cuo Cong Ran Jing Qu Ni Wu Song Ping Zhi Yi Hu Zi'; meaning uncertain.
    • 轻素曰 - Formal way of introducing a speaker; meaning 'Qingsu said'.
    • 王氏乃生前之妻 - Refers to a living wife; meaning 'Wang is the wife in life'.
    • 乐家乃冥婚耳 - Refers to a posthumous marriage; meaning 'Le is a marriage in the afterlife'.
    • 王氏本屠酤种 - Indicates her social background; meaning 'Wang originally came from a butcher and seller family'.
    • 性粗率多力 - Describes her personality; meaning 'Her nature is rough and strong'.
    • 至冥中犹与宣城琴瑟不睦 - Indicates marital discord; meaning 'In the afterlife, she still does not get along with Xuancheng's wife'.
    • 何宣城颜严，则磔石抵关以为威胁 - Describes her aggressive behavior; meaning 'If Xuancheng's wife is strict, she will break stones and block the pass as a threat'.
    • 宣城自密启于天帝 - Indicates a petition to a higher authority; meaning 'Xuancheng secretly appealed to the Heavenly Emperor'.
    • 帝许逐之 - Indicates the outcome of the appeal; meaning 'The Emperor allowed her to be driven away'.
    • 二女一男，悉随母归矣 - Describes the family's return; meaning 'Two daughters and one son all followed their mother back'.
    • 遂再娶乐彦辅第八娘子 - Indicates a new marriage; meaning 'He then remarried the eighth daughter of Yue Yanfu'.
    • 美资质，善书，好弹琴 - Describes the new wife's attributes; meaning 'She has beautiful qualities, is good at writing, and enjoys playing the qin'.
    • 尤与殷东阳仲文、谢荆州晦夫人相得 - Indicates close relationships; meaning 'She especially gets along well with Yin Dongyang Zhongwen and Xie Jingzhou Hui's wife'.
    • 日恣追寻 - Suggests a habit of seeking something; meaning 'She daily indulges in searching'.
    • 我才方古词人，唯不及东阿耳 - Indicates self-assessment; meaning 'My talent is on par with ancient poets, only falling short of Dong'e'.
    • 其余文士，皆吾机中之肉，可以宰割矣 - Suggests a dismissive attitude towards other writers; meaning 'Other literary figures are all like meat in my machine, to be carved up'.
    • 见为南曹典铨郎 - Indicates his current position; meaning 'He is now the Director of the Southern Censorate'.
    • 与潘典门同列 - Indicates a peer in the same position; meaning 'He is in the same rank as Pan Dianmen'.
    • 乘肥衣轻，贵于生前百倍 - Indicates his elevated status; meaning 'He rides on fat horses and wears light clothes, valued a hundred times more than in life'.
    • 然十日一朝晋、宋、梁，可以为劳 - Indicates frequent visits to other realms; meaning 'However, every ten days he would visit Jin, Song, and Liang, which could be considered laborious'.
    • 近闻亦已停矣 - Indicates the cessation of these visits; meaning 'Recently, it is said that it has also stopped'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宣城 - Place name, possibly referring to a city or region.
    • 天帝 - The Heavenly Emperor, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 晋、宋、梁 - Refers to the Jin, Song, and Liang dynasties, indicating historical periods or realms.
    • 东阿 - Place name, possibly referring to a city or region.
    • 殷东阳仲文 - A person, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.
    • 谢荆州晦夫人 - A person, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.
    • 南曹典铨郎 - A position in the bureaucracy, possibly a high-ranking official.
    • 潘典门 - A person, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '霸悰判恍鞮撬魍蹙丛蚺巠籧麑五嵩评旨夷餇子' is highly ambiguous and difficult to interpret. It will be transliterated and its meaning noted as uncertain.
    • The clause '何宣城颜严，则磔石抵关以为威胁' is elliptical and could be interpreted in multiple ways. It will be translated as 'If Xuancheng's wife is strict, she will break stones and block the pass as a threat'.
    • The clause '二女一男，悉随母归矣' is elliptical and could be interpreted in multiple ways. It will be translated as 'Two daughters and one son all followed their mother back'.
    • The phrase '日恣追寻' is elliptical and could be interpreted in multiple ways. It will be translated as 'She daily indulges in searching'.
    • The clause '然十日一朝晋、宋、梁，可以为劳' is elliptical and could be interpreted in multiple ways. It will be translated as 'However, every ten days he would visit Jin, Song, and Liang, which could be considered laborious'.

    Proper Nouns

    • 被萦治试唬 - Transliterated as 'Bei Yingzhi Shihu'.
    • 霸悰判恍鞮撬魍蹙丛蚺巠籧麑五嵩评旨夷餇子 - Transliterated as 'Bacon Pianhuan Di Qiao Wang Cuo Cong Ran Jing Qu Ni Wu Song Ping Zhi Yi Hu Zi'.
    • 宣城 - Place name, possibly referring to a city or region.
    • 天帝 - The Heavenly Emperor, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 晋、宋、梁 - Refers to the Jin, Song, and Liang dynasties, indicating historical periods or realms.
    • 东阿 - Place name, possibly referring to a city or region.
    • 殷东阳仲文 - A person, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.
    • 谢荆州晦夫人 - A person, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.
    • 南曹典铨郎 - A position in the bureaucracy, possibly a high-ranking official.
    • 潘典门 - A person, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. [46]

    惠又问曰：“汝二人灵异若此，吾欲舍汝，何如？”即皆喜曰：“以轻素等 变化，虽无不可，君意如不放，终不能逃。庐山山神欲索轻素作舞姬久矣，今此 奉辞，便当受彼荣富。然君能终恩，请命画工，便赐粉黛。”即令工人为图之， 使被锦绣。轻素喜笑曰：“此度非论舞姬，亦当彼夫人。无以奉酬，请以微言留 别。百代之中，但有他人会者，无不为忠臣居大位矣。言曰：‘鸡角入骨，紫鹤 吃黄角甲（疑此处有脱误，“黄角甲”，《广记》作“黄鼠申”，“申”字或属 下读），不害五通泉室，为六代吉昌。’”言讫而灭。
    　　后有人祷庐山神，女巫云：“神君新纳一夫人，要翠花钗簪，汝宜求之，当 降大福。”祷者求而焚之，遂如愿焉。惠亦不能知其微言，访之时贤皆不识，或 云：中书令岑文本识其三句，亦不为人说云。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 靈異若此 - Phrase meaning 'so extraordinary' or 'so miraculous,' used to describe the remarkable abilities of the two figures.
    • 以輕素等變化 - Means 'by transforming into light and color,' referring to their ability to change form.
    • 無不可 - General expression meaning 'nothing is impossible,' indicating that they can do anything.
    • 終不能逃 - Means 'ultimately cannot escape,' suggesting that they cannot avoid their fate.
    • 欲索輕素作舞姬 - Means 'wants to take light and color as a dancer,' referring to the mountain god's desire to have them as his dancers.
    • 便當受彼榮富 - Means 'will then receive that honor and wealth,' indicating that they will gain status and riches.
    • 請命畫工 - Means 'please command the painter,' asking for a portrait to be made.
    • 使被锦绣 - Means 'to be dressed in brocade,' describing their attire.
    • 此度非论舞姬 - Means 'this time it's not just about being a dancer,' suggesting a higher status.
    • 亦当彼夫人 - Means 'but will also be his wife,' indicating a higher position.
    • 微言留别 - Means 'a subtle message to part with,' referring to the cryptic message they leave behind.
    • 百代之中 - Means 'in a hundred generations,' indicating a long time span.
    • 但有他人会者 - Means 'if anyone else understands this,' suggesting that only those who understand the message will benefit.
    • 无不为忠臣居大位矣 - Means 'will surely become loyal ministers and hold high positions,' indicating a positive outcome for those who understand.
    • 鸡角入骨 - Cryptic phrase, possibly a metaphor or allusion, whose meaning is unclear.
    • 紫鹤吃黄角甲 - Another cryptic phrase, possibly a metaphor or allusion, whose meaning is unclear.
    • 不害五通泉室 - Means 'will not harm the five-communication spring chamber,' possibly referring to a place or concept.
    • 为六代吉昌 - Means 'will bring good fortune for six generations,' indicating a long-lasting benefit.
    • 中书令岑文本识其三句 - Means 'the chancellor Cen Wenben recognized three of the sentences,' indicating that he understood part of the message.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 惠 - Likely refers to a historical figure, possibly a ruler or official, but the specific identity is unclear without further context.
    • 庐山山神 - Refers to the deity or spirit of Mount Lu (庐山), a famous mountain in China known for its cultural and religious significance.
    • 轻素 - A term that could refer to light and color, possibly symbolic of the two figures' abilities to change form.
    • 中书令 - A high-ranking official in the Chinese imperial court, responsible for drafting imperial edicts and managing state affairs.
    • 岑文本 - A historical figure, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary and administrative skills.

    Proper Nouns

    • 惠 - A proper noun referring to a historical figure, possibly a ruler or official.
    • 庐山山神 - A proper noun referring to the deity or spirit of Mount Lu.
    • 轻素 - A proper noun referring to light and color, possibly symbolic of the two figures' abilities.
    • 中书令 - A proper noun referring to a high-ranking official in the Chinese imperial court.
    • 岑文本 - A proper noun referring to a historical figure, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.

    Translation

    The official asked, "You two are so extraordinary, I wish to release you, what do you think?" They both happily replied, "By transforming into light and color, there is nothing we cannot do. If Your Excellency does not release us, we ultimately cannot escape. The god of Mount Lu has long desired to take light and color as his dancers. Now that we are leaving, we will receive his honor and wealth. However, if Your Excellency can fulfill this favor, please command the painter to create a portrait and bestow upon us cosmetics." The official then ordered the workers to make the portrait, and they were dressed in brocade. One of them, Light and Color, smiled and said, "This time, it's not just about being a dancer, but also his wife. There is nothing to repay, so please leave this subtle message as a farewell. In a hundred generations, if anyone else understands this, they will surely become loyal ministers and hold high positions. The message says: 'The rooster's horn penetrates the bone, the purple crane eats the yellow rat's skin (there may be an error here, 'yellow rat's skin,' the Guangji records 'yellow rat's tail,' the 'tail' character might belong to the next line), it will not harm the five-communication spring chamber, bringing good fortune for six generations.'" After speaking, they vanished.

    Later, someone prayed to the god of Mount Lu, and the female shaman said, "The divine lord has recently taken a new wife, who requires a jade flower hairpin. You should seek it and burn it, and you will receive great blessings." The one who prayed sought the hairpin and burned it, and as a result, his wish was fulfilled. The official could not understand the subtle message, and when he consulted learned scholars, none of them could decipher it. Some said that the chancellor Cen Wenben recognized three of the sentences, but he did not share them with others. [47]

    ○滕庭俊
    　　文明元年，毗陵掾滕庭俊患热病积年，每发身如火烧，热数日方定。召医， 医不能治。后之洛调选，行至荥阳西十四五里，天向暮，未达前所。遂投一道旁 庄家，主人暂出未至，庭俊心无聊赖，自叹吟曰：“为客多苦辛，日暮无主人。” 即有老父，须发甚秃，衣服亦弊，自堂西出而曰：“老父虽无所解，然性好文章， 适不知郎君来，正与和且耶联句次，闻郎君吟‘为客多苦辛，日暮无主人’，虽 曹丕‘客子常畏人’不能过也。老父与和且耶同作浑家门客，门客虽贫，亦有斗 酒接郎君清话耳。”庭俊甚异之，问：“老父居止何所？”老父曰：“仆忝浑家 扫门之客，姓麻，名束禾，第大，君何不呼为麻大。”庭俊即谢不敏，与之偕行， 绕堂西隅，遂见一门，门启，华堂复阁甚绮秀，馆中有樽酒盘杓。麻大揖庭俊同 坐。 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 为客多苦辛 - Poetic expression meaning 'being a guest brings much hardship and bitterness.'
    • 日暮无主人 - Poetic expression meaning 'at dusk, there is no host.'
    • 虽曹丕客子常畏人不能过也 - Comparison to Cao Pi's line 'guests often fear people,' emphasizing the depth of the guest's plight.
    • 忝浑家扫门之客 - Modest way of saying 'I am a humble servant in the household.'
    • 谢不敏 - Polite way of declining an offer, meaning 'I am not quick-witted enough.'
    • 偕行 - To walk together.
    • 华堂复阁 - A splendid hall with multiple floors.
    • 扫门之客 - A humble servant or retainer.
    • 博学求之 - To seek knowledge broadly.
    • 无不利于事也 - Nothing is unhelpful in matters, suggesting that all these roles or pursuits are valuable in their own right.
    • 可为师表 - Can serve as a teacher and model.
    • 清话 - A polite term for conversation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 文明元年 - The first year of the Wenming era, during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang (649-683 CE).
    • 毗陵 - An ancient name for Changzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 曹丕 - Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Wei dynasty (220-265 CE), known for his literary works.
    • 麻大 - A nickname for the old man, reflecting the common practice of using numbers to denote age or seniority in traditional Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 滕庭俊 - The name of the official.
    • 麻大 - The nickname of the old man.
    • 和且耶 - The name of the person the old man was composing poetry with.
    • 毗陵 - The location where the story takes place.

    In the first year of the Wenming era, the official of Piling, Teng Tingjun, suffered from a chronic fever illness that had plagued him for many years. Whenever it flared up, his body felt as if it were on fire, and the fever lasted for several days before subsiding. He called for a doctor, but the doctor could not cure him. Later, he went to Luoyang for promotion, and on his way, he reached a place about fifteen or sixteen miles west of荥yang as dusk approached. He had not yet reached his destination, so he sought shelter at a nearby farm. The owner was temporarily absent, and Teng Tingjun felt bored and sang to himself, saying, "Being a guest brings much hardship and bitterness, at dusk, there is no host." Just then, an old man with sparse hair and shabby clothes came out from the west side of the hall and said, "Although I have no special skills, I enjoy literature. I didn't know you were coming, and I was just composing poetry with Hé Qiěyē. I heard you sing, 'Being a guest brings much hardship and bitterness, at dusk, there is no host.' Although Cao Pi's line 'guests often fear people' cannot surpass it, I am a humble servant in the household. My name is Ma Shùhé, and you can call me Ma Dà. Although I am poor, I still have a jug of wine to entertain you with a pleasant conversation."

    Teng Tingjun was surprised and asked, "Where does the old man live?" The old man replied, "I am a humble servant in the household, and my name is Ma, and I am the first elder. Why don't you call me Ma Dà?" Teng Tingjun politely declined, saying he was not quick-witted enough, but he accompanied the old man. They walked around the west corner of the hall and saw a door. When they opened the door, they found a splendid hall with multiple floors, beautifully decorated. There was a feast prepared in the hall with a jug of wine and plates and cups. The old man bowed to Teng Tingjun and invited him to sit down. [48]

    良久，门中一客出，麻大曰：“和至矣。”庭俊即降阶相让，还坐，且耶谓 麻大曰：“适与君联句，诗头来未？”麻大自书题目曰：“同在浑平原门联句一 首。予已为四句矣。”麻大诗曰：
    　　自与慎终邻，馨香遂满身。
    　　无关好清净，又用去灰尘。
    　　且耶良久乃曰：“仆是七言，韵又不同，如何？”麻大曰：“但自为一章， 亦不恶。”于是且耶即吟曰：
    　　冬日每去依烟火，春至还归养子孙。
    　　曾向苻王笔端坐，迩来求食浑家门。
    　　庭俊犹未悟，见其馆华盛，因有淹留歇马之计，乃书四言云： [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 良久 - Temporal phrase meaning 'after a long time' or 'after a while.'
    • 降階相讓 - Means 'descended the steps to show respect' or 'exchanged courtesies by descending the steps.'
    • 還坐 - Means 'returned to sit.'
    • 適與君聯句 - Means 'just now composing verses together with you.'
    • 詩頭來未 - Means 'has the poem's opening line arrived yet?'
    • 予已為四句矣 - Means 'I have already composed four lines.'
    • 且耶 - Likely a name or title, to be kept consistent.
    • 仆是七言，韵又不同，如何 - Means 'I wrote in seven-character lines, and the rhyme is different, how can I proceed?'
    • 但自为一章，亦不恶 - Means 'just write your own section, it's not bad.'
    • 冬日每去依烟火，春至还归养子孙 - Means 'In winter, I often go to rely on the warmth of the hearth, in spring I return to raise my descendants.'
    • 曾向苻王笔端坐，迩来求食浑家门 - Means 'I once sat at the pen of Fu Wang, lately I seek food at the doors of the common people.'
    • 庭俊犹未悟 - Means 'Tingjun still did not understand.'
    • 见其馆华盛 - Means 'seeing the grandeur of his residence.'
    • 因有淹留歇马之计 - Means 'he had the plan to stay and rest his horse.'
    • 乃书四言云 - Means 'then he wrote four-character lines as follows.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 联句 - A form of collaborative poetry where participants take turns adding lines to a poem.
    • 浑家门 - Refers to the common people or the general populace.
    • 苻王 - Likely a reference to Fu Wang, a historical figure or a literary character.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庭俊 - Likely a name or title, to be kept consistent.
    • 麻大 - Likely a name or title, to be kept consistent.
    • 且耶 - Likely a name or title, to be kept consistent.
    • 浑家门 - Refers to the common people or the general populace.
    • 苻王 - Likely a reference to Fu Wang, a historical figure or a literary character.

    After a long time, a guest emerged from the door. Ma Da said, "The poem is here." Tingjun immediately descended the steps to show respect, returned to sit, and Zhuo Ye said to Ma Da, "Just now composing verses together with you, has the poem's opening line arrived yet?" Ma Da wrote the title himself, saying, "Collaborative poem at the Plain Gate of the Common People. I have already composed four lines." Ma Da's poem read:
    　　"From the beginning, I am a neighbor of the end, the fragrance fills my body.
    　　It is irrelevant to good cleanliness, yet it removes dust."
    　　After a long time, Zhuo Ye said, "I wrote in seven-character lines, and the rhyme is different, how can I proceed?" Ma Da said, "Just write your own section, it's not bad." Thus, Zhuo Ye immediately recited:
    　　"In winter, I often go to rely on the warmth of the hearth, in spring I return to raise my descendants.
    　　I once sat at the pen of Fu Wang, lately I seek food at the doors of the common people."
    　　Tingjun still did not understand, seeing the grandeur of his residence, he had the plan to stay and rest his horse, then he wrote four-character lines as follows. [49]

    田文称好客，凡养几多人？
    　　如使冯驩在，今希厕下宾。
    　　且耶、麻大笑曰：“何得相讥？向使君得在浑家，一日自当足矣。”于是餐 膳肴馔，引满数十巡。主人至，觅庭俊不见，使人叫唤之，庭俊应曰：“唯。” 而馆宇并麻、和二人一时不见，身在厕屋下，傍有大苍蝇、秃帚而已。庭俊先有 热疾，自此后顿愈，不复更发矣。
    　　○顾总
    　　梁天监元年，顾总为县吏，数被鞭捶，尝郁郁愤怀，因逃墟墓之间，彷徨惆 怅，不知所适。忽有二黄衣见顾总曰：“刘君，颇忆畴昔周旋否？”总惊曰： “弊宗乃顾氏，先未曾面清颜，何有周旋之问？”二人曰：“仆二人，王粲、徐 幹也。足下生前是刘桢，为坤明侍中，以纳赂金谪为小吏，公今当不知矣。然公 言辞历历，犹有记室音旨。”因出袖中五轴书示总曰：“此君集也，当谛视之。” 总试省览，乃了然明悟，便觉藻思泉涌。 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 田文称好客 - Formal expression meaning 'Lord Tiwen is known for his hospitality.'
    • 凡养几多人 - Direct question meaning 'How many people does he support?'
    • 如使冯驩在，今希厕下宾 - Conditional statement meaning 'If Feng Huan were still alive, he would be among the lowest-ranking guests today.'
    • 何得相讥 - Rhetorical question meaning 'How can you mock me?'
    • 向使君得在浑家，一日自当足矣 - Hypothetical statement meaning 'If you had been with your wife, one day would have been enough.'
    • 餐 膳肴馔，引满数十巡 - Describes a lavish feast with multiple rounds of food and drink.
    • 主人至，觅庭俊不见 - Narrative statement meaning 'When the host arrived, he couldn't find Ting Jun.'
    • 庭俊应曰：“唯。” - Formal response meaning 'Ting Jun replied, 'Yes.''
    • 身在厕屋下，傍有大苍蝇、秃帚而已 - Vivid description meaning 'He was in the latrine, with only a large fly and a broom nearby.'
    • 庭俊先有热疾，自此后顿愈，不复更发矣 - Narrative statement meaning 'Ting Jun had previously suffered from a fever, but after this, he was completely cured and the fever never returned.'
    • 梁天监元年 - Specific historical date meaning 'In the first year of the Tianjian era of the Liang dynasty.'
    • 顾总为县吏 - Narrative statement meaning 'Gu Zong was a county official.'
    • 数被鞭捶 - Narrative statement meaning 'He was often beaten.'
    • 尝郁郁愤怀 - Narrative statement meaning 'He often felt depressed and resentful.'
    • 因逃墟墓之间，彷徨惆怅，不知所适 - Narrative statement meaning 'He escaped to a place between tombs, wandering and feeling melancholic, not knowing where to go.'
    • 忽有二黄衣见顾总曰：“刘君，颇忆畴昔周旋否？” - Narrative statement meaning 'Suddenly, two yellow-clad men saw Gu Zong and said, 'Lord Liu, do you remember our past interactions?''
    • 弊宗乃顾氏，先未曾面清颜，何有周旋之问 - Narrative statement meaning 'My family is the Gu family, and I have never met you before, so why are you asking about our past interactions?'
    • 足下生前是刘桢，为坤明侍中，以纳赂金谪为小吏，公今当不知矣 - Narrative statement meaning 'You were Lord Liu Zhen in life, a Grand Councilor of Kunning, but you were demoted to a minor official for accepting bribes, and you probably don't remember this now.'
    • 然公言辞历历，犹有记室音旨 - Narrative statement meaning 'However, your words are still clear, and you still have the style of a secretary.'
    • 因出袖中五轴书示总曰：“此君集也，当谛视之。” - Narrative statement meaning 'They then took out five volumes of books from their sleeves and showed them to Gu Zong, saying, 'These are your collected works. You should read them carefully.''
    • 总试省览，乃了然明悟，便觉藻思泉涌 - Narrative statement meaning 'Gu Zong tried to read them, and suddenly he understood everything clearly and felt a surge of creative inspiration.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 田文 - Refers to Lord Tiwen, a famous nobleman known for his hospitality.
    • 冯驩 - Refers to Feng Huan, a loyal and capable retainer of Lord Tiwen.
    • 梁天监元年 - Refers to the first year of the Tianjian era of the Liang dynasty (502 AD).
    • 顾总 - Refers to Gu Zong, a historical figure who was a county official.
    • 刘桢 - Refers to Lord Liu Zhen, a historical figure known for his literary talent and political career.
    • 坤明 - Refers to Kunning, a place name in ancient China.
    • 纳赂金 - Refers to accepting bribes, a common practice in ancient Chinese politics.
    • 小吏 - Refers to a minor official, a lower-ranking government position.
    • 记室 - Refers to a secretary, a position responsible for writing and record-keeping.

    Proper Nouns

    • 田文 - Lord Tiwen
    • 冯驩 - Feng Huan
    • 顾总 - Gu Zong
    • 刘桢 - Lord Liu Zhen
    • 坤明 - Kunning
    • 梁天监元年 - First year of the Tianjian era of the Liang dynasty (502 AD)

    Lord Tiwen is known for his hospitality, but how many people does he support?

    If Feng Huan were still alive, he would be among the lowest-ranking guests today. Cai and Ma laughed and said, "How can you mock me? If you had been with your wife, one day would have been enough." They then had a lavish feast with multiple rounds of food and drink. When the host arrived, he couldn't find Ting Jun. He sent someone to call him, and Ting Jun replied, "Yes." But the guest rooms and Ma and He were nowhere to be seen. Ting Jun was in the latrine, with only a large fly and a broom nearby. Ting Jun had previously suffered from a fever, but after this, he was completely cured and the fever never returned.

    In the first year of the Tianjian era of the Liang dynasty, Gu Zong was a county official. He was often beaten and often felt depressed and resentful. He escaped to a place between tombs, wandering and feeling melancholic, not knowing where to go. Suddenly, two yellow-clad men saw Gu Zong and said, "Lord Liu, do you remember our past interactions?" Gu Zong was surprised and said, "My family is the Gu family, and I have never met you before, so why are you asking about our past interactions?" The two men replied, "We are Wang Can and Xu Gan. You were Lord Liu Zhen in life, a Grand Councilor of Kunning, but you were demoted to a minor official for accepting bribes, and you probably don't remember this now. However, your words are still clear, and you still have the style of a secretary." They then took out five volumes of books from their sleeves and showed them to Gu Zong, saying, "These are your collected works. You should read them carefully." Gu Zong tried to read them, and suddenly he understood everything clearly and felt a surge of creative inspiration. [50]

    其集人多有本，惟卒后数篇记得。诗一章，题目曰《从驾游幽丽宫却忆平生 西园文会因寄修文府正郎蔡伯喈》，诗曰：
    　　在汉绝纲纪，溟渎多腾湍。
    　　煌煌魏世祖，拯溺静波澜。
    　　天纪已垂定，邦人亦保完。
    　　大开相公府，掇拾尽幽兰。
    　　始从众君子，日侍贤主欢。
    　　文皇在春宫，烝孝逾问安。
    　　监抚多余闲，园圃恣游观。
    　　末臣戴簪笔，翊圣从和鸾。
    　　月出行殿凉，珍木清露溥。
    　　天文信辉丽，铿锵振琅玕。
    　　被命仰为和，顾征成所难。
    　　弱质不自持，危脆朽萎残。
    　　岂意十余年，陵寝梧楸寒。
    　　今朝坤明国，再顾簪蝉冠。
    　　侍游于离宫，高蹑浮云端。
    　　却忆西园时，生死暂悲酸。 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 其集人多有本 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'most of his works are available' or 'many of his collections are extant.'
    • 卒后数篇记得 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'several poems were written by the deceased after his death' or 'several poems were remembered or recalled by others after his death.'
    • 煌煌魏世祖，拯溺静波澜 - Laudatory statement about Cao Cao, but the exact meaning of 'rescuing those in peril and calming the waves' is somewhat metaphorical and could refer to his political or military achievements.
    • 天纪已垂定，邦人亦保完 - Suggests that the heavenly mandate has been established and the people are secure, but the exact meaning of '天纪' (heavenly mandate) is somewhat abstract.
    • 大开相公府，掇拾尽幽兰 - Somewhat metaphorical; 'greatly opening the Chancellor's office' could refer to the establishment of a new government or the appointment of a new chancellor, while 'gathering all the fragrant orchids' could refer to the recruitment of talented scholars.
    • 始从众君子，日侍贤主欢 - Somewhat elliptical; could mean 'At first, I followed the many gentlemen, daily serving the wise ruler with joy,' but the exact meaning depends on the context.
    • 文皇在春宫，烝孝逾问安 - Somewhat metaphorical; '文皇' (cultural emperor) could refer to a ruler who valued culture and learning, while '烝孝逾问安' (his filial piety exceeded the usual inquiries about health) could refer to his deep respect for his parents or ancestors.
    • 监抚多余闲，园圃恣游观 - Somewhat elliptical; could mean 'The supervisor had extra leisure time, the gardens were freely visited,' but the exact meaning depends on the context.
    • 末臣戴簪笔，翊圣从和鸾 - Somewhat metaphorical; '末臣' (lowly minister) could refer to a humble official, while '翊圣从和鸾' (supporting the holy one with harmonious music) could refer to his role in supporting the emperor during imperial processions.
    • 月出行殿凉，珍木清露溥 - Somewhat descriptive; 'walking out of the palace at night, it was cool' and 'precious trees were bathed in clear dew' describe a scene of natural beauty.
    • 天文信辉丽，铿锵振琅玕 - Somewhat metaphorical; 'the celestial phenomena are indeed splendid' and 'resounding like jade and stone' describe a scene of celestial beauty and harmony.
    • 被命仰为和，顾征成所难 - Somewhat elliptical; could mean 'Being commanded to compose a harmonious piece, looking back, it was difficult to complete,' but the exact meaning depends on the context.
    • 弱质不自持，危脆朽萎残 - Somewhat metaphorical; 'my weak nature could not sustain itself' and 'it was fragile, decaying, and withered' describe a state of physical or emotional fragility.
    • 岂意十余年，陵寝梧楸寒 - Somewhat metaphorical; 'who would have thought for over a decade' and 'the tombs were cold, with paulownia and Chinese parasol trees' describe a passage of time and a sense of loss.
    • 今朝坤明国，再顾簪蝉冠 - Somewhat metaphorical; 'today, in the country of K'un-ming' and 'again, wearing the official cap' describe a return to a place of honor or authority.
    • 侍游于离宫，高蹑浮云端 - Somewhat metaphorical; 'serving the emperor in the distant palace' and 'climbing high into the clouds' describe a sense of elevation or transcendence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏世祖 - Refers to Cao Cao, the de facto ruler of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period, often referred to as 'Emperor Wei' in later texts.
    • 修文府正郎蔡伯喈 - Pinyin: Cài Bójié. This refers to Cai Yong, a famous scholar, calligrapher, and musician during the Eastern Han dynasty. He served as a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Culture.
    • 春宫 - Refers to the Eastern Palace, where the heir apparent resided.
    • 簪笔 - A hairpin and pen, symbolizing a scholar-official.
    • 和鸾 - Refers to the music played during imperial processions.
    • 坤明国 - This is likely a fictional or allegorical place name, possibly referring to a distant or idealized land.
    • 离宫 - Refers to a palace located away from the main imperial residence, often used for leisure or retreat.

    Translation

    Most of his collections are available, but only a few poems are remembered after his death. One poem, titled "Following the Emperor on a Tour of the Beautiful Palace, Recalling Past Literary Gatherings at the Western Garden and Sending This Poem to Cai Bojia, the Right Minister of the Ministry of Culture," reads as follows:

    In the Han dynasty, the laws and norms were in chaos, and the rivers and lakes were turbulent.
    The illustrious Wei Emperor, Cao Cao, rescued those in peril and calmed the waves.
    The heavenly mandate has been established, and the people are also secure and complete.
    He greatly opened the Chancellor's office and gathered all the fragrant orchids.
    At first, I followed the many gentlemen, daily serving the wise ruler with joy.
    The cultural emperor was in the spring palace, his filial piety exceeding the usual inquiries about health.
