People write three-character and four-character poetic lines, one line starting and one line ending. The spring poem says: 'Flowers fall, butterflies dance, why do people suffer so much, it's enough to cause worry and distress.' There are over a hundred such poems, but I cannot remember them all." [66]

    ”夸郎甚异之，曰：“汝是谁家青衣，乃得至此？且 汝封郎，吾可屈致之乎？”婢子曰：“某王家二十七娘子从嫁，本名翡翠，偶因 化身游行，使为袁郎子罗得。封郎去此不远，但具主人之礼，少顷封郎即至。” 夸郎乃命酒具茶器，未移时，翡翠至，曰：“封郎在门外。”出见一少年，可二 十余，言辞温雅，风流爽迈。揖让登席，讨论子史，自哺竟夕，宾主相得。夸郎 曰：“足下高居，当垂见喻。”封郎曰：“平仲来日当有蔬馔奉邀，然非仆本居， 赘于琅琊耳。”再三殷勤而别。 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 甚异之 - Greatly surprised by it
    • 言辞温雅 - Words are gentle and refined
    • 风流爽迈 - Graceful and charming, vigorous and spirited
    • 自哺竟夕 - From dawn to dusk, throughout the night
    • 宾主相得 - Guest and host got along well
    • 当垂见喻 - Should explain this to me
    • 再三殷勤而别 - Repeatedly bid farewell sincerely

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 夸郎 - Character name, likely from a literary work or historical context
    • 封郎 - Character name, likely from a literary work or historical context
    • 翡翠 - Character name, likely from a literary work or historical context
    • 袁郎子罗得 - Character name, likely from a literary work or historical context
    • 平仲 - Character name, likely from a literary work or historical context
    • 琅琊 - Place name, historically significant in Chinese culture

    Proper Nouns

    • 夸郎 - Character name
    • 封郎 - Character name
    • 翡翠 - Character name
    • 袁郎子罗得 - Character name
    • 平仲 - Character name
    • 琅琊 - Place name

    Kuāláng was greatly surprised by it and said, "Who is this young lady in service, and how did she come to be here? And you, Fēngláng, can I invite you here?" The servant girl replied, "I am the twenty-seventh daughter of a certain king, originally named Fěicuì. I was once transformed to travel and made to serve as the son of Yuánlángzǐ, Luódé." Kuāláng then said, "Fēngláng is not far from here. Just prepare the host's courtesy, and in a short while, Fēngláng will arrive immediately." Soon, Fěicuì arrived and said, "Fēngláng is at the door." Upon seeing a young man, about twenty years old, with gentle and refined speech and graceful and spirited demeanor, they bowed and yielded, sat down, discussed Confucian and historical texts, spent the entire day and night together, and got along well. Kuāláng said, "Since you reside in a high position, you should explain this to me." Fēngláng replied, "Píngzhòng will have vegetable dishes to invite you the next day, but I am not from my original residence; I am currently staying in Lángyá." They repeatedly bid farewell sincerely. [67]

    及明日辰后，有小童前拜曰：“封郎使归儿送书，令从二郎引路。”启书读 曰：“佳辰气茂，思得良会，驻足层台，企俟光仪，唯足下但东驰耳。”夸郎即 策马从之，可行十里，忽见泉石萦彻，异花骈植，宾馆宏敞，穷极瑰宝。门悬青 绡幕，下宛一尺余，皆爇兽炭。夸郎与封郎相见，方顾异之，平仲回叱一小童曰： “捧笔奴，早令汝煎火浣幕，何故客至犹未毕！”但令去火，而幕色尤鲜。坐未 几，又有四人出宅，皆风雅士也。封生曰：“主人王二兄、三兄、四兄、六郎子， 其名曰准、曰推、曰惟、曰淮。? [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 及明日辰后 - By the next day after noon or The following day after midday.
    • 有小童前拜曰 - A little boy came forward to bow and say.
    • 封郎使归儿送书 - Lord Feng has sent me to deliver a letter.
    • 令从二郎引路 - Ordering Second Brother to lead the way.
    • 启书读曰 - Opening the letter to read it, it says.
    • 佳辰气茂 - On a fine day with abundant vitality.
    • 思得良会 - Desiring a good meeting.
    • 驻足层台 - Standing on a high platform.
    • 企俟光仪 - Waiting eagerly for your presence.
    • 唯足下但东驰耳 - Just ride eastward, my lord.
    • 夸郎即策马从之 - Lord Kua immediately mounted his horse and followed.
    • 可行十里 - After traveling ten miles.
    • 忽见泉石萦彻 - Suddenly saw springs and stones winding through.
    • 异花骈植 - Strange flowers growing in pairs.
    • 宾馆宏敞 - The guesthouse was spacious and grand.
    • 穷极瑰宝 - Filled with the most exquisite treasures.
    • 门悬青绡幕 - The door hung with green silk curtains.
    • 下宛一尺余 - Below, about a foot thick.
    • 皆爇兽炭 - All heated with animal charcoal.
    • 夸郎与封郎相见 - Lord Kua met with Lord Feng.
    • 方顾异之 - He was just about to look at it in wonder.
    • 平仲回叱一小童曰 - Pingzhong called back a little boy and said.
    • 捧笔奴 - The servant who holds the brush.
    • 早令汝煎火浣幕 - Why didn't you have the fire-washed curtain ready?
    • 何故客至犹未毕 - Why is it not finished when the guest arrives?
    • 但令去火 - Just remove the fire.
    • 而幕色尤鲜 - And the curtain color is even fresher.
    • 坐未几 - Not long after sitting.
    • 又有四人出宅 - Four more people came out of the house.
    • 皆风雅士也 - All were scholars of refined taste.
    • 封生曰 - Lord Feng said.
    • 主人王二兄、三兄、四兄、六郎子 - The host's Second Brother, Third Brother, Fourth Brother, and Sixth Son.
    • 其名曰准、曰推、曰惟、曰淮 - Their names are Quan, Tui, Wei, and Huai.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 封郎 - Lord Feng, a title indicating a noble or respected person.
    • 二郎 - Second Brother, a term of respect or familial relationship.
    • 平仲 - Pingzhong, a name that may refer to a specific historical or literary figure.
    • 风雅士 - Scholars of refined taste, referring to individuals with high cultural and literary standards.
    • 准、推、惟、淮 - Names of individuals, possibly characters in a literary work or historical figures.

    Proper Nouns

    • 封郎 - Lord Feng, a title indicating a noble or respected person.
    • 二郎 - Second Brother, a term of respect or familial relationship.
    • 平仲 - Pingzhong, a name that may refer to a specific historical or literary figure.
    • 风雅士 - Scholars of refined taste, referring to individuals with high cultural and literary standards.
    • 准、推、惟、淮 - Names of individuals, possibly characters in a literary work or historical figures.

    By the next day after noon, a little boy came forward to bow and say, "Lord Feng has sent me to deliver a letter, ordering Second Brother to lead the way." Opening the letter to read it, it said, "On a fine day with abundant vitality, I desire a good meeting. Standing on a high platform, I wait eagerly for your presence. Just ride eastward, my lord." Lord Kua immediately mounted his horse and followed. After traveling ten miles, he suddenly saw springs and stones winding through, strange flowers growing in pairs, and a guesthouse that was spacious and grand, filled with the most exquisite treasures. The door hung with green silk curtains, below which was about a foot thick, all heated with animal charcoal. Lord Kua met with Lord Feng and was just about to look at it in wonder. Pingzhong called back a little boy and said, "The servant who holds the brush, why didn't you have the fire-washed curtain ready? Why is it not finished when the guest arrives?" Just remove the fire, and the curtain color is even fresher. Not long after sitting, four more people came out of the house, all scholars of refined taste. Lord Feng said, "The host's Second Brother, Third Brother, Fourth Brother, and Sixth Son, their names are Quan, Tui, Wei, and Huai." [68]

    笨淅上嗉嚬淴掞鷩从辛斝嘁拢杂袪殊色，悉 衣珠翠，捧方丈盘至，珍羞万品，中有珍录，无不殚尽。王淮曰：“有少家乐， 请此奉娱。”即有女娃十余人并出，别有胡优，咬指翘足，一时拜员外，资次即 为给舍。淮指二妓曰：“石崇妾仙娥娘也，名称亚于绿珠。”于是丝竹并作，铿 锵清亮。日晚，王氏昆弟醉寝，封生谓夸郎曰：“此亦足为富贵，然丈人为太守， 当不以此盛。”夸郎曰：“不以鄙贱，百倍行采，不审何以致之？”封生曰： “君诚能结同心，仆便请为行人。拙室有姨，美淑善音，请袁君思之。”夸郎曰： “但恐龙门下难为鱼耳。”封生因入白王氏尊长，即出曰：“允矣！明日吉，便 为迎日。”夸郎大悦，许之。 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 悉 衣珠翠 - All dressed in pearls and jade, describing elaborate and luxurious attire.
    • 捧方丈盘至，珍羞万品 - Carrying a large tray filled with a myriad of delicacies, emphasizing abundance and variety.
    • 中有珍录，无不殚尽 - There are precious records, none of which are lacking, suggesting completeness or thoroughness.
    • 丝竹并作，铿锵清亮 - String and wind instruments played together, sounding clear and resonant, describing the music.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 石崇 - A famous figure from the Jin Dynasty, known for his wealth and the concubine Lu Zhu.
    • 绿珠 - A famous courtesan of the Jin Dynasty, known for her beauty and loyalty to Shi Chong.
    • 龙门下难为鱼 - A metaphorical expression from the story of the Dragon Gate, where fish must leap through a gate to transform into dragons. It symbolizes the difficulty of achieving something great.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王淮 - A historical or fictional figure, likely a member of the Wang family, a prominent clan in Chinese history.
    • 封生 - A character in the text, likely a messenger or matchmaker.
    • 夸郎 - A character in the text, likely a young man seeking to improve his social status.
    • 袁君 - A character in the text, likely a third party involved in the marriage proposal.

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. She was all dressed in pearls and jade, carrying a large tray filled with a myriad of delicacies, none of which were lacking. Wang Huai said, "There is some family entertainment; please enjoy this." Then, a group of young women and foreign entertainers performed a ritual or dance, bowing to the officials in a graceful manner. Wang Huai pointed to two courtesans and said, "They are the concubines of Shi Chong, known as Xian'e Nang, whose fame is second only to that of Lu Zhu." Then the string and wind instruments played together, sounding clear and resonant. By evening, the Wang brothers had drunk themselves to sleep. Feng Sheng said to Kua Lang, "This is also enough for wealth and honor, but since your father is the governor, it is not yet grand enough." Kua Lang said, "Not because of my lowly status, but how can I achieve it?" Feng Sheng said, "If you can truly unite in heart, I will ask to be your messenger. My humble home has an aunt who is beautiful, virtuous, and skilled in music. Please consider her, Mr. Yuan." Kua Lang said, "I am only afraid that it will be difficult to become a fish under the Dragon Gate." Feng Sheng then went inside to inform the respected elders of the Wang family, and upon returning, said, "It is agreed! Tomorrow is auspicious; it will be the day of the wedding." Kua Lang was greatly pleased and agreed. [69]

    明日，王氏昆弟方陈设于堂下，茵榻帷帐，赫然炫目。及夸郎入，帘下有女 郎曰：“袁郎行动趋跄，犹似把书入学时。”又老青衣过，夸郎拜谢讫，目之。 即又笑曰：“禽霏□无乳久矣，袁郎何用目之！”将暮，傧来皆至，有青衣持笺 催妆诗，夸郎下笔赋诗曰：
    　　
    　　好花本自有春晖，不偶红妆乱玉姿。
    　　
    　　若用何郎面上粉，任将多少借光仪。
    　　
    　　其余吉礼，无不毕备。篇咏甚多，而不悉记得。唯忆得咏花扇诗曰：
    　　
    　　圆扇画方新，金花照锦茵。
    　　
    　　那言灯下见，更值月中人。
    　　
    　　夸郎妻殊丽绝国，举止闲雅，小名曰从从，正名携。第二十七仪质亦得类娣 娣，辩捷善戏谑，赠袁郎诗曰： [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 明日 - Means 'the next day' or 'tomorrow', setting the temporal context.
    • 王氏昆弟 - Refers to the brothers of the Wang family, indicating a group of individuals.
    • 茵榻帷帐 - Describes a luxurious setting with cushions, beds, and curtains, emphasizing opulence.
    • 赫然炫目 - Means 'brightly dazzling' or 'strikingly eye-catching', describing the vividness of the scene.
    • 夸郎 - A term of endearment or affectionate address, likely referring to a young man or a close associate.
    • 老青衣 - Refers to an elderly servant or attendant, often dressed in simple clothing.
    • 禽霏□无乳久矣 - A playful or humorous phrase, possibly a pun or a cultural reference, indicating that someone has been without milk for a long time.
    • 何用目之 - Means 'why look at it', suggesting a dismissive or teasing tone.
    • 傧来皆至 - Means 'all the attendants have arrived', indicating the completion of preparations.
    • 青衣持笺催妆诗 - Refers to a servant holding a note with a poem to hurry the bridegroom.
    • 好花本自有春晖 - A poetic line meaning 'beautiful flowers naturally have the warmth of spring'.
    • 不偶红妆乱玉姿 - Means 'not coinciding with the red makeup of the fair lady, disrupting her graceful form'.
    • 若用何郎面上粉 - A reference to the beauty of a man, possibly a historical figure, suggesting the use of his face powder.
    • 任将多少借光仪 - Means 'one can borrow as much of his radiance as needed'.
    • 吉礼 - Refers to auspicious rituals, typically associated with weddings.
    • 圆扇画方新 - Describes a round fan with a new square design.
    • 金花照锦茵 - Means 'golden flowers illuminating the brocade mat'.
    • 那言灯下见 - Means 'how can one see it under the lamp', suggesting a moment of surprise or revelation.
    • 更值月中人 - Means 'even more so, encountering the person in the moonlight', adding a romantic or mystical element.
    • 殊丽绝国 - Means 'extraordinarily beautiful, surpassing the realm', describing the bride's exceptional beauty.
    • 举止闲雅 - Means 'graceful and elegant manners'.
    • 小名曰从从 - Means 'her pet name is Congcong'.
    • 正名携 - Means 'her formal name is Xie'.
    • 第二十七仪质亦得类娣娣 - Refers to the second wife or concubine, indicating a similar level of grace and beauty.
    • 辩捷善戏谑 - Means 'quick-witted and good at joking', describing the second wife's personality.
    • 赠袁郎诗曰 - Means 'gave a poem to Yuan Lang saying', indicating the start of a poem.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王氏 - The Wang family, a prominent clan in Chinese history, often associated with nobility and wealth.
    • 夸郎 - Likely a term of endearment for a close associate or a young man, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 袁郎 - A term of endearment for a close associate or a young man.
    • 何郎 - A reference to a man, possibly a historical figure, known for his beauty.
    • 从从 - A pet name, often given to children or close associates, indicating a personal and affectionate relationship.
    • 携 - A formal name, often used in official documents or formal settings, indicating a more serious or official relationship.
    • 第二十七仪质亦得类娣娣 - A reference to the second wife or concubine, indicating a similar level of grace and beauty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王氏 - The Wang family, a prominent clan in Chinese history.
    • 夸郎 - A term of endearment for a close associate or a young man.
    • 袁郎 - A term of endearment for a close associate or a young man.
    • 何郎 - A reference to a man, possibly a historical figure, known for his beauty.
    • 从从 - A pet name, often given to children or close associates.
    • 携 - A formal name, often used in official documents or formal settings.
    • 第二十七仪质亦得类娣娣 - A reference to the second wife or concubine.

    The Next Day, the brothers of the Wang family had just set up the arrangements in the hall, with cushions, beds, and curtains, brightly dazzling the eyes. When the young man entered, a young woman under the curtain said, "Yuan Lang's movements are still as graceful as when he was carrying books to school." Another elderly servant passed by, and the young man bowed in thanks and looked at him. The servant then laughed and said, "It has been a long time since Qinfen has had milk, why does Yuan Lang look at it!" As dusk approached, all the attendants arrived, and a servant held a note with a poem to hurry the bridegroom. The young man then wrote a poem in response:

    Beautiful flowers naturally have the warmth of spring, not coinciding with the red makeup of the fair lady, disrupting her graceful form. If one uses the face powder of He Lang, one can borrow as much of his radiance as needed.

    The other auspicious rituals were all fully prepared. There were many poems, but not all of them can be remembered. Only the poem about the fan can be recalled:

    The round fan with a new square design, golden flowers illuminating the brocade mat. How can one see it under the lamp, even more so, encountering the person in the moonlight.

    The young man's wife is extraordinarily beautiful, surpassing the realm, with graceful and elegant manners. Her pet name is Congcong, and her formal name is Xie. The second wife or concubine is also similarly graceful and beautiful, quick-witted and good at joking, and gave a poem to Yuan Lang saying: [70]

    人家女美大须愁，往往丑郎门外求。
    　　
    　　昨日金刚脚下见，今朝何得此间游？
    　　
    　　及后，班坐桐阴，封平仲鼓琴，顾谓夸郎曰：“姨夫岂无一言相赠？”夸郎 即赋诗曰：
    　　
    　　宾匣开玉琴，高梧追烦暑。
    　　
    　　商弦一以发，白云飘然举。
    　　
    　　何必苍梧东，激琴怀怨浦。
    　　
    　　夸郎日恣余嗛，遂无归思。忽觉妻皆惨，又饰行装。夸郎问封生，封生曰： “丈人晋侍中王济也，久为阴道交州牧，近改并州刺史。若足下以贤尊在此，不 能俱往，则当从此有终天之别。”其妻呜咽流涕曰：“君本自殊途，不期与会， 致今日之别，亦封郎二兄之过。”遂闻外人呼声，走出，回顾已苍然不复见一物。 太守求不得已近一年，及至，数月犹恍，往往奔至前所，别无所见，复涕泣而退， 终岁乃如故。 [71]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [71]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 人家女美大须愁 - Colloquial expression meaning 'if a woman is very beautiful, she must be worried.'
    • 往往丑郎门外求 - Means 'often, ugly men seek her outside the door.'
    • 金刚脚下见 - Refers to encountering someone at the feet of a Buddha statue, symbolizing a chance encounter or a moment of fate.
    • 及后，班坐桐阴 - Means 'later, they sat under the shade of a paulownia tree.'
    • 封平仲鼓琴 - Means 'Feng Pingzhong played the qin (a traditional Chinese zither).'
    • 姨夫岂无一言相赠 - A polite way of asking, 'Uncle, do you have any words of advice for me?'
    • 宾匣开玉琴 - Means 'the guest opened the jade qin.'
    • 高梧追烦暑 - Means 'the tall paulownia tree chased away the heat.'
    • 商弦一以发 - Means 'the qin strings played a piece of music.'
    • 白云飘然举 - Means 'the white clouds floated up.'
    • 何必苍梧东 - Means 'why go to the east of Cangwu (a place name).'
    • 激琴怀怨浦 - Means 'play the qin with a heart full of resentment.'
    • 夸郎日恣余嗛 - Means 'the arrogant man daily indulges in his conceit.'
    • 遂无归思 - Means 'thus he has no thoughts of returning.'
    • 忽觉妻皆惨 - Means 'suddenly he felt his wife was sad.'
    • 又饰行装 - Means 'he then prepared his traveling clothes.'
    • 夸郎问封生 - Means 'the arrogant man asked Feng Pingzhong.'
    • 丈人晋侍中王济也 - Means 'my father-in-law is Jin Shizhong Wang Ji (a historical figure).'
    • 久为阴道交州牧 - Means 'he has been the governor of Jiaozhou for a long time.'
    • 近改并州刺史 - Means 'recently, he was appointed as the governor of Bingzhou.'
    • 若足下以贤尊在此，不能俱往 - Means 'if you are here with your respected father-in-law, we cannot go together.'
    • 则当从此有终天之别 - Means 'then we will have a final farewell here.'
    • 遂闻外人呼声 - Means 'then he heard the calls of people outside.'
    • 走出，回顾已苍然不复见一物 - Means 'he went out, looked back, and found everything had turned gray and nothing was seen.'
    • 太守求不得已近一年 - Means 'the magistrate sought for him for nearly a year.'
    • 及至，数月犹恍 - Means 'when he arrived, for several months he was still dazed.'
    • 往往奔至前所 - Means 'often he rushed to the place where he met her.'
    • 别无所见 - Means 'but saw nothing else.'
    • 复涕泣而退 - Means 'he cried and withdrew.'
    • 终岁乃如故 - Means 'finally, he returned to normal after a year.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 人家女美大须愁 - Reflects a traditional Chinese belief that beautiful women often face more challenges or jealousy.
    • 金刚脚下见 - The Buddha statue symbolizes a moment of fate or destiny.
    • 封平仲 - A historical figure known for his musical talent.
    • 王济 - A historical figure, Jin Shizhong Wang Ji, who was a high-ranking official.
    • 交州 - An ancient Chinese province, now part of modern-day Vietnam.
    • 并州 - An ancient Chinese province, now part of northern China.
    • 终天之别 - A term referring to a final and permanent farewell.
    • 太守 - An ancient Chinese title for a magistrate or governor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 封平仲 - A historical figure known for his musical talent.
    • 王济 - A historical figure, Jin Shizhong Wang Ji, who was a high-ranking official.
    • 交州 - An ancient Chinese province, now part of modern-day Vietnam.
    • 并州 - An ancient Chinese province, now part of northern China.
    • 太守 - An ancient Chinese title for a magistrate or governor.

    A beautiful woman must often be worried, for she is frequently sought after by unattractive men outside her door.

    Yesterday, I encountered her at the feet of the Buddha statue; how could I have come to this place today?

    Later, we sat under the shade of a paulownia tree, and Feng Pingzhong played the qin. He turned to the arrogant man and said, "Uncle, do you have any words of advice for me?" The arrogant man immediately composed a poem:

    The guest opened the jade qin, and the tall paulownia tree chased away the heat.
    The qin strings played a piece of music, and the white clouds floated up.
    Why go to the east of Cangwu? Play the qin with a heart full of resentment.

    The arrogant man daily indulged in his conceit, thus he had no thoughts of returning. Suddenly, he felt his wife was sad, so he prepared his traveling clothes. The arrogant man asked Feng Pingzhong, who replied, "My father-in-law is Jin Shizhong Wang Ji, who has been the governor of Jiaozhou for a long time. Recently, he was appointed as the governor of Bingzhou. If you are here with your respected father-in-law, we cannot go together. Then we will have a final farewell here." His wife wept and said, "We were originally on different paths, and it was unexpected that we met. This separation today is also the fault of Feng Lang's second brother." Then he heard the calls of people outside. He went out, looked back, and found everything had turned gray and nothing was seen. The magistrate sought for him for nearly a year. When he arrived, for several months he was still dazed. Often, he rushed to the place where he met her, but saw nothing else. He cried and withdrew, finally returning to normal after a year. [71]

    ○张左
    　　
    　　前进士张左，尝为叔父言：
    　　
    　　少年南次鄠杜，郊行，见有老父乘青驴，四足白，腰背鹿革囊，颜甚悦怿， 旨趣非凡。叟自斜径合路，左甚异之，试问所从来，叟但笑而不答。至于再三， 叟忽怒叱曰：“年少子，乃敢相逼！吾岂盗贼椎埋者耶？何必问所从来。”左逊 谢曰：“向慕先生高躅，愿从事左右耳，何赐深责？”叟曰：“吾无术教子，但 寿永者。子当嗤我潦倒，欲噱吾释志耳。”遂鞭乘促走，左亦扑马趋，俱至逆旅。 叟枕鹿囊，寝未熟，左方疲倦，取酒将饮，就请曰：“箪醪期先生共之。”叟跳 起曰：“此正吾所好，何子解吾意？”饮讫，左觇其色悦，徐请曰：“小生寡味， 愿先生赐言以广闻见，然非所敢望。”叟曰：“吾所见梁陈隋唐耳，贤愚治乱， 国史已具。然请以身所录者语子。” [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 前進士張左 - Refers to Zhang Zuo, a former candidate for the imperial examinations.
    • 鄠杜 - A region near present-day Xi'an, historically significant for its proximity to the capital.
    • 斜徑合路 - Refers to a path that meets the main road at an angle.
    • 向慕先生高躅 - Means 'to admire the lofty conduct of a sage,' where '高躅' metaphorically refers to the steps of a sage.
    • 壽永者 - Means 'one who grants longevity,' a common expression in classical texts for immortals or those with long life.
    • 潦倒 - Describes a state of poverty or disrepute, but here it is used ironically.
    • 噱吾釋志 - Means 'to laugh at my aspirations,' suggesting a dismissive attitude towards the old man's desires.
    • 鹿囊 - Refers to a bag made from deer skin, commonly used for carrying items.
    • 小生寡味 - A modest self-reference, meaning 'a student of little taste or understanding.'
    • 願先生賜言以廣聞見 - Means 'I wish for the teacher to impart words to broaden my knowledge and vision.'
    • 賢愚治亂 - Refers to the wise and foolish, the well-governed and chaotic, summarizing the state of affairs in history.
    • 國史已具 - Means 'the national history is already recorded,' indicating that historical records are comprehensive.
    • 身所錄者 - Means 'what I have personally recorded,' suggesting personal experience or observation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 進士 - Refers to a degree granted by the imperial examination system, a key part of the civil service recruitment process.
    • 梁陳隋唐 - Refers to the Southern Dynasties (Liang, Chen) and the Sui and Tang dynasties, a period of significant cultural and political development in Chinese history.
    • 國史 - Refers to the official historical records of a country, which in this context encompasses the comprehensive histories of the mentioned dynasties.
    • 先達 - Refers to earlier accomplished figures or predecessors who can be learned from, emphasizing the importance of tradition and mentorship.
    • 高躅 - A metaphorical expression for the conduct or footsteps of a sage, often used to describe admirable behavior or achievements.
    • 壽永者 - A reference to immortals or those with long life, reflecting the Chinese cultural fascination with longevity and immortality.
    • 鹿囊 - A practical item, but also carries symbolic weight, as deer are often associated with nobility and grace in Chinese culture.
    • 身所錄者 - Suggests a personal record or experience, which in this context implies the old man's unique insights or observations.

    Proper Nouns

    • 張左 - Zhang Zuo, the main character of the story.
    • 鄠杜 - A historical region near present-day Xi'an.
    • 梁陳隋唐 - The Southern Dynasties (Liang, Chen) and the Sui and Tang dynasties.
    • 國史 - Official historical records of a country.

    A Former Candidate Zhang Zuo

    Zhang Zuo, a former candidate for the imperial examinations, once told his uncle:

    When I was young and traveling south to the Hu Du region, I was walking in the outskirts when I saw an old man riding a green donkey. The donkey's four legs were white, and he had a bag made of deer skin on his back. His face was very joyful, and his demeanor was extraordinary. The old man came from a side path that met the main road. I was very surprised by him and asked where he had come from. The old man just smiled and did not answer. After asking him repeatedly, the old man suddenly became angry and shouted, "Young man, how dare you press me! Am I a thief or a murderer? Why do you need to ask where I came from?" I apologized humbly, saying, "I admire the lofty conduct of a sage and wish to learn from you. Why do you blame me so severely?" The old man replied, "I have no skills to teach you, only the gift of longevity. You will probably mock my poverty and want to laugh at my aspirations." He then whipped his donkey and hurried away, and I also spurred my horse to follow, and we both arrived at an inn. The old man lay down with his deer skin bag as a pillow and had not yet fallen asleep. I was very tired and took out wine to drink, and I invited him, saying, "Let us share this small amount of wine together." The old man jumped up and said, "This is exactly what I like, how did you understand my meaning?" After drinking, I noticed that his face was very pleased. I then politely asked, "I have little taste, I wish for the teacher to impart words to broaden my knowledge and vision, but I dare not hope for much." The old man said, "What I have seen is the Liang, Chen, Sui, and Tang dynasties. The wise and foolish, the well-governed and chaotic, are all recorded in the national history. However, let me tell you what I have personally recorded." [72]

    吾宇文周时居岐，扶风人也，姓申名宗，慕齐神武，因改为欢。十八，从燕 公于谨征梁元帝于荆州，陷大将军。旋梦青衣二人谓余曰：“吕走天年，人向主 寿。”既觉，吾乃诣占梦者于江陵市，占梦者谓余曰：“吕走，回字也。人向主， 住字也。岂子住乃寿也。”时留兵于江陵，吾遂陈情于校尉托跋烈，许之。 [73]

    I lived in the region of Qishan (岐) during the time of the Northern Wei dynasty, which was ruled by the Yuwen clan. I am from the region of Fufeng (扶风), and my surname is Shen (申) and my given name is Zong (宗). I admired the military prowess of the Qi dynasty, particularly its founder, Gao Huan (高欢), who was known as the "Wu" (武) due to his military achievements. Therefore, I changed my name to "Huan" (欢), which means "joyful" or "happy."

    At the age of eighteen, I participated in a military campaign led by Yu Jin (于谨) against Emperor Yuan of Liang (梁元帝) in Jingzhou (荆州). During the campaign, I was promoted to the rank of Grand General (大将军).

    Shortly after, I had a dream in which two people dressed in green clothing spoke to me. They said, "吕走天年，人向主寿." Upon waking, I went to a dream interpreter in the market of Jiangling (江陵). The dream interpreter explained that "吕走" means "回" (回), and "人向主" means "住" (住). He suggested that I would live a long life if I remained in place.

    At the time, my army was stationed in Jiangling (江陵). I then approached the military officer Tuoba Lie (托跋烈) and expressed my desire to remain in place. He agreed to my request. [73]

    因却诣占梦者曰：“住即合矣，寿有术乎？”占者曰：“汝生前梓潼薛君曹 也，好服木蕊散，多寻异书，日诵黄老一百纸，徙居鹤鸣山下，草堂三间，户外 骈植花竹，泉石萦绕。”八月十五日，长啸独饮，因酒酣畅，大言曰：“薛君曹 疏澹若此，何无异人降止？”忽觉两耳中有车马声，因颓然思寝，才至席，遂有 小车，朱轮青盖，驾赤犊出耳中，各高二三寸，亦不知出耳之难。车有二童，绿 帻青帔，亦长二三寸，凭轼呼御者，踏轮扶下，而谓君曹曰：“吾自兜玄国来， 向闻长啸月下，韵甚清激，私心奉慕，愿接清论。?本翙艽蠛霑唬骸熬薁出吾 耳，何谓兜玄国来？”二童子曰：“兜玄国在吾耳中，君耳安能处我？”君曹曰： “君长二三寸，岂复耳有国土！倘若有之，国人当尽焦螟耳。”二童曰：“胡为 其然！吾国与汝国无异，不信，盍从吾游。或能使留，则君无生死苦矣。 [74]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [74]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 因却诣占梦者曰 - A formal expression meaning 'then he went to consult a dream interpreter and said.'
    • 住即合矣 - A somewhat ambiguous phrase that could mean 'if you stay, everything will be fine' or 'if you stay, it will be harmonious.'
    • 寿有术乎 - A question meaning 'Is there a method to prolong life?'
    • 汝生前梓潼薛君曹也 - A formal statement meaning 'You were previously the man named Xue Juncao from Zitong.'
    • 好服木蕊散 - Means 'enjoyed taking the wood essence powder,' referring to a specific herbal remedy.
    • 多寻异书 - Means 'often sought out unusual books,' indicating a habit of seeking rare or esoteric texts.
    • 日诵黄老一百纸 - Means 'daily recited a hundred sheets of Huang-Lao,' referring to the teachings of Laozi and Huangdi.
    • 徙居鹤鸣山下 - Means 'moved to live at the foot of Crane Chirping Mountain.'
    • 草堂三间 - Means 'a thatched cottage with three rooms.'
    • 户外骈植花竹 - Means 'in front of the house, flowers and bamboo were planted in rows.'
    • 泉石萦绕 - Means 'surrounded by springs and stones.'
    • 八月十五日 - A specific date, the 15th day of the 8th lunar month.
    • 长啸独饮 - Means 'sang loudly and drank alone.'
    • 因酒酣畅 - Means 'as he became drunk and cheerful.'
    • 大言曰 - Means 'said loudly.'
    • 薛君曹疏澹若此 - Means 'Xue Juncao is so reclusive and indifferent.'
    • 何无异人降止 - Means 'why hasn't any extraordinary person come to visit?'
    • 忽觉两耳中有车马声 - Means 'suddenly heard the sound of carriages and horses in his ears.'
    • 因颓然思寝 - Means 'then he leaned back and thought of sleeping.'
    • 才至席 - Means 'as soon as he reached his mat.'
    • 遂有小车 - Means 'a small carriage appeared.'
    • 朱轮青盖 - Describes the carriage with a red wheel and green canopy.
    • 驾赤犊 - Means 'driven by red calves.'
    • 各高二三寸 - Means 'each about two or three inches tall.'
    • 亦不知出耳之难 - Means 'nor did he know how difficult it was for them to come out of his ear.'
    • 二童 - Refers to two children.
    • 绿帻青帔 - Describes the children wearing green headbands and blue cloaks.
    • 凭轼呼御者 - Means 'leaning on the carriage's front rail, they called the driver.'
    • 踏轮扶下 - Means 'stepped on the wheel and helped him down.'
    • 兜玄国 - A mythical place.
    • 长啸月下 - Means 'sang loudly under the moon.'
    • 韵甚清激 - Means 'the tone was very clear and piercing.'
    • 私心奉慕 - Means 'we privately admire and respect you.'
    • 愿接清论 - Means 'we wish to engage in a clear and profound discussion.'
    • 吾自兜玄国来 - Means 'we have come from the Land of Douchuan.'
    • 向闻长啸月下 - Means 'we heard you singing loudly under the moon.'
    • 岂复耳有国土 - Means 'how can there be a country inside your ear?'
    • 国人当尽焦螟耳 - Means 'the people of that country would all be tiny insects.'
    • 胡为其然 - Means 'why is that so?'
    • 吾国与汝国无异 - Means 'our country is no different from yours.'
    • 不信，盍从吾游 - Means 'if you don't believe us, why not come with us on a journey?'
    • 或能使留 - Means 'perhaps we can make you stay.'
    • 则君无生死苦矣 - Means 'then you would no longer suffer from the pain of birth and death.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 占梦者 - Refers to a person who interprets dreams, a common practice in ancient China.
    • 木蕊散 - A specific herbal remedy, likely a mixture of herbs used for health purposes.
    • 黄老 - Refers to the teachings of Laozi and Huangdi, central to Daoist philosophy.
    • 鹤鸣山 - A mountain in Sichuan Province, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 黄老一百纸 - Refers to the practice of daily recitation of Daoist texts.
    • 长啸 - A traditional Chinese vocal technique often associated with expressing emotions or calling out.
    • 兜玄国 - A mythical place, possibly a reference to a spiritual or otherworldly realm.
    • 焦螟 - Tiny insects, often used in classical literature to symbolize something very small.

    Proper Nouns

    • 薛君曹 - The name of the main character, Xue Juncao.
    • 梓潼 - A place name, likely referring to a region in Sichuan Province.
    • 鹤鸣山 - A specific mountain in Sichuan Province.
    • 兜玄国 - A mythical place, possibly a reference to a spiritual or otherworldly realm.

    Then he went to consult a dream interpreter and said, "If you stay, everything will be fine, but is there a method to prolong life?" The dream interpreter replied, "You were previously the man named Xue Juncao from Zitong. You enjoyed taking the wood essence powder, often sought out unusual books, and daily recited a hundred sheets of Huang-Lao. You moved to live at the foot of Crane Chirping Mountain, where you had a thatched cottage with three rooms. In front of the house, flowers and bamboo were planted in rows, and the area was surrounded by springs and stones."

    On the 15th day of the 8th lunar month, Xue Juncao sang loudly and drank alone. As he became drunk and cheerful, he said aloud, "Xue Juncao is so reclusive and indifferent. Why hasn't any extraordinary person come to visit?" Suddenly, he heard the sound of carriages and horses in his ears. He then leaned back and thought of sleeping. As soon as he reached his mat, a small carriage appeared. The carriage had a red wheel and a green canopy, driven by red calves, each about two or three inches tall. He did not know how difficult it was for them to come out of his ear.

    There were two children in the carriage, wearing green headbands and blue cloaks, also about two or three inches tall. They leaned on the carriage's front rail, called the driver, stepped on the wheel, and helped Xue Juncao down. They said to him, "We have come from the Land of Douchuan. We heard you singing loudly under the moon, and the tone was very clear and piercing. We privately admire and respect you and wish to engage in a clear and profound discussion."

    Xue Juncao said, "How can there be a country inside my ear, and how can the people of that country all be tiny insects?" The children replied, "Why is that so? Our country is no different from yours. If you don't believe us, why not come with us on a journey? Perhaps we can make you stay, and then you would no longer suffer from the pain of birth and death." [74]

    ”一童 因倾耳示君曹，君曹觇之，乃别有天地，花卉繁茂，甍栋连接，清泉翠竹，萦绕 香甸。因扪耳投之，已至一都会，城池楼堞，穷极瑰丽。君曹彷徨，未知所之， 顾见向之二童已在侧，谓君曹曰：“此国大小与君国，既至此，盍从吾谒蒙玄真 伯。”蒙玄真伯居大殿，墙垣阶陛，尽饰以金碧，垂翡翠帘帷。中间独坐真伯， 身衣云霞日月衣，冠通天冠，垂旒皆与身等。玉童四人，立侍左右，一执白拂， 一执犀如意。二人既入，皆拱手拜伏，不敢仰视。有高冠长鬣绛纱衣人，宣青纸 制曰：“肇分大素，国既百亿，尔沦下土，贱卑万品，聿臻于此，实由冥合，况 尔清乃躬诚，叶于真宰，大官厚爵，俾宜享之。可为主录大夫。”君曹拜舞出门， 即有黄帔三四人，引至一曹署。其中文薄，多所不识，每月亦无请受，但意有所 念，左右必先知，当便供给。因暇登楼远望，忽有归思，赋诗曰： [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 一童因倾耳示君曹 - A child leans forward to show the gentlemen, implying attentiveness or curiosity.
    • 君曹觇之 - Observing with curiosity or suspicion.
    • 别有天地 - A different world or separate realm.
    • 花卉繁茂 - Flowers and plants are lush and flourishing.
    • 甍栋连接 - Well-constructed buildings with ridged roofs and main beams.
    • 清泉翠竹 - Clear spring and green bamboo, evoking a serene natural setting.
    • 萦绕香甸 - Winding around fragrant fields or meadows.
    • 扪耳投之 - Touching one's ear and throwing it, somewhat ambiguous and possibly metaphorical.
    • 未知所之 - Not knowing where to go or what to do.
    • 盍从吾谒蒙玄真伯 - Why not follow me to visit Mont Xuan Zhen Bo.
    • 蒙玄真伯居大殿 - Mont Xuan Zhen Bo resides in the grand hall.
    • 墙垣阶陛，尽饰以金碧 - Walls and steps are all decorated with gold and jade.
    • 垂翡翠帘帷 - Hanging jade-green curtains.
    • 中间独坐真伯 - Sitting alone in the middle, likely a title or name.
    • 身衣云霞日月衣 - Wearing a robe adorned with patterns of clouds,霞 (霞), sun, and moon.
    • 冠通天冠 - Wearing a specific type of ceremonial hat.
    • 垂旒皆与身等 - Pendants or tassels hanging down to the height of the body.
    • 玉童四人，立侍左右 - Four young attendants standing on both sides.
    • 一执白拂，一执犀如意 - One holding a white fly whisk, another holding an ivory wish-fulfilling object.
    • 皆拱手拜伏，不敢仰视 - Bowing with hands clasped, kneeling and bowing, daring not to look up.
    • 高冠长鬣绛纱衣人 - Person with a high hat, long hair, and a red silk robe.
    • 宣青纸制曰 - Proclaiming a decree or order written on blue paper.
    • 肇分大素，国既百亿 - Originally divided, the country is already a hundred billion.
    • 尔沦下土，贱卑万品 - Fallen to the lower realms, lowly and humble beings.
    • 聿臻于此，实由冥合 - Gradually reaching a certain state, due to a mysterious union.
    • 况尔清乃躬诚，叶于真宰 - How much more you, pure and sincere in your actions, in harmony with the true ruler.
    • 大官厚爵，俾宜享之 - High-ranking officials and rich titles, should enjoy them appropriately.
    • 可为主录大夫 - Can be appointed as Chief Recorder.
    • 拜舞出门 - Bowing and dancing, going out of the door.
    • 黄帔三四人，引至一曹署 - Three or four people with yellow cloaks, leading to a certain office.
    • 其中文薄，多所不识 - Documents in it, many of which are not recognized.
    • 每月亦无请受 - Monthly, also not receiving requests or receptions.
    • 但意有所念，左右必先知，当便供给 - If you think of something, the attendants will know beforehand, they will immediately provide it.
    • 因暇登楼远望，忽有归思 - Taking a break, climbing the tower to look far, suddenly feeling a desire to return home.
    • 赋诗曰 - Composing a poem, said.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 童子 - Refers to a child or young person, often used in a respectful or formal context.
    • 君曹 - Refers to the gentlemen or noblemen, indicating a formal or official setting.
    • 蒙玄真伯 - A proper name or title, possibly referring to a high-ranking official or sage.
    • 大殿 - Refers to a grand hall or palace, often associated with imperial or religious contexts.
    • 金碧 - Refers to gold and jade, symbols of wealth and nobility.
    • 翡翠帘帷 - Refers to jade-green curtains, often used in descriptions of luxurious settings.
    • 真伯 - A title or name, possibly referring to a high-ranking official or sage.
    • 云霞日月衣 - Describes a robe adorned with patterns of clouds,霞 (霞), sun, and moon, often associated with deities or high-ranking officials.
    • 通天冠 - A specific type of ceremonial hat, often worn by high-ranking officials or emperors.
    • 玉童 - Refers to young attendants or servants, often depicted in classical literature as beautiful and graceful.
    • 白拂 - A white fly whisk, often used as a symbol of authority or status.
    • 犀如意 - An ivory wish-fulfilling object, often used as a symbol of good fortune or power.
    • 高冠长鬣绛纱衣人 - Refers to a person with a high hat, long hair, and a red silk robe, often associated with high-ranking officials or deities.
    • 青纸制 - Refers to a decree or order written on blue paper, often used in formal or official contexts.
    • 大素 - Refers to the great primordial, often associated with the concept of the origin or beginning.
    • 国既百亿 - Refers to a vast number of countries or realms, often used to describe a grand or expansive domain.
    • 尔沦下土 - Refers to falling to the lower realms, often used to describe a decline in status or position.
    • 贱卑万品 - Refers to lowly and humble beings, often used to describe ordinary people or commoners.
    • 聿臻于此 - Refers to gradually reaching a certain state or condition, often used to describe a process of development or growth.
    • 冥合 - Refers to a mysterious union or connection, often used to describe a deep or spiritual bond.
    • 清乃躬诚 - Refers to being pure and sincere in one's actions, often used to describe a virtuous or noble character.
    • 叶于真宰 - Refers to being in harmony with the true ruler, often used to describe a relationship with a divine or supreme authority.
    • 大官厚爵，俾宜享之 - High-ranking officials and rich titles, should enjoy them appropriately.
    • 可为主录大夫 - Can be appointed as Chief Recorder.
    • 拜舞出门 - Bowing and dancing, going out of the door.
    • 黄帔三四人，引至一曹署 - Three or four people with yellow cloaks, leading to a certain office.
    • 其中文薄，多所不识 - Documents in it, many of which are not recognized.
    • 每月亦无请受 - Monthly, also not receiving requests or receptions.
    • 但意有所念，左右必先知，当便供给 - If you think of something, the attendants will know beforehand, they will immediately provide it.
    • 因暇登楼远望，忽有归思 - Taking a break, climbing the tower to look far, suddenly feeling a desire to return home.
    • 赋诗曰 - Composing a poem, said.

    A child leaned forward to show the gentlemen, who observed it and found themselves in a different world, lush with flowers and plants, with well-constructed buildings connected by clear springs and green bamboo, winding around fragrant fields. They touched their ears and were thrown into a grand city, with walls, towers, and palaces reaching the limits of beauty. The gentlemen wandered, unsure of where to go, and saw the two children they had met earlier standing by them. The children said to the gentlemen, "This country is as large as yours. Since you have arrived here, why not follow us to pay respects to Lord Meng Xuanzhen." Lord Meng Xuanzhen resided in a grand hall, with walls and steps all decorated with gold and jade, and hanging jade-green curtains. In the middle, Lord Meng Xuanzhen sat alone, wearing a robe adorned with patterns of clouds,霞 (霞), sun, and moon, and wearing a high ceremonial hat with tassels equal to the height of his body. Four jade boys stood on both sides, one holding a white fly whisk and one holding an ivory wish-fulfilling object. The two gentlemen entered, bowed and knelt, daring not to look up. A person with a high hat, long hair, and a red silk robe proclaimed on blue paper, "Originally divided from the great primordial, the country is already a hundred billion. You have fallen to the lower realms, lowly and humble beings, gradually reaching this place, truly due to a mysterious union. How much more so, then, for you who are pure and sincere in your actions, in harmony with the true ruler. High-ranking officials and rich titles should be enjoyed appropriately. You can be the Chief Recorder." The gentlemen bowed and danced out of the door, and immediately there were three or four people in yellow cloaks, leading them to a certain office. Among the documents, many were not recognized, and there were no monthly requests or receptions. But if they thought of something, the attendants would know beforehand and immediately provide it. Taking a break, they climbed the tower to look far and suddenly felt a desire to return home. They composed a poem, saying: [75]

    风软景和丽，录花馥林塘。
    　　
    　　登高一怅望，信美非吾乡。
    　　
    　　因以诗示二童子，童子怒曰：“吾以君质性冲寂，引至吾国，鄙俗余态果乃 未去，卿有何自忆耶！”遂疾逐君曹，如陷落地，仰视乃自童子耳中落，已在旧 居处，随视童子亦不见，因问诸邻人，邻人云：“失君曹已七八年矣。”君曹在 彼如数月。未几而君曹卒，遂生于申家，即今身也。 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 风软景和丽 - Poetic expression meaning 'soft wind, harmonious and beautiful scenery,' describing a pleasant environment.
    • 录花馥林塘 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the fragrance of flowers fills the forest and pond,' evoking a sensory experience of nature.
    • 登高一怅望 - Compound phrase meaning 'to climb high and gaze longingly,' conveying a sense of longing or reflection.
    • 信美非吾乡 - Phrase meaning 'truly beautiful, but not my hometown,' expressing a feeling of displacement or nostalgia.
    • 质性冲寂 - Compound meaning 'natural disposition is calm and secluded,' describing a person's inherent temperament.
    • 鄙俗余态果乃未去 - Phrase meaning 'the remnants of vulgar and ordinary manners have not yet disappeared,' indicating a criticism of superficial behavior.
    • 卿有何自忆耶 - Question meaning 'what do you have to remember?' or 'what are you thinking about?' directed at someone.
    • 如陷落地 - Metaphor meaning 'as if falling into the earth,' describing a sudden and dramatic change in state.
    • 仰视乃自童子耳中落 - Phrase meaning 'looking up, one sees falling from the ear of the child,' describing a surreal or magical event.
    • 随视童子亦不见 - Phrase meaning 'following the gaze, the child also disappears,' indicating a mysterious disappearance.
    • 失君曹已七八年矣 - Statement meaning 'you have been missing for seven or eight years,' indicating a long absence.
    • 未几而君曹卒 - Phrase meaning 'not long after, you died,' indicating a sudden death.
    • 遂生于申家 - Phrase meaning 'then you were born into the Shen family,' indicating a rebirth into a new life.
    • 即今身也 - Phrase meaning 'that is your current body,' indicating the present state of existence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 风软景和丽 - Classical description of a pleasant natural environment, often used in poetry to evoke a sense of harmony and beauty.
    • 录花馥林塘 - Classical description of a natural setting, often used in poetry to evoke a sense of sensory richness.
    • 登高一怅望 - Classical image of a person climbing high and gazing longingly, often used to express feelings of nostalgia or longing.
    • 信美非吾乡 - Classical expression of displacement or nostalgia, often used in poetry to express a sense of not belonging.
    • 质性冲寂 - Classical description of a person's natural disposition, often used to describe a calm and secluded temperament.
    • 鄙俗余态果乃未去 - Classical criticism of superficial behavior, often used to describe a person who has not fully shed their ordinary manners.
    • 卿有何自忆耶 - Classical question often used in dialogue to express curiosity or concern.
    • 如陷落地 - Classical metaphor often used to describe a sudden and dramatic change in state.
    • 仰视乃自童子耳中落 - Classical description of a surreal or magical event, often used in stories to evoke a sense of wonder.
    • 随视童子亦不见 - Classical description of a mysterious disappearance, often used in stories to evoke a sense of mystery.
    • 失君曹已七八年矣 - Classical statement indicating a long absence, often used in stories to express a sense of time passing.
    • 未几而君曹卒 - Classical phrase indicating a sudden death, often used in stories to express a sense of tragedy.
    • 遂生于申家 - Classical phrase indicating a rebirth into a new life, often used in stories to express a sense of transformation.
    • 即今身也 - Classical phrase indicating the present state of existence, often used in stories to express a sense of continuity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 申家 - The surname or family name 'Shen,' indicating the family into which the person was reborn.

    Soft winds and harmonious, beautiful scenery; the fragrance of flowers fills the forest and pond.
    Climbing high and gazing longingly, truly beautiful, but not my hometown.
    I recited a poem to two children, and they angrily said, "We thought you had a natural disposition that was calm and secluded, but you have not yet shed your vulgar and ordinary manners. What are you thinking about?" They then swiftly chased you, as if you were falling into the earth. Looking up, you saw yourself falling from the ear of the child, already in your old residence. Following their gaze, the children also disappeared. I then asked the neighbors, and they said, "You have been missing for seven or eight years." You were there for a few months. Not long after, you died, and then you were born into the Shen family, which is your current body. [76]

    占者又云：“吾前生乃出耳中童子。以汝前生好道，以得到兜玄国，然俗想 未尽，不可长生。然汝由此寿千岁矣。吾授汝符，即归。”“因吐朱绢尺余，令 吞之。占者遂复童子形而灭。自是不复有疾，周行天下名山，迨兹向二百余岁。 然吾所见异事甚多，并记鹿革中。”
    　　
    　　因启囊，出二轴书甚大，字颇细。左不能读，请叟自宣，略述十余事，其半 昭然可纪。此卷八事，无非叟之所说。其夕将明，佐略寝，及觉已失叟。后数日， 有人于炭谷湫见之，叟曰：“为我致意于张君。”左遽寻之，已复不见。时贞元 中。
    　　
    　　○萧至忠
    　　
    　　唐中书令萧至忠，景云元年为晋州刺史，将以腊日畋游，大事置罗。 [77]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [77]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 占者又云 - Continuation of the soothsayer's statements.
    • 出耳中童子 - Metaphorical or symbolic expression, possibly referring to a supernatural or divine entity.
    • 好道 - Pursuit of spiritual or philosophical truth.
    • 兜玄国 - Mythical or spiritual realm.
    • 俗想未尽 - Incomplete spiritual enlightenment.
    • 寿千岁 - Very long lifespan.
    • 授汝符 - Giving of a talisman.
    • 令吞之 - Instructed to swallow the talisman or similar object.
    • 遂复童子形而灭 - Transformation and disappearance.
    • 周行天下名山 - Extensive travel and exploration around famous mountains.
    • 迨兹向二百余岁 - Long period of time, nearly two hundred years.
    • 所见异事甚多 - Numerous unusual experiences.
    • 记鹿革中 - Writing or recording on a deer skin.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贞元中 - Specific period in Chinese history (785-805 AD).
    • 萧至忠 - Historical figure who served as a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 晋州刺史 - High-ranking administrative position.
    • 腊日畋游 - Hunting on the day of the winter solstice, a traditional Chinese festival.
    • 置罗 - Setting traps, a method of hunting.

    Proper Nouns

    • 占者 - The soothsayer, a title or role.
    • 萧至忠 - Historical figure.
    • 晋州 - Place name.
    • 腊日 - The day of the winter solstice, a traditional Chinese festival.
    • 贞元 - The Zhenyuan era, a specific period in Chinese history.

    The soothsayer further said, "In my previous life, I was a child emerging from your ear. Because you loved the Dao in your past life, you were able to reach the Douxuan State, but you have not fully transcended worldly thoughts, so you cannot achieve immortality. However, you will live a thousand years as a result. I have given you a talisman, and you should return now." "He then spat out a red silk cloth about a foot long and instructed you to swallow it. The soothsayer then resumed his child form and disappeared. From then on, you no longer suffered from illness, and you traveled around the famous mountains of the world. Until now, nearly two hundred years have passed. However, I have seen many strange things and have recorded them in a deer hide."

    He then opened his bag and took out two large scrolls, the characters of which were quite fine. The left scroll could not be read, so he asked the old man to explain it. He briefly recounted about ten events, half of which were clearly recorded. This scroll contains eight events, none of which are different from what the old man had said. As the night was about to break, the assistant went to sleep, and upon waking up, the old man was gone. Several days later, someone saw him at the Carbon Valley Spring, and the old man said, "Please convey my regards to Zhang Jun." The left person hurriedly searched for him, but he was gone again. At that time, it was during the Zhenyuan era.

    ○Xiao Zhizhong

    During the reign of the Tang Dynasty, Xiao Zhizhong, the Grand Secretary, was the Governor of Jizhou in the first year of Jingyun. He planned to hunt on the day of the winter solstice and set up traps for a grand occasion. [77]

    先一日，有薪者樵于霍山，暴疟不能归，因止岩穴之中，呻吟不寐。夜将艾， 似闻悉窣有人声。初以为盗贼将至，则匍匐于林木中。时山月甚明，有一人身长 丈余，鼻有三角，体被豹鞟，目闪闪如电，向谷长啸。俄有虎、兕、鹿、豕、 狐、兔、雉、雁骈匝百许步。长人即宣言曰：“余玄冥使者，奉北帝之命，明日 腊日，萧使君当顺时畋腊。尔等若干合箭死，若干合枪死，若干合网死，若干合 棒死，若干合狗死，若干合鹰死。”言讫，群兽皆俯伏战惧，若请命者。老虎洎 老麋，皆屈膝向长人言曰：“以某等之命，即实以分。然萧公仁者，非意欲害物， 以行时令耳。若有少故则止。使者岂无术救某等乎？”使者曰：“非余欲杀汝辈， 但今自以帝命宣示汝等刑名，即余使乎之事毕矣，自此任尔自为计。然余闻东谷 严四兄善谋，尔等可就彼祈求。”群兽皆轮转欢叫。使者即东行，群兽毕从。 [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 先一日 - Means 'the day before' or 'one day prior.'
    • 樵于霍山 - Means 'chopping wood in Huoshan,' where '樵' is an archaic term for chopping wood.
    • 暴疟不能归 - Means 'suffering from malaria and unable to return home,' where '暴疟' refers to a sudden onset of malaria.
    • 因止岩穴之中 - Means 'so he stayed in a cave,' where '因止' means 'therefore stayed.'
    • 呻吟不寐 - Means 'moaning and unable to sleep,' where '呻吟' describes moaning or groaning.
    • 夜将艾 - Means 'as night was ending,' where '艾' is an archaic term for the end of something.
    • 似闻悉窣有人声 - Means 'he seemed to hear a rustling sound of human voices,' where '悉窣' describes a rustling or whispering sound.
    • 匍匐于林木中 - Means 'crawling in the woods,' where '匍匐' means to crawl or creep.
    • 时山月甚明 - Means 'the mountain moon was very bright,' where '时' indicates 'at that time.'
    • 有一人身长丈余 - Means 'there was a man over ten feet tall,' where '丈余' is an approximate measurement.
    • 鼻有三角 - Means 'with three horns on his nose,' where '三角' literally means 'three angles' or 'three horns.'
    • 体被豹鞟 - Means 'clothed in a leopard skin,' where '鞟' refers to the skin of an animal.
    • 目闪闪如电 - Means 'his eyes flashed like lightning,' where '闪闪' describes a flashing or sparkling appearance.
    • 向谷长啸 - Means 'he roared towards the valley,' where '长啸' means a loud, prolonged cry.
    • 俄有虎、兕、鹿、豕、狐、兔、雉、雁骈匝百许步 - Means 'soon there were tigers, rhinos, deer, pigs, foxes, rabbits, pheasants, and geese gathered around for about a hundred steps,' where '骈匝' means 'gathered around' and '百许步' is an approximate measurement.
    • 玄冥使者 - Means 'messenger of Xuanming,' where '玄冥' is a deity associated with water and winter.
    • 奉北帝之命 - Means 'carrying out the order of the Northern Emperor,' where '北帝' refers to a deity associated with the north and winter.
    • 明日腊日 - Means 'tomorrow is the day of the winter solstice,' where '腊日' refers to the winter solstice festival.
    • 萧使君当顺时畋腊 - Means 'Xiao the magistrate will follow the season to hunt and sacrifice,' where '畋' means 'hunting' and '腊' refers to the winter solstice festival.
    • 尔等若干合箭死，若干合枪死，若干合网死，若干合棒死，若干合狗死，若干合鹰死 - Means 'you will die by arrows, spears, nets, clubs, dogs, and eagles,' where '若干' means 'some' or 'a number of.'
    • 群兽皆俯伏战惧，若请命者 - Means 'the animals all cowered in fear, as if pleading for mercy,' where '俯伏' means 'to cower' and '战惧' means 'fearfully trembling.'
    • 老虎洎老麋 - Means 'the tiger and the old elk,' where '洎' means 'and' or 'including.'
    • 屈膝向长人言曰 - Means 'bowed their knees and spoke to the tall man,' where '屈膝' means 'to bow the knees.'
    • 以某等之命，即实以分 - Means 'our lives are at your disposal,' where '即实以分' means 'to give up completely.'
    • 然萧公仁者，非意欲害物，以行时令耳 - Means 'however, Xiao the magistrate is kind-hearted and does not intend to harm creatures; he is simply following the seasonal command,' where '仁者' means 'kind-hearted' and '时令' means 'seasonal command.'
    • 若有少故则止 - Means 'if there is a slight reason, then it will stop,' where '少故' means 'a slight reason' or 'a minor issue.'
    • 使者岂无术救某等乎 - Means 'does the messenger have no way to save us?' where '岂无术' means 'does not have a method.'
    • 非余欲杀汝辈，但今自以帝命宣示汝等刑名，即余使乎之事毕矣 - Means 'it is not my intention to kill you; I am merely fulfilling the divine command by announcing your fates,' where '刑名' means 'fates' or 'destinies.'
    • 自此任尔自为计 - Means 'from now on, you must fend for yourselves,' where '自为计' means 'to take care of yourselves.'
    • 然余闻东谷严四兄善谋，尔等可就彼祈求 - Means 'however, I have heard that Elder Yan in Eastern Valley is good at strategy; you may seek his help,' where '善谋' means 'good at strategy' and '祈求' means 'to seek help.'
    • 群兽皆轮转欢叫 - Means 'the animals all rejoiced and called out,' where '轮转' means 'to rejoice' and '欢叫' means 'to call out joyfully.'
    • 使者即东行，群兽毕从 - Means 'the messenger then headed east, and the animals followed him,' where '毕从' means 'all followed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 霍山 - Refers to Huoshan, a mountain in China.
    • 玄冥使者 - Refers to a messenger of Xuanming, a deity associated with water and winter.
    • 北帝 - Refers to the Northern Emperor, a deity associated with the north and winter.
    • 腊日 - Refers to the winter solstice festival, a traditional Chinese holiday.
    • 萧使君 - Refers to Xiao the magistrate, a local official.
    • 老虎、兕、鹿、豕、狐、兔、雉、雁 - These are various animals mentioned in the text, including tigers, rhinos, deer, pigs, foxes, rabbits, pheasants, and geese.
    • 严四兄 - Refers to Elder Yan, a wise and strategic person in Eastern Valley.

    Proper Nouns

    • 霍山 - Refers to Huoshan, a mountain in China.
    • 玄冥使者 - Refers to a messenger of Xuanming, a deity associated with water and winter.
    • 北帝 - Refers to the Northern Emperor, a deity associated with the north and winter.
    • 腊日 - Refers to the winter solstice festival, a traditional Chinese holiday.
    • 萧使君 - Refers to Xiao the magistrate, a local official.
    • 严四兄 - Refers to Elder Yan, a wise and strategic person in Eastern Valley.

    The day before, a woodcutter was chopping wood in Huoshan when he contracted malaria and could not return home. He therefore took refuge in a cave, moaning and unable to sleep. As night was ending, he seemed to hear a rustling sound of human voices. At first, he thought bandits were approaching, so he crawled into the woods. The mountain moon was very bright, and there was a man over ten feet tall with three horns on his nose, clothed in a leopard skin, whose eyes flashed like lightning. He roared towards the valley. Soon, there were tigers, rhinos, deer, pigs, foxes, rabbits, pheasants, and geese gathered around for about a hundred steps. The tall man then declared, "I am the messenger of Xuanming, carrying out the order of the Northern Emperor. Tomorrow is the day of the winter solstice, and Xiao the magistrate will follow the season to hunt and sacrifice. You will die by arrows, spears, nets, clubs, dogs, and eagles." After speaking, the animals all cowered in fear, as if pleading for mercy. The tiger and the old elk both bowed their knees and spoke to the tall man, saying, "Our lives are at your disposal. However, Xiao the magistrate is kind-hearted and does not intend to harm creatures; he is simply following the seasonal command. If there is a slight reason, then it will stop. Does the messenger have no way to save us?" The messenger replied, "It is not my intention to kill you; I am merely fulfilling the divine command by announcing your fates. From now on, you must fend for yourselves. However, I have heard that Elder Yan in Eastern Valley is good at strategy; you may seek his help." The animals all rejoiced and called out. The messenger then headed east, and the animals followed him. [78]

    时薪者疾亦少间，随往觇之。即至东谷，有茅堂数间，黄冠一人，架悬虎皮， 身正熟寝。惊起，见使者曰：“阔别既久，每多思望。今日至此，得非配群生猎 日刑名乎？”使者曰：“正如高明所问。然彼皆求救于四兄，四兄当为谋之。” 老虎、老麋即屈膝哀请，黄冠曰：“萧使君每役人，必恤其饥寒。若祈滕六降雪， 巽二起风，即不复游猎矣。余昨得滕六书，知已丧偶。又闻索泉家第五娘子为歌 姬，以妒忌黜矣。若汝求得美人纳之，则雪立降矣。又巽二好饮，汝若求得醇醪 赂之，则风立至矣。”有二狐自称多媚，能取之。“河东县尉崔知之第三妹，美 淑娇艳。绛州卢司户善酿醪，妻产，必有美酒。”言讫而去。诸兽皆有欢声。黄 冠乃谓使者曰：“忆含质在仙都，岂意千年为兽身，悒悒不得志。聊有《述怀》 一章。”乃吟曰： [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 時薪者疾亦少間 - 時薪者 refers to a laborer or worker who earns by the hour. 疾亦少間 means the illness has somewhat improved, indicating a partial recovery.
    • 隨往觇之 - 隨往 means to go along or follow, and 视之 means to observe or inspect. Together, it suggests a visit to check on someone.
    • 有茅堂数间 - 茅堂数间 describes a few thatched huts or simple dwellings.
    • 黄冠一人，架悬虎皮 - 黄冠 refers to a Taoist priest or hermit, and 架悬虎皮 means hanging a tiger skin on a frame, symbolizing strength or authority.
    • 身正熟寝 - 身正熟寝 means soundly sleeping or deeply asleep.
    • 驚起，見使者曰 - 驚起 means startled awake, and 見使者曰 means saw the messenger and said.
    • 闊别既久，每多思望 - 闊别既久 means long separated, and 每多思望 means often thinking and hoping, indicating a sense of longing.
    • 今日至此，得非配群生獵日刑名乎 - 得非 is a rhetorical question meaning is it not? 配群生獵日刑名 is unclear but likely refers to some form of judgment or punishment involving animals or daily life.
    • 然彼皆求救於四兄，四兄當為謀之 - 然彼皆求救於四兄 means but they all seek help from the fourth elder brother, and 四兄當為謀之 means the fourth elder brother should plan for them.
    • 老虎、老麋即屈膝哀請 - 老虎、老麋 refers to a tiger and an old deer, and 屈膝哀請 means to kneel and plead, indicating a plea for mercy.
    • 蕭使君每役人，必恤其饥寒 - 蕭使君 is likely a proper name, 每役人 means every time he uses people, and 必恤其饥寒 means he always cares about their hunger and cold, indicating compassion.
    • 若祈滕六降雪，巽二起风 - 滕六 and 巽二 are likely mythological figures or deities associated with snow and wind. 祈 means to pray for, and 降雪 and 起风 mean snowfall and wind, respectively.
    • 即不复游猎矣 - 即不复游猎矣 means then he will no longer go hunting, indicating a cessation of hunting.
    • 余昨得滕六书，知已丧偶 - 余昨得滕六书 means I received a letter from滕六 yesterday, and 知已丧偶 means knowing that he has lost his wife, indicating a personal loss.
    • 又闻索泉家第五娘子为歌姬，以妒忌黜矣 - 又闻 means also heard, 索泉家第五娘子 refers to the fifth daughter of the Suoquan family, 为歌姬 means to be a singer, and 以妒忌黜矣 means was dismissed due to jealousy, indicating a social or personal issue.
    • 若汝求得美人纳之，则雪立降矣 - 若汝求得美人纳之 means if you can find a beautiful woman to marry him, and 则雪立降矣 means then snow will immediately fall, indicating a condition for snowfall.
    • 又巽二好饮，汝若求得醇醪赂之，则风立至矣 - 又巽二好饮 means and巽二 loves to drink, 汝若求得醇醪赂之 means if you can find strong wine to bribe him, and 则风立至矣 means then the wind will immediately come, indicating a condition for wind.
    • 有二狐自称多媚，能取之 - 有二狐自称多媚 means there are two foxes who claim to be very charming, and 能取之 means can get them, indicating a potential solution.
    • 河东县尉崔知之第三妹，美淑娇艳 - 河东县尉崔知之第三妹 refers to the third sister of Cui Zhi, the county magistrate of Hedong, and 美淑娇艳 means beautiful and graceful, indicating a suitable candidate.
    • 绛州卢司户善酿醪，妻产，必有美酒 - 绛州卢司户善酿醪 means Lu the household officer of Jiangzhou is good at brewing strong wine, and 妻产，必有美酒 means when his wife gives birth, there will certainly be fine wine, indicating a source of wine.
    • 言讫而去 - 言讫 means after speaking, and 而去 means and left, indicating the end of the conversation.
    • 诸兽皆有欢声 - 诸兽 means all the animals, and 皆有欢声 means all have joyful sounds, indicating a positive reaction.
    • 黄冠乃谓使者曰 - 黄冠乃谓使者曰 means the Taoist priest then said to the messenger, indicating a direct address.
    • 忆含质在仙都，岂意千年为兽身，悒悒不得志 - 忆含质在仙都 means remembering when I was in the celestial realm, 岂意千年为兽身 means how could I have thought I would be a beast for a thousand years, and 悒悒不得志 means depressed and unfulfilled, indicating a sense of regret.
    • 聊有《述怀》一章 - 聊有 means just have, and 《述怀》一章 means a chapter called 'Expressing Aspirations,' indicating a poem.
    • 乃吟曰 - 乃吟曰 means then recited, indicating the start of a poem.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 时薪者 - Refers to a laborer or worker who earns by the hour, a common occupational role in ancient China.
    • 黄冠 - Refers to a Taoist priest or hermit, a figure often associated with wisdom and spiritual guidance.
    • 滕六 - Likely a mythological figure or deity associated with snow, possibly a reference to the Snow King.
    • 巽二 - Likely a mythological figure or deity associated with wind, possibly a reference to the Wind God.
    • 萧使君 - A proper name, likely a historical or fictional figure known for his compassion and governance.
    • 索泉家第五娘子 - Refers to the fifth daughter of the Suoquan family, a specific individual with a social role as a singer.
    • 河东县尉崔知之 - Refers to Cui Zhi, the county magistrate of Hedong, a specific historical or fictional figure.
    • 绛州卢司户 - Refers to Lu the household officer of Jiangzhou, a specific historical or fictional figure known for brewing wine.
    • 仙都 - Refers to the celestial realm, a common concept in Chinese mythology and Taoism.

    The Hourly Wage Earner's Illness Had Slightly Improved, So He Followed Along to Check on Him. Upon Arriving at the Eastern Valley, There Were Several Thatched Huts, And Inside One, A Taoist Priest Was Soundly Sleeping With a Tiger Skin Hung on a Frame. Startled Awake, He Saw the Messenger and Said, "Long Separated, I Often Think and Hope. Today You Have Come Here, Is It Not to Determine the Fate of the Beasts Through Hunting and Punishment?" The Messenger Replied, "Just As Your Eminence Has Guessed. But They All Seek Help from the Fourth Elder Brother, And the Fourth Elder Brother Should Plan for Them." The Tiger and the Old Deer Then Kneeled and Pleaded, And the Taoist Priest Said, "The County Magistrate Xiao Always Uses People, But He Always Cares About Their Hunger and Cold. If You Pray to Tengliu for Snowfall and Xun'er for Wind, He Will No Longer Go Hunting. I Received a Letter from Tengliu Yesterday, Knowing That He Has Lost His Wife. Also Heard That the Fifth Daughter of the Suoquan Family, A Singer, Was Dismissed Due to Jealousy. If You Can Find a Beautiful Woman to Marry Him, Then Snow Will Immediately Fall. And Xun'er Loves to Drink, If You Can Find Strong Wine to Bribe Him, Then the Wind Will Immediately Come." Two Foxes Claimed to Be Very Charming and Could Get Them. "The Third Sister of Cui Zhi, the County Magistrate of Hedong, Is Beautiful and Graceful. Lu the Household Officer of Jiangzhou Is Good at Brewing Strong Wine, And When His Wife Gives Birth, There Will Certainly Be Fine Wine." After Speaking, He Left. All the Animals Had Joyful Sounds. The Taoist Priest Then Said to the Messenger, "Remembering When I Was in the Celestial Realm, How Could I Have Thought I Would Be a Beast for a Thousand Years, Depressed and Unfulfilled. Just Have a Chapter Called 'Expressing Aspirations.'" He Then Recited: [79]

    昔为仙子今为虎，流落阴涯足风雨。
    　　
    　　更将斑毳被余身，千载空山万般苦。
    　　
    　　“然含质谴谪已满，唯有十一日即归紫府矣。久居于此，将别不无恨恨。因 题数行于壁，使后人知仆曾居于此矣。”乃书北壁曰：“下玄八千亿甲子，丹飞 先生严含质，谪下中天被斑革，六十甲子血食涧饮，厕猿■，下浊界，景云元纪 升太一。” [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昔为仙子今为虎 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'once a fairy, now a tiger,' symbolizing a dramatic transformation from a divine or noble state to a wild and untamed one.
    • 流落阴涯足风雨 - Literal translation is 'wandering on the edge of darkness, feet soaked in wind and rain,' used to depict a life of hardship and exposure to the elements.
    • 更将斑毳被余身 - Literal translation is 'further, the speckled fur is draped over my body,' a poetic description of being covered in tiger fur.
    • 千载空山万般苦 - Literal translation is 'a thousand years in the empty mountains, myriad sufferings,' expressing a long period of solitude and suffering.
    • 然含质谴谪已满 - Literal translation is 'yet, the punishment of exile has been completed,' indicating that the time of punishment has ended.
    • 唯有十一日即归紫府矣 - Literal translation is 'only eleven days remain until I return to the Purple Palace,' referring to a divine or celestial abode.
    • 久居于此，将别不无恨恨 - Literal translation is 'having stayed here for a long time, parting is not without sorrow,' expressing a sense of melancholy at leaving.
    • 因题数行于壁 - Literal translation is 'therefore, I inscribe several lines on the wall,' indicating the act of writing a message.
    • 使后人知仆曾居于此矣 - Literal translation is 'so that future generations may know that I once lived here,' expressing a desire to leave a legacy.
    • 下玄八千亿甲子 - Literal translation is 'below the dark, eight hundred billion cycles of the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches,' referring to an extremely long period of time.
    • 丹飞先生严含质 - Literal translation is 'Master Dan Fei, Yan Hanzi,' where '严含质' is likely a name or title.
    • 谪下中天被斑革 - Literal translation is 'exiled to the middle heaven, covered in speckled fur,' describing the state of being exiled and transformed into a tiger.
    • 六十甲子血食涧饮 - Literal translation is 'sixty cycles of the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches, eating blood and drinking from streams,' indicating a long period of survival in a wild state.
    • 厕猿■ - Literal translation is 'among apes,' where '■' is an uncertain character, possibly referring to a type of ape or a specific context.
    • 下浊界 - Literal translation is 'descending to the impure world,' referring to the earthly realm.
    • 景云元纪升太一 - Literal translation is 'in the first year of Jingyun, ascending to Taiyi,' where 'Jingyun' is likely a year name, and 'Taiyi' refers to the supreme deity or a celestial realm.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 仙子 - Refers to a fairy or celestial being, often associated with immortality and divine grace.
    • 虎 - Symbolizes strength, ferocity, and wildness, often used in Chinese mythology and literature.
    • 阴涯 - Refers to the edge of darkness or the underworld, a place of hardship and suffering.
    • 紫府 - Refers to the Purple Palace, a celestial abode often associated with the immortals or deities.
    • 甲子 - Refers to the Chinese sexagenary cycle, used to mark time in Chinese culture.
    • 丹飞 - Likely a name or title, possibly referring to a specific figure in Chinese mythology or literature.
    • 严含质 - Likely a name or title, possibly referring to a specific figure in Chinese mythology or literature.
    • 景云 - Likely a year name, possibly referring to a specific historical period.
    • 太一 - Refers to the supreme deity or a celestial realm, often associated with the ultimate source of all things.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紫府 - The Purple Palace, a celestial abode.
    • 丹飞 - Likely a name or title, possibly referring to a specific figure in Chinese mythology or literature.
    • 严含质 - Likely a name or title, possibly referring to a specific figure in Chinese mythology or literature.
    • 景云 - Likely a year name, possibly referring to a specific historical period.
    • 太一 - The supreme deity or a celestial realm.

    Once a fairy, now a tiger, wandering on the edge of darkness, feet soaked in wind and rain.
    Further, the speckled fur is draped over my body, a thousand years in the empty mountains, myriad sufferings.
    "Yet, the punishment of exile has been completed, only eleven days remain until I return to the Purple Palace. Having stayed here for a long time, parting is not without sorrow. Therefore, I inscribe several lines on the wall, so that future generations may know that I once lived here." He wrote on the north wall: "Below the dark, eight hundred billion cycles of the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches, Master Dan Fei, Yan Hanzi, exiled to the middle heaven, covered in speckled fur, sixty cycles of the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches, eating blood and drinking from streams, among apes, descending to the impure world, in the first year of Jingyun, ascending to Taiyi." [80]

    时薪者素晓书诵，因密记得之。少顷，老狐负美女至，才及笄岁，红袂拭目， 残妆妖媚。又有一狐负美酒二瓶，香气酷烈。严四兄即以美女洎美酒瓶，各纳一 囊中，以朱书二符，取水噀之，二符即飞去。
    　　
    　　薪者惧且为所见，即寻路却回。未明，风雪暴至，竟日乃罢，而萧使君不复 猎矣。
    　　
    　　○李汭言
    　　
    　　汉中从事李汭言：
    　　
    　　天宝中有士人崔姓者，尉于巴蜀，才至成都而卒。时连帅章仇兼琼哀其妻少 而无投止，因与青城山下置一别墅。又以其色美，欲聘纳之，计无所出，谓其夫 人曰：“贵为诸侯妻，何不盛为盘筵，邀召女客，五百里内，尽可迎致。”夫人 甚悦。兼琼因命衙官遍报五百里内女郎，即日会成都，意欲因会便留亡尉妻，不 谓已为族舅卢生纳之矣。卢舅密知兼琼意，令尉妻辞疾不行，兼琼大怒，促左右 百骑往收捕。 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 時薪者素曉書誦 - Refers to a person who earns by the hour and has always been skilled in reciting books, indicating literacy or familiarity with texts.
    • 因密記 - Secretly memorized, suggesting the person retained information without being noticed.
    • 才及笄歲 - Refers to the age of a girl who has reached the traditional age of marriage (around 15), indicating the woman's youth.
    • 紅袂拭目 - Refers to the red sleeves of a woman's robe and wiping her eyes, likely describing a gesture of emotion or attention.
    • 殘妝妖媚 - Refers to unfinished makeup and a charming or seductive appearance.
    • 負美女至 - To carry a beautiful woman.
    • 負美酒二瓶 - To carry two bottles of fine wine.
    • 以朱書二符 - Red writing on two talismans or charms.
    • 取水噀之 - To sprinkle water on it, a ritualistic act often used in exorcism or purification.
    • 薪者惧且為所見 - Fearing that he would be seen, indicating the person's anxiety about being discovered.
    • 風雪暴至 - Describes a sudden and violent storm.
    • 蕭使君不復獵矣 - No longer hunts, indicating that the magistrate has stopped hunting.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天寶 - The Tianbao era (742-756) of the Tang dynasty, a period of cultural and artistic flourishing.
    • 巴蜀 - The historical region of Sichuan, known for its fertile land and cultural significance.
    • 青城山 - A famous mountain in Sichuan, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.
    • 章仇兼琼 - A historical figure who served as the governor of Sichuan during the Tang dynasty.
    • 卢生 - A historical figure, likely a cousin or relative of the deceased officer.
    • 聘纳 - A traditional practice of marriage, often involving formal arrangements and ceremonies.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李汭言 - The name of the speaker or author.
    • 天寶 - The Tianbao era (742-756) of the Tang dynasty.
    • 巴蜀 - The historical region of Sichuan.
    • 青城山 - Qingcheng Mountain in Sichuan.
    • 章仇兼琼 - A historical figure who served as the governor of Sichuan.
    • 卢生 - A historical figure, likely a cousin or relative of the deceased officer.

    A Person Who Earns by the Hour

    A person who earns by the hour, having always been skilled in reciting books, secretly memorized something. Shortly afterward, an old fox carried a beautiful woman, who was just coming of age, her red sleeves wiping her eyes, her makeup unfinished and charmingly seductive. Another fox carried two bottles of fine wine, their aroma strong and pungent. The person named Yan Si, then placed the woman and the wine bottles into separate bags, wrote two red talismans, sprinkled water on them, and the talismans flew away.

    Fearing that he would be seen, the person quickly found a way to return. Before dawn, a violent storm arrived, lasting all day, and as a result, the magistrate no longer went hunting.

    ○ Li Ruiyan

    Li Ruiyan, an official from Hanzhong, said:

    During the Tianbao era, there was a scholar surnamed Cui who served as a military officer in Bashu. He died shortly after arriving in Chengdu. At the time, the governor, Zhang Qiu Jinqiong, felt pity for his young widow, who had no place to go, and so provided her with a summer house in Qingcheng Mountain. Additionally, because of her beauty, he considered marrying her but had no plan, so he told his wife, "As a wife of a feudal lord, why not host a grand banquet and invite female guests from within a 500-li radius? All of them can be invited." The wife was very pleased. The governor then ordered his officials to invite all the women within 500 li to gather in Chengdu the same day, intending to keep the widow during the gathering, but he did not know that she had already been married to his cousin-in-law, Lu Sheng. Lu Sheng, knowing the governor's intentions, had the widow feign illness and not attend, which greatly angered the governor, who then ordered his attendants to capture her. [81]

    卢舅时方食，兵骑绕宅亦合，卢谈笑自若，殊不介怀，食讫，谓尉 妻曰：“兼琼之意可知矣，夫人不可不行。少顷即当送素色衣服来，便可服之而 往。”言讫，乘驴出门，兵骑前揽不得，徐徐而去，追不及矣。俄使一小童捧箱， 内有故青裙、白衫子，绿帔子、绯罗縠绡素，皆非世人之所有。尉妻服之至成都， 诸女郎皆先期而至，兼琼觇于帷下。及尉妻入，光彩绕身，美色旁射，不可正视， 坐皆慑气，不觉起拜。食归，三日而卒，红坏立尽。 [82]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [82]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 時方 - Indicates an action was ongoing at the time.
    • 亦合 - Suggests that the soldiers not only surrounded the house but also assembled or gathered.
    • 殊不介怀 - Means 'not at all worried' or 'completely unconcerned.'
    • 讫 - Indicates the completion of an action.
    • 谓...曰 - A classical way of introducing direct speech.
    • 可知矣 - Means 'can be understood' or 'are clear.'
    • 不可不行 - Means 'cannot help but go' or 'must go.'
    • 少顷 - Means 'in a short while' or 'soon.'
    • 便可 - Means 'may' or 'can.'
    • 徐徐 - Means 'slowly' or 'gradually.'
    • 俄 - Means 'soon' or 'shortly.'
    • 光彩绕身 - Means 'radiant light surrounded her.'
    • 不可正视 - Means 'too dazzling to look directly at.'
    • 慑气 - Means 'awestruck' or 'frightened.'
    • 不觉起拜 - Means 'involuntarily stood up to bow.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 卢舅 - Refers to Lu's uncle, likely a figure in the story.
    • 兼琼 - Refers to Jianqiong, likely a person of high status or authority.
    • 尉妻 - Refers to the wife of the magistrate, a local official.
    • 成都 - Refers to Chengdu, a major city in Sichuan province.
    • 诸女郎 - Refers to the young ladies present at the event.
    • 帷下 - Refers to behind the curtain, a common setting in classical Chinese literature for eavesdropping or spying.

    Proper Nouns

    • 卢舅 - Lu's uncle.
    • 兼琼 - Jianqiong.
    • 尉妻 - The wife of the magistrate.
    • 成都 - Chengdu.
    • 诸女郎 - The young ladies.
    • 帷下 - Behind the curtain.

    Lu's uncle was just finishing his meal when soldiers and cavalry surrounded the house. Lu remained calm and composed, laughing and talking as usual, and was not at all concerned. After finishing his meal, he told the wife of the magistrate, "The intentions of Jianqiong are known. The lady cannot avoid going. In a short while, they will send white-colored clothes. You may wear them and go." After finishing his words, he rode a donkey out. The soldiers and cavalry tried to grab him but could not. He left slowly, and they could not catch up. Soon, he sent a young boy to carry a box, inside which were old blue skirts, white shirts, green cloaks, and red silk gauze. All of these were not things that ordinary people possessed. The wife of the magistrate wore them and went to Chengdu. All the young ladies arrived ahead of time, and Jianqiong peeped from behind the curtain. When the wife of the magistrate entered, radiant light surrounded her, her beauty shone out, and it was too dazzling to look directly at. Everyone present was awestruck and involuntarily stood up to bow. She returned home to eat and died three days later, with the red color fading away immediately. [82]

    兼琼大骇，具状录奏闻。帝问张果，果云：“知之，不敢言。请问青城王老。” 帝即召兼琼求访王老进之。兼琼搜索青城山前后，并无此人。惟草市药肆云： “常有二人日来买山药，称王老所使。”二人至，兼琼即令衙官随访。入山数里， 至一草堂，王老皤然鬓发，隐几危坐。衙官随入，遂宣诏，兼致兼琼意。王老曰： “此必多言小子张果也。”因与兼琼克期至京师，令先发表，不肯乘传，兼琼从 之。使才至银台，王老亦到。帝召问，张果犹在席侧，见王老，惶恐再拜。王老 叱果曰：“小子何不言之！又遣远取吾来。”果言：“小仙不敢，专俟仙伯言耳。” 因奏曰：“卢二舅即太元夫人库子，因假下游，以亡尉妻微有仙骨，故纳为媵。 无何，盗太元夫人衣服与着，已受谪至重，为郁单天子矣。亡尉妻以衣太元夫人 衣服，堕无间狱矣。”奏讫，苦不愿留，帝放还，出后不知所在。 [83]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [83]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 兼琼大骇 - Jianshao was greatly alarmed.
    • 具状录奏闻 - prepared a report and submitted it for the emperor's attention.
    • 知之，不敢言 - knows about it, but dares not speak.
    • 请问青城王老 - ask about the old man of Qingcheng.
    • 克期至京师 - agreed to a specific time to arrive in the capital.
    • 苦不愿留 - strongly unwilling to stay.
    • 出后不知所在 - after leaving, no one knows where he is.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 兼琼 - A person's name, likely a historical figure or a character in a story.
    • 青城山 - A famous mountain in Sichuan, China, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.
    • 王老 - Refers to the old man of Qingcheng, a legendary figure often associated with Taoist lore.
    • 张果 - A historical figure, a Taoist sage and alchemist known for his longevity and magical abilities.
    • 太元夫人 - A Taoist deity, often associated with the goddess of the moon and the queen mother of the west.
    • 郁单天子 - A term from Buddhist cosmology, referring to a ruler in the Pure Abodes Heaven.
    • 无间狱 - A place of eternal punishment in Buddhist and Hindu cosmology, equivalent to hell.

    Proper Nouns

    • 兼琼 - A person's name.
    • 张果 - A historical figure, a Taoist sage and alchemist.
    • 青城山 - A famous mountain in Sichuan, China.
    • 王老 - The old man of Qingcheng.
    • 太元夫人 - A Taoist deity.
    • 郁单天子 - A term from Buddhist cosmology.
    • 无间狱 - A place of eternal punishment in Buddhist and Hindu cosmology.

    Jianshao was greatly alarmed and prepared a report, which he submitted to the emperor. The emperor asked Zhang Guo, who replied, "I know about it, but I dare not speak. Please ask the old man of Qingcheng." The emperor immediately summoned Jianshao and requested him to find the old man of Qingcheng and bring him to the capital. Jianshao searched the areas around Qingcheng Mountain but found no trace of him. Only a pharmacist at a grass market said, "There are usually two men who come every day to buy yam, claiming they are sent by the old man." When these two men arrived, Jianshao ordered an official to follow them. They entered the mountain for several miles and came to a thatched cottage where the old man of Qingcheng sat upright with white hair, resting his head on a table. The official followed in and then announced the imperial edict, conveying Jianshao's intentions. The old man of Qingcheng said, "This must be the talkative little Zhang Guo." He then agreed to a specific time to arrive in the capital and ordered the report to be published first, refusing to travel by official carriage, and Jianshao followed his wishes.
