The envoy arrived at the Silver Pavilion, and the old man of Qingcheng also arrived. The emperor summoned them, and Zhang Guo was still standing by the side of the seat. Seeing the old man, he was shocked and bowed twice. The old man of Qingcheng scolded Zhang Guo, saying, "Why didn't you speak up! You sent someone far away to fetch me." Zhang Guo replied, "The little immortal dares not, waiting only for the words of the immortal elder." He then reported, "Lu Er's second uncle is the servant of the Lady Taiyuan, who went down to play and took his deceased magistrate's wife, who had a bit of immortal bone, as a concubine. Soon after, she stole the clothes of Lady Taiyuan and wore them, and has already been severely punished, becoming the ruler of the Pure Abodes Heaven. The deceased magistrate's wife, by wearing the clothes of Lady Taiyuan, has fallen into the realm of eternal punishment." After the report, the old man of Qingcheng strongly refused to stay, and the emperor allowed him to return. After leaving, no one knew where he was. [83]

    ○南缵
    　　
    　　广汉守南缵尝为人言：
    　　
    　　至德中有调选得同州督邮者，姓崔，忘名字，轻骑赴任。出春明门，见一青 袍人乘马出，亦不知其姓字，因相揖偕行。徐问何官，青袍人云：“新授同州督 邮。”崔云：“某新授此官，君岂不错误乎？”青袍人笑而不答。又相与行，悉 云赴任。去同州数十里，于斜路中，有官吏拜迎。青袍入谓崔君曰：“君为阳道 录事，某为阴道录事。路从此别，岂不相送耶？”崔生录之，即与连辔入斜路， 遂至一城郭，街衢局署，亦甚壮丽。 [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 南缵 - A name, likely referring to a historical figure or official.
    • 广汉守 - Refers to the governor of Guanghan, a region in ancient China.
    • 至德 - Refers to the era of great virtue or a specific historical period.
    • 调选 - Refers to the process of selecting or appointing officials.
    • 督邮 - An official responsible for overseeing postal services and local administration.
    • 轻骑 - Refers to a swift or light horse, symbolizing speed or urgency.
    • 春明门 - A gate or entrance, possibly a symbolic or historical reference.
    • 青袍人 - A person wearing a green robe, likely a symbolic or allegorical figure.
    • 新授 - Refers to being newly appointed or granted a position.
    • 阳道录事 - A term that may refer to a specific role or office, possibly related to the 'yang' (positive) aspect of governance.
    • 阴道录事 - A term that may refer to a specific role or office, possibly related to the 'yin' (negative) aspect of governance.
    • 斜路 - Refers to a side path or detour, possibly symbolic.
    • 局署 - Refers to government offices or administrative buildings.
    • 街衢 - Refers to streets or thoroughfares.
    • 壮丽 - Refers to grandeur or magnificence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 广汉 - A region in ancient China, known for its administrative significance.
    • 至德 - A period or era of great virtue, possibly referring to a specific historical time.
    • 督邮 - An official role in ancient China responsible for overseeing postal services and local administration.
    • 阳道录事 - These terms may refer to specific roles or offices, possibly related to the philosophical concepts of 'yang' (positive) and 'yin' (negative) in governance.
    • 阴道录事 - These terms may refer to specific roles or offices, possibly related to the philosophical concepts of 'yang' (positive) and 'yin' (negative) in governance.
    • 斜路 - A symbolic or allegorical path, possibly representing a detour or deviation from the norm.
    • 局署 - Refers to government offices and streets, indicating a structured and organized environment.
    • 街衢 - Refers to streets or thoroughfares.
    • 壮丽 - Describes the grandeur and magnificence of the place, possibly symbolizing the importance or power of the location.

    Proper Nouns

    • 南缵 - A name, likely referring to a historical figure or official.
    • 广汉 - A region in ancient China.
    • 至德 - A period or era of great virtue.
    • 同州 - A region or city in ancient China.
    • 崔 - A surname, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 春明门 - A gate or entrance, possibly a symbolic or historical reference.
    • 青袍人 - A person wearing a green robe, likely a symbolic or allegorical figure.
    • 阳道录事 - A term that may refer to a specific role or office.
    • 阴道录事 - A term that may refer to a specific role or office.
    • 斜路 - A symbolic or allegorical path.
    • 局署 - Government offices or administrative buildings.
    • 街衢 - Streets or thoroughfares.
    • 壮丽 - Grandeur or magnificence.

    Governor Nan Zuan of Guanghan once told someone:

    During the era of great virtue, there was someone selected to be the supervisor of Tongzhou. His surname was Cui, but his name is forgotten. He rode a light horse to take up his post. As he went out through the Chunming Gate, he saw a man in a green robe riding a horse out. He did not know the man's name, so they bowed to each other and walked together. Slowly, he asked what the man's position was. The man in the green robe said, "I have just been appointed as the supervisor of Tongzhou." Cui said, "I have just been appointed to this position, are you not mistaken?" The man in the green robe smiled and did not answer. They continued walking together, and both said they were going to take up their posts. After traveling dozens of miles from Tongzhou, on a side path, officials came to greet them. The man in the green robe entered and said to Cui, "You are the officer of the yang aspect, and I am the officer of the yin aspect. Our paths diverge from here, should we not bid farewell?" Cui recorded this and immediately rode alongside the man in the green robe into the side path, and they eventually arrived at a city. The streets and government offices were also very grand and magnificent. [84]

    青袍人至厅，与崔生同坐受谒，通胥徒、僧道等讫，次通辞讼狱囚，崔之妻 与焉。崔生大惊，谓青袍人曰：“不知拙室何得至此？”青袍人即避大案后，令 崔生自与妻言。妻云：“被迫至此，已是数日，君宜哀请录事耳。”崔生即祈求 青袍人，青袍人因令胥吏促放崔生妻令回。崔生试问妻犯何罪至此，青袍人曰： “君寄家同州，应同州亡人，皆在此厅勘过。盖君管阳道，某管阴道。”崔生淹 留半日，即请却回。青袍人令胥吏拜送，曰：“虽阴阳有殊，然具是同州也，可 不拜送督邮哉！”青袍人亦偕饯送，再三勤款，挥袂，又令斜路口而去。
    　　
    　　崔生至同州，问妻子，妻子云：“病七八日，冥然无知，神不识生，愈才得 一日。”崔生计之，恰放回日也。妻不记阴道见崔生时，崔生言之，妻始悟如梦， 亦不审记也。 [85]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [85]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 青袍人 - A term referring to a magistrate or official, as '青袍' (green robe) was a traditional attire for lower-ranking officials in ancient China.
    • 受谒 - Means 'to receive an audience' or 'to be greeted,' often used in the context of formal meetings.
    • 通胥徒、僧道等 - '胥徒' refers to clerks or low-ranking officials, '僧道' refers to Buddhist monks and Taoist priests. This phrase means 'to announce the presence of clerks, monks, and Taoists.'
    • 辞讼狱囚 - Refers to litigants, defendants, and prisoners. '辞讼' means 'litigation,' and '狱囚' means 'prisoners.'
    • 拙室 - A humble term for one's wife, used to show respect and modesty.
    • 被迫至此 - Means 'forced to come here,' indicating coercion or compulsion.
    • 录事 - Refers to a recorder or clerk, often responsible for recording and managing official documents.
    • 管阳道，某管阴道 - '阳道' and '阴道' refer to the administration of different regions or jurisdictions, possibly metaphorically representing male and female roles or responsibilities.
    • 淹留半日 - Means 'to stay for half a day,' indicating a prolonged stay.
    • 挥袂 - Means 'to wave the sleeves,' a gesture of farewell.
    • 斜路口 - Means 'a side road,' indicating a specific location for departure.
    • 寄家同州 - Means 'to leave one's family in Tongzhou,' indicating a place where the family is temporarily staying.
    • 冥然无知 - Means 'unconscious and unaware,' describing a state of being out of touch with reality.
    • 神不识生 - Means 'the spirit does not recognize the living,' suggesting a disconnection between the physical and spiritual realms.
    • 如梦 - Means 'like a dream,' indicating a state of confusion or unreality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 青袍人 - The term '青袍人' is associated with lower-ranking officials, reflecting the hierarchical structure of ancient Chinese bureaucracy.
    • 同州 - A place name, indicating a specific region in China.
    • 冥然无知，神不识生 - These phrases reflect the ancient Chinese belief in the separation of the physical and spiritual worlds, where the soul or spirit can be disconnected from the body.
    • 阴道 - In this context, '阴道' is used metaphorically to represent a different jurisdiction or region, possibly reflecting a dualistic view of governance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 青袍人 - The green-robed official.
    • 崔生 - The protagonist, a man.
    • 崔之妻 - The wife of the protagonist.
    • 同州 - A place name, indicating a specific region in China.
    • 胥徒 - Clerks or low-ranking officials.
    • 僧道 - Buddhist monks and Taoist priests.
    • 辞讼狱囚 - Litigants, defendants, and prisoners.
    • 录事 - A recorder or clerk.
    • 督邮 - A local official responsible for maintaining order and collecting taxes.

    The green-robed official arrived at the hall and sat down with Cui Sheng to receive an audience. After announcing the presence of clerks, monks, and Taoists, he proceeded to handle litigants and prisoners, including Cui Sheng's wife. Cui Sheng was greatly surprised and asked the official, "How did my humble wife come to be here?" The official stepped back behind the large desk and instructed Cui Sheng to speak with his wife himself. The wife replied, "I was forced to come here and have been here for several days. You should plead with the recorder on my behalf." Cui Sheng immediately begged the official, who then ordered the clerk to hasten the release of Cui Sheng's wife. Cui Sheng asked his wife what crime she had committed to be brought here, and the official explained, "You left your family in Tongzhou. All the deceased from Tongzhou have been examined here. I am in charge of the Yangdao, and I am in charge of the Yindao." Cui Sheng stayed for half a day and then requested to return. The official ordered the clerk to see him off, saying, "Although there is a difference between Yang and Yin, we are both from Tongzhou. How can we not bid farewell to the local official?" The official also accompanied them to see them off, offering repeated hospitality, and waved his sleeves, instructing them to go down a side road.

    When Cui Sheng arrived in Tongzhou and asked his wife about her condition, she said, "I have been ill for seven or eight days, unconscious and unaware, with my spirit not recognizing the living. I only recovered a day ago." Cui Sheng calculated that it was the same day she was released. His wife did not remember seeing Cui Sheng in the Yindao, but when Cui Sheng mentioned it, she began to realize it was like a dream and did not clearly remember it either. [85]

    ○侯遹
    　　
    　　隋开皇初，广都孝廉侯遹入城，至剑门外，忽见四黄石，皆大如斗。遹爱之， 收藏于笼，负之以驴，因歇鞍取看，皆化为金。遹至城货之，得钱百万，市美妾 十余人，大开第宅，近甸良田别墅，货买甚多。
    　　
    　　后乘春景出游，尽载妓妾随从，下车陈设酒肴。忽有一老翁，负大笈至，厕 下坐。遹怒诟之，命苍头扶出，叟不动亦不嗔恚，但引满杯啖炙而笑云：“吾此 来求君偿债耳。君昔将我金去，不忆记乎？”尽取遹妓妾十余人，投之于笈，亦 不觉笈中之窄，负之而趋，走若飞鸟。遹令苍头驰马逐之，斯须已失所在。自后 遹家日贫，却复昔日生计。十余年，却归蜀，到剑门，又见前者老翁，携所将妓 妾游行，傧从极多，见遹皆大笑。问之不言，逼之又失所在。访剑门前后，并无 此人，竟不能测也。
    　　
    　　○巴邛人 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 開皇初 - Refers to the early years of Emperor Wen of Sui's reign (581-604 AD), specifically the first year of the Kaihuang era (581 AD).
    • 孝廉 - An official title for those recommended for office based on their moral integrity and literary talent.
    • 劍門 - The name of a famous mountain pass in Sichuan, known for its strategic importance.
    • 四黄石 - Refers to four yellow stones, which are described as being as large as a dou (an ancient Chinese unit of volume).
    • 負之以驴 - Means 'carried them on a donkey.'
    • 收藏于笼 - Means 'collected and stored in a cage.'
    • 皆化为金 - Means 'all turned into gold.'
    • 大开第宅 - Means 'built a large mansion.'
    • 近甸良田别墅 - Refers to nearby good farmland and villas.
    • 货买甚多 - Means 'bought many things.'
    • 乘春景出游 - Means 'went out for a spring outing.'
    • 尽载妓妾随从 - Means 'loaded all his concubines and maidservants to follow.'
    • 下车陈设酒肴 - Means 'got off the carriage and set up wine and food.'
    • 引满杯啖炙而笑 - Means 'took a full cup, ate roasted meat, and laughed.'
    • 吾此来求君偿债耳 - Means 'I came here to demand repayment of a debt from you.'
    • 投之于笈 - Means 'threw them into a bag.'
    • 负之而趋，走若飞鸟 - Means 'carried them away, running like a bird.'
    • 令苍头扶出 - Means 'ordered the servants to escort him out.'
    • 不动亦不嗔恚 - Means 'did not move and was not angry.'
    • 逼之又失所在 - Means 'when pressured, disappeared again.'
    • 访剑门前后 - Means 'visited the area around the Jiemeng Pass.'
    • 无此人 - Means 'there was no one like him.'
    • 竟不能测也 - Means 'could not be understood or measured.'
    • 广都孝廉侯遹 - Refers to a man named Hou Yu, who was recommended for office based on his moral integrity and literary talent.
    • 巴邛人 - Refers to people from Baqing, a place in Sichuan.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋朝 - The Sui Dynasty (581-618 AD) was a short-lived but significant dynasty in Chinese history.
    • 开皇 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Sui, who reigned from 581 to 604 AD.
    • 孝廉 - A system of recommending officials based on moral integrity and literary talent, which was part of the imperial examination system.
    • 剑门 - A famous mountain pass in Sichuan, known for its strategic importance and natural beauty.
    • 黄金 - Gold was highly valued in ancient China and often used as a symbol of wealth and power.
    • 妓妾 - Concubines and maidservants, who were often part of the household of wealthy men.
    • 第宅 - Refers to a large mansion or residence.
    • 良田别墅 - Refers to good farmland and villas, which were often owned by the wealthy.
    • 苍头 - Servants or attendants, often referred to as 'black heads' due to their dark hair.
    • 笈 - A bag or container, often used to carry books or other items.

    Proper Nouns

    • 侯遹 - The name of the protagonist in the story.
    • 广都 - The place where Hou Yu was from.
    • 剑门 - The name of the mountain pass.
    • 隋朝 - The name of the dynasty.
    • 开皇 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Sui.
    • 巴邛 - A place in Sichuan.

    In the early years of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, Hou Yu, a filial and honest official from Guangdu, entered the city and reached the outskirts of the Jiemeng Pass. Suddenly, he saw four yellow stones, all as large as a dou. Hou Yu loved them and collected them in a cage, carrying them on a donkey. He stopped to rest and looked at them, and they all turned into gold. Hou Yu sold the gold in the city and made a million coins, buying more than ten beautiful concubines, building a large mansion, and purchasing nearby good farmland and villas.

    Later, he went out for a spring outing, loading all his concubines and maidservants to follow. He got off the carriage and set up wine and food. Suddenly, an old man appeared, carrying a large bag and sitting down. Hou Yu became angry and scolded him, ordering the servants to escort him out. The old man did not move and was not angry, but instead took a full cup, ate roasted meat, and laughed, saying, "I came here to demand repayment of a debt from you. Do you not remember when you took my gold?" The old man then took more than ten of Hou Yu's concubines and threw them into his bag, which did not seem narrow at all. He carried them away, running like a bird. Hou Yu ordered the servants to chase him on horseback, but soon the old man disappeared.

    After that, Hou Yu's family gradually became poor, returning to their former circumstances. Ten years later, he returned to Sichuan and reached the Jiemeng Pass. He saw the old man from before, who was traveling with the concubines he had taken, accompanied by many attendants. When they saw Hou Yu, they all laughed. Hou Yu asked them, but they did not respond. When he pressured them, they disappeared again. He visited the area around the Jiemeng Pass, but there was no one like him, and he could not understand or measure what had happened.

    ○巴邛人

    In Baqing, there was a man who, after drinking a cup of wine, suddenly transformed into a bird and flew away. The people of Baqing were amazed and followed him, but they could not catch him. He flew to a distant mountain and disappeared. The people of Baqing never saw him again. [86]

    有巴邛人，不知姓名，家有桔园。因霜后，诸桔尽收，余有两大桔，如三斗 盎。巴人录之，即令攀摘，轻重亦如常桔。剖开，每桔有二老叟，鬓眉皤然，肌 体红润，皆相对象戏，身长尺余，谈笑自若，剖开后亦不惊怖，但相与决赌。决 赌讫，一叟曰：“君输我海上龙王第七女须发十两，智琼额黄十二枝，紫绢帔一 副，绛台山霞宝散二庾，瀛洲玉尘九斛，阿母疗髓凝酒四钟，阿母女态盈娘子跻 虚龙缟袜八纟两，后日于王先生青城草堂还我耳。”又有一叟曰：“王先生许来， 竟待不得，桔中之乐，不减商山，但不得深根固蒂，为愚人摘下耳。”又一叟曰： “仆饥矣，须龙根脯食之。”即于袖中抽出一草根，方圆径寸，形状宛转如龙， 毫厘罔不周悉，因削食之，随削随满。食讫，以水噀之，化为一龙，四叟共乘 之，足下泄泄云起。须叟，风雨晦冥，不知所在。巴人相传云：百五十年来如此， 似在陈隋之间，但不知的年号耳。 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不知姓名 - Means 'unknown name,' indicating that the person's identity is not recorded.
    • 家有桔园 - Means 'has an orange grove,' referring to the man's property.
    • 因霜后 - Means 'after the frost,' indicating a seasonal event.
    • 余有两大桔 - Means 'there are two large oranges left,' describing the remaining fruit.
    • 如三斗盎 - Means 'as large as a three-dou bowl,' using a unit of measurement to describe size.
    • 巴人录之 - Means 'the people of Ba recorded it,' indicating that the event was documented.
    • 轻重亦如常桔 - Means 'their weight is also like that of ordinary oranges,' describing the weight of the large oranges.
    • 剖开，每桔有二老叟 - Means 'when cut open, each orange contains two old men,' describing the contents of the oranges.
    • 鬓眉皤然 - Means 'with white hair and eyebrows,' describing the appearance of the old men.
    • 肌体红润 - Means 'with rosy and smooth skin,' describing the physical condition of the old men.
    • 皆相对象戏 - Means 'they all playfully interact with each other,' describing the behavior of the old men.
    • 身长尺余 - Means 'each about a foot long,' describing the size of the old men.
    • 谈笑自若 - Means 'chatting and laughing naturally,' describing the demeanor of the old men.
    • 剖开后亦不惊怖 - Means 'they are not frightened after being cut open,' describing the reaction of the old men.
    • 但相与决赌 - Means 'but they only gamble with each other,' describing the activity of the old men.
    • 君输我海上龙王第七女须发十两 - Means 'you owe me ten taels of the seventh daughter of the Dragon King of the Sea's hair,' describing a bet involving a mythical figure.
    • 智琼额黄十二枝 - Means 'twelve strands of Zhijiong's forehead yellow,' referring to a mythical or legendary item.
    • 紫绢帔一副 - Means 'a purple silk shawl,' describing a garment.
    • 绛台山霞宝散二庾 - Means 'two庾of the precious powder from the Red Platform Mountain,' referring to a mythical or legendary item.
    • 瀛洲玉尘九斛 - Means 'nine斛of the jade dust from Yingzhou,' referring to a mythical or legendary item.
    • 阿母疗髓凝酒四钟 - Means 'four zhou of the bone-marrow curing wine from Mother,' referring to a mythical or legendary item.
    • 阿母女态盈娘子跻虚龙缟袜八纟两 - Means 'eight两of the dragon silk stockings from Mother's daughter Tai Ying,' referring to a mythical or legendary item.
    • 后日于王先生青城草堂还我耳 - Means 'you will return them to me at Mr. Wang's Qingcheng Hermitage in the future,' describing a future action.
    • 王先生许来，竟待不得 - Means 'Mr. Wang promised to come, but he never arrived,' describing a missed appointment.
    • 桔中之乐，不减商山 - Means 'the joy in the orange is no less than in Shangshan,' comparing the enjoyment to a famous place.
    • 但不得深根固蒂，为愚人摘下耳 - Means 'but they cannot have deep roots and a strong stem, as they are picked by foolish people,' describing the fate of the oranges.
    • 仆饥矣，须龙根脯食之 - Means 'I am hungry, and I need to eat the dragon root meat,' describing the old men's request.
    • 即于袖中抽出一草根 - Means 'he immediately pulled out a grass root from his sleeve,' describing the action of one of the old men.
    • 方圆径寸 - Means 'one inch in diameter,' describing the size of the grass root.
    • 形状宛转如龙 - Means 'shaped like a dragon,' describing the appearance of the grass root.
    • 毫厘罔不周悉 - Means 'every detail is perfect,' describing the precision of the grass root.
    • 因削食之，随削随满 - Means 'he cut and ate it, and it refilled as he cut,' describing the magical property of the grass root.
    • 食讫，以水噀之 - Means 'after eating, he spat water on it,' describing the action of one of the old men.
    • 化为一龙 - Means 'it turned into a dragon,' describing the transformation of the grass root.
    • 四叟共乘之 - Means 'the four old men rode it together,' describing the action of the old men.
    • 足下泄泄云起 - Means 'clouds rose under their feet,' describing the magical effect of the dragon.
    • 须叟，风雨晦冥，不知所在 - Means 'soon, there was wind, rain, and darkness, and they disappeared,' describing the disappearance of the old men and the dragon.
    • 巴人相传云 - Means 'the people of Ba say,' introducing a traditional account.
    • 百五十年来如此 - Means 'it has been like this for over a hundred and fifty years,' describing the duration of the tradition.
    • 似在陈隋之间 - Means 'it seems to have occurred during the Chen and Sui dynasties,' providing a historical context.
    • 但不知的年号耳 - Means 'but the exact year is unknown,' indicating the uncertainty of the specific date.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 巴邛人 - Refers to people from Ba and Qiong, regions in ancient China.
    • 龙王第七女 - Refers to the seventh daughter of the Dragon King of the Sea, a mythical figure.
    • 智琼 - A legendary figure often associated with beauty and grace.
    • 阿母 - Refers to a mythical mother figure, possibly the Queen Mother of the West.
    • 青城草堂 - Refers to a hermitage in Qingcheng, a famous Taoist site.
    • 商山 - Refers to Shangshan, a famous mountain known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
    • 陈隋之间 - Refers to the period between the Chen and Sui dynasties, a time of transition in Chinese history.

    Proper Nouns

    • 巴邛人 - People from Ba and Qiong.
    • 龙王第七女 - The seventh daughter of the Dragon King of the Sea.
    • 智琼 - A legendary figure.
    • 阿母 - A mythical mother figure.
    • 青城草堂 - A hermitage in Qingcheng.
    • 商山 - Shangshan.
    • 陈隋之间 - The period between the Chen and Sui dynasties.

    A Man from Ba and Qiong

    There was a man from Ba and Qiong, whose name is unknown, who had an orange grove. After the frost, all the oranges were harvested, leaving two large oranges, each as big as a three-dou bowl. The people of Ba recorded this, and they were instructed to climb and pick the oranges, which were as heavy as ordinary oranges. When cut open, each orange contained two old men, with white hair and eyebrows, rosy and smooth skin, who all playfully interacted with each other, each about a foot long, chatting and laughing naturally. They were not frightened after being cut open, but they only gambled with each other. After the gambling, one old man said, "You owe me ten taels of the seventh daughter of the Dragon King of the Sea's hair, twelve strands of Zhijiong's forehead yellow, a purple silk shawl, two庾of the precious powder from the Red Platform Mountain, nine斛of the jade dust from Yingzhou, four zhou of the bone-marrow curing wine from Mother, and eight两of the dragon silk stockings from Mother's daughter Tai Ying. You will return them to me at Mr. Wang's Qingcheng Hermitage in the future." Another old man said, "Mr. Wang promised to come, but he never arrived. The joy in the orange is no less than in Shangshan, but they cannot have deep roots and a strong stem, as they are picked by foolish people." Another old man said, "I am hungry, and I need to eat the dragon root meat." He immediately pulled out a grass root from his sleeve, one inch in diameter, shaped like a dragon, with every detail perfect. He cut and ate it, and it refilled as he cut. After eating, he spat water on it, and it turned into a dragon. The four old men rode it together, and clouds rose under their feet. Soon, there was wind, rain, and darkness, and they disappeared. The people of Ba say that it has been like this for over a hundred and fifty years, and it seems to have occurred during the Chen and Sui dynasties, but the exact year is unknown. [87]

    ○刘法师
    　　
    　　贞元中，华州云台观有刘法师者，炼气绝粒，迨二十年。每三元设斋，则见 一人，衣缝掖而面黧瘦，来居末座，斋毕而去，如此者十余年，而衣服颜色不改。 法师异而问之，对曰：“余姓张名公弼，住莲花峰东隅。”法师意此处无人之境， 请同往。公弼怡然许之，曰：“此中甚乐，师能便往，亦当无闷。”
    　　
    　　法师遂随公弼行，三二十里，援萝攀葛，才有鸟道，经过崖谷险绝，虽猿狖 不能过也，而公弼履之若夷途，法师从行亦无难。遂至一石壁，削成，高直千余 仞，下临无底之谷。一迳阔数寸，法师与公弼侧足而立。公弼乃以指扣石壁，中 有人问曰：“为谁？”曰：“某。”遂划然开一门，门中有天地日月。公弼将入， 法师随公弼亦入，其人乃怒谓公弼：“何引外人来？”其人因阖门，则又成石壁 矣。 [88]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [88]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 煉氣絕粒 - Refers to the practice of cultivating qi (vital energy) and abstaining from food, a technique associated with Daoist immortality practices.
    • 三元設齋 - Refers to the three major festivals in the Daoist calendar when rituals are performed: the Beginning of Spring, the Beginning of Summer, and the Beginning of Autumn.
    • 縫掖 - Refers to the simple robes worn by scholars or monks, often associated with Confucian or Daoist attire.
    • 黧瘦 - Describes a person with a dark, emaciated appearance, often used to depict someone who has undergone hardship or ascetic practices.
    • 衣缝掖而面黧瘦 - Describes the appearance of the mysterious figure, emphasizing his ascetic and possibly Daoist nature.
    • 援萝攀葛 - Metaphorically describes climbing through dense vegetation, often used to depict a journey through a challenging or mystical landscape.
    • 崖谷险绝 - Refers to extremely dangerous cliffs and valleys, emphasizing the perilous nature of the journey.
    • 猿狖不能过也 - Indicates that even the most agile creatures, such as apes and monkeys, cannot pass through, highlighting the extreme difficulty of the terrain.
    • 履之若夷途 - Means 'treading it as if it were a flat road,' describing how effortlessly the mysterious figure navigates the difficult terrain.
    • 石壁削成 - Refers to a sheer, vertical cliff face, emphasizing the natural beauty and danger of the landscape.
    • 高直千余仞 - Describes the height of the cliff, emphasizing its towering and imposing nature.
    • 下临无底之谷 - Describes the cliff as overlooking a bottomless valley, adding to the sense of awe and danger.
    • 一迳阔数寸 - Refers to a narrow path, emphasizing the precariousness of the journey.
    • 侧足而立 - Means 'standing sideways,' describing the precarious position of the travelers on the narrow path.
    • 划然开一门 - Describes the sudden opening of a door, emphasizing the mystical and unexpected nature of the event.
    • 天地日月 - Refers to a miniature world with its own sky, earth, sun, and moon, emphasizing the magical and otherworldly nature of the hidden realm.
    • 阖门 - Means 'to close the door,' describing the action that reverts the scene back to a solid stone wall.
    • 何引外人来 - Means 'why bring outsiders here,' expressing the surprise and concern of the inhabitants of the hidden realm.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贞元中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Dezong of Tang (780-805 CE).
    • 华州 - Refers to Huazhou, a prefecture in Shaanxi province during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 云台观 - Refers to Yuntai Temple, a Daoist temple in Huazhou, known for its spiritual significance.
    • 刘法师 - Refers to Liu, a Daoist priest, emphasizing his role in the story.
    • 张公弼 - Refers to Zhang Gongbi, the mysterious figure who appears at the temple, emphasizing his enigmatic nature.
    • 莲花峰 - Refers to a mountain peak, likely a sacred or mystical location in Daoist tradition.
    • 石壁 - Refers to a natural stone wall, emphasizing the natural beauty and danger of the landscape.
    • 天地日月 - Refers to a miniature world with its own sky, earth, sun, and moon, emphasizing the magical and otherworldly nature of the hidden realm.

    Proper Nouns

    • 贞元 - The reign period of Emperor Dezong of Tang (780-805 CE).
    • 华州 - Huazhou, a prefecture in Shaanxi province during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 云台观 - Yuntai Temple, a Daoist temple in Huazhou.
    • 刘法师 - Liu, a Daoist priest.
    • 张公弼 - Zhang Gongbi, the mysterious figure who appears at the temple.
    • 莲花峰 - A mountain peak, likely a sacred or mystical location in Daoist tradition.
    • 石壁 - A natural stone wall.
    • 天地日月 - A miniature world with its own sky, earth, sun, and moon.

    During the reign of Emperor Dezong in the Tang Dynasty, there was a Daoist priest named Liu at the Yuntai Temple in Huazhou. He had been cultivating qi and abstaining from food for nearly twenty years. Every time the three major festivals arrived and rituals were performed, Liu would see a man dressed in simple robes with a dark, emaciated appearance. This man would sit in the last seat during the rituals and leave after they concluded. This happened for over ten years, and the man's clothes and complexion never changed. The priest found this strange and asked him, "What is your name?" The man replied, "My name is Zhang Gongbi, and I live on the eastern slope of Lotus Peak." Liu thought that this place was a remote and uninhabited area and asked Zhang if he could accompany him. Zhang agreed happily and said, "This place is very pleasant. If you can come here, you will also find no boredom."

    Liu then followed Zhang as they traveled for about thirty to forty miles, climbing through dense vegetation where only birds could pass. They passed through extremely dangerous cliffs and valleys, places even apes and monkeys could not cross, but Zhang walked through them as if they were flat roads, and Liu followed without difficulty. They eventually reached a sheer cliff, standing over a thousand feet high, overlooking a bottomless valley below. A narrow path, only a few inches wide, led to a spot where Liu and Zhang stood sideways. Zhang then tapped the stone wall with his finger, and someone inside asked, "Who is it?" Zhang replied, "It's me." Suddenly, the wall opened, revealing a door with its own sky, earth, sun, and moon. As Zhang was about to enter, Liu followed him, and the person inside angrily said to Zhang, "Why have you brought outsiders here?" The person then closed the door, and it reverted to a solid stone wall. [88]

    公弼曰：“此非他，乃云台刘法师也，余交战，故请来此，何见拒之深也？” 又开门，内公弼及法师，公弼曰：“法师此来甚饥，君可丰食遣之。”其人遂问 法师：“便能住否？”法师请以后期。其人遂取一盂水，以肘后青囊中刀圭粉糁 之以饮法师，味甚甘香，饮毕而饥渴之想除矣。公弼曰：“余昨云山中甚乐，君 盍为戏，令法师观之。”其人乃以水噀东谷中，乃有苍龙白象各一，对舞，舞 甚妙，威凤彩鸾各一对歌，歌甚清。顷之，公弼送法师回，回顾，惟见青崖丹壑， 向之歌舞，一无所见矣。及去观将近，公弼乃辞。 [89]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [89]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 此非他，乃云台刘法师也 - Formal statement of identity, meaning 'This is not someone else; it is the Daoist Master Liu of Yun Tai.'
    • 余交战，故请来此 - Statement of reason, meaning 'I was engaged in battle, so I asked to come here.'
    • 何见拒之深也 - Rhetorical question expressing surprise or frustration, meaning 'Why are you rejecting me so deeply?'
    • 内公弼及法师 - Command or action, meaning 'Let Gong Bi and the Daoist Master in.'
    • 法师此来甚饥，君可丰食遣之 - Statement of need and request, meaning 'The Daoist Master has come here very hungry; you may give him a generous meal.'
    • 其人遂问法师：“便能住否？” - Question, meaning 'The person then asked the Daoist Master, 'Can you stay here?''
    • 法师请以后期 - Statement of request, meaning 'The Daoist Master requested to stay for a later time.'
    • 其人遂取一盂水，以肘后青囊中刀圭粉糁之以饮法师 - Description of an action, meaning 'The person then took a bowl of water and sprinkled a small amount of powder from a green bag behind his elbow to drink to the Daoist Master.'
    • 味甚甘香，饮毕而饥渴之想除矣 - Description of the effect, meaning 'The taste was very sweet and fragrant, and after drinking, the thoughts of hunger and thirst were dispelled.'
    • 余昨云山中甚乐，君盍为戏，令法师观之 - Statement of past enjoyment and a suggestion, meaning 'I said yesterday that the mountain was very enjoyable; why don't you perform a play, so the Daoist Master can watch?'
    • 其人乃以水噀东谷中，乃有苍龙白象各一，对舞，舞甚妙，威凤彩鸾各一对歌，歌甚清 - Description of a magical performance, meaning 'The person then sprayed water into the eastern valley, and there appeared one blue dragon and one white elephant, dancing together, with their dance being very wonderful. There were also pairs of majestic phoenixes and colorful cranes singing, with their songs being very clear.'
    • 顷之，公弼送法师回，回顾，惟见青崖丹壑，向之歌舞，一无所见矣 - Description of the aftermath, meaning 'After a while, Gong Bi sent the Daoist Master back, and when he looked back, he only saw green cliffs and red ravines, and the previous dancing and singing were no longer visible.'
    • 及去观将近，公弼乃辞 - Statement of departure, meaning 'As they were about to leave, Gong Bi bid farewell.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 公弼 - A name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 云台刘法师 - A Daoist master named Liu, associated with Yun Tai (云台), which could be a place or a title.
    • 苍龙白象 - Blue dragon and white elephant, often symbols of power and majesty in Chinese mythology.
    • 威凤彩鸾 - Majestic phoenix and colorful crane, often symbols of beauty and grace in Chinese mythology.
    • 青崖丹壑 - Green cliffs and red ravines, a poetic description of a natural landscape.

    Proper Nouns

    • 公弼 - A name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 云台刘法师 - A Daoist master named Liu, associated with Yun Tai (云台), which could be a place or a title.
    • 苍龙白象 - Blue dragon and white elephant, often symbols of power and majesty in Chinese mythology.
    • 威凤彩鸾 - Majestic phoenix and colorful crane, often symbols of beauty and grace in Chinese mythology.
    • 青崖丹壑 - Green cliffs and red ravines, a poetic description of a natural landscape.

    Gong Bi said, "This is not someone else; it is the Daoist Master Liu of Yun Tai. I was engaged in battle, so I asked to come here. Why are you rejecting me so deeply?" He then opened the door and let Gong Bi and the Daoist Master in. Gong Bi said, "The Daoist Master has come here very hungry; you may give him a generous meal." The person then asked the Daoist Master, "Can you stay here?" The Daoist Master requested to stay for a later time. The person then took a bowl of water and sprinkled a small amount of powder from a green bag behind his elbow to drink to the Daoist Master. The taste was very sweet and fragrant, and after drinking, the thoughts of hunger and thirst were dispelled. Gong Bi said, "I said yesterday that the mountain was very enjoyable; why don't you perform a play, so the Daoist Master can watch?" The person then sprayed water into the eastern valley, and there appeared one blue dragon and one white elephant, dancing together, with their dance being very wonderful. There were also pairs of majestic phoenixes and colorful cranes singing, with their songs being very clear. After a while, Gong Bi sent the Daoist Master back, and when he looked back, he only saw green cliffs and red ravines, and the previous dancing and singing were no longer visible. As they were about to leave, Gong Bi bid farewell. [89]

    法师至观，处置事毕，却寻公弼，则步步险阻，杳不可阶，痛恨前者不住， 号天叫地，遂成腰疾。公弼更不复至矣。
    　　
    　　昭应县尉薛公幹为僧孺叔父言也。
    　　
    　　○刁俊朝
    　　
    　　安康伶人刁俊朝，其妻巴妪，项瘿者，初微若鸡卵，渐巨如三四升瓶盎。积 五年，大如数斛之囊，重不能行。其中有琴瑟笙磬埙篪之响。细而听之，若合音 律，泠泠可乐。积数年，瘿外生小穴如针芒者，不知几亿。每天欲雨，则穴中吹 白烟，霏霏如丝缕，渐高布散，结为屯云，雨则立降。其家少长惧之，咸请远送 岩穴。俊朝恋恋不能已，因谓妻曰：“吾迫以众议，将不能庇于伉俪。送君于无 人之境，如何？”妻曰：“吾此疾诚可憎恶，送之亦死，拆之亦死。君当为我决 拆之，看有何物。”俊朝即磨淬利刃，挥挑将及妻前，瘿中轩然有声，遂四分披 裂，有一大猱，跳走腾踏而去。即以帛絮裹之。虽瘿疾顿愈，而冥然大渐矣。 [90]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [90]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 处置事毕 - A formal expression meaning 'after handling affairs,' indicating the completion of tasks.
    • 号天叫地 - An idiomatic expression meaning to cry out in despair or frustration, often used to describe extreme emotional distress.
    • 冥然大渐矣 - Means 'soon became very ill,' indicating a sudden and severe deterioration in health.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 昭应县尉薛公幹为僧孺叔父言也 - Indicates that the story is told by Xue Gonggan, a county magistrate, on behalf of his uncle Sengru, reflecting the social hierarchy and familial relationships in ancient China.
    • 安康伶人刁俊朝 - Refers to a performer from Ankang, indicating the cultural significance of performing arts in ancient China.
    • 琴瑟笙磬埙篪 - Refers to various traditional Chinese musical instruments, highlighting the rich musical culture of the time.

    Proper Nouns

    • 公弼 - The name of a person, likely a friend or associate of the monk.
    • 昭应县尉薛公幹 - Xue Gonggan, a county magistrate from Zhao Ying.
    • 僧孺叔父 - Sengru, the uncle of Xue Gonggan.
    • 安康伶人刁俊朝 - Diao Junzhao, a performer from Ankang.
    • 巴妪 - The name of Diao Junzhao's wife, who had a goiter.
    • 大猱 - A large monkey, which appears as a result of removing the goiter.

    The monk arrived at the temple, handled all the affairs, and then went back to look for Gong Bi, but every step was fraught with danger, utterly unreachable. Regretting not stopping earlier, he cried out to the heavens and the earth, and thus developed a backache. Gong Bi no longer came.

    Zhao Ying County Magistrate Xue Gonggan spoke these words on behalf of his uncle Sengru.

    Diao Junzhao, a performer from Ankang, had a wife named Ba Yu, who suffered from a goiter. Initially, it was as small as a chicken egg, but gradually grew to the size of a jar or pot holding three to four sheng. After five years, it became as large as a bag holding several dou, so heavy that she could not move. Inside the goiter were sounds resembling those of zithers, flutes, bells, and pipes. When listened to carefully, they seemed to follow the rules of music, clear and pleasant. After several years, tiny holes formed on the surface of the goiter, likened to needles, with an unknown number. Before rain, white smoke would be emitted from these holes, rising and spreading, forming clouds, and rain would fall immediately. The family, young and old, feared this and all requested to send her far away to a cave. Junzhao was deeply attached and could not bear to part. He said to his wife, "I am pressured by public opinion, and I will not be able to protect you in marriage. To send you to a place where no one lives, what do you think?" She replied, "My disease is truly disgusting, whether sent away or removed, I will die. You should remove it for me, and see what is inside." Junzhao immediately sharpened a sharp knife and was about to cut into her when the goiter split into four pieces with a loud sound, revealing a large monkey that jumped away, running and jumping. He then wrapped it in cloth and cotton. Although the goiter disease was suddenly cured, she soon became very ill. [90]

    明日，有黄冠扣门曰：“吾乃昨日瘿中走出之猱也。吾本猕猴之精，解致风 雨。无何与汉江鬼愁潭老蛟还往，常与觇船。舸将至，俾他覆之，以求舟中，饣 侯粮，以养孙息。昨者太一诛蛟，搜索党与，故借君夫人蝤蛴之领，以匿性命。 虽分不相干，然为累亦甚矣。今于凤凰山神处求得少许灵膏，请君涂之，幸当立 愈。”俊朝如其言涂之，随手疮合。俊朝因留黄冠，烹鸡设食。食讫，贳酒欲饮， 黄冠因啭喉高歌。又为丝匏琼玉之音，罔不铿锵可爱。既而辞去，莫知所诣。时 大定中也。 [91]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [91]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 明日 - Means 'the next day' or 'tomorrow,' indicating a specific point in time.
    • 黄冠 - Refers to a Taoist priest or monk, often depicted wearing a yellow hat.
    • 吾乃昨日瘿中走出之猱也 - Self-introduction where '瘿中走出' means 'came out of a goiter,' and '猱' refers to a type of monkey.
    • 吾本猕猴之精 - Means 'I am originally a spirit of a macaque,' suggesting a supernatural origin.
    • 解致风雨 - Means 'can summon wind and rain,' indicating magical abilities.
    • 无何与汉江鬼愁潭老蛟还往 - Means 'frequently interacted with the old dragon in the Ghost Sorrow Pool of the Han River,' suggesting a connection with mythical creatures.
    • 俾他覆之 - Means 'make them capsize,' indicating the intent to cause destruction.
    • 以求舟中饣侯粮 - Means 'to seek food from the boat,' indicating a motive for causing the boat to capsize.
    • 以养孙息 - Means 'to feed my descendants,' indicating a familial motive.
    • 太一诛蛟 - Means 'the Supreme Being punished the dragon,' referring to a divine intervention.
    • 搜索党与 - Means 'searched for accomplices,' indicating a legal or divine investigation.
    • 故借君夫人蝤蛴之领，以匿性命 - Means 'so I borrowed the neck of your wife, the Qiuqi, to hide my life,' indicating a supernatural act.
    • 虽分不相干 - Means 'though we are not directly related,' indicating a lack of direct connection.
    • 然为累亦甚矣 - Means 'but it has also been a great burden,' indicating the negative impact.
    • 今于凤凰山神处求得少许灵膏 - Means 'now I obtained a little bit of magical ointment from the god of Phoenix Mountain,' indicating a source of healing.
    • 俊朝如其言涂之 - Means 'Junzhao applied it as instructed,' indicating compliance with the instructions.
    • 随手疮合 - Means 'the wound healed immediately,' indicating the effectiveness of the ointment.
    • 俊朝因留黄冠，烹鸡设食 - Means 'Junzhao then kept the Taoist priest, cooked chicken, and set a meal,' indicating hospitality.
    • 食讫，贳酒欲饮 - Means 'after eating, he bought wine to drink,' indicating a social activity.
    • 黄冠因啭喉高歌 - Means 'the Taoist priest then sang a high-pitched song,' indicating a performance.
    • 又为丝匏琼玉之音 - Means 'also played sounds of silk, gourds, and jade,' indicating musical instruments.
    • 罔不铿锵可爱 - Means 'all were resonant and lovely,' indicating the quality of the music.
    • 既而辞去，莫知所诣 - Means 'afterwards, he left, and no one knew where he went,' indicating the departure of the Taoist priest.
    • 时大定中也 - Means 'it was during the Dading period,' indicating the historical context.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黄冠 - Refers to a Taoist priest or monk, often depicted wearing a yellow hat.
    • 太一 - Refers to the Supreme Being in Taoist cosmology.
    • 汉江 - Refers to the Han River, a major river in China.
    • 鬼愁潭 - Refers to the Ghost Sorrow Pool, a mythical location.
    • 老蛟 - Refers to an old dragon, a common figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 凤 - Refers to the phoenix, a mythical bird symbolizing nobility and grace.
    • 大定 - Refers to the Dading period, a reign title of Emperor Shizong of Jin.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太一 - The Supreme Being in Taoist cosmology.
    • 汉江 - The Han River.
    • 鬼愁潭 - The Ghost Sorrow Pool.
    • 老蛟 - An old dragon.
    • 凤 - The phoenix.
    • 大定 - The Dading period.

    The next day, a Taoist priest knocked on the door and said, "I am the monkey that came out of the goiter yesterday. I am originally a spirit of a macaque, capable of summoning wind and rain. I frequently interacted with the old dragon in the Ghost Sorrow Pool of the Han River, often observing boats. When a ship approached, I made it capsize to seek food from it to feed my descendants. Yesterday, the Supreme Being punished the dragon and searched for accomplices, so I borrowed the neck of your wife, the Qiuqi, to hide my life. Though we are not directly related, it has also been a great burden. Now I obtained a little bit of magical ointment from the god of Phoenix Mountain. Please apply it as instructed, and it should heal immediately." Junzhao applied it as instructed, and the wound healed immediately. Junzhao then kept the Taoist priest, cooked chicken, and set a meal. After eating, he bought wine to drink, and the Taoist priest then sang a high-pitched song, also playing sounds of silk, gourds, and jade, all resonant and lovely. Afterwards, he left, and no one knew where he went. It was during the Dading period. [91]

    ○古元之
    　　
    　　后魏尚书令古弼族子元之，少养于弼，因饮酒而卒。弼怜之特甚，三日殓毕， 追思，欲与再别。因命斫棺，开已却生矣。元之云：
    　　
    　　当昏醉时，忽然如梦。有人沃冷水于体，仰视，乃见一神人衣冠绛裳霓帔， 仪貌甚伟。顾元之曰：“吾乃古说也，是汝远祖。适欲至和神国中，无人担囊侍 从，因来取汝。”即令负一大囊，可重一钧。又与一竹杖，长丈二余。令元之乘 骑随后，飞举甚速，常在半天，西南行，不知里数，山河逾远，欻然下地，已至 和神国。其国无大山，高者不过数十丈，皆积碧珉。石际生青彩簵筿，异花 珍果。软草香媚，好禽嘲哳。山顶皆平正如砥，清泉迸下者三二百道。原野无凡 树，悉生百果及相思、石榴之辈。 [92]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [92]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 追思 - To reflect on or remember, often used in a solemn or reflective context.
    • 欲与再别 - Wanted to say goodbye again, implying a desire to communicate with someone who is deceased.
    • 飞举甚速 - Flying up very quickly, describing rapid movement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后魏 - The Later Wei Dynasty, a period in Chinese history (386–534 AD).
    • 尚书令 - A high-ranking official in the imperial government, equivalent to a modern-day prime minister.
    • 和神国 - A mythical or spiritual realm, often associated with paradise or a place of peace and harmony.
    • 碧珉 - Green jade, a precious stone often used in Chinese literature to describe beautiful landscapes.
    • 相思 - A type of tree, often associated with love and longing in Chinese poetry.
    • 石榴 - Pomegranate, a fruit often used in Chinese literature to symbolize prosperity and fertility.

    Proper Nouns

    • 古元之 - A personal name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 古弼 - A historical figure, likely a prominent official during the Later Wei Dynasty.
    • 和神国 - A mythical or spiritual realm.
    • 碧珉 - Green jade.
    • 相思 - A type of tree.
    • 石榴 - Pomegranate.

    Gu Yuanzhi, a descendant of Gu Bi, the Chancellor of the Later Wei Dynasty, was raised by Gu Bi from a young age. One day, he died from drinking. Gu Bi was extremely saddened and, after three days of burial rites, began to reflect on him, wishing to say goodbye again. He then ordered the coffin to be chopped open. When it was opened, Gu Yuanzhi had come back to life. Gu Yuanzhi said:

    "At the time of being drunk, I suddenly felt as if I was in a dream. Someone poured cold water on my body. Looking up, I saw a divine figure in red robes and a colorful cloak, with an imposing appearance. The figure looked at me and said, 'I am Gu Shuo, your distant ancestor. I just wanted to go to the land of peace and spirits, but there was no one to carry the bag or accompany me, so I came to take you.' He then ordered me to carry a large bag, weighing about one catty. He also gave me a bamboo staff, about twelve feet long, and told me to ride after him. We flew up very quickly, always in mid-air, traveling southwest, not knowing the distance. The mountains and rivers became more distant, and suddenly we landed on the ground, having arrived at the land of peace and spirits. This country has no big mountains; the highest ones are no more than several dozen zhang high, all covered with green jade. Green and colorful bamboo grows at the edges of the stones, with strange flowers and precious fruits. Soft grass with a fragrant and charming scent, and beautiful birds chirping and chattering. The tops of the mountains are flat like grindstones, with clear springs gushing down in three or four hundred streams. The plains have no ordinary trees, all growing a hundred kinds of fruits and things like the xiangsi tree and pomegranates." [92]

    每果树花卉俱发，实色鲜红，映翠叶于香丛之 上，纷错满树，四时不敢，唯一岁一度暗换花实，更生新嫩，人不知觉。田畴尽 长大瓠，瓠中实以五谷，甘香珍美，非中国稻粱可比，人得足食，不假耕种。原 隰滋茂，莸秽不生，一年一度，树木枝干间悉生五色丝纩。人得随色收取，任意 纟任织。异锦纤罗，不假蚕杼。四时之气，常熙熙和淑，如中国二三月。无蚊、 虻、蟆、蚁、虱、蜂、蝎、蛇、虺、守宫、娱蚣、蛛蠓之虫，又无枭、鸱、鸦、 鹞、鸲、鹆、蝙蝠之属，及无虎、狼、豺、豹、狐狸、蓦驳之兽，又无猫、鼠、 猪、犬扰害之类。其人长短妍蚩皆等，无有嗜欲爱憎之者。 [93]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [93]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 俱发 - Means 'all bloom together,' describing the simultaneous flowering of trees and flowers.
    • 实色鲜红 - Describes the fruit as vividly red.
    • 映翠叶于香丛之上 - Literally 'reflecting on the green leaves among fragrant clusters,' used to describe the visual effect of the red fruit against the green foliage.
    • 四时不敢 - This phrase is somewhat ambiguous and could be interpreted as 'all seasons are not afraid' or 'all seasons are not timid,' but it is unclear without further context.
    • 唯一岁一度暗换花实 - Means 'only once a year, secretly changing flowers and fruits,' suggesting a hidden or subtle transformation.
    • 人不知觉 - Means 'people do not notice,' indicating that the change is imperceptible.
    • 田畴尽长大瓠 - Means 'all fields are filled with large gourds,' describing the abundance of gourds.
    • 瓠中实以五谷 - Means 'the gourds are filled with five grains,' indicating that the gourds contain various types of grains.
    • 甘香珍美 - Describes the grains as sweet, fragrant, and precious.
    • 非中国稻粱可比 - Means 'not comparable to the rice and grains of China,' emphasizing the superior quality of the grains.
    • 人得足食，不假耕种 - Means 'people have enough food, without the need for farming,' indicating self-sufficiency.
    • 原隰滋茂 - Means 'the plains and lowlands are lush and abundant,' describing the fertility of the land.
    • 莸秽不生 - Means 'noxious weeds do not grow,' indicating the absence of harmful plants.
    • 树木枝干间悉生五色丝纩 - Means 'between the branches and trunks of trees, all kinds of colored silk and cotton grow,' describing the growth of colorful fibers.
    • 人得随色收取，任意紡织 - Means 'people can collect them according to color, and weave them at will,' indicating the ease of harvesting and processing the fibers.
    • 异锦纤罗 - Describes the fabrics as exotic, fine, and delicate.
    • 不假蚕杼 - Means 'without the need for silkworms or looms,' emphasizing the natural origin of the fabrics.
    • 四时之气，常熙熙和淑 - Means 'the four seasons' air is always warm and pleasant,' describing the climate.
    • 如中国二三月 - Means 'like the air in China during February and March,' comparing the climate to a specific time in China.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中国 - Refers to China, the central and culturally significant region.
    • 稻粱 - Refers to rice and grains, which are staple foods in Chinese culture.
    • 五谷 - Refers to the five grains, traditionally including rice, wheat, millet, soybeans, and barley.
    • 原隰 - Refers to the natural landscape, with '原' meaning plains and '隰' meaning lowlands.
    • 五色丝纩 - Refers to the five colors of silk and cotton, which are often associated with traditional Chinese textiles.
    • 蚕杼 - Refers to silkworms and looms, which are essential for producing silk.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sequence '俱发，实色鲜红，映翠叶于香丛之上，纷错满树' describes the simultaneous blooming and fruiting of trees and flowers, the vivid color of the fruit, and the visual effect on the green leaves.
    • The sequence '四时不敢，唯一岁一度暗换花实，更生新嫩，人不知觉' is somewhat ambiguous and could be interpreted as describing a natural cycle where the flowers and fruits change subtly each year without people noticing.
    • The sequence '田畴尽长大瓠，瓠中实以五谷，甘香珍美，非中国稻粱可比' describes the abundance of large gourds filled with high-quality grains.
    • The clause '人得足食，不假耕种' emphasizes the self-sufficiency of the people.
    • The sequence '原隰滋茂，莸秽不生' describes the fertile and clean land.
    • The sequence '树木枝干间悉生五色丝纩，人得随色收取，任意紡织' describes the growth of colorful fibers and the ease of harvesting and processing them.
    • The clause '异锦纤罗，不假蚕杼' emphasizes the natural origin of the fabrics.
    • The sequence '四时之气，常熙熙和淑，如中国二三月' describes the pleasant climate.
    • The long list '无蚊、虻、蟆、蚁、虱、蜂、蝎、蛇、虺、守宫、娱蚣、蛛蠓之虫，又无枭、鸱、鸦、鹞、鸲、鹆、蝙蝠之属，及无虎、狼、豺、豹、狐狸、蓦驳之兽，又无猫、鼠、猪、犬扰害之类' describes the absence of various pests and animals.
    • The clause '其人长短妍蚩皆等，无有嗜欲爱憎之者' describes the uniformity and contentment of the people.

    Every tree and flower blooms together, with the fruit vividly red, reflecting on the green leaves among fragrant clusters, creating a colorful display on the trees. All seasons are not timid; only once a year, the flowers and fruits change subtly, and new, tender ones grow, but people do not notice. All fields are filled with large gourds, which are filled with five grains, sweet, fragrant, and precious, not comparable to the rice and grains of China. People have enough food, without the need for farming. The plains and lowlands are lush and abundant, with noxious weeds not growing. Between the branches and trunks of trees, all kinds of colored silk and cotton grow, and people can collect them according to color, and weave them at will. The fabrics are exotic, fine, and delicate, without the need for silkworms or looms. The four seasons' air is always warm and pleasant, like the air in China during February and March. There are no mosquitoes, flies, frogs, ants, lice, bees, scorpions, snakes, vipers, geckos, centipedes, or spiders. There are no owls, hawks, crows, falcons, sparrows, magpies, or bats. There are no tigers, wolves, jackals, leopards, foxes, or hyenas. There are no cats, mice, pigs, or dogs causing disturbance. The people are all of equal height and beauty, with no one having desires, love, or hatred. [93]

    人生二男二女，为邻 则世世为婚姻。笄年而嫁，二十而娶，人寿一百二十。中无夭折、疾病、瘖聋、 跛躄之患。百岁已下，皆自记忆；百岁已外，不知其寿几何。寿尽则欻然失其所 在，虽亲族子孙皆忘其人，故常无忧戚。每日午时一餐，中间唯食酒浆果实耳。 餐亦不知所化，不置溷所。人无私积囷仓，余粮栖亩，要者取之。无灌园鬻蔬， 野菜皆足人食。十亩有一酒泉，味甘而香。国人日相携游览歌咏，陶陶然，暮夜 而散，未尝昏醉。人人有俾仆，皆自然谨慎，知人所要，不烦促使。随意屋室， 靡不壮丽。 [94]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [94]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為鄰則世世為婚姻 - Social arrangement where being neighbors leads to generations of intermarriage.
    • 笄年而嫁，二十而娶 - Girls marry at 15 (笄年) and men marry at 20.
    • 餐亦不知所化，不置溷所 - Food is not digested in the usual sense, and there is no need for a toilet.
    • 人無私積囷倉，余糧栖亩，要者取之 - No private storage of grain; surplus grain is left in the fields for those who need it.
    • 國人日相携游览歌咏，陶陶然，暮夜而散，未嘗昏醉 - Citizens gather daily to stroll, sing, and enjoy themselves, dispersing at dusk without ever getting drunk.
    • 人人有俾仆，皆自然謹慎，知人所要，不煩促使 - Each person has a servant who is naturally cautious and anticipates their needs without being told.
    • 隨意屋室，靡不壯麗 - Houses are built according to one's will, all of which are magnificent.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 無灌園鬻蔬，野菜皆足人食 - Reflects a utopian ideal where natural abundance meets human need, and there is no commercial exchange for basic sustenance.
    • 十亩有一酒泉，味甘而香 - Concept of a naturally occurring source of wine suggests a paradise-like setting with abundant resources.

    In this society, every household has two sons and two daughters. Being neighbors leads to generations of intermarriage. Girls come of age at 15 and marry, while men marry at 20. People live to be 120 years old, free from premature death, illness, deafness, muteness, or lameness. Those under 100 years old have perfect memory, but those over 100 lose track of their age. When life ends, one disappears suddenly without trace, and even close relatives and descendants forget the deceased. They eat one meal a day at noon, consisting mainly of wine, fruit juice, and fruits. The food is not digested in the usual sense, and there is no need for a toilet. There is no private storage of grain; surplus grain is left in the fields for those who need it. There is no market for vegetables; wild plants are sufficient for food. Every ten acres has a spring of wine that is sweet and fragrant. Citizens gather daily to stroll, sing, and enjoy themselves, dispersing at dusk without ever getting drunk. Each person has a servant who is naturally cautious and anticipates their needs without being told. Houses are built according to one's will, all of which are magnificent. [94]

    其国六畜唯有马，驯极而骏，不用刍秣，自食野草，不近积聚。人要 乘则乘，乘讫而却放。亦无主守。其国千官皆足，而仕官不自知身之在仕，杂于 下人，以无职事操断也。虽有君主，而君不自知为君，杂于千官，以无职事升贬 故也。又无迅雷风雨，其风常微轻如煦，袭万物不至于摇落；其雨十日一降，降 必以夜，津润条畅，不至地有淹流。一国之人，皆自相亲，有如戚属，人各相惠 多与。无市易商贩之事，以不求利故也。古说既至其国，顾谓元之曰：“此和神 国也。虽非神仙，风俗不恶。汝回，当为世人说之。吾既至此，回既别求人负囊， 不用汝矣。”因以酒令元之饮，饮满数巡，不觉沉醉。既而复醒，身已活矣。 [95]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [95]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 驯极而骏 - Extremely well-trained and swift, describing the exceptional quality of the horses.
    • 津润条畅 - Moistening and refreshing, describing the beneficial effects of the rain.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 和神国 - A harmonious and divine country, a concept often found in classical Chinese literature to describe idealized states.
    • 元之 - Refers to Yuan Zhi, a historical figure or a character in the narrative.
    • 古说 - An ancient saying or legend, often used to introduce cultural or historical references.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元之 - Refers to Yuan Zhi, a historical figure or a character in the narrative.
    • 和神国 - A harmonious and divine country, a concept often found in classical Chinese literature to describe idealized states.
    • 古说 - An ancient saying or legend, often used to introduce cultural or historical references.

    The country raises only horses among its six livestock. These horses are extremely well-trained and swift, requiring no fodder as they feed themselves on wild grass and do not approach stored grain. People ride them when they need to, and after riding, they are released. There are no owners or caretakers. All officials are sufficient, but they do not know they are officials, mixed among common people because there are no duties to manage. Even though there is a king, the king does not know he is a king, mixed among the officials because there are no duties to promote or demote. The country also lacks sudden thunderstorms or heavy rains; the wind is always gentle and warm, not shaking or damaging things. It rains once every ten days, always at night, moistening and refreshing the land without causing flooding. All the people in the country are naturally close to each other, like family members, and generously help one another. There are no market transactions or trade activities because they do not seek profit. An ancient saying has reached the country, and the speaker turned to Yuan Zhi and said, "This is a harmonious and divine country. Though it is not a land of immortals, the customs are not bad. You return, and you should tell the world about it. I have already arrived here, and Yuan Zhi will find someone else to carry the bag; I will not use you anymore." He gave Yuan Zhi wine to drink, and after several rounds, Yuan Zhi did not realize he was drunk. Afterward, he woke up, and his body was alive. [95]

    自是元之疏逸人事，都忘宦情，游行山水，自号知和子，后竟不知其所终也。
    　　
    　　○卢公焕
    　　
    　　黄门侍郎卢公焕，为明州刺史，属邑象山县，溪谷迥无人处，有盗发墓者云： 初见车辙中有花砖，因揭之，知是古冢墓。乃结十人于县投状，请路旁居止，县 尹允之。遂种麻，令外人无所见。即悉力发掘，入其隧路，渐至圹中，有三石门， 皆以铁封之。
    　　
    　　其盗先能诵咒，因斋戒禁之。翌日，两门开，每门中各有铜人铜马数百，持 执干戈，其制精巧。盗又斋戒三日，中门一扇开，有黄衣人出，传语曰：“汉征 南将军刘使来相闻，某生有征伐大勋，及死，敕令护葬及铸铜人马等，以象存日 仪卫。奉计来此，必要财货，所居之室，实无他物，且官葬不瘗货宝，何必苦以 神咒相侵，若更不见已，尝不免两损。”言讫却入，门复合如初。 [96]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [96]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自是 - Classical connective meaning 'from this time on' or 'since then.'
    • 疏逸人事 - Refers to distancing oneself from worldly affairs, often implying a retreat from officialdom or society.
    • 都忘宦情 - Means 'completely forgot about the desire for office,' indicating a complete disengagement from political ambitions.
    • 游行山水 - Literally 'wandering among mountains and waters,' referring to traveling and enjoying nature.
    • 自号知和子 - Means 'gave himself the name Zhihezi,' where '自号' (self-named) indicates a personal title given by the individual.
    • 后竟不知其所终也 - Means 'later, no one knew what happened to him,' indicating an unknown or uncertain fate.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元之 - Refers to Yuan Zhi, a historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 卢公焕 - Refers to Lu Gonghuan, another historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 黄门侍郎 - A high-ranking official position in the imperial court, typically responsible for attending to the emperor's personal needs and advising on state affairs.
    • 明州刺史 - Refers to the position of governor of Mingzhou (modern-day Ningbo, Zhejiang).
    • 象山县 - A county in Zhejiang province, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 汉征南将军刘使 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a general who was granted the title 'Zhengnan' (Southwest Campaigner) by the Han dynasty.
    • 敕令 - Means 'imperial decree' or 'royal command,' indicating an order from the emperor.
    • 护葬 - Refers to the protection of a tomb or grave.
    • 铜人铜马 - Refers to bronze statues of people and horses, often used as tomb guardians or symbols of military power.
    • 斋戒 - Refers to a period of fasting and abstinence, often practiced before religious or important events.
    • 隧路 - Refers to the entrance or passage leading into a tomb.
    • 圹中 - Refers to the interior of a tomb.
    • 石门 - Refers to stone doors or gates, often used in tombs.
    • 铁封 - Refers to sealing with iron, indicating a strong and secure closure.
    • 神咒 - Refers to magical or divine incantations, often used in rituals or to ward off evil spirits.
    • 官葬 - Refers to official burial, often implying a formal and regulated process.

    Proper Nouns

    • 知和子 - A self-given title by Yuan Zhi.
    • 县尹 - The local official in charge of the county.
    • 刘使 - A historical figure, likely a general.

    Since then, Yuan Zhi distanced himself from worldly affairs, completely forgot about the desire for office, and wandered among mountains and waters, giving himself the name Zhihezi. Later, no one knew what happened to him.

    Lu Gonghuan, a Yellow Gate Attendant, served as the Governor of Mingzhou. In the county of Xiangshan, there was a place in the remote valleys where bandits were digging up a tomb. They reported:

    "At first, we saw flower bricks in the wheel tracks. We uncovered them and found that it was an ancient tomb. We then gathered ten people and went to the county to submit a petition, requesting to stay by the roadside. The county magistrate agreed. We then planted hemp to hide our activities. We then worked hard to excavate, entering the tunnel, gradually reaching the interior of the tomb, where there were three stone doors, all sealed with iron.

    The bandits could recite spells and fasted to purify themselves. The next day, two doors opened, and in each door, there were hundreds of bronze figures and horses holding spears and halberds, the craftsmanship being exquisite. The bandits fasted for three more days, and one of the middle doors opened. A man in yellow robes appeared and said:

    'General Liu of the Han dynasty's Southern Expedition has come to inform you. He had great military achievements in life, and upon his death, the emperor ordered his tomb to be protected and bronze figures and horses to be cast to represent his ceremonial guards. We have come here, but you must seek treasures. The room we occupy has nothing else, and official burials do not bury valuables. Why bother to use divine spells to intrude? If you do not leave, both sides will suffer.' After speaking, he returned, and the door closed as before." [96]

    盗又诵咒数日不已，门开，一青衣又出传语，盗弗允说，两扇欻辟，大水漂 荡，盗皆溺死。一盗解泅而出，自缚诣官，具说本末。黄门令覆视其墓，其中门 内有一石床，骸枕之类，水漂已半垂于下，因却为封两门，窒其隧路矣。
    　　
    　　○吴全素
    　　
    　　吴全素，苏州人，举孝廉，五上不第。元和十二年，寓居长安永兴里。十二 月十三日夜既卧，见二人白衣执简，若贡院引牌来召者，全素曰：“礼闱引试， 分甲有期，何烦夜引？”使者固邀，不得已而下床随行，不觉过子城，出开远门 二百步，正北行，有路阔二尺已来，此外尽目深泥。见丈夫妇人，捽之者，拽倒 者，枷杻者，锁身者，连裾者，僧者，道者，囊盛其头者，面缚者，散驱行者， 数百辈皆行泥中，独全素行平路。约数里。入城郭见官府，同列者千余人，军吏 佩刀者分部其人，率五十人为一引，引过，全素在第三引中。 [97]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [97]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 盗又诵咒数日不已 - Literally 'the thief continued reciting spells for several days,' indicating persistence in an act.
    • 门开，一青衣又出传语 - 'The door opened, and a person in green clothes came out to convey a message,' where '青衣' is a metaphorical term for a messenger or attendant.
    • 盗弗允说 - 'The thief did not agree to the terms,' where '弗允说' is a formal expression of refusal.
    • 两扇欻辟 - 'The two doors suddenly opened,' where '欻辟' is an onomatopoeic term for a sudden, forceful opening.
    • 大水漂荡，盗皆溺死 - 'A great flood surged, drowning all the thieves,' where '漂荡' vividly describes the chaotic movement of water.
    • 一盗解泅而出 - 'One thief managed to swim out,' where '解泅' is a technical term for swimming out of a difficult situation.
    • 自缚诣官，具说本末 - 'He tied himself up and went to the authorities, detailing the whole story,' where '具说本末' is a formal expression for providing a complete account.
    • 黄门令覆视其墓 - 'The Huangmen Ling (a high-ranking official) inspected the tomb,' where '覆视' means to carefully examine.
    • 其中门内有一石床，骸枕之类 - 'Inside the inner gate, there was a stone bed, with bones and pillows,' where '骸枕之类' refers to remnants of burial objects.
    • 水漂已半垂于下 - 'The water had carried it halfway down,' where '垂于下' describes the partial descent due to the flood.
    • 因却为封两门，窒其隧路矣 - 'Therefore, the two gates were sealed, and the tunnel was blocked,' where '窒其隧路' means to block the passage.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黄门令 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial court, specifically the Huangmen Ling, who was responsible for guarding the emperor’s palace and handling important matters.
    • 墓 - Refers to a tomb or burial site, which in this context is a supernatural or magical place.
    • 青衣 - A term for a messenger or attendant, often dressed in green, symbolizing a lower-ranking official or servant.
    • 诵咒 - The act of reciting spells or incantations, often associated with magical or supernatural powers.
    • 水漂荡 - A vivid description of a flood, which in this context is likely a supernatural event.

    Proper Nouns

    • 黄门令 - The title of a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 墓 - The term for a tomb or burial site, which in this context is a supernatural location.

    The thief continued reciting spells for several days without ceasing. The door opened, and a person in green clothes came out to convey a message. The thief did not agree to the terms, and the two doors suddenly opened. A great flood surged, drowning all the thieves. One thief managed to swim out, tied himself up, and went to the authorities, detailing the whole story. The Huangmen Ling carefully examined the tomb. Inside the inner gate, there was a stone bed, with bones and pillows. The water had carried it halfway down, and therefore, the two gates were sealed, and the tunnel was blocked.

    ○ Wu Quansu

    Wu Quansu, a native of Suzhou, was recommended as a filial and honest official but failed five times in the imperial examinations. In the twelfth year of the Yuanhe era, he was staying in Yongxing Lane in Chang'an. On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, after he had gone to bed, he saw two men in white robes holding tablets, as if they were officials from the examination hall summoning him. Wu Quansu said, "The imperial examinations are divided into categories with a set time, so why bother to summon at night?" The messengers insisted, and Wu Quansu, reluctantly, got out of bed and followed them. He did not realize that they had passed through the inner city and exited the Kaiyuan Gate after two hundred steps, traveling north on a road about two feet wide, with deep mud everywhere else. He saw men and women being dragged, pulled down, shackled, bound, with their clothes torn, monks, Taoists, heads in sacks, hands tied behind their backs, and hundreds of people being driven through the mud, while Wu Quansu alone walked on level ground. After walking about three miles, he entered a city where he saw an official building, with over a thousand people lined up, and military officers with swords dividing the people into groups of fifty. As each group passed, Wu Quansu was in the third group. [97]

    其正衙有大殿，当 中设床几，一人衣绯而坐，左右立吏数十人，衙吏点名，便判付司狱者，付硙司 狱者，付鑛狱者，付汤狱者，付火狱者，付案者。闻其付狱者，方悟身死。见 四十九人皆点付讫，独全素在，因问其人曰：“当衙者何官？”曰：“判官也。” 遂诉曰：“全素恭履儒道，年禄未终，不合死。”判官曰：“冥司案牍，一一分 明。据籍帖追，岂合妄诉！”全素曰：“审知年命末尽，今请对验命籍。”乃命 取吴郡户籍到，检得吴全素，元和十三年明经出身，其后三年衣食，亦无官禄。 判官曰：“人世三年，才同瞬息，且无荣禄，何必却回！既去即来，徒烦案牍。” 全素曰：“辞亲五载，得归即荣，何况成名尚余三载，伏乞哀察。”判官曰： “任归。”仍诫引者曰：“此人命薄，宜令速去。稍以延迟，即突明矣。”引者 受命，即与同行。出门外，羡而泣者不可胜纪。 [98]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [98]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 其正衙有大殿 - Refers to the main hall or central administrative building in ancient Chinese government.
    • 当 中设床几 - Describes the arrangement of furniture in the hall, with beds and tables set up in the center.
    • 一人衣绯而坐 - Means a person wearing red robes, which was a sign of high status or authority, sitting in the center.
    • 左右立吏数十人 - Describes dozens of officials standing on both sides of the person in red robes.
    • 衙吏点名 - Means officials in the administrative office calling out names.
    • 便判付司狱者 - Means immediately judging and sending to the prison officials.
    • 付硙司 狱者，付鑛狱者，付汤狱者，付火狱者，付案者 - Describes different types of punishments or assignments to various prison officials or departments.
    • 闻其付狱者，方悟身死 - Means hearing that he is sent to prison, only then realizes he is dead.
    • 见 四十九人皆点付讫，独全素在 - Means seeing that all forty-nine people have been called out and assigned, and only Quansu remains.
    • 因问其人曰：“当衙者何官？” - Means asking the person what is the position of the person in charge of the hall.
    • 曰：“判官也。” - Means saying he is a judge.
    • 遂诉曰：“全素恭履儒道，年禄未终，不合死。” - Means then complaining that Quansu has always followed the Confucian path with respect, his years of service are not yet complete, and he should not die.
    • 判官曰：“冥司案牍，一一分明。据籍帖追，岂合妄诉！” - Means the judge saying that records in the underworld are clearly detailed, and according to the records, he is summoned, so how can you falsely accuse?
    • 全素曰：“审知年命末尽，今请对验命籍。” - Means Quansu saying if it is true that his years of life are not yet finished, he requests to verify the records now.
    • 乃命取吴郡户籍到，检得吴全素，元和十三年明经出身，其后三年衣食，亦无官禄。 - Means then ordered to bring the household register of Wu Prefecture, found Quansu, who passed the imperial examination in 818 as a scholar, and in the following three years, he had no official salary.
    • 判官曰：“人世三年，才同瞬息，且无荣禄，何必却回！既去即来，徒烦案牍。” - Means the judge saying that three years in the world are like a blink of an eye, and he has no honor or salary, so why return? Once gone, he will come back, and it will only complicate the records.
    • 全素曰：“辞亲五载，得归即荣，何况成名尚余三载，伏乞哀察。” - Means Quansu saying he left his parents for five years, returning would be honorable, not to mention that he still has three more years to achieve fame, and he humbly asks for your pity and consideration.
    • 判官曰：“任归。”仍诫引者曰：“此人命薄，宜令速去。稍以延迟，即突明矣。” - Means the judge saying go ahead, then warned the guide that this person has a short life, should be sent away quickly, and if delayed, dawn will break soon.
    • 引者受命，即与同行。出门外，羡而泣者不可胜纪。 - Means the guide accepting the order and leading him away, out of the hall, and those who envied and cried could not be counted.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 正衙 - The main hall or central administrative building in ancient Chinese government.
    • 绯 - Red robes, often worn by high-ranking officials or judges.
    • 冥司 - The underworld, often used in stories about the afterlife and judgment.
    • 元和十三年 - The 13th year of the Yuanhe era (818 AD) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 明经出身 - Passing the imperial examination as a scholar, which was a significant achievement in ancient China.
    • 判官 - A judge or official responsible for legal matters in the underworld.
    • 案牍 - Records or documents, often used in administrative and legal contexts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吴郡 - Wu Prefecture, an administrative region in ancient China.
    • 元和 - The era name of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (806-820 AD).
    • 明经 - A type of imperial examination for scholars.
    • 判官 - Judge or official in the underworld.
    • 案牍 - Records or documents.

    In the main hall, there was a grand hall, with beds and tables set up in the center. A man wearing red robes was seated, and dozens of officials stood on both sides. The hall officials called out names, and the judge immediately judged and sent people to the prison officials, to the prison of grinding, to the prison of mining, to the prison of boiling, to the prison of fire, and to the prison of case. Hearing that he was sent to prison, he realized he was dead. Seeing that all forty-nine people had been called out and assigned, only Quansu remained. He asked the person, "What is the position of the person in charge of the hall?" The person replied, "He is a judge." Quansu then complained, "I have always followed the Confucian path with respect, and my years of service are not yet complete. I should not die." The judge said, "The records in the underworld are clearly detailed. According to the records, he is summoned. How can you falsely accuse?" Quansu said, "If it is true that his years of life are not yet finished, I request to verify the records now." The judge then ordered to bring the household register of Wu Prefecture, and found Quansu, who had passed the imperial examination in 818 as a scholar. In the following three years, he had no official salary. The judge said, "Three years in the world are like a blink of an eye, and he has no honor or salary. Why return? Once gone, he will come back, and it will only complicate the records." Quansu said, "I left my parents for five years. Returning would be honorable. Not to mention that he still has three more years to achieve fame. I humbly ask for your pity and consideration." The judge said, "Go ahead." He then warned the guide, "This person has a short life. Should be sent away quickly. If delayed, dawn will break soon." The guide accepted the order and led him away. Out of the hall, those who envied and cried could not be counted. [98]

    既出其城，不复见泥矣。复至开远门，二吏谓全素曰：“君命甚薄，突明即 归不得，见判官之命乎？我皆贫，各惠钱五十万，即无虑矣。”全素曰：“远客 又贫，如何可致？”吏曰：“从母之夫，居宣阳为户部吏者甚富，一言可致也。” 既同诣其家，二吏不肯上阶，全素入告，其家方食煎饼，全素至灯前拱曰：“阿 姨万福！”又曰：“姨夫安和！”又不应。乃以手笼灯，满堂皆暗。姨夫曰： “何不抛少物？夜食香物，鬼神便合恼人。”全素既憾其不应，又目为鬼神，意 颇忿之。青衣有执食者，其面正当，因以力掌之，应手而倒，家人竞来拔发喷水， 呼唤良久方悟。全素既言情不得，下阶问二吏，吏曰：“固然，君未还生，非鬼 而何。鬼语而人不闻，笼灯行掌，诚足以骇之。 [99]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [99]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 既出其城，不复见泥矣 - Indicates a transition from difficulty to ease; after leaving the city, the mud (or metaphorical obstacles) is no longer visible.
    • 复至开远门 - Returning to the 'Kaiyuan Gate,' which is likely a significant location in the narrative.
    • 君命甚薄 - The command or fate given to the person is meager or insufficient.
    • 突明即归不得 - If the person returns suddenly at dawn, they will not be able to return.
    • 见判官之命乎 - Asks if the person has seen the order from the 'Judge' (likely a supernatural or official authority).
    • 各惠钱五十万 - Each person gives 500,000 coins as a gift or bribe.
    • 从母之夫 - Refers to the husband of the person's mother, indicating a familial relationship.
    • 居宣阳为户部吏者甚富 - Describes a wealthy official in the Ministry of Finance who lives in Xuanyang.
    • 一言可致也 - A single word or request can achieve the desired result.
    • 满堂皆暗 - The room becomes completely dark.
    • 何不抛少物 - Suggests throwing something small to appease the spirits or ghosts.
    • 夜食香物，鬼神便合恼人 - Eating fragrant things at night will anger the spirits or ghosts.
    • 青衣有执食者 - A servant holding food.
    • 因以力掌之 - Striking the servant with force.
    • 应手而倒 - The servant falls immediately.
    • 家人竞来拔发喷水 - Family members rush to pull out hair and splash water.
    • 呼唤良久方悟 - The person was called for a long time before they regained consciousness.
    • 君未还生，非鬼而何 - If the person has not returned to life, they must be a ghost.
    • 鬼语而人不闻 - The ghost speaks, but no one else can hear.
    • 笼灯行掌 - Covering the lamp and striking, which is used to create a sense of fear or confusion.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 开远门 - A specific gate in a city, possibly with historical or symbolic significance.
    • 判官 - A judge or official in the supernatural realm, often associated with the underworld or afterlife.
    • 户部吏 - An official in the Ministry of Finance, indicating a high-ranking position.
    • 宣阳 - A place name, possibly a district or region.
    • 青衣 - A servant or low-ranking official, often dressed in green or blue.

    Proper Nouns

    • 开远门 - The Kaiyuan Gate.
    • 判官 - The Judge (likely a supernatural authority).
    • 宣阳 - Xuanyang (a place name).
    • 青衣 - The servant or low-ranking official.

    After leaving the city, the mud (or metaphorical obstacles) was no longer visible. Returning to the Kaiyuan Gate, two officials told Quansu, "Your command is very meager; if you return suddenly at dawn, you will not be able to return. Have you seen the order from the Judge? We are all poor, so each of us will give you 500,000 coins, and you will have no worries." Quansu replied, "I am a distant guest and also poor; how can I accept such a gift?" The officials said, "The husband of your mother, who lives in Xuanyang and is a wealthy official in the Ministry of Finance, can achieve this with just one word." They then went together to his home. The two officials refused to go up the steps, and Quansu entered to inform him. The household was just eating fried pancakes when Quansu bowed before the lamp and said, "Auntie, may you be blessed with longevity!" He then said, "Uncle, may you be at peace!" But there was no response. Quansu then covered the lamp with his hand, and the entire room became dark. The uncle said, "Why don't you throw something small? Eating fragrant things at night will anger the spirits." Quansu, already frustrated by the lack of response, saw the situation as ghostly and became quite angry. A servant holding food was directly in front of him, so Quansu struck him with force, and the servant fell immediately. The family rushed to pull out his hair and splash water on him, calling him for a long time before he regained consciousness. Quansu, having failed to express his feelings, went down the steps to ask the two officials. The officials said, "Indeed, you have not returned to life; you must be a ghost. Ghosts speak, but no one else can hear. Covering the lamp and striking, this is certainly enough to frighten people." [99]

    ”曰：“然则何以言事？”曰： “以吾唾涂人大门，一家睡；涂人中门，门内人睡；涂堂门，满堂人睡。可以手 承吾唾而涂之。”全素掬手，二吏交唾。逡巡掬手以涂堂门。才华，满堂欠伸， 促去食器，遂入寝。二吏曰：“君入，去床三尺立言之。慎勿近床，以手摇动， 则魇不悟矣。”全素依其言言之，其姨惊起，泣谓夫曰：“全素晚来归宿，何忽 致死。今者见梦求钱，言有所遗，如何？”其夫曰：“忧念外甥，偶为热梦，何 足遽信！”又寝，又梦，惊起而泣，求纸于柜，适有二百幅，乃令遽剪焚之，火 绝，则千缗宛在地矣。二吏曰：“钱数多，某固不能胜。而君之力，生人之力也。 可以尽举，请负以致寄之。”全素初以为难，试以两手上承，自肩挑之，巍巍然 极高，其实甚轻，乃引行寄介公庙，主人者紫衣腰金，敕吏受之。 [100]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [100]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 曰：“然則何以言事？”曰： - Direct dialogue format in Classical Chinese, where '曰' means 'said' or 'answered.' The phrase '然則何以言事' can be translated as 'Then how can one discuss matters?'
    • 以吾唾涂人大门，一家睡；涂人中门，门内人睡；涂堂门，满堂人睡。 - Metaphorical expression using the action of spitting on doors to illustrate the effect of spreading information or influence. '涂' means 'to smear' or 'to apply,' and '大门,' '中门,' and '堂门' refer to the outer gate, inner gate, and main hall door, respectively.
    • 可以手承吾唾而涂之。 - One can use one's hand to catch my spittle and apply it, continuing the metaphor of spreading influence.
    • 全素掬手，二吏交唾。 - '掬手' means 'to clench one's hands,' and '交唾' means 'to spit alternately.' This describes the action of two officials spitting into the hands of Quansu.
    • 逡巡掬手以涂堂门。 - '逡巡' means 'to hesitate' or 'to pause,' and '掬手以涂堂门' means 'to clench one's hands and apply it to the main hall door.'
    • 满堂欠伸，促去食器，遂入寝。 - '欠伸' means 'to yawn and stretch,' and '促去食器' means 'to quickly remove the food dishes.' This describes the reaction of the people in the hall.
    • 二吏曰：“君入，去床三尺立言之。慎勿近床，以手摇动，则魇不悟矣。” - '去床三尺' means 'three feet away from the bed,' and '以手摇动' means 'to shake with one's hand.' This is a warning to avoid disturbing the sleeper.
    • 全素依其言言之，其姨惊起，泣谓夫曰：“全素晚来归宿，何忽致死。今者见梦求钱，言有所遗，如何？” - '依其言言之' means 'to speak according to what was said,' and '见梦求钱' means 'to appear in a dream to ask for money.' This describes the aunt's reaction to the dream.
    • 又寝，又梦，惊起而泣，求纸于柜，适有二百幅，乃令遽剪焚之，火绝，则千缗宛在地矣。 - '遽剪焚之' means 'to quickly cut and burn it,' and '火绝' means 'the fire goes out.' This describes the process of burning the paper.
    • 二吏曰：“钱数多，某固不能胜。而君之力，生人之力也。可以尽举，请负以致寄之。” - '生人之力也' means 'the strength of a living person,' and '请负以致寄之' means 'please carry it to deliver it.' This is a request for help in carrying the money.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 全素 - The name of the protagonist, Quansu, who is involved in the story.
    • 二吏 - The names of the two officials who are part of the narrative.
    • 介公庙 - The Temple of Jie Gong, a historical figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with protection and wealth.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '以吾唾涂人大门，一家睡；涂人中门，门内人睡；涂堂门，满堂人睡。' uses a series of parallel constructions to describe the effect of spitting on different doors. The structure is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the parallelism.
