    • The sentence '可以手承吾唾而涂之。' is a continuation of the previous one, describing the action of catching and applying the spittle. The structure is straightforward but requires attention to the metaphorical meaning.
    • The sentence '二吏曰：“君入，去床三尺立言之。慎勿近床，以手摇动，则魇不悟矣。”' contains a warning with a conditional structure. The syntax is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the warning's tone.
    • The sentence '全素依其言言之，其姨惊起，泣谓夫曰：“全素晚来归宿，何忽致死。今者见梦求钱，言有所遗，如何？”' describes the aunt's reaction to the dream. The syntax is clear but requires attention to the emotional tone.
    • The sentence '又寝，又梦，惊起而泣，求纸于柜，适有二百幅，乃令遽剪焚之，火绝，则千缗宛在地矣。' describes the process of burning the paper. The syntax is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the sequence of events.
    • The sentence '二吏曰：“钱数多，某固不能胜。而君之力，生人之力也。可以尽举，请负以致寄之。”' contains a request for help. The syntax is clear but requires attention to the polite tone.

    Proper Nouns

    • 全素 - The name of the protagonist.
    • 二吏 - The names of the two officials.
    • 介公庙 - The Temple of Jie Gong.

    "Asked, 'Then how can one discuss matters?' The response was: 'By applying my spittle to the main gate, the entire household falls asleep; by applying it to the inner gate, those inside fall asleep; by applying it to the hall gate, everyone in the hall falls asleep. One can use one's hands to catch my spittle and apply it.' Quansu clenched his hands, and the two officials spat alternately. After hesitating, he clenched his hands and applied it to the hall gate. Suddenly, everyone in the hall yawned and stretched, quickly removing their food dishes, and then went to sleep. The two officials said, 'When you enter, stand three feet away from the bed to speak. Be careful not to get too close to the bed, and do not shake it with your hand, or else the sleeper will not wake up.' Quansu spoke according to what was said, and his aunt was startled awake, crying and telling her husband, 'Quansu came home late tonight, and suddenly he died. Now he appears in a dream asking for money, saying he left something behind. What should we do?' Her husband replied, 'He was just worried about his nephew and had a vivid dream. Why should we believe it so quickly!' She went back to sleep, and again she had a dream, startling awake and crying, she asked for paper from the cabinet, and there happened to be two hundred sheets. She then ordered them to be quickly cut and burned, and when the fire went out, a thousand strings of cash appeared on the ground. The two officials said, 'There is too much money, I cannot carry it. But your strength, the strength of a living person, can bear it all. Please carry it and deliver it to the Temple of Jie Gong.' Quansu initially thought it was difficult, but he tried using both hands to support it, and when he lifted it with his shoulders, it seemed extremely high, but it was actually very light. He then carried it to the Temple of Jie Gong, where the owner, dressed in purple and wearing a gold belt, ordered the officials to receive it." [100]

    寄毕，二吏曰：“君之还生必矣，且思便归，为亦有所见耶？今欲取一人送 之受生，能略观否？”全素曰：“固所愿也。”乃相引入西市绢行南尽人家，灯 火荧煌，呜呜而泣，数僧当门读经，香烟满户。二吏不敢近，乃从堂后檐上，让 当寝床，有抽瓦折椽，开一大穴。穴中下视，一老人气息奄然，相向而泣者周其 床。一吏出怀中绳。大如指，长二丈余，令全素安坐执之，一头垂于穴中，诫全 素曰：“吾寻取彼人，人来，当掣绳。”遂出绳下之，而以右手捽老人，左手掣 绳，全素遽掣出之，拽于堂前，以绳囚缚。二吏更荷而出，相顾曰：“何处有屠 案最大？”其一曰：“布政坊十字街南王家案最大。”乃相与往焉。既到，投老 人于案上，脱衣缠身，更上推扑。 [101]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [101]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 寄畢 - Literally means 'after sending off,' but in this context, it likely refers to a ritual or action that has been completed, possibly related to a spiritual or ceremonial process.
    • 便歸 - Means 'to return easily' or 'to return conveniently,' suggesting a smooth or effortless return.
    • 受生 - Refers to the concept of receiving life or being reborn, often in a spiritual or metaphysical context.
    • 燈火荧煌 - Describes a scene of flickering or dim lights, often associated with a mysterious or eerie atmosphere.
    • 呜呜而泣 - Means 'to weep softly' or 'to sob,' often used to describe a subdued or mournful sound.
    • 读经 - Means 'to recite or read scriptures,' typically referring to religious or spiritual texts.
    • 香烟满户 - Describes a house filled with incense smoke, often associated with religious or spiritual rituals.
    • 当门 - Means 'at the door' or 'in front of the door,' indicating a position near the entrance.
    • 堂后檐上 - Refers to the eaves or roof overhang behind the main hall, a common architectural feature in traditional Chinese buildings.
    • 寝床 - Refers to a bed or sleeping area, often used in the context of a home or living space.
    • 抽瓦折椽 - Describes the act of pulling out tiles and breaking beams, likely referring to the process of creating a hole in the roof or ceiling.
    • 奄然 - Means 'faintly' or 'weakly,' often used to describe someone who is on the verge of death or in a state of extreme weakness.
    • 掣绳 - Means 'to pull the rope,' often used in the context of a signal or command.
    • 拽于堂前 - Means 'dragged in front of the hall,' indicating a physical action of moving someone or something to a specific location.
    • 囚缚 - Means 'to bind or restrain,' often used in the context of capturing or controlling someone.
    • 荷而出 - Means 'to carry out,' indicating the act of taking someone or something out of a place.
    • 何处有屠案最大 - Means 'where is the largest slaughterhouse?' or 'where is the biggest place for butchering?' This question suggests a search for a specific location.
    • 布政坊十字街南王家案最大 - Refers to a specific location: the slaughterhouse of Wang's family, located at the south end of the cross street in the Bu Zheng坊 (a district in an ancient city).
    • 脱衣缠身 - Means 'to remove clothing and tie it around the body,' often used in the context of preparing someone for a ritual or execution.
    • 推扑 - Means 'to push and strike,' often used in the context of a violent or forceful action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 全素 - Likely the name of a character, possibly a scholar, official, or spiritual practitioner, though no specific historical figure is known by this name.
    • 二吏 - Refers to two officials or servants, likely associated with a spiritual or administrative role.
    • 受生 - The concept of receiving life or being reborn is central to many Chinese spiritual and religious practices, including Buddhism and Taoism.
    • 西市绢行 - The Western Market was a common location in ancient Chinese cities, often associated with commerce and trade.
    • 读经 - Reciting or reading scriptures is a common practice in Chinese religious traditions, particularly Buddhism and Confucianism.
    • 香烟满户 - Incense burning is a significant part of Chinese religious rituals, symbolizing purification and communication with the divine.
    • 堂后檐上 - The architectural description reflects traditional Chinese building practices, where eaves and roof overhangs were common features.
    • 寝床 - The mention of a bed or sleeping area suggests a domestic or personal setting, possibly within a temple or spiritual retreat.
    • 抽瓦折椽 - The act of pulling out tiles and breaking beams is a physical action that could be associated with creating a passage or escape route, possibly in a spiritual or ritual context.
    • 奄然 - The description of someone who is faint or weak is often used in Chinese literature to describe a dying person or someone in a state of extreme vulnerability.
    • 掣绳 - Pulling a rope is a common signal or command in many contexts, including military, spiritual, or ceremonial practices.
    • 拽于堂前 - Dragging someone in front of a hall suggests a public or formal action, possibly in a ritual or judicial context.
    • 囚缚 - Binding or restraining someone is a common action in both physical and spiritual contexts, often associated with punishment or control.
    • 荷而出 - Carrying someone out of a place suggests a physical action, possibly in the context of a ritual or execution.
    • 屠案 - A slaughterhouse is a place where animals are killed for meat, often associated with violence and ritual sacrifice.
    • 布政坊十字街南王家案最大 - The specific location of the largest slaughterhouse suggests a detailed knowledge of the city's layout and landmarks.
    • 脱衣缠身 - Removing clothing and tying it around the body is a common preparation for a ritual or execution, often associated with a sense of vulnerability or submission.
    • 推扑 - Pushing and striking is a violent action, often used in the context of a physical confrontation or execution.

    After completing the ritual, the two officials said, "Your return to life is certain. Consider returning easily, for is there also something to be seen? Now we wish to take one person to be reborn, can you briefly observe?" Quansu replied, "It is indeed my wish." They then led him to the southern end of the silk shop in the Western Market, where the houses were dimly lit with flickering lights and soft weeping. Several monks were reciting scriptures at the door, and the house was filled with incense smoke. The two officials dared not approach, so they climbed onto the eaves behind the main hall, making it the sleeping area. They pulled out tiles and broke beams, creating a large hole. Looking down into the hole, they saw an elderly man faintly breathing, with those weeping around his bed. One official took out a rope from his chest, as thick as a finger and over two zhang long, and instructed Quansu to sit calmly and hold it, with one end hanging into the hole. He warned Quansu, "I will seek out that person. When the person comes, pull the rope." He then descended the rope and, with his right hand, seized the elderly man, while his left hand pulled the rope. Quansu quickly pulled him out and dragged him in front of the hall, binding him with the rope. The two officials carried him out and looked at each other, asking, "Where is the largest slaughterhouse?" One of them replied, "The largest slaughterhouse is at Wang's family in the south end of the cross street in the Bu Zheng坊." They then went there together. Upon arriving, they placed the elderly man on the slaughterhouse table, removed his clothing and tied it around his body, and pushed and struck him. [101]

    老人曰苦，其声感人，全素曰：“有罪当刑， 此亦非法，若无罪责，何以苦之？”二吏曰：“讶君之问何迟也。凡人有善功清 德，合生天堂者，仙乐彩云霓旌鹤驾来迎也，某何以见之？若有重罪及秽恶，合 堕地狱者，牛头奇鬼铁叉枷杻来取，某又何以见之？此老人无生天之福，又无 入地狱之罪，虽能修身，未离尘俗，但洁其身，净无瑕秽，既舍此身，只合更受 男子之身。当其上计之时，其母已孕，此命既尽，彼命合生，今若不团扑，令彼 妇人，何以能产？”又尽力揉扑，实觉渐小，须臾，其形才如拳大，百骸九窍， 莫不依然。于是依依提行，逾子城大胜业坊西南下东回第二曲北壁，入第一家， 其家复有灯火荧煌，言语切切，沙门二人，当窗读《八阳经》。因此不敢逼僧， 直上阶，见堂门斜掩，一吏执老人于堂中，才似到床，新子已啼矣。 [102]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [102]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 老人曰苦，其声感人 - The old man expresses his suffering, and his voice moves people emotionally.
    • 全素曰 - Quansu says.
    • 有罪当刑，此亦非法，若无罪责，何以苦之 - If there is a crime, punishment should follow, but this is also not legal; if there is no guilt, why should he suffer?
    • 二吏曰 - The two officials say.
    • 讶君之问何迟也 - Why are you asking this question so late?
    • 凡人有善功清德，合生天堂者，仙乐彩云霓旌鹤驾来迎也，某何以见之 - All people with good deeds and purity of virtue, those who should be born in heaven, are welcomed by heavenly music, colorful clouds, rainbow flags, and crane-drawn chariots; how can I see this?
    • 若有重罪及秽恶，合堕地狱者，牛头奇鬼铁叉枷杻来取，某又何以见之 - If there are serious crimes or filthiness, those who should fall into hell are taken by ogres with ox heads, iron forks, shackles, and manacles; how can I see this?
    • 此老人无生天之福，又无入地狱之罪，虽能修身，未离尘俗，但洁其身，净无瑕秽，既舍此身，只合更受男子之身 - This old man has no merit for heaven, and no sin for hell; although he can cultivate himself, he has not yet transcended the mundane world; but he keeps his body clean, pure and without blemish; after giving up this body, he should only be reborn as a man.
    • 当其上计之时，其母已孕，此命既尽，彼命合生，今若不团扑，令彼妇人，何以能产 - When he was planning his next life, his mother was pregnant; this life has ended, that life should be born; now if we do not roll him up, to make that woman, how can she give birth?
    • 又尽力揉扑，实觉渐小，须臾，其形才如拳大 - And they roll and rub him with all their might, they feel it getting smaller; in a short time, its form is just the size of a fist.
    • 百骸九窍，莫不依然 - All bones and nine orifices, nothing is different.
    • 于是依依提行 - Then they carry him gently.
    • 逾子城大胜业坊西南下东回第二曲北壁 - Passing by the southern part of the outer city, the Great Success Avenue, the southwest corner, turning east, the second curve, and the northern wall.
    • 入第一家 - Entering the first house.
    • 其家复有灯火荧煌 - There are lights shining brightly in the house.
    • 言语切切 - There is urgent conversation.
    • 沙门二人 - Two Buddhist monks.
    • 当窗读《八阳经》 - Reading the *Eight Sun Sutra* by the window.
    • 因此不敢逼僧 - Therefore they dare not disturb the monks.
    • 直上阶 - They go directly up the steps.
    • 见堂门斜掩 - They see the hall door slightly ajar.
    • 一吏执老人于堂中 - One official takes the old man into the hall.
    • 才似到床 - Just as he reaches the bed.
    • 新子已啼矣 - The newborn baby has already cried.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 老人 - Refers to an old man, likely a character in the story.
    • 全素 - Likely a name or title of a character in the text.
    • 二吏 - Refers to two officials, likely characters in the story.
    • 仙乐彩云霓旌鹤驾 - Describes celestial beings and symbols, reflecting traditional Chinese beliefs about the afterlife.
    • 牛头奇鬼铁叉枷杻 - Describes demonic beings and symbols, reflecting traditional Chinese beliefs about punishment in the afterlife.
    • 生天之福 - Refers to the merit or virtue that leads to a good afterlife.
    • 入地狱之罪 - Refers to the sin or wrongdoing that leads to a bad afterlife.
    • 修身 - Refers to self-cultivation, a key concept in Confucian and Taoist thought.
    • 尘俗 - Refers to the mundane world, contrasting with the spiritual realm.
    • 团扑 - Refers to the physical action of rolling up the old man, likely a ritual or symbolic act.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks, indicating the presence of Buddhism in the story.
    • 《八阳经》 - Refers to the *Eight Sun Sutra*, a Buddhist text.
    • 堂门斜掩 - Describes the hall door slightly ajar, indicating the setting of the story.

    The old man said it was bitter, his voice moved people. Quansu said, "If there is a crime, punishment should follow, but this is also not legal. If there is no guilt, why should he suffer?" The two officials said, "Why are you asking this question so late? All people with good deeds and purity of virtue, those who should be born in heaven, are welcomed by heavenly music, colorful clouds, rainbow flags, and crane-drawn chariots. How can I see this? If there are serious crimes or filthiness, those who should fall into hell, are taken by ogres with ox heads, iron forks, shackles, and manacles. How can I see this? This old man has no merit for heaven, and no sin for hell. Although he can cultivate himself, he has not yet transcended the mundane world. But he keeps his body clean, pure and without blemish. After giving up this body, he should only be reborn as a man. When he was planning his next life, his mother was pregnant. This life has ended, that life should be born. Now if we do not roll him up, to make that woman, how can she give birth?" They rolled and rubbed him with all their might, and they felt it getting smaller. In a short time, its form is just the size of a fist, all bones and nine orifices, nothing is different. Then they carried him gently, passing by the southern part of the outer city, the Great Success Avenue, the southwest corner, turning east, the second curve, and the northern wall, entering the first house. There are lights shining brightly in the house, with urgent conversation. Two Buddhist monks are reading the Eight Sun Sutra by the window. Therefore, they dare not disturb the monks, and go directly up the steps, seeing the hall door slightly ajar. One official takes the old man into the hall, just as he reaches the bed, the newborn baby has already cried. [102]

    一吏曰：“事毕矣，送君去。”又偕入永兴里旅舍，到寝房，房内尚黑，略 无所见。二吏随自后，乃推全素大呼曰：“吴全素！”若失足而坠，既苏，头眩 苦，良久方定。而衙鼓方动，姨夫者自宣阳走马来，则已苏矣，其仆不知觉也。 乘肩舆憩于宣阳，数日复故，再由子城入胜业生男之家，历历在眼。自以明经中 第，不足为荣，思速侍亲。卜得行日，或头眩不果去，或驴来脚损，或雨雪连日， 或亲故往来，因循之问，遂逼试日，入场而过，不复以旧日之望为意。俄而成名， 笑别长安而去。乃知命当有成，弃之不可；时苟未会，躁亦何为。举此端，足可 以诫其知进而不知退者。
    　　
    　　○掠剩使
    　　
    　　杜陵韦元方外兄裴璞，任邠州新平县尉，元和五年卒于官。 [103]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [103]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 事畢矣，送君去 - Formal expression meaning 'the matter is concluded, now I shall escort you away.'
    • 偕入永興里旅舍 - Means 'together they entered the inn in Yongxing Lane.'
    • 房内尚黑 - Means 'the room was still dark,' indicating it was early morning or late at night.
    • 略无所见 - Means 'nothing could be seen,' emphasizing the darkness.
    • 二吏随自后 - Means 'two officials followed from behind.'
    • 乃推全素大呼曰 - Means 'they then pushed Quansu and shouted.'
    • 若失足而坠 - Means 'as if he had lost his footing and fallen.'
    • 既苏 - Means 'after regaining consciousness.'
    • 头眩苦 - Means 'he suffered from dizziness.'
    • 良久方定 - Means 'it took a long time before he stabilized.'
    • 衙鼓方动 - Means 'the official drum had just started beating,' indicating the beginning of the day.
    • 姨夫者自宣阳走马来 - Means 'his father-in-law came running on horseback from Xuanyang.'
    • 乘肩舆憩于宣阳 - Means 'he rode in a sedan chair and rested in Xuanyang.'
    • 数日复故 - Means 'after several days, he returned to his old self.'
    • 再由子城入胜业生男之家 - Means 'he again entered the house of his son-in-law in the inner city.'
    • 历历在眼 - Means 'everything was clearly in his mind.'
    • 自以明经中第 - Means 'he considered himself to have passed the imperial examination through the *mingjing* (明经) route.'
    • 不足为荣 - Means 'it was not worth much honor.'
    • 思速侍亲 - Means 'he wanted to quickly serve his parents.'
    • 卜得行日 - Means 'he chose a date for his journey.'
    • 或头眩不果去 - Means 'sometimes he was dizzy and did not leave.'
    • 或驴来脚损 - Means 'sometimes the donkey was injured.'
    • 或雨雪连日 - Means 'sometimes it rained or snowed for days.'
    • 或亲故往来 - Means 'sometimes relatives and friends came and went.'
    • 因循之问 - Means 'through these delays.'
    • 遂逼试日 - Means 'he was then close to the exam day.'
    • 入场而过 - Means 'he entered the examination hall and passed.'
    • 不复以旧日之望为意 - Means 'he no longer cared about his previous expectations.'
    • 俄而成名 - Means 'soon he gained fame.'
    • 笑别长安而去 - Means 'he laughed and left Chang'an.'
    • 乃知命当有成 - Means 'he then realized that his fate was to succeed.'
    • 弃之不可 - Means 'it was not possible to abandon it.'
    • 时苟未会 - Means 'if the time was not right.'
    • 躁亦何为 - Means 'what would be the point of being impatient?'
    • 举此端 - Means 'by way of this example.'
    • 足以可以诫其知进而不知退者 - Means 'it is enough to warn those who know how to advance but not how to retreat.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 永兴里 - A specific location in Chang'an (modern Xi'an).
    • 宣阳 - A district in Chang'an.
    • 子城 - The inner city of Chang'an.
    • 胜业生男之家 - The house of his son-in-law, indicating a familial connection.
    • 明经 - One of the categories of the imperial examinations, focusing on Confucian classics.
    • 长安 - The capital of the Tang Dynasty, modern Xi'an.
    • 掠剩使 - A title or role, possibly a metaphorical or symbolic position.
    • 杜陵 - A place name, possibly referring to a family or a specific location.
    • 韦元方外兄裴璞 - A person, 'Weiyuanfang's brother-in-law Pei Pu.'
    • 邠州新平县尉 - An official title, 'County Magistrate of Xinping County in Bin Prefecture.'
    • 元和五年 - The year 810 AD during the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吳全素 - A person, 'Wu Quansu.'
    • 韋元方 - A person, 'Wei Yuanfang.'
    • 裴璞 - A person, 'Pei Pu.'
    • 邠州 - A place name, 'Bin Prefecture.'
    • 新平縣尉 - An official title, 'County Magistrate of Xinping County.'
    • 元和五年 - A year, '810 AD.'

    One official said, "The matter is concluded, now I shall escort you away." They then entered the inn in Yongxing Lane together and reached the bedroom, which was still dark, and nothing could be seen. Two officials followed from behind, and they then pushed Quansu and shouted, "Wu Quansu!" As if he had lost his footing and fallen, he regained consciousness, suffering from dizziness, and it took a long time before he stabilized. The official drum had just started beating, and his father-in-law came running on horseback from Xuanyang, by which time he had already regained consciousness, though his servant was unaware. He rode in a sedan chair and rested in Xuanyang, and after several days, he returned to his old self. He again entered the house of his son-in-law in the inner city, and everything was clearly in his mind. He considered himself to have passed the imperial examination through the mingjing route, but it was not worth much honor, and he wanted to quickly serve his parents. He chose a date for his journey, but sometimes he was dizzy and did not leave, or the donkey was injured, or it rained or snowed for days, or relatives and friends came and went, through these delays, he was then close to the exam day. He entered the examination hall and passed, and he no longer cared about his previous expectations. Soon he gained fame, laughed, and left Chang'an. He then realized that his fate was to succeed, and it was not possible to abandon it; if the time was not right, what would be the point of being impatient? By way of this example, it is enough to warn those who know how to advance but not how to retreat.

    ○Plundering Leftovers

    Du Ling's brother-in-law, Wei Yuanfang, Pei Pu, served as the County Magistrate of Xinping County in Bin Prefecture and died in office in the fifth year of the Yuanhe era (810 AD). [103]

    长庆初，元方下第，将客于陇右。出开远门数十里抵偏店，将憩，逢武吏跃 马而来，骑从数十，而貌似璞。见元方若识，而急下马避之，入茶坊，垂帘于小 室中，其徒御散坐帘外。元方疑之，亦造其邸。及褰帘入见，实裴璞也，惊喜拜 之，曰：“兄去人间，复效武职，何从吏之赳赳焉？”裴曰：“吾为阴官，职辖 武士，故武饰耳。”元方曰：“何官？”陇右三川掠剩使耳。”曰：“何为典耶？” 曰：“吾职司人剩财而掠之。”韦曰：“何谓剩财？”裴曰：“人之转货求丐也， 命当即□，忽遇物之箱稀，或主人深顾所得，乃逾数外之财，即谓之剩，故掠之 焉。”曰：“安知其剩而掠之？”裴曰：“生人一饮一啄，无非前定，况财实乎？ 阴司所籍，其获有限，获而逾籍，阴吏状来，乃掠之也。”韦曰：“所谓掠者， 夺之于囊耶，窃之于怀耶？”裴曰：“非也。当数而得，一一有成，数外之财， 为吾所运。 [104]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [104]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 长庆初 - Refers to the early years of the Changqing era, a period during the reign of Emperor Muzong of Tang (821–824).
    • 下第 - A term used in the imperial examination system to denote failing the exams.
    • 陇右 - Refers to the region west of the Long Mountains, historically a frontier area of China.
    • 开远门 - The name of a gate in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an), the capital of the Tang dynasty.
    • 垂帘于小室中 - Literally 'hanging a curtain in a small room,' this phrase likely means to retreat to a private space.
    • 造其邸 - Means 'to visit his residence.'
    • 褰帘入见 - Literally 'to lift the curtain and enter to see,' this phrase describes entering a private space.
    • 效武职 - Means 'to take up a military post.'
    • 武饰 - Refers to military attire or appearance.
    • 阴官 - Refers to an official in the underworld or a position related to the afterlife.
    • 掠剩使 - Literally 'seizer of surplus,' this term refers to a position responsible for taking excess wealth.
    • 生人一饮一啄 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'every drink and bite of food' for a person, often used to imply that everything is predestined.
    • 阴司所籍 - Refers to the records kept by the underworld.
    • 数外之财 - Means 'wealth beyond the allotted amount' or 'excess wealth.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 长庆 - The era name of Emperor Muzong of Tang, indicating the time period of the narrative.
    • 陇右 - A region historically significant for its role in the Tang dynasty's military campaigns and frontier defense.
    • 开远门 - A specific gate in Chang'an, the capital city, which was a hub of political and cultural activity.
    • 阴官 - A reference to the concept of officials in the underworld, reflecting the Tang dynasty's belief in the afterlife and the existence of supernatural beings.
    • 掠剩使 - A position that reflects the Tang dynasty's administrative practices, particularly in managing wealth and resources.
    • 生人一饮一啄 - A common metaphor in Chinese literature, emphasizing the idea of fate and predestination.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence introduces the setting and characters, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
    • This clause describes the actions of the mysterious figure, which need to be broken down to avoid confusion.
    • This section involves a series of actions and dialogue, which should be translated in a way that preserves the flow of events.
    • This response needs to be translated to clearly convey the nature of the position.
    • This dialogue should be segmented to maintain the conversational flow.
    • This explanation of '剩财' should be translated to clarify the concept.
    • This philosophical discussion should be translated to preserve the deeper meaning.
    • This final exchange should be translated to maintain the logical progression of the conversation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 长庆 - The era name of Emperor Muzong of Tang.
    • 元方 - The name of the protagonist.
    • 陇右 - The region west of the Long Mountains.
    • 开远门 - A gate in Chang'an.
    • 裴璞 - The name of the mysterious figure.
    • 阴官 - A position in the underworld.
    • 掠剩使 - A specific official position.

    In the early years of the Changqing era, Yuan Fang failed the imperial examinations and was traveling to the region west of the Long Mountains. After leaving the Kaiyuan Gate and traveling dozens of miles to a remote inn, he was about to rest when he encountered a military officer riding a horse, accompanied by several attendants. The officer appeared to resemble Pei Pu. Seeing Yuan Fang, the officer seemed to recognize him and quickly dismounted to avoid him, entering a teahouse and hanging a curtain in a small room, while his attendants sat scattered outside the curtain. Yuan Fang was puzzled by this and decided to visit his residence. Upon lifting the curtain and entering, he found it was indeed Pei Pu. Yuan Fang was surprised and greeted him, saying, "Brother, after leaving the mortal world, you have taken up a military post again. Why do you appear so stern and imposing?" Pei Pu replied, "I am an official in the underworld, responsible for overseeing warriors, hence my martial appearance." Yuan Fang asked, "What position is that?" Pei Pu answered, "I am the Seizer of Surplus from the Three Rivers of Longyou." Yuan Fang inquired, "What is your duty?" Pei Pu explained, "My role is to seize surplus wealth from people." Yuan Fang asked, "What do you mean by surplus wealth?" Pei Pu said, "When people trade goods or seek alms, their fate is predetermined, but sometimes they encounter rare items or receive more than what was expected. This excess wealth is what we call surplus, and we seize it." Yuan Fang asked, "How do you know it is surplus and seize it?" Pei Pu replied, "Every drink and bite of food a person has is predestined; how much more so wealth? The underworld keeps records of what people are entitled to. If they acquire more than what is recorded, the underworld officials report it, and we seize it." Yuan Fang asked, "What do you mean by seizing? Do you steal it from their bags or pockets?" Pei Pu said, "No. It is determined by fate, and each person receives what is due to them. Any wealth beyond what is due is taken by me." [104]

    或令虚耗，或洁横事，或买卖不及常价，殊不关身尔。始吾之生也， 常谓商勤得财，农勤得谷，士勤得禄，只叹其不勤而不得也。夫覆舟之商，旱岁 之农，屡空之士，岂不勤乎？而今乃知勤者德之基，学者善之本。德之为善，乃 理身之道耳，亦未足以邀财而求禄也。子之逢吾，亦是前定，合得白金二斤，过 此遗子，又当复掠，故不厚矣。子之是行也，岐甚厚而邠甚薄，于泾殊无所得， 诸镇平平耳。 [105]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [105]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 或令虚耗 - Literally means 'some make it wasteful,' but refers to people who squander resources or opportunities.
    • 或洁横事 - Literally means 'some handle strange or unusual matters,' but refers to people who engage in irregular or unconventional activities.
    • 或买卖不及常价 - Literally means 'some trade at prices below the usual,' suggesting undervalued transactions.
    • 殊不关身尔 - Literally means 'it is entirely unrelated to oneself,' but means that these actions do not directly affect the speaker.
    • 商勤得财，农勤得谷，士勤得禄 - Phrases following the pattern of '勤' (diligence) leading to specific rewards: merchants gain wealth, farmers gain grain, and scholars gain official rank.
    • 覆舟之商，旱岁之农，屡空之士 - Metaphors for merchants who lose their ships, farmers who face drought, and scholars who repeatedly fail to secure positions, emphasizing hard work despite failure.
    • 勤者德之基，学者善之本 - Ideas that diligence is the foundation of virtue and learning is the root of goodness.
    • 德之为善，乃理身之道耳 - Means that virtue is the way to cultivate oneself, but does not necessarily lead to material gain or official rank.
    • 子之逢吾，亦是前定 - Suggests that the speaker's meeting with the listener was predestined.
    • 合得白金二斤 - Refers to a specific amount of wealth, likely silver, that the speaker was supposed to receive.
    • 过此遗子，又当复掠 - Suggests that after giving the listener the silver, the speaker will lose it again, implying a cycle of gain and loss.
    • 岐甚厚而邠甚薄 - Compares the richness of one place (Qì) to the poverty of another (Bīn), suggesting a disparity in fortune or opportunity.
    • 诸镇平平耳 - Means that the other regions are average or unremarkable in comparison.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 商、农、士 - Refers to the three traditional social classes in ancient China: merchants, farmers, and scholars.
    • 覆舟之商 - Uses the metaphor of a capsized ship to describe merchants who lose their wealth or resources.
    • 旱岁之农 - Uses the metaphor of a drought to describe farmers who face poor harvests.
    • 屡空之士 - Describes scholars who repeatedly fail to secure official positions, despite their hard work.
    • 德之为善 - Reflects the Confucian emphasis on virtue as the foundation of a good life.
    • 理身之道 - Refers to the Confucian idea of self-cultivation as a path to moral and personal development.
    • 子之逢吾，亦是前定 - Reflects the Taoist or Buddhist concept of predestination or fate.
    • 白金二斤 - Refers to a specific amount of wealth, likely silver, which was a common form of currency in ancient China.
    • 岐、邠 - Are place names, possibly referring to regions or cities, but their specific historical significance is unclear without further context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 岐、邠、泾 - Are place names, possibly referring to regions or cities, but their specific historical significance is unclear without further context.

    Some people squander resources, others handle strange matters, and still others trade at prices below the usual; these things have nothing to do with me. When I was born, I used to think that merchants gain wealth through diligence, farmers gain grain through diligence, and scholars gain official rank through diligence, only lamenting those who do not work hard and therefore do not achieve anything. Merchants who lose their ships, farmers who face drought, and scholars who repeatedly fail—how hard do they not work? But now I understand that diligence is the foundation of virtue, and learning is the root of goodness. Virtue is the way to cultivate oneself, but it is not enough to gain wealth or seek official rank. Your meeting with me was predestined; you were supposed to receive two pounds of silver, but after giving it to you, I will lose it again, so it is not a large amount. Your journey to Qì is rich, but to Bīn it is poor, and in Jing, you gain nothing at all; the other regions are average, nothing remarkable. [105]

    人生有命，时不参差，以道静观，无复违挠，勉之哉！璞以公事， 顷入城中，阴冥数限，不可逾越。”遂以白金二斤授之，揖而上马。元方固请曰： “阔别多年，忽此集会，款言未几，又隔晦明，何遽如此？”璞曰：“本司廨署， 置在汧陇，阻吐蕃，将来虑其侵轶，当与阴道京尹，共议会盟。虽非远图，聊亦 纾患，亦粗安之计也。戎马已驾，来期不遥，事非早谋，不可为备，且去！且去！” 上马数里，遂不复见。其所遗，乃真白金也。怅然而西，所历之获，无差其说。 [106]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [106]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 人生有命 - Common classical expression meaning 'life is determined by fate.'
    • 時不參差 - Means 'time does not vary' or 'time is consistent,' implying that fate operates within a fixed framework.
    • 以道靜觀 - Means 'to observe calmly through the Dao,' suggesting a philosophical or spiritual approach to life.
    • 無復違挠 - Means 'no longer to resist or disturb,' indicating acceptance of one's fate.
    • 勉之哉 - Common admonitory phrase meaning 'strive hard!'
    • 陰冥數限 - Refers to 'dark and ominous limits' or 'mysterious boundaries,' likely alluding to supernatural or cosmic constraints.
    • 款言未幾 - Means 'brief conversation' or 'a short exchange of words.'
    • 闊別多年 - Means 'having been apart for many years.'
    • 隔晦明 - Means 'separated by day and night' or 'parted by light and darkness,' symbolizing the passage of time.
    • 本司廨署 - Refers to 'the official residence or office of the bureau.'
    • 置在汧陇 - Means 'located in the Qian and Long regions,' likely referring to specific geographical or administrative areas.
    • 阻吐蕃 - Means 'blocked by the Tibetan people,' indicating a military or territorial threat.
    • 侵轶 - Means 'to invade or raid,' suggesting a military action.
    • 阴道京尹 - Refers to 'the governor of the capital,' likely a title or role in the administrative hierarchy.
    • 共议会盟 - Means 'to hold a council and make an alliance.'
    • 戎馬已駕 - Means 'the military is already mobilized,' indicating imminent action.
    • 來期不遙 - Means 'the arrival is not far off,' suggesting that the event is imminent.
    • 事非早謀 - Means 'the matter was not planned early,' indicating a lack of foresight.
    • 不可為備 - Means 'cannot be prepared for,' suggesting that the situation is beyond preparation.
    • 且去！且去！ - Repeated imperative meaning 'go! go!' with urgency.
    • 闊別多年，忽此集會，款言未幾，又隔晦明，何遽如此？ - Rhetorical question expressing surprise at the sudden departure after a brief reunion.
    • 本司廨署，置在汧陇，阻吐蕃，將來慮其侵轶，當與阴道京尹，共议会盟。雖非遠圖，聊亦纾患，亦粗安之計也。戎馬已駕，來期不遙，事非早謀，不可為備，且去！且去！ - Complex sentence explaining the urgency of the departure due to impending military action and the need for immediate preparation.
    • 其所遺，乃真白金也。 - Means 'what he left behind was genuine white gold,' indicating the value of the gift.
    • 悵然而西 - Means 'disappointed and went westward,' expressing a sense of loss or sadness.
    • 所歷之獲，無差其說。 - Means 'what was experienced and gained matched what was said,' suggesting that the account was accurate.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吐蕃 - Refers to the Tibetan people, who were a major power in the region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 阴道京尹 - Likely refers to a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, possibly the governor of the capital.
    • 汧陇 - Refers to specific regions in the Tang Dynasty, likely areas of strategic importance.
    • 白金 - Refers to silver, which was a valuable metal in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吐蕃 - The Tibetan people.
    • 阴道京尹 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, possibly the governor of the capital.
    • 汧陇 - Specific regions in the Tang Dynasty, likely areas of strategic importance.
    • 白金 - Silver, a valuable metal in ancient China.

    Life is determined by fate, and time does not vary; observe calmly through the Dao, no longer to resist or disturb, strive hard! Pú entered the city on official business, and the dark and ominous limits were such that they could not be crossed. He then presented two pounds of genuine white gold to him, bowed, and mounted his horse. Yuánfāng insisted, saying, "Having been apart for many years, we suddenly met, had a brief conversation, and now we are separated by day and night. Why are you leaving so suddenly?" Pú replied, "The official residence of my bureau is located in the Qian and Long regions, blocked by the Tibetan people. We are concerned that they will invade, so we must hold a council and make an alliance with the governor of the capital. Although this is not a long-term plan, it is a way to alleviate the crisis and a rough plan for temporary security. The military is already mobilized, and the arrival is not far off. The matter was not planned early, so we cannot prepare for it. Go! Go!" After riding several miles, he was no longer seen. What he left behind was genuine white gold. Disappointed and went westward, what was experienced and gained matched what was said. [106]

    彼乐天知命者，盖知事皆前定矣。俄而蕃浑骚动，朝廷知之，又虑其叛，思 援臣以为谋，宰相莅盟，相国崔公不欲临境，遂为城下之盟，卒如其说也。
    　　
    　　○叶天师
    　　
    　　开元中，道士叶静能讲于明州奉化县兴唐观。自升座也，有老父白衣而髯者， 每先来而后去，必迟迟然，若有意欲言而未能者。讲将罢去，愈更淹留。听徒毕 去，师乃召问。泣拜而言，自称鳞位，曰：“有意求哀，不敢自陈，既蒙不问， 敢不尽其诚恳。位实非人，乃实藏之守龙也。职在观南小海中，千秋无失，乃获 稍迁，苟或失之，即受炎沙之罚。今九百余年矣，胡僧所禁且三十春，其僧虔心， 有大咒力，今忧午日午时，其术即成，来喝水乾，宝无所隐。弟子当死，不敢望 荣迁，然千载之炎海，诚不可忍。惟仙师哀之，必免斯难，不敢忘德。”师许之， 乃泣谢而去。 [107]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [107]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 彼乐天知命者，盖知事皆前定矣 - Classical phrase meaning 'those who are content with their fate and know their destiny, understand that all events are predestined.'
    • 俄而蕃浑骚动 - Phrase indicating a sudden disturbance or unrest, where '俄而' means 'suddenly' or 'in a short while,' and '蕃浑骚动' refers to a disturbance among foreign tribes.
    • 朝廷知之，又虑其叛 - The court knew about the disturbance and was concerned about potential rebellion.
    • 思援臣以为谋 - The thought of seeking a minister's advice for a strategy.
    • 宰相莅盟 - The prime minister presiding over an alliance.
    • 相国崔公不欲临境 - The prime minister, Cui Gong, did not want to go to the border.
    • 遂为城下之盟 - Eventually, a surrender was made under the walls of the city, a metaphor for a humiliating peace agreement.
    • 卒如其说也 - In the end, it turned out as he had said.
    • 开元中 - During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, specifically the Kaiyuan period.
    • 道士叶静能讲于明州奉化县兴唐观 - The Taoist master Ye Jingneng lectured at the Xingtang Temple in Fenghua County, Mingzhou.
    • 升座也，有老父白衣而髯者 - When he ascended the dais, there was an old man in plain clothes with a beard.
    • 每先来而后去 - Always arriving first and leaving last.
    • 迟迟然，若有意欲言而未能者 - Slowly, as if he had something to say but couldn't.
    • 讲将罢去，愈更淹留 - As the lecture was about to end, he lingered even more.
    • 听徒毕去，师乃召问 - After all the students left, the master then called him to ask.
    • 泣拜而言 - He wept and bowed while speaking.
    • 自称鳞位 - He called himself a fish position, a metaphor for a low-ranking official or a humble position.
    • 有意求哀，不敢自陈 - He had something to plead, but dared not speak for himself.
    • 既蒙不问，敢不尽其诚恳 - Since he was not questioned, he dared to express his sincerity fully.
    • 位实非人，乃实藏之守龙也 - His position is not human; he is actually the guardian dragon of the hidden treasure.
    • 职在观南小海中 - His duty is in the small sea to the south of the temple.
    • 千秋无失，乃获稍迁 - If he does not fail for a thousand years, he will be promoted slightly.
    • 苟或失之，即受炎沙之罚 - If he fails, he will be punished by the scorching sands.
    • 今九百余年矣，胡僧所禁且三十春 - Now it has been over nine hundred years, and he has been forbidden by a foreign monk for nearly thirty years.
    • 其僧虔心，有大咒力 - The monk is devout and has great magical power.
    • 今忧午日午时，其术即成 - Now he fears that on the day of the fifth month and the fifth hour, the monk's spell will be completed.
    • 来喝水乾，宝无所隐 - The monk will come to drink up the water, and the treasure will be revealed.
    • 弟子当死，不敢望荣迁 - I will die, but I dare not hope for promotion.
    • 然千载之炎海，诚不可忍 - However, a thousand years in the scorching sea is truly unbearable.
    • 惟仙师哀之，必免斯难，不敢忘德 - Only you, the immortal master, can pity me and save me from this difficulty; I will never forget your kindness.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 开元中 - The Kaiyuan period (713-741 AD) during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for its cultural and economic prosperity.
    • 明州奉化县兴唐观 - Xingtang Temple in Fenghua County, Mingzhou (modern-day Ningbo, Zhejiang Province).
    • 叶静能 - A historical figure, a Taoist master who lived during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 鳞位 - A metaphor for a low-ranking official or a humble position, often used in Taoist contexts.
    • 胡僧 - Refers to a foreign monk, likely from India or Central Asia, who brought Buddhism to China.
    • 炎沙之罚 - A metaphorical punishment involving scorching sands, possibly referring to a hellish realm in Buddhist or Taoist cosmology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 开元 - The Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 明州 - Modern-day Ningbo, Zhejiang Province.
    • 奉化县 - Fenghua County, a district in Zhejiang Province.
    • 兴唐观 - Xingtang Temple, a Taoist temple.
    • 叶静能 - A historical Taoist master.
    • 胡僧 - A foreign monk.

    Those who are content with their fate and know their destiny understand that all events are predestined. Suddenly, the foreign tribes became restless, and the court was aware of it, fearing potential rebellion. They thought of seeking a minister's advice for a strategy, and the prime minister presided over an alliance. The prime minister, Cui Gong, did not want to go to the border, so a humiliating peace agreement was made, and it turned out as he had said.

    ○ Ye the Taoist Master

    During the Kaiyuan period, the Taoist master Ye Jingneng lectured at the Xingtang Temple in Fenghua County, Mingzhou. When he ascended the dais, there was an old man in plain clothes with a beard. He always arrived first and left last, lingering slowly, as if he had something to say but couldn't. As the lecture was about to end, he lingered even more. After all the students left, the master then called him to ask. The old man wept and bowed while speaking, calling himself a fish position, and said, "I have something to plead, but dared not speak for myself. Since I was not questioned, I dare to express my sincerity fully. My position is not human; I am actually the guardian dragon of the hidden treasure. My duty is in the small sea to the south of the temple. If I do not fail for a thousand years, I will be promoted slightly. If I fail, I will be punished by the scorching sands. Now it has been over nine hundred years, and I have been forbidden by a foreign monk for nearly thirty years. The monk is devout and has great magical power. Now I fear that on the day of the fifth month and the fifth hour, the monk's spell will be completed. The monk will come to drink up the water, and the treasure will be revealed. I will die, but I dare not hope for promotion. However, a thousand years in the scorching sea is truly unbearable. Only you, the immortal master, can pity me and save me from this difficulty; I will never forget your kindness." The master agreed, and the old man wept and thanked him before departing. [107]

    师恐遗忘，乃大书其柱曰：“午日午时救龙。”其日赴食于邑人，既回方憩， 门人忽读其柱曰：“午日午时救龙。今方欲午，吾师正憩，岂忘之乎？”将入， 师已闻，遽问曰：“今何时？”对曰：“顷刻未午耳。”仙师遂使青衣门人执墨 符，奔往海。一里余，见黑云惨空，毒风四起，有婆罗门仗剑，乘黑云，持咒于 海上连喝，海水寻减半矣。青衣使亦随声堕焉。又使黄衣门人执朱符奔马以往， 去海一百余步，又喝，寻堕，海水十涸七八矣。有白龙跳跃浅波中，喘喘焉。 [108]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [108]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 乃大书其柱曰 - Formal way of saying 'then wrote in large letters on the pillar saying.'
    • 持咒 - Act of reciting a spell or incantation.
    • 寻减半 - Soon reduced by half.
    • 寻堕 - Soon fell.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 师 - Teacher or master, likely a spiritual or scholarly figure.
    • 门人 - Disciples or students.
    • 仙师 - Immortal or highly revered teacher, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist traditions.
    • 婆罗门 - Brahmin or high priest, typically from Hindu tradition, but used in a Chinese context, possibly indicating a foreign or exotic element.
    • 黑云惨空，毒风四起 - Describes a dark, ominous sky and toxic winds, which could be symbolic of a dangerous or supernatural event.
    • 午日午时 - Refers to noon, a significant time in Chinese culture often associated with the peak of the sun's power.

    Proper Nouns

    • 青衣门人 - Green-clad disciple.
    • 黄衣门人 - Yellow-clad disciple.
    • 白龙 - White dragon.

    The teacher, fearing he might forget, wrote in large letters on the pillar: "Rescue the dragon at noon on the noon day." On that day, he went to dine with the townspeople and, upon returning, was just resting when his disciple suddenly read from the pillar: "Rescue the dragon at noon on the noon day. It is now just before noon, and my teacher is resting; how could he have forgotten it?" As the disciple was about to enter, the teacher had already heard and quickly asked, "What time is it now?" The disciple replied, "It has not yet reached noon." The immortal teacher then sent a green-clad disciple to hold a black talisman and rush to the sea. After a mile or so, they saw black clouds darkening the sky and toxic winds arising from all sides. There was a Brahmin wielding a sword, riding on black clouds, and shouting spells repeatedly over the sea, causing the seawater to soon reduce by half. The green-clad disciple also fell along with the sound. The teacher then sent a yellow-clad disciple to hold a red talisman and rush on horseback. After a hundred or more steps from the sea, they shouted again, and soon fell, causing the seawater to almost completely dry up. There was a white dragon jumping in the shallow waves, breathing heavily. [108]

    又 使朱衣使执黄符以往，僧又喝之，连喝不堕。及岸，则海水才一二尺，白龙者奋 鬣张口于沙中。朱衣使投符于海，随手水复。婆罗门抚剑而叹曰：“三十年精勤， 一旦术尽，何道士之多能哉！”拗怒而去。既空海恬然，波停风息，前堕二使， 亦渐能起，相与偕归，具白于师。未毕，老父者已到，泣拜曰：“向者几死于胡 术，非仙师之力，不能免矣。位也，惧不克报，然终天依附，愿出门人，可指使 也。若承师命，虽秦越地阻，江山路殊，一念召之，即立左右矣。”自是朝夕定 省，若门人焉。 [109]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [109]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 又使朱衣使执黄符以往 - Classical imagery of a messenger and a talisman, symbolic in Chinese culture.
    • 僧又喝之，连喝不堕 - '喝' means to shout or scold, '不堕' implies the person did not lose composure.
    • 及岸，则海水才一二尺 - Describes a sudden change in water level.
    • 白龙者奋鬣张口于沙中 - Vivid description of the dragon's appearance and action.
    • 朱衣使投符于海，随手水复 - Describes a magical or supernatural event.
    • 婆罗门抚剑而叹曰 - Shows the Brahmin's reaction to the situation.
    • 三十年精勤，一旦术尽，何道士之多能哉 - Rhetorical expression of admiration and frustration.
    • 拗怒而去 - Describes the Brahmin's emotional state.
    • 既空海恬然，波停风息 - Describes the aftermath of the Brahmin's departure.
    • 前堕二使，亦渐能起，相与偕归 - Describes the recovery of the messengers and their return.
    • 具白于师 - Indicates the completion of the task and reporting back to the master.
    • 未毕，老父者已到，泣拜曰 - Describes the old man's arrival and emotional reaction.
    • 向者几死于胡术，非仙师之力，不能免矣 - Expresses the old man's gratitude and relief.
    • 位也，惧不克报，然终天依附，愿出门人，可指使也 - Shows the old man's desire to become a disciple.
    • 若承师命，虽秦越地阻，江山路殊，一念召之，即立左右矣 - Expresses the old man's commitment and willingness to follow the master's orders.
    • 自是朝夕定省，若门人焉 - Describes the old man's regular visits and status as a disciple.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 朱衣使 - A messenger dressed in red robes, often associated with official or religious authority.
    • 黄符 - A yellow talisman, a symbol of magical or protective power.
    • 僧 - A monk, representing a spiritual figure.
    • 婆罗门 - A Brahmin, a member of the priestly class in Hinduism.
    • 空海 - Reference to the sea becoming calm, possibly alluding to divine intervention.
    • 胡术 - Foreign magic or sorcery, often associated with non-Chinese cultures.
    • 仙师 - An immortal master, a figure of great spiritual power.
    • 门人 - A disciple, a follower or student of a master.

    Proper Nouns

    • 朱衣使 - The messenger dressed in red robes.
    • 黄符 - The yellow talisman.
    • 僧 - The monk.
    • 婆罗门 - The Brahmin.
    • 空海 - The sea becoming calm.
    • 胡术 - Foreign magic.
    • 仙师 - The immortal master.
    • 门人 - The disciple.

    Again, he sent a messenger dressed in red robes with a yellow talisman to go. The monk shouted at him again, and he did not fall despite the shouting. When they reached the shore, the sea water was only one or two feet deep. The white dragon raised its mane and opened its mouth on the sand. The messenger in red robes threw the talisman into the sea, and the water immediately returned to its original state. The Brahmin touched his sword and sighed, saying, "Thirty years of diligent practice, all lost in an instant. How many skills does this Taoist have!" He left in anger. Afterwards, the sea became calm, and the waves and wind ceased. The two messengers who had fallen before gradually got up and returned together, reporting everything to the master. Before the report was finished, the old man arrived, wept, and said, "Earlier, I almost died from the foreign magic, but without the power of the immortal master, I could not have escaped. I, Wei, fear I cannot repay you, but I will always be attached to you. I wish to be your disciple, and you can command me. If I receive your command, even if there are great distances between Qin and Yue, or mountains and rivers, with just a thought, I will be at your side." From then on, he came to visit every morning and evening, like a disciple. [109]

    师以其观在原上，不可穿井，童稚汲水，必于十里之外，阖观患之。他日， 师谓髯父曰：“吾居此多日，怜其汲远，思绕观有泉以济之，子可致乎？”曰： “泉水之流，天界所有，非力可致。然师能见活，又脱千年之苦，岂可辞乎！夫 非可致而致之，界神将拒，俟战胜然后可。令诸人皆他徙。其日晦明三复，然后 归，庶几有从命□□之功。”合观从之。过期而还，则石甃绕观，清流潺潺，既 周而南，入于海，黄冠赖焉。乃题渠曰：“仙师渠”。师所以妙术广大天下，盖 龙之所助焉。
    　　
    　　○许元长
    　　
    　　许元长者，江陵术士焉，客淮南。御史陆俊之从事广陵也，有贤妻，待之情 分倍愈于常。俄而妻亡，俊之伤悼，情又过之。每至春风动处，秋月明时，众乐 声悲，征鸿韵咽，或展转忘寐，思苦畏叹，或伫立无憀，心伤永日。如此者逾年 矣，全失壮容，骤或雪鬓。 [110]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [110]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 師以其觀在原上，不可穿井 - The master's residence being on high ground made it impossible to dig a well.
    • 童稚汲水，必于十里之外 - Children had to fetch water from ten miles away, indicating the distance and difficulty.
    • 泉水之流，天界所有，非力可致 - The flow of water is beyond human control, suggesting a supernatural element.
    • 俟战胜然后可 - Only after winning a battle can the spring be brought, indicating a ritual or symbolic act.
    • 其日晦明三复，然后归 - After three cycles of darkness and light, they returned, suggesting a period of waiting or ritual.
    • 石甃绕观，清流潺潺 - Stones were laid around the residence, and clear water flowed gently, describing the result.
    • 既周而南，入于海 - After circling around, the water flowed south into the sea, indicating the direction and destination.
    • 黄冠赖焉 - The Daoist priests relied on the water, showing the practical benefit.
    • 乃题渠曰：‘仙师渠’ - They named the channel 'Fairy Master's Channel,' reflecting the mystical context.
    • 師所以妙術廣大天下，蓋龍之所助焉 - The master's skills spread widely, attributed to the help of the dragon, emphasizing the supernatural aspect.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 師 - Refers to a Daoist master or priest.
    • 髯父 - Refers to a bearded father, likely a senior figure in the Daoist community.
    • 黄冠 - Refers to Daoist priests, who often wear yellow hats.
    • 仙師渠 - A name given to the channel, reflecting the mystical and Daoist context.
    • 龍之所助 - The dragon's assistance, a common theme in Daoist and Chinese mythology, symbolizing divine or supernatural aid.

    Proper Nouns

    • 師 - The master, likely a Daoist priest.
    • 髯父 - The bearded father, a senior figure in the Daoist community.
    • 黄冠 - Daoist priests.
    • 仙師渠 - The name of the channel.
    • 龍 - The dragon, a supernatural being in Chinese mythology.

    Final Translation

    The master, seeing that his residence was on high ground, realized that it was impossible to dig a well. As a result, children had to fetch water from ten miles away, causing the entire community great trouble. One day, the master said to the bearded father, "I have lived here for a long time and feel pity for the children who have to fetch water so far. I am thinking of having a spring around the residence to help them. Can you bring it?" The bearded father replied, "The flow of water is something in the heavens, not something that can be brought by force. However, since you have seen the living, you have also escaped the suffering of a thousand years. How can I refuse? Although the spring cannot be brought by force, it can be brought after a battle. Order everyone to move elsewhere. On that day, after three cycles of darkness and light, they returned, perhaps there would be some success in following the command." The entire community followed the instructions. After the designated period, they returned to find stones laid around the residence, with clear water flowing gently. After circling around, the water flowed south into the sea, and the Daoist priests relied on it. They then named the channel "Fairy Master's Channel." The master's marvelous skills spread widely throughout the world, which was due to the help of the dragon.

    Translation of the Second Part

    Xu Yuanchang, a sorcerer from Jiangling, was a guest in Huainan. When the censor Lu Jun served in Guangling, he had a virtuous wife whom he treated with exceptional care and affection. Soon after, his wife passed away, and Lu Jun was deeply grieved, his sorrow even surpassing that of ordinary people. Whenever the spring wind blew or the autumn moon shone bright, the sounds of music would evoke sadness, and the cries of geese in flight would choke his throat. Sometimes he would toss and turn, unable to sleep, tormented by grief and fear; at other times, he would stand motionless, feeling utterly desolate, his heart heavy with sorrow. This state of mind lasted for over a year, and he completely lost his youthful appearance, his hair turning white overnight. [110]

    他日元长来，陆生知有奇术，试以汉武帝李夫人之事诱之，元长曰：“此甚 易耳。”曰：“然则能为我致亡妻之神乎？”曰：“彼所致者，但致其魂，瞥见 而已。元长又异焉。”陆曰：“然则子能致者何？”曰：“可致其身若生人，有 以从容尽平生之意。”陆喜极拜曰：“先生诚致之，顾某骨肉，手足无所措矣。” 曰：“亡夫人周身之衣，亦仿佛能记乎？”曰：“然。”于是择癸丑日，艮宫直 音，空其室，陈设焚香之外，悉无外物。乃备美食，夜分，使陆生公服以俟焉。 老青衣一人侍立。元长曰：“夫人之来，非元长在此不可。元长若去，夫人隐矣。 侍御夫人久丧，枕席单然，魂（以下缺）。”
    　　
    　　
    　　卷四 [111]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [111]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 他日 - Means 'another day' or 'some time later,' indicating a future time.
    • 试以 - Means 'to try with.'
    • 可致其身若生人 - Means 'can bring her body as if she were alive.'
    • 有以从容尽平生之意 - Means 'thereby fulfill all the intentions of life at ease.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, one of the most famous emperors of the Han dynasty, known for his military conquests and cultural achievements.
    • 李夫人 - A favorite concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, known for her beauty and the tragic story of her death.
    • 癸丑日 - A specific day in the Chinese calendar, often used in traditional Chinese texts for precise timing.
    • 艮宫 - One of the eight directions in the Chinese compass, associated with the northeast direction and the element of earth.
    • 直音 - A term that can refer to a direct sound or a straight tone, often used in the context of rituals or specific instructions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元长 - A name or title, likely referring to a person with supernatural abilities.
    • 陆生 - A name or title, likely referring to Lu Sheng, the person performing the ritual.

    One day, Yuan Chang arrived, and Lu Sheng, knowing of his extraordinary skills, tried to tempt him with the story of Emperor Wu of Han and his favorite concubine, Lady Li. Yuan Chang replied, "This is very easy." Lu Sheng asked, "Then can you bring the spirit of my deceased wife for me?" Yuan Chang said, "What I can bring is only her spirit, and you will only get a brief glimpse of her." Yuan Chang found this strange. Lu Sheng asked, "Then what can you bring?" Yuan Chang replied, "I can bring her body as if she were alive, and we can interact at ease, fulfilling all the intentions of life." Lu Sheng, extremely happy, bowed and said, "Master, if you truly bring her, my family and I will be overjoyed." Yuan Chang asked, "Can you remember the entire outfit of your deceased wife?" Lu Sheng replied, "Yes." So they chose the day of Gui Chou, with the direction of Gen Gong and the direct sound. They emptied the room, setting up only incense and nothing else. They then prepared a feast at midnight, having Lu Sheng dress formally and wait. An old servant stood by. Yuan Chang said, "Lady Li's arrival depends on me being here. If I leave, she will disappear. Your wife has been deceased for a long time, and your bed is now empty and lonely, her spirit (the rest is missing)." [111]

    ○马仆射总
    　　
    　　检校右仆射总，元和末节制东平。长庆二年六月十日午时，寝熟，梦二军吏 乘马入中门，及阶而下，一人握刀拱手而前，曰：“都统屈公。”公惊曰：“都 统谁耶？”曰：“见则知矣。”公欲不去，使者曰：“都统之命，仆射不合辞。” 不觉衣服上马。一吏引，一吏从，遂出郓州北郭门数百里，入城又数十里，见城 门题曰：“六押大都统府”。门吏武饰，威容甚严。
    　　
    　　入一二百步，有大衙门，正北百余步，有殿九间，垂帘下有大声曰：“屈上 阶。”阴知其声，乃杜司徒佑也，遂趋而升，二阉竖出卷帘。既而见之，果杜司 徒也。公素承知友，交契甚深，相见极喜，慰劳如平生。遂揖坐，都统曰：“莫 怪奉邀否？佑任此官，年劳将转，上司许自择替。中朝之堪付重权者，今揣量无 逾于阁下者，将欲奉托耳。此官名‘六押大都统’，□□不是过也，且以大庇亲 族知友耳。人之生世，白驹过隙，谁能不死。 [112]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [112]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 检校右仆射 - Title meaning 'Inspector of the Right Privy Councilor,' a high-ranking official position.
    • 寝熟 - Literally 'sleeping soundly,' used here to indicate a deep state of sleep.
    • 梦二军吏 - Dreaming of two military officials, a common motif in classical Chinese literature often symbolizing divine or supernatural messages.
    • 乘马入中门 - Riding horses into the main gate, a vivid description of the officials' arrival.
    • 握刀拱手 - Holding a knife and bowing, a formal gesture indicating respect and authority.
    • 都统屈公 - The Grand Commander summons you, a formal command or invitation.
    • 都统谁耶 - Who is the Grand Commander? a question expressing confusion or curiosity.
    • 见则知矣 - You will know when you see, a phrase indicating that the answer will become clear upon witnessing the event.
    • 欲不去 - Wanting not to go, indicating hesitation or reluctance.
    • 都统之命，仆射不合辞 - The Grand Commander's command, the Privy Councilor should not refuse, emphasizing the necessity of compliance.
    • 不觉衣服上马 - Without realizing, dressed and mounted the horse, describing a sudden and involuntary action.
    • 六押大都统府 - The Sixth Battalion Grand Commander's Office, a specific administrative office.
    • 武饰 - Militarily adorned, describing the appearance of the guards.
    • 威容甚严 - Having a very stern demeanor, indicating a serious and imposing presence.
    • 大衙门 - Grand hall, a large and important building.
    • 垂帘下有大声曰 - Below the curtain, a loud voice said, a dramatic description of the voice.
    • 阴知其声 - Secretly knowing the voice, indicating recognition or familiarity with the voice.
    • 杜司徒佑也 - It is Duke Du You, identifying the person behind the voice.
    • 素承知友 - Having always been acquainted with him, indicating a long-standing friendship.
    • 交契甚深 - Having a deep bond, emphasizing the closeness of the relationship.
    • 相见极喜 - Meeting with great joy, expressing happiness at the reunion.
    • 慰劳如平生 - Comforting and consoling as usual, indicating a normal and familiar interaction.
    • 莫怪奉邀否 - Do not blame me for inviting you, a polite request for understanding.
    • 年劳将转 - Years of service are about to be rewarded, indicating a promotion or change in position.
    • 上司许自择替 - Superiors allow self-selection for replacement, indicating the freedom to choose a successor.
    • 中朝之堪付重权者 - Those in the capital who can be entrusted with heavy responsibilities, referring to capable officials.
    • 揣量无逾于阁下者 - No one surpasses you in capability, a high compliment.
    • 六押大都统 - Sixth Battalion Grand Commander, a specific military rank.
    • 大庇亲属知友 - To greatly protect relatives and friends, indicating a protective role.
    • 白驹过隙 - Like a white horse passing through a crevice, a metaphor for the fleeting nature of life.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 马仆射总 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元和末节制东平 - Refers to the end of the Yuanhe era (806-820 AD) when the official was in charge of Dongping (modern-day Shandong).
    • 长庆二年六月十日 - June 10, 822 AD, during the Changqing era (821-824 AD).
    • 杜司徒佑 - Duke Du You, a prominent Tang Dynasty official known for his military and administrative skills.
    • 六押大都统 - A specific military rank, likely referring to a high-ranking military commander.

    Proper Nouns

    • 马仆射总 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 杜司徒佑 - Duke Du You, a prominent Tang Dynasty official known for his military and administrative skills.
    • 六押大都统 - A specific military rank, likely referring to a high-ranking military commander.
    • 郓州 - A city in Shandong province, China.
    • 元和 - A reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (806-820 AD).
    • 长庆 - A reign title of Emperor Muzong of Tang (821-824 AD).

    Inspector of the Right Privy Councilor Ma Zong, in the late Yuanhe era, was in charge of Dongping. On the sixth day of the sixth month of the second year of Changqing (822 AD), at noon, he was asleep and had a dream in which two military officials rode horses into the main gate, dismounted at the steps, and one of them held a knife and bowed, saying, "The Grand Commander summons you." Ma was startled and asked, "Who is the Grand Commander?" The official replied, "You will know when you see." Ma wanted not to go, but the messenger said, "The Grand Commander's command, the Privy Councilor should not refuse." Without realizing, he dressed and mounted his horse. One official led him, and another followed, and they rode out of the northern gate of Yuncheng (modern-day Shandong) hundreds of miles, then entered the city again for dozens of miles, where they saw a gate inscribed with "The Sixth Battalion Grand Commander's Office." The gate guards were militarily adorned and had a very stern demeanor.

    After entering for a hundred or two hundred steps, they came to a grand hall. A hundred or more steps to the north, there was a nine-roomed hall, and below the curtain, a loud voice said, "Ascend the steps." Secretly knowing the voice, it was Duke Du You. He hurried to ascend, and two eunuchs unrolled the curtain. Upon seeing him, it was indeed Duke Du You. Ma had always been acquainted with him, having a deep bond, and met with great joy, comforting and consoling as usual. They bowed and sat down, and the Grand Commander said, "Do not blame me for inviting you. I am about to be rewarded for my years of service, and superiors allow self-selection for replacement. Those in the capital who can be entrusted with heavy responsibilities, today, no one surpasses you in capability, and I wish to entrust this position to you. This position is called 'Sixth Battalion Grand Commander,' it is not too much, and it will greatly protect relatives and friends. Life in this world is like a white horse passing through a crevice, who can avoid death?" [112]

    而又福不再遇，良时易失，苟非深 分，岂荐自代。权位既到，幸勿因循。”公曰：“生为节制，死岂为民？阳禄方 崇，阴位谁顾。直使为王且不愿，况都统哉？”杜曰：“上请授公，天命难拒。 文符即下，何能违天！”公曰：“天听甚卑，亦从人欲，奈何自取求替，诬其天 命乎？”杜曰：“终与公，公岂能免。”公曰：“终不受，都统安能与？必若以 鬼相逼，岂无天乎？”杜乃顾谓群吏曰：“公既拒，事不谐矣！”公曰：“渴， 请两盂茶。”杜仍促煎茶。从吏曰：“仆射既不住，不合饮此茶。况时热，不可 久住，宜速命驾。” [113]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [113]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 福不再遇 - Classical phrase meaning 'good fortune does not come twice,' emphasizing the rarity of favorable opportunities.
    • 良时易失 - Means 'good times are easily lost,' highlighting the fleeting nature of opportune moments.
    • 苟非深分 - Conditional phrase meaning 'unless there is a deep understanding or bond,' suggesting that true insight or connection is necessary.
    • 岂荐自代 - Means 'how could one recommend oneself in place of another,' implying that self-recommendation is inappropriate or presumptuous.
    • 权位既到 - Means 'once power and position have been attained,' referring to the achievement of a high office.
    • 幸勿因循 - Means 'do not be complacent or negligent,' urging vigilance and diligence in maintaining one's position.
    • 生为节制，死岂为民 - Rhetorical question meaning 'If one lives as a general, how can one die as a commoner?' Emphasizes the importance of maintaining one's status even in death.
    • 阳禄方崇 - Means 'official rank and salary are just rising,' indicating the current elevation in one's career.
    • 阴位谁顾 - Means 'who cares about the afterlife?' Suggests a focus on present rewards over future concerns.
    • 直使为王且不愿 - Means 'even if made king, one would not want it,' expressing a preference for a lower position.
    • 上请授公，天命难拒 - Means 'the emperor requests to grant this to you, and heavenly mandate is hard to resist,' indicating a divine or imperial decree.
    • 文符即下，何能违天 - Means 'once the official document is issued, how can one defy heaven?' Reinforces the idea of inevitability.
    • 天听甚卑，亦从人欲 - Means 'heaven listens closely, and also follows human desires,' suggesting that divine will aligns with human wishes.
    • 奈何自取求替，诬其天命乎 - Means 'how can one seek to replace oneself, thus falsely claiming heavenly mandate?' Criticizes self-serving actions under the guise of divine will.
    • 终不受，都统安能与 - Means 'if one ultimately refuses, how can the general grant it?' Highlights the futility of the offer.
    • 必若以鬼相逼，岂无天乎 - Means 'if it's forced by ghosts, isn't there still heaven?' Questions the legitimacy of supernatural coercion.
    • 渴，请两盂茶 - Means 'thirsty, please bring two cups of tea,' a simple request for refreshment.
    • 仆射既不住，不合饮此茶 - Means 'since the chancellor does not stay, it is not appropriate to drink this tea,' indicating a change in circumstances.
    • 况时热，不可久住，宜速命驾 - Means 'especially in the heat, one cannot stay long, it is best to leave quickly,' suggests urgency due to the weather.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 节制 - Refers to a high military rank, specifically a general or commander.
    • 都统 - Refers to a high-ranking military official, often a commander-in-chief.
    • 仆射 - Refers to a high-ranking civil official, often a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 天命 - Refers to the concept of divine will or mandate, often used in political and religious contexts.
    • 文符 - Refers to official documents or decrees issued by the imperial court.
    • 天听 - Refers to the idea that heaven listens closely to human affairs, often used to justify political decisions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 节制 - A high military rank.
    • 都统 - A high-ranking military official.
    • 仆射 - A high-ranking civil official.
    • 天命 - Divine will or mandate.
    • 文符 - Official documents or decrees.
    • 天听 - The idea that heaven listens closely to human affairs.

    Translation:

    However, good fortune does not come twice, and good times are easily lost. Unless there is a deep understanding, how could one recommend oneself in place of another? Once power and position have been attained, do not be complacent or negligent. The chancellor replied, "If one lives as a general, how can one die as a commoner? Official rank and salary are just rising; who cares about the afterlife? Even if made king, one would not want it, how much more so as a general?" Du said, "The emperor requests to grant this to you, and heavenly mandate is hard to resist. Once the official document is issued, how can one defy heaven!" The chancellor replied, "Heaven listens closely, and also follows human desires. How can one seek to replace oneself, thus falsely claiming heavenly mandate?" Du said, "I will ultimately grant it to you, how can you avoid it?" The chancellor replied, "I will ultimately refuse, how can the general grant it? If it's forced by ghosts, isn't there still heaven?" Du then turned to the officials and said, "Since the chancellor has refused, the matter will not be successful!" The chancellor said, "I am thirsty, please bring two cups of tea." Du still urged the preparation of tea. The attendant said, "Since the chancellor does not stay, it is not appropriate to drink this tea. Especially in the heat, one cannot stay long, it is best to leave quickly." [113]

    俄而牵马立于故处，公辞将去，都统步步送之。既下阶，执手曰：“勉修令 图，此位终奉。”遂乘马南行，旧吏引从如初，乃却从故道（以下疑有阙文）
    　　
    　　○华山客
    　　
    　　党超元者，同州郃阳县人。元和二年隐居华山罗敷水南。明年冬十二月十六 日，夜近二更，天晴月朗，风景甚好，忽闻扣门之声。令童候之，云：“一女子， 年可十七八，容色绝代，异香满路。”超元邀之而入，与坐，言词清辨，风韵甚 高，固非人世之材。良久，曰：“君识妾何人也？”超元曰：“夫人非神仙，即 必非寻常人也。”女曰：“非也。”又曰：“君知妾此来何欲？”超元曰：“不 以陋愚，特垂枕席之欢耳。”女笑曰：“殊不然也。 [114]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [114]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 俄而 - Temporal phrase meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly,' indicating a brief passage of time.
    • 牽馬立於故處 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'lead the horse to stand at the old place,' indicating a specific location.
    • 公辭將去 - Formal expression meaning 'the official (or elder) respectfully declined to stay and was about to leave.'
    • 都统步步送之 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the marshal (or chief) walked step by step to see him off,' indicating a formal farewell.
    • 既下阶，执手曰 - Formal expression meaning 'after descending the steps, they took each other's hands and said,' indicating a moment of farewell.
    • 勉修令图 - Motivational phrase meaning 'strive to improve your future plans,' encouraging the person to work hard.
    • 此位终奉 - Formal expression meaning 'this position will eventually be yours,' indicating a promise or prophecy.
    • 遂乘马南行 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'then he rode south on horseback,' indicating the direction of travel.
    • 旧吏引从如初 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'his former subordinates followed as before,' indicating continuity.
    • 乃却从故道 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'then he returned along the old path,' indicating a return to a previous route.
    • 以下疑有阙文 - Note indicating that the following text may be missing or incomplete.
    • 华山客 - Title or name, likely referring to a person or a story set in Mount Hua.
    • 党超元 - Proper name, likely referring to a specific individual.
    • 同州郃阳县 - Place name, referring to a specific location in ancient China.
    • 元和二年 - Date, referring to the second year of the Yuanhe era during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 隐居华山罗敷水南 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'retired to live south of Luo Fu Water in Mount Hua,' indicating a specific location.
    • 明年冬十二月十六日 - Date, referring to the sixteenth day of the twelfth month of the following year.
    • 夜近二更 - Temporal phrase meaning 'near the second watch of the night,' indicating a specific time.
    • 天晴月朗 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the sky was clear and the moon was bright,' indicating good weather.
    • 风景甚好 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the scenery was very beautiful,' indicating pleasant surroundings.
    • 忽闻扣门之声 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'suddenly heard the sound of knocking on the door,' indicating an unexpected event.
    • 令童候之 - Formal expression meaning 'ordered the servant to wait for her,' indicating a specific action.
    • 年可十七八 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'about seventeen or eighteen years old,' indicating the age of the person.
    • 容色绝代 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'her appearance was extraordinary,' indicating exceptional beauty.
    • 异香满路 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'a strange fragrance filled the road,' indicating a sensory experience.
    • 邀之而入 - Formal expression meaning 'invited her in,' indicating a specific action.
    • 与坐 - Formal expression meaning 'sat with her,' indicating a specific action.
    • 言词清辨 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'her words were clear and eloquent,' indicating her speech.
    • 风韵甚高 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'her grace was very high,' indicating her demeanor.
    • 固非人世之材 - Formal expression meaning 'she was certainly not of this world,' indicating her supernatural nature.
    • 良久 - Temporal phrase meaning 'after a long time,' indicating a passage of time.
    • 君识妾何人也 - Formal expression meaning 'do you know who I am,' indicating a question.
    • 夫人非神仙，即必非寻常人也 - Formal expression meaning 'if you are not a goddess, then you must not be an ordinary person,' indicating a response.
    • 非也 - Formal expression meaning 'no,' indicating a denial.
    • 君知妾此来何欲 - Formal expression meaning 'do you know why I have come here,' indicating a question.
    • 不以陋愚，特垂枕席之欢耳 - Formal expression meaning 'I am not ashamed of my lowly status, but I seek only the pleasure of your bed,' indicating a request.
    • 殊不然也 - Formal expression meaning 'certainly not,' indicating a denial.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 华山 - Famous mountain in China, often associated with Daoist traditions and spiritual practices.
    • 罗敷水 - Specific location in Mount Hua, likely a body of water or a scenic spot.
    • 元和 - Reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, indicating a specific historical period.
    • 隐居 - Practice of retiring from public life to live in seclusion, often associated with Daoist or Confucian ideals.
    • 都统 - Military title, likely referring to a high-ranking official or commander.
    • 旧吏 - Refers to former subordinates or officials, indicating continuity in service.
    • 华山客 - Likely refers to a person or a story set in Mount Hua, possibly a Daoist or hermit figure.
    • 党超元 - Specific individual, likely a historical or literary figure.
    • 同州郃阳县 - Specific location in ancient China, likely a county or town.
    • 元和二年 - The second year of the Yuanhe era (807 AD) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 隐居华山罗敷水南 - Indicates a specific location for retirement, likely a scenic and spiritual place.
    • 明年冬十二月十六日 - The sixteenth day of the twelfth month of the following year, indicating a specific date.
    • 夜近二更 - The second watch of the night, indicating a specific time.
    • 天晴月朗 - Indicates good weather, often associated with auspicious or serene moments.
    • 风景甚好 - Indicates pleasant surroundings, often associated with a peaceful or harmonious setting.
    • 忽闻扣门之声 - Indicates an unexpected event, often associated with supernatural or mysterious occurrences.
    • 年可十七八 - Indicates the age of the person, likely a young woman.
    • 容色绝代 - Indicates exceptional beauty, often associated with supernatural or divine qualities.
    • 异香满路 - Indicates a sensory experience, often associated with supernatural or divine presence.
    • 邀之而入 - Indicates a specific action, often associated with hospitality or respect.
    • 与坐 - Indicates a specific action, often associated with social interaction or conversation.
    • 言词清辨 - Indicates clear and eloquent speech, often associated with intelligence or wisdom.
    • 风韵甚高 - Indicates a graceful demeanor, often associated with nobility or refinement.
    • 固非人世之材 - Indicates supernatural or divine qualities, often associated with otherworldly beings.
    • 良久 - Indicates a passage of time, often associated with deep thought or reflection.
    • 君识妾何人也 - Indicates a question, often associated with curiosity or mystery.
    • 夫人非神仙，即必非寻常人也 - Indicates a response, often associated with supernatural or divine qualities.
    • 非也 - Indicates a denial, often associated with clarity or truth.
    • 君知妾此来何欲 - Indicates a question, often associated with curiosity or mystery.
    • 不以陋愚，特垂枕席之欢耳 - Indicates a request, often associated with intimacy or desire.
    • 殊不然也 - Indicates a denial, often associated with clarity or truth.

    Soon after, the official led the horse to stand at the old place and respectfully declined to stay, preparing to leave. The marshal walked step by step to see him off. After descending the steps, they took each other's hands and said, "Strive to improve your future plans; this position will eventually be yours." Then he rode south on horseback, his former subordinates following as before, and returned along the old path. (The following text is suspected to be missing or incomplete.)

    ○ The Guest of Mount Hua

    Dang Chaoyuan was a native of He County, Tongzhou. In the second year of the Yuanhe era, he retired to live south of Luo Fu Water in Mount Hua. On the sixteenth day of the twelfth month of the following year, near the second watch of the night, the sky was clear and the moon was bright, the scenery was very beautiful. Suddenly, he heard the sound of knocking on the door. He ordered the servant to wait for her, and she said, "A young woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, with an extraordinary appearance, and a strange fragrance filled the road." Dang Chaoyuan invited her in and sat with her. Her words were clear and eloquent, her grace was very high, and she was certainly not of this world. After a long time, she said, "Do you know who I am?" Dang Chaoyuan replied, "If you are not a goddess, then you must not be an ordinary person." The woman said, "No." She then asked, "Do you know why I have come here?" Dang Chaoyuan said, "I am not ashamed of my lowly status, but I seek only the pleasure of your bed." The woman smiled and said, "Certainly not." [114]

    妾非神仙，乃南冢之妖狐也。 学道多年，遂成仙业。今者业满愿足，须从凡例，祈君活之耳。枕席之娱，笑言 之会，不置心中有年矣，乞不以此怀疑，若徇微情，愿以命托。”超元唯唯。又 曰：“妾命后日当死于五坊箭下。来晚猎徒有过者，宜备酒食以待之。彼必问其 所须，即曰：‘亲爱有疾，要一猎狐，能遂私诚，必有殊赠。’以此恳请，其人 必从。赠礼所须，今便留献。”因出束素与党，曰：“得妾之尸，请夜送旧穴。 道成之后，奉报不轻。”乃拜泣而去。 [115]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [115]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 枕席之娛，笑言之會 - Refers to intimate pleasures and conversations, implying a past relationship.
    • 道成之后，奉报不轻 - Means 'after achieving enlightenment, I will repay you generously.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南冢 - Refers to a specific location, possibly a burial ground or a place associated with spirits.
    • 五坊 - Refers to the imperial stables.
    • 束素 - A piece of silk, often used as a gift or token of gratitude in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 南冢 - A specific location, possibly a burial ground or a place associated with spirits.
    • 五坊 - Refers to the imperial stables.
    • 束素 - A piece of silk, often used as a gift or token of gratitude.

    I am a woman, not a divine being, but a fox spirit from the Southern Graveyard. After studying the Dao for many years, I attained the state of being a spirit. Now that my spiritual goals are fulfilled, I must follow the rules of the ordinary world, and I beg you to save my life. The pleasures of our intimate moments and the laughter and conversation we shared have not been in my thoughts for years. I ask you not to doubt me because of this. If you agree to this small request, I will entrust my life to you. My fate is to die tomorrow at the hands of the archers in the imperial stables. Any hunter who passes by late in the evening should be prepared with food and drink for him. He will ask what you need, so you should say: "My beloved is ill and needs a hunting fox to fulfill a private wish, and there will be a special reward." If you make this request, he will surely agree. The gifts he will bring, I leave for you now. I am giving you this silk as a token of my gratitude. If you find my body, please send it back to my old den at night. After achieving enlightenment, I will repay you generously. She bowed and wept before departing. [115]

    至明，乃鬻束素以市酒肉，为待宾之具。其夕，果有五坊猎骑十人来求宿， 遂厚遇之。十人相谓曰：“我猎徒也，宜为衣冠所恶。今党郎倾盖如此，何以报 之？”因问所须，超元曰：“亲戚有疾，医藉猎狐，其疾见困，非此不愈。”乃 祈于诸人：“幸得而见惠，愿奉五素为酒楼费。”十人许诺而去。南行百余步， 有狐突走绕大冢者，作围围之，一箭而毙。其徒喜曰：“昨夜党人固求，今日果 获。”乃持来与超元，奉之五素。既去，超元洗其血，卧于寝床，覆以衣衾。至 夜分人寂，潜送穴中，以土封之。 [116]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [116]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 至明 - Means 'until dawn' or 'at dawn', indicating the time when the action takes place.
    • 乃鬻束素以市酒肉 - Means 'then sold a roll of white silk to buy wine and meat', describing the action of selling silk to purchase food and drink.
    • 為待賓之具 - Means 'to prepare for the guest', indicating the purpose of buying the food and drink.
    • 果有五坊猎骑十人来求宿 - Means 'indeed, ten hunters from the Five Fences came to request lodging', describing the arrival of the hunters.
    • 遂厚遇之 - Means 'then treated them generously', indicating the manner in which the hosts treated the hunters.
    • 我猎徒也，宜为衣冠所恶 - Means 'We are hunters, and should be disliked by the literati', expressing the hunters' self-perception and social standing.
    • 今党郎倾盖如此，何以报之 - Means 'Now that Lord Tang has treated us so generously, how can we repay him?', expressing the hunters' gratitude and desire to reciprocate.
    • 因问所须 - Means 'then asked what was needed', indicating the hunters' inquiry about the host's needs.
    • 超元曰 - Means 'Chao Yuan said', introducing the host's response.
