    • 亲戚有疾，医藉猎狐，其疾见困，非此不愈 - Means 'My relative is ill, and the doctor needs a hunting fox. His illness is worsening, and it will not improve without this', explaining the host's request.
    • 乃祈于诸人 - Means 'then asked the others', indicating the host's request to the hunters.
    • 幸得而见惠，愿奉五素为酒楼费 - Means 'If you could kindly give it to me, I would be willing to offer five rolls of white silk as payment for the wine', expressing the host's offer in exchange for the fox.
    • 十人许诺而去 - Means 'the ten men agreed and left', indicating the hunters' agreement to the host's request.
    • 南行百余步 - Means 'they walked south for more than a hundred steps', describing the hunters' direction and distance traveled.
    • 有狐突走绕大冢者 - Means 'a fox suddenly ran around a large tomb', describing the appearance of the fox.
    • 作围围之，一箭而毙 - Means 'they surrounded it and shot it with an arrow, killing it instantly', describing the hunters' action to capture the fox.
    • 其徒喜曰 - Means 'his companions were delighted and said', introducing the hunters' reaction.
    • 昨夜党人固求，今日果获 - Means 'Last night, Lord Tang earnestly requested it, and today we have indeed obtained it', expressing the hunters' satisfaction in fulfilling the host's request.
    • 乃持来与超元 - Means 'then brought it to Chao Yuan', indicating the delivery of the fox to the host.
    • 奉之五素 - Means 'gave him five rolls of white silk', describing the exchange of the fox for the silk.
    • 既去，超元洗其血 - Means 'After they left, Chao Yuan washed its blood', describing the host's action after the hunters departed.
    • 卧于寝床，覆以衣衾 - Means 'he laid it on the bed and covered it with a quilt', describing the host's care for the fox.
    • 至夜分人寂 - Means 'by midnight when everyone was quiet', indicating the time when the host took further action.
    • 潜送穴中，以土封之 - Means 'he secretly sent it to a den and sealed it with earth', describing the host's final action to release the fox.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 五坊猎骑 - Refers to the Five Fences, a term that could denote a group of hunters or a specific region known for hunting.
    • 党郎 - Refers to Lord Tang, likely a noble or respected individual.
    • 衣冠 - Refers to the literati or educated class, often associated with the gentry or scholar-officials.
    • 亲戚 - Refers to relatives or close family members.
    • 医藉猎狐 - Refers to a medical practice that requires a hunting fox, possibly for medicinal purposes.
    • 酒楼 - Refers to a place where wine is served, often a social gathering spot.
    • 寝床 - Refers to a bed, specifically a sleeping bed.
    • 衣衾 - Refers to a quilt or bedding, used for covering.
    • 夜分 - Refers to midnight, a time of day often associated with quiet and solitude.
    • 穴 - Refers to a den or burrow, a place where animals live or hide.

    Proper Nouns

    • 党郎 - Refers to Lord Tang, a noble or respected individual.
    • 五坊猎骑 - Refers to the Five Fences, a term that could denote a group of hunters or a specific region known for hunting.
    • 超元 - Refers to Chao Yuan, the host.
    • 衣冠 - Refers to the literati or educated class, often associated with the gentry or scholar-officials.
    • 亲戚 - Refers to relatives or close family members.
    • 医 - Refers to a doctor or healer.
    • 酒楼 - Refers to a place where wine is served, often a social gathering spot.
    • 寝床 - Refers to a bed, specifically a sleeping bed.
    • 衣衾 - Refers to a quilt or bedding, used for covering.
    • 夜分 - Refers to midnight, a time of day often associated with quiet and solitude.
    • 穴 - Refers to a den or burrow, a place where animals live or hide.

    By dawn, he had sold a roll of white silk to buy wine and meat, preparing for the guest. That night, indeed, ten hunters from the Five Fences came to request lodging, and he treated them generously. The ten men said to each other, "We are hunters and should be disliked by the literati. Now that Lord Tang has treated us so generously, how can we repay him?" They then asked what was needed, and Chao Yuan said, "My relative is ill, and the doctor needs a hunting fox. His illness is worsening, and it will not improve without this." He then asked the others, "If you could kindly give it to me, I would be willing to offer five rolls of white silk as payment for the wine." The ten men agreed and left, walking south for more than a hundred steps. Suddenly, a fox ran around a large tomb, and they surrounded it, shooting it with an arrow and killing it instantly. His companions were delighted and said, "Last night, Lord Tang earnestly requested it, and today we have indeed obtained it." They then brought it to Chao Yuan and gave him five rolls of white silk. After they left, Chao Yuan washed its blood, laid it on the bed, and covered it with a quilt. By midnight when everyone was quiet, he secretly sent it to a den and sealed it with earth. [116]

    后七日夜半，复有扣门者，超元出视，乃前女子也，又延入。泣谢曰：“道 业虽成，准例当死，为人所食，无计复生。今蒙深恩，特全毙质，修理得活，以 证此身。磨顶至踵，无以奉报。人尘已去，云驾有期，仙路遥遥，难期会面。请 从此辞。药金五十斤，收充赠谢。此金每两值四十缗，非胡客勿示。”乃出其金， 再拜而去，且曰：“金乌未分，有青云出于冢上者，妾去之候也。火宅之中，愁 焰方炽，能思静理，少息俗心，亦可一念之间，暂臻凉地。勉之！勉之！”言讫 而去。明晨专视，果有青云出于冢上，良久方散。
    　　
    　　人验其金，真奇宝也。即日携入市，市人只酬常价。后数年，忽有胡客来诣， 曰：“知君有异金，愿一观之。”超元出示，胡笑曰：“此乃九天掖金，君何以 致之？”于是每两酬四十缗，收之而去。后不知其所在耳。 [117]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [117]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 後七日夜半 - Time reference meaning 'seven days after midnight.'
    • 復有扣門者 - Phrase meaning 'there was again someone knocking at the door.'
    • 泣謝 - Means 'to weep and thank.'
    • 道業雖成 - Means 'even though the spiritual path has been completed.'
    • 准例當死 - Means 'by rule, one should die.'
    • 無計復生 - Means 'there is no way to be reborn.'
    • 蒙深恩 - Means 'to receive deep grace.'
    • 特全毙质 - Means 'to specially preserve the mortal body.'
    • 修理得活 - Means 'to be repaired and thus live again.'
    • 以证此身 - Means 'to prove this body.'
    • 磨顶至踵 - Means 'from head to toe.'
    • 无以奉报 - Means 'there is nothing to repay.'
    • 人尘已去 - Means 'the dust of the human world has departed.'
    • 云驾有期 - Means 'the chariot of the clouds has a set time.'
    • 仙路遥遥 - Means 'the path to immortality is long and distant.'
    • 请从此辞 - Means 'please allow me to take my leave.'
    • 药金五十斤 - Means 'fifty pounds of medicinal gold.'
    • 收充赠谢 - Means 'to collect and use as a gift and thanks.'
    • 每两值四十缗 - Means 'each pound is worth forty strings of cash.'
    • 非胡客勿示 - Means 'do not show to anyone except a foreigner.'
    • 金乌未分 - Means 'before the golden bird (sun) has risen.'
    • 青云出于冢上 - Means 'blue clouds emerge from the grave.'
    • 妾去之候也 - Means 'the time when I am leaving.'
    • 火宅之中 - Means 'in the house of fire' (a metaphor for the world).
    • 愁焰方炽 - Means 'the flames of sorrow are just beginning to blaze.'
    • 能思静理 - Means 'if one can think of calm principles.'
    • 少息俗心 - Means 'to slightly rest from worldly desires.'
    • 暂臻凉地 - Means 'to temporarily reach a cool place.'
    • 勉之 - Means 'encourage yourself.'
    • 言讫而去 - Means 'after speaking, he/she left.'
    • 果有青云出于冢上 - Means 'indeed, blue clouds emerged from the grave.'
    • 真奇宝也 - Means 'truly a rare treasure.'
    • 携入市 - Means 'carried to the market.'
    • 市人只酬常价 - Means 'the market people only paid the regular price.'
    • 忽有胡客来诣 - Means 'suddenly, a foreigner came to visit.'
    • 知君有异金 - Means 'knowing that you have unusual gold.'
    • 愿一观之 - Means 'to wish to see it once.'
    • 乃出其金 - Means 'then he/she took out the gold.'
    • 此乃九天掖金 - Means 'this is the gold from the celestial treasury.'
    • 君何以致之 - Means 'how did you obtain it?'
    • 收之而去 - Means 'he took it and left.'
    • 后不知其所在耳 - Means 'later, no one knew where he/she was.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 道业 - Refers to the spiritual path or religious practice.
    • 准例 - Refers to rules or regulations, often in a religious or legal context.
    • 道业虽成，准例当死 - A reference to the idea that even if one has completed their spiritual path, they may still be subject to certain rules or fates.
    • 药金 - Refers to medicinal gold, a type of alchemical substance believed to have healing properties.
    • 九天掖金 - Refers to gold from the celestial treasury, a metaphor for extremely valuable or divine treasure.
    • 火宅 - A Buddhist metaphor for the world, likening it to a burning house.
    • 青云 - A symbol of good fortune and high status, often associated with the heavens or immortals.
    • 胡客 - Refers to foreigners, often used in a negative or xenophobic context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 无 - No proper nouns are present in the text.

    Seven days after midnight, there was again someone knocking at the door. Chao Yuan went out to see, and it was the same woman who had come before. He invited her in again. She wept and thanked him, saying, "Even though my spiritual path has been completed, by rule, one should die. Being consumed by others, there is no way to be reborn. Now I have received your deep grace, and you have specially preserved my mortal body. I have been repaired and thus live again, to prove this body. From head to toe, there is nothing to repay. The dust of the human world has departed, and the chariot of the clouds has a set time. The path to immortality is long and distant, and it is difficult to meet again. Please allow me to take my leave. I have fifty pounds of medicinal gold, which I will use as a gift and thanks. This gold is worth forty strings of cash per pound, and do not show it to anyone except a foreigner." Then she took out the gold, bowed twice, and left, saying, "Before the golden bird (sun) has risen, if blue clouds emerge from the grave, that is the time when I am leaving. In the house of fire, the flames of sorrow are just beginning to blaze. If one can think of calm principles and slightly rest from worldly desires, one can temporarily reach a cool place. Encourage yourself! Encourage yourself!" After speaking, she left. The next morning, he observed, and indeed, blue clouds emerged from the grave, lingering for a long time before dissipating.

    The people tested the gold, and it was truly a rare treasure. That day, he carried it to the market, but the market people only paid the regular price. Several years later, suddenly, a foreigner came to visit, saying, "Knowing that you have unusual gold, I wish to see it once." Chao Yuan showed it to him, and the foreigner laughed, saying, "This is the gold from the celestial treasury. How did you obtain it?" Then he paid forty strings of cash per pound, took it, and left. Later, no one knew where he was. [117]

    ○尹纵之
    　　
    　　尹纵之，元和四年八月肄业中条山西峰。月朗风清，必吟啸鼓琴以怡中。一 夕，闻檐外履步之声，若女子行者。纵之遥谓曰：“行者何人？”曰：“妾山下 王氏女，所居不远，每闻郎君吟咏鼓琴之声，未尝不倾耳向风，凝思于蓬户。以 父母训严，不敢来听。今夕之亲有适人者，父母俱往，妾乃独止。复闻久慕之声， 故来潜听。不期郎之闻也。”纵之曰：“居止接近，相见是常。既来听琴，何不 入坐？”纵之出迎，女子乃拜。纵之略复之，引以入户，设榻命坐。仪貌风态， 绰约异常，但耳稍黑。纵之以为真村女之尤者也。山居闲寂，颇积愁思，得此甚 惬心也。命仆夫具果煮茗，弹琴以怡之。山深景静，琴思清远，女意欢极。因留 宿，女辞曰：“父母如何？”纵之曰：“喜会是赴，固不夜归。五更潜复闭户为 独宿者，父母曙到，亦何觉之。”女笑而止。相得之欢，誓将白首。绸缪之意， 无不备尽。 [118]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [118]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元和四年八月 - Specific date in the reign of Emperor Yuan of Tang, indicating a precise historical context.
    • 肄业中条山西峰 - "肄业" means "to study" or "to engage in scholarly pursuits," and "中条山西峰" refers to a mountain peak in the Zhongtiao Mountains.
    • 吟啸鼓琴 - "吟啸" means "to sing and shout," often used to describe a carefree and joyful state; "鼓琴" means "to play the qin" (a traditional Chinese zither).
    • 行者何人 - A direct question asking "Who is walking here?"
    • 妾山下王氏女 - "妾" is a humble term used by women to refer to themselves, and "王氏女" means "a woman of the Wang family."
    • 所居不远 - Means "my residence is not far away."
    • 倾耳向风，凝思于蓬户 - "倾耳向风" means "to listen intently to the wind," and "凝思于蓬户" means "to think deeply while sitting in a thatched hut."
    • 父母训严 - Means "my parents are strict in their teachings."
    • 适人 - Means "to marry someone."
    • 久慕之声 - Means "the sound of long admiration."
    • 潜听 - Means "to listen secretly."
    • 不期郎之闻也 - Means "I did not expect the young master to hear me."
    • 居止接近 - Means "our residences are close to each other."
    • 相见是常 - Means "meeting is a common occurrence."
    • 何不入坐 - Means "why not come in and sit down?"
    • 略复之 - Means "to lightly return the courtesy."
    • 设榻命坐 - Means "to set up a mat and invite to sit."
    • 仪貌风态 - Means "appearance and demeanor."
    • 绰约异常 - Means "graceful and unusual."
    • 耳稍黑 - Means "the ears are slightly dark."
    • 真村女之尤者也 - Means "a particularly beautiful village girl."
    • 山居闲寂 - Means "living in the mountains, which is quiet and secluded."
    • 颇积愁思 - Means "accumulating a lot of worries."
    • 惬心也 - Means "satisfying the heart."
    • 命仆夫具果煮茗 - Means "to order the servant to prepare fruits and tea."
    • 弹琴以怡之 - Means "to play the qin to please her."
    • 山深景静 - Means "the mountains are deep and the scenery is quiet."
    • 琴思清远 - Means "the thoughts expressed through the qin are clear and distant."
    • 女意欢极 - Means "the girl's joy is extreme."
    • 父母如何 - Means "what about my parents?"
    • 喜会是赴，固不夜归 - Means "since we are happy to meet, I won't return home at night."
    • 五更潜复闭户为独宿者 - Means "at the fifth watch of the night, secretly close the door to sleep alone."
    • 父母曙到，亦何觉之 - Means "when my parents arrive at dawn, they will not notice."
    • 笑而止 - Means "laugh and stop."
    • 相得之欢，誓将白首 - Means "the joy of getting along well, vowing to be together until old age."
    • 绸缪之意，无不备尽 - Means "the affectionate feelings, all fully expressed."

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元和四年八月 - The year 809 AD during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Tang.
    • 中条山西峰 - The Zhongtiao Mountains, located in Shanxi Province, China.
    • 吟啸鼓琴 - A common practice in ancient China, where scholars and literati would often sing, shout, and play the qin to express their emotions and thoughts.
    • 妾 - A humble term used by women, reflecting the social norms of the time.
    • 父母训严 - Emphasizes the strictness of parental education, which was highly valued in traditional Chinese society.
    • 适人 - Refers to marriage, a significant social and cultural event in ancient China.
    • 久慕之声 - Indicates the deep admiration and longing of the girl for the scholar's music.
    • 潜听 - Reflects the girl's cautious and respectful behavior, typical of the time.
    • 山居闲寂 - Describes the quiet and peaceful life in the mountains, often associated with scholars seeking solitude and inspiration.
    • 颇积愁思 - Suggests the scholar's inner turmoil and worries, which are common themes in classical Chinese literature.
    • 惬心也 - Expresses the satisfaction and contentment derived from the girl's presence.
    • 命仆夫具果煮茗 - Shows the scholar's hospitality and the importance of tea and fruits in Chinese culture.
    • 弹琴以怡之 - Highlights the role of music in classical Chinese literature and the emotional connection between the scholar and the girl.
    • 山深景静 - Describes the serene and tranquil environment, often used in classical Chinese poetry to evoke a sense of peace and harmony.
    • 琴思清远 - Conveys the deep and clear thoughts expressed through the qin, reflecting the scholar's inner world.
    • 女意欢极 - Expresses the girl's extreme joy, emphasizing the emotional bond between them.
    • 父母如何 - Reflects the girl's concern for her parents, showing her filial piety.
    • 喜会是赴，固不夜归 - Demonstrates the scholar's willingness to stay and enjoy the moment, despite the potential consequences.
    • 五更潜复闭户为独宿者 - Describes the girl's secret plan to stay overnight, highlighting her boldness and the scholar's understanding.
    • 父母曙到，亦何觉之 - Shows the girl's consideration for her parents, ensuring they would not notice her absence.
    • 笑而止 - Conveys the girl's playful and affectionate nature.
    • 相得之欢，誓将白首 - Expresses the deep bond and commitment between the scholar and the girl.
    • 绸缪之意，无不备尽 - Emphasizes the completeness and depth of their affectionate feelings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 尹纵之 - The name of the scholar.
    • 中条山西峰 - The specific location in the Zhongtiao Mountains.
    • 元和四年八月 - The specific date in the reign of Emperor Yuan of Tang.
    • 王氏女 - The name of the girl, indicating her family background.
    • 父母 - The girl's parents.

    尹纵之，元和四年八月在中条山西峰读书。月光明亮，风清气爽，他常常吟诗啸歌，弹琴自娱。一天晚上，他听到屋檐外有脚步声，像是女子走动。尹纵之远远问道：“你是谁？”女子回答说：“我是山下的王氏女子，住处不远，每次听到郎君吟诗弹琴的声音，总是倾耳聆听，凝神于茅屋之中。因父母管教严厉，不敢前来听。今晚父母有亲事，一同前往，我独自留下。又听到久慕之声，所以前来偷听。没想到被郎君听见了。”尹纵之说：“我们住得近，相见是常事。既然来听琴，为什么不进来坐坐？”尹纵之出门迎接，女子便行礼。尹纵之略施礼，将她请进屋，设下坐榻请她坐下。女子仪态风姿，绰约异常，只是耳朵稍黑。尹纵之认为她是村中女子中最美的。山居生活闲适寂静，他心中积了不少愁思，有了她的陪伴，心情十分舒畅。他吩咐仆人准备水果和茶水，弹琴以娱她。山深景静，琴声清远，女子听后非常高兴。于是尹纵之留她过夜，女子推辞说：“父母怎么办？”尹纵之说：“既然高兴相会，就不必连夜回家。五更时分，悄悄关门独宿，父母天亮来，也不会察觉。”女子笑着同意了。两人相处十分愉快，发誓要白头偕老。彼此的深情厚意，无不尽情表达。 [118]

    天欲曙，衣服将归，纵之深念，虑其得归而难召也，思留质以系之。顾床有 青花毡履，遽起取一只锁于柜中。女泣曰：“妾贫，无他履，所以承足止此耳。 郎若留之，当跣足而去，父母召问，何以说告焉？杖固不辞，绝将来之望也。” 纵之不听，女泣曰：“妾父母严，闻此恶声，不复存命。岂以承欢一宵，遂令死 谢？缱绻之言，声未绝矣，必忘陋拙，许再侍枕席，每夕尊长寝后，犹可潜来。 若终留之，终将杀妾，非深念之道也。绸缪之欢，弃不旋踵耳，且信誓安在？” 又拜乞曰：“但请与之，一夕不至，任言于邻里。”自五更至晓，泣拜床前，言 辞万端。纵之以其辞恳，益疑，坚留之。将明，又不敢住，又泣曰：“妾前生负 郎君，送命于此。然郎之用心，神理所殛，修文求名，终无成矣！”收泪而去。 [119]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [119]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天欲曙 - Means 'the sky is about to dawn,' indicating the time of day.
    • 衣服将归 - Literally 'clothes will return,' but in context, it likely means 'the clothes will be returned' or 'the clothes will be put back on.'
    • 纵之深念 - Means 'he deeply pondered' or 'he thought deeply.'
    • 虑其得归而难召也 - Means 'he worried that if she returns, it will be difficult to call her back.'
    • 思留质以系之 - Means 'he thought of keeping a pledge to bind her.'
    • 顾床有青花毡履 - Means 'looking at the bed, there was a green-patterned felt shoe.'
    • 遽起取一只锁于柜中 - Means 'he hastily got up and took one and locked it in a cabinet.'
    • 妾贫，无他履 - Means 'I am poor, and I have no other shoes.'
    • 所以承足止此耳 - Means 'this is the only one I have to support my feet.'
    • 郎若留之，当跣足而去 - Means 'if you keep it, I will go barefoot.'
    • 父母召问，何以说告焉 - Means 'what will I tell my parents when they ask?'
    • 杖固不辞，绝将来之望也 - Means 'I won't refuse the cane (punishment), but it will cut off all future hopes.'
    • 缱绻之言，声未绝矣 - Means 'our affectionate words, their sound has not yet faded.'
    • 必忘陋拙，许再侍枕席 - Means 'please forget my clumsiness and allow me to serve you again in bed.'
    • 每夕尊长寝后，犹可潜来 - Means 'every night after the elders go to sleep, I can still come secretly.'
    • 若终留之，终将杀妾 - Means 'if you keep it, you will eventually kill me.'
    • 非深念之道也 - Means 'it is not the way of deep thought.'
    • 绸缪之欢，弃不旋踵耳 - Means 'the intimate pleasure will be abandoned in an instant.'
    • 且信誓安在 - Means 'and where is the trust and oath?'
    • 又拜乞曰 - Means 'she then bowed and begged.'
    • 但请与之，一夕不至，任言于邻里 - Means 'just give it to me; if I don't come for one night, you can tell the neighbors.'
    • 自五更至晓，泣拜床前，言辞万端 - Means 'from the fifth watch until dawn, she wept and bowed at the foot of the bed, speaking in countless ways.'
    • 纵之以其辞恳，益疑，坚留之 - Means 'he was moved by her earnest words and became even more doubtful, so he firmly kept her.'
    • 将明，又不敢住 - Means 'as it was getting light, she dared not stay.'
    • 收泪而去 - Means 'she wiped away her tears and left.'
    • 妾前生负郎君 - Means 'I owe you from my past life.'
    • 送命于此 - Means 'to give my life here.'
    • 然郎之用心，神理所殛 - Means 'but your heart's intent is punished by divine justice.'
    • 修文求名，终无成矣 - Means 'seeking fame through writing will ultimately be in vain.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天欲曙 - Refers to the early morning, a time often associated with new beginnings or transitions.
    • 衣服将归 - Likely refers to the act of getting dressed, a common morning ritual.
    • 纵之深念 - Indicates deep thought or contemplation, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 虑其得归而难召也 - Reflects the concern over loss and the difficulty of regaining something once it is lost.
    • 思留质以系之 - Suggests the use of a pledge or token to bind someone, a common practice in ancient times.
    • 顾床有青花毡履 - Refers to a specific item (a green-patterned felt shoe) that serves as a symbol or token.
    • 遽起取一只锁于柜中 - Describes a hurried action, indicating urgency or importance.
    • 妾贫，无他履 - Highlights the character's poverty and the value of the single item she possesses.
    • 郎若留之，当跣足而去 - Suggests the extreme measures the character is willing to take to regain her item.
    • 父母召问，何以说告焉 - Reflects the social pressure and the need to explain actions to family.
    • 杖固不辞，绝将来之望也 - Indicates the character's willingness to face punishment but the fear of losing future opportunities.
    • 缱绻之言，声未绝矣 - Suggests the lingering effects of past affection and promises.
    • 必忘陋拙，许再侍枕席 - Reflects the character's desire for reconciliation and continued intimacy.
    • 每夕尊长寝后，犹可潜来 - Describes a secretive and nocturnal arrangement, common in stories of forbidden love.
    • 若终留之，终将杀妾 - Suggests the extreme consequences of the character's actions.
    • 非深念之道也 - Indicates that the current situation is not aligned with deep thought or wisdom.
    • 绸缪之欢，弃不旋踵耳 - Suggests the fleeting nature of intimate pleasure.
    • 且信誓安在 - Reflects the character's concern about broken promises and trust.
    • 又拜乞曰 - Describes a humble and desperate plea, a common literary device in classical Chinese texts.
    • 但请与之，一夕不至，任言于邻里 - Suggests a conditional agreement, with the threat of public exposure.
    • 自五更至晓，泣拜床前，言辞万端 - Describes a prolonged and emotional plea, a common narrative technique.
    • 纵之以其辞恳，益疑，坚留之 - Reflects the character's influence and the complexity of the situation.
    • 将明，又不敢住 - Indicates the character's fear and the urgency of the situation.
    • 收泪而去 - Describes the character's final act of resignation and departure.
    • 妾前生负郎君 - Suggests a karmic or spiritual connection between the characters.
    • 送命于此 - Indicates the character's willingness to sacrifice her life.
    • 然郎之用心，神理所殛 - Reflects the character's belief in divine justice and the consequences of the other character's actions.
    • 修文求名，终无成矣 - Suggests the futility of seeking fame through writing, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

    The sky was just beginning to dawn, and the clothes were about to be put back on. He deeply pondered, worrying that if she returned, it would be difficult to call her back. He thought of keeping a pledge to bind her. Looking at the bed, there was a green-patterned felt shoe. He hastily got up and took one and locked it in a cabinet. The woman wept and said, "I am poor and have no other shoes. This is the only one I have to support my feet. If you keep it, I will go barefoot. What will I tell my parents when they ask? I won't refuse the cane, but it will cut off all future hopes." He did not listen to her, and she wept again, saying, "My parents are strict. If they hear this bad news, they will not spare my life. Is it worth my dying for one night of pleasure? Our affectionate words, their sound has not yet faded. Please forget my clumsiness and allow me to serve you again in bed. Every night after the elders go to sleep, I can still come secretly. If you keep it, you will eventually kill me. It is not the way of deep thought. The intimate pleasure will be abandoned in an instant, and where is the trust and oath?" She then bowed and begged, "Just give it to me. If I don't come for one night, you can tell the neighbors." From the fifth watch until dawn, she wept and bowed at the foot of the bed, speaking in countless ways. He was moved by her earnest words and became even more doubtful, so he firmly kept her. As it was getting light, she dared not stay, and she wept again, saying, "I owe you from my past life. To give my life here. But your heart's intent is punished by divine justice. Seeking fame through writing will ultimately be in vain!" She wiped away her tears and left. [119]

    纵之以通宵之倦，忽寝熟，日及窗方觉，闻床前腥气，起而视之，则一方凝 血在地，点点而去。开柜验毡履，乃猪蹄壳也。遽策杖寻血而行，至山下王朝猪 圈，血踪入焉。乃视之，一大母猪，无后右蹄壳，血引墙下，见纵之怒目而走。 纵之告王朝，朝执弓矢逐之，一矢而毙。其年纵之山下求贡，虽声华籍盛，终终 无成，岂负之罪欤？
    　　
    　　○王煌
    　　
    　　太原王煌，元和三年五月初申时，自洛之缑氏庄。乃出建春门二十五里，道 左有新冢，前有白衣姬设祭而哭甚哀。煌微觇之，年适十八九，容色绝代。傍有 二婢，无丈夫。侍婢曰：“小娘子秦人，既笄适河东裴直，未二年，裴郎乃游洛 不复，小娘子讶焉，与某辈二人，偕来到洛，则裴已卒矣。其夫葬于此，故来祭 哭耳。”煌曰：“然即何归？”曰：“小娘子少孤无家，何归？顷婚礼者外族， 其舅已亡。今且驻洛，必谋从人耳。”煌喜曰：“煌有正官，少而无妇。 [120]

    A man exhausted after a night of weariness suddenly fell into a deep sleep. He woke up when the sun reached the window and noticed a strong smell of blood near his bed. He got up and saw a pool of coagulated blood on the ground, with drops leading away. He opened a cabinet to check his felt shoes and found they were covered in pig's hooves. Hurriedly taking a staff, he followed the blood trail to the pigpen at the foot of the mountain. There, he saw a large sow missing its right hind hoof, with blood leading to the wall. The sow glared at him and ran off. He reported this to Wang Huang, who took his bow and arrows and shot the sow, killing it with one arrow. That year, Wang Huang sought contributions from the mountain, but despite his fame and reputation being at their peak, he ultimately achieved nothing. Was this due to some burden he bore?

    ○ Wang Huang

    Wang Huang of Taiyuan, during the 3rd year of the Yuanhe era (808 CE) in the 5th month, on the hour of Shen (1-3 PM), traveled from Luoyang to Gouzi Village. He exited Jianchun Gate and traveled twenty-five miles, where he saw a new grave on the left side of the road. In front of the grave, a young woman dressed in white was offering a sacrifice and crying bitterly. Wang Huang secretly observed her and found her to be about eighteen or nineteen years old, with extraordinary beauty. Two maids stood beside her, but there was no man present. One of the maids said, "This young lady is from Qin. After her coming-of-age ceremony, she married Pei Zhi of Hedong. Before two years had passed, Pei Zhi went to Luoyang and did not return. The young lady was surprised and, accompanied by two of us, came to Luoyang, only to find that Pei Zhi had already died. Her husband is buried here, so she has come to offer a sacrifice and cry." Wang Huang asked, "If so, where will she go?" The maid replied, "The young lady was orphaned at a young age and has no family. Where can she go? Her husband's family is from another clan, and her uncle has already died. For now, she will stay in Luoyang and must seek a new husband." Wang Huang was pleased and said, "I have a government post and am young and unmarried." [120]

    庄居缑 氏，亦不甚贫，今愿领微诚，试为咨达。”婢笑，徐诣姬言之。姬闻而哭愈哀， 婢牵衣止之，曰：“今日将夕矣，野外无所止，归秦无生业。今此郎幸有正官而 少年，行李且赡，固不急于衣食。必欲他行，舍此何适？若未能抑情从变，亦得 归体，奈何不听其言耶？”姬曰：“吾结发事裴，今客死洛下，绸缪之情，已隔 明晦。碎身粉骨，无谢裴恩。未展哀诚，岂忍他适。汝勿言，吾且当还洛。”其 婢以告煌，煌又曰：“归洛非有第宅，决为客之于缑，何伤？”婢复以告。姬顾 日将夕，回称所抵，乃敛哀拜煌，言礼欲申，哀咽良久。 [121]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [121]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 庄居缑氏 - Compound noun meaning 'residing in Gou,' a place name.
    • 亦不甚贫 - Means 'is not very poor.'
    • 试为咨达 - Means 'please try to convey this message.'
    • 绸缪之情 - Refers to deep, intimate feelings.
    • 已隔明晦 - Means 'already separated by light and darkness,' suggesting a great distance or time.
    • 碎身粉骨 - A hyperbolic expression meaning 'to break one's body and powder one's bones,' indicating extreme devotion or sacrifice.
    • 无谢裴恩 - Means 'no gratitude can repay the grace of Pei.'
    • 未展哀诚 - Means 'have not yet expressed grief and sincerity.'
    • 岂忍他适 - Means 'how could one bear to go elsewhere.'
    • 归洛非有第宅 - Means 'returning to Luoyang has no mansion or residence.'
    • 决为客之于缑 - Means 'decide to be a guest in Gou.'
    • 何伤 - Means 'what harm.'
    • 敛哀拜煌 - Means 'gather one's grief and bow to Huang.'
    • 言礼欲申 - Means 'intend to express respect and propriety.'
    • 哀咽良久 - Means 'grieve and sob for a long time.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 缑氏 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in ancient China.
    • 裴 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 洛 - Refers to Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 秦 - Refers to Qin, another place name or region.
    • 正官 - Refers to a high-ranking official.
    • 行李且赡 - Means 'traveling expenses are generous.'
    • 结发事裴 - Means 'married and served Pei,' indicating a long-term relationship or service.
    • 客死洛下 - Means 'died as a guest in Luoyang.'
    • 明晦 - Refers to light and darkness, often used metaphorically to indicate a great distance or time.
    • 他适 - Means 'go elsewhere.'
    • 体 - Refers to the body or physical form.
    • 第宅 - Refers to a mansion or residence.
    • 客之于缑 - Means 'be a guest in Gou.'
    • 何伤 - Means 'what harm.'
    • 敛哀 - Means 'gather one's grief.'
    • 申 - Means 'express' or 'declare.'
    • 哀咽 - Means 'grieve and sob.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 缑氏 - A place name.
    • 裴 - A proper name.
    • 洛 - A place name (Luoyang).
    • 秦 - A place name or region.
    • 煌 - A proper name.

    Residing in Gou, she is not very poor. Now, I wish to convey this message, please try to communicate it. The maid smiled and slowly went to tell the lady. Upon hearing the message, the lady wept even more sorrowfully. The maid held her robe and stopped her, saying, "It is almost evening now, there is nowhere to stay in the wild, and returning to Qin has no means of livelihood. Now this young man, fortunately, is a high-ranking official and young, with generous traveling expenses, so there is no urgent need for clothing and food. If you must go elsewhere, where else can you go? If you cannot suppress your emotions and accept the change, you can return to your body, why not listen to his words?" The lady said, "I married and served Pei, and now died as a guest in Luoyang. Our deep, intimate feelings have already been separated by light and darkness. Even if I break my body and powder my bones, there is no gratitude that can repay Pei's grace. I have not yet expressed my grief and sincerity, how could I bear to go elsewhere. Do not speak, I will return to Luoyang." The maid reported this to Huang, who said, "Returning to Luoyang has no mansion or residence, decide to be a guest in Gou, what harm?" The maid relayed this again. The lady, looking at the approaching evening, returned to her destination, then gathered her grief and bowed to Huang, intending to express respect and propriety, and grieved and sobbed for a long time. [121]

    煌召左右师骑。与煌同行十余里，偕宿彭婆店，礼设别榻。每闻煌言，必呜 咽而泣，不敢不以礼待之。先曙而到芝田别业，于中堂泣而言曰：“妾诚陋拙， 不足辱君子之顾。身今无归，已沐深念。请备礼席，展相见之仪。”煌遽令陈设， 对食毕，入成结褵之礼，自是相欢之意，日愈殷勤。观其容容婉娩，言词闲雅， 工容之妙，卓绝当时。信誓之诚，惟死而已。 [122]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [122]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 煌召左右师骑 - Huang summoned his attendant soldiers, where '左右师骑' refers to the soldiers or guards around Huang.
    • 与煌同行十余里，偕宿彭婆店 - Traveled with Huang for more than ten miles and stayed together at Pengbo Inn.
    • 礼设别榻 - Arranged a separate bed according to etiquette.
    • 每闻煌言，必呜咽而泣 - Every time they heard Huang speak, they would weep uncontrollably.
    • 不敢不以礼待之 - They could not help but treat him with courtesy.
    • 先曙而到芝田别业 - Arrived at the Zhitian Villa before dawn.
    • 于中堂泣而言曰 - In the main hall, they wept and said...
    • 妾诚陋拙，不足辱君子之顾 - I am truly plain and clumsy, unworthy of the noble gentleman's attention.
    • 身今无归，已沐深念 - Now without a place to return to, I have been deeply thought of.
    • 请备礼席，展相见之仪 - Please prepare a banquet, to show the proper etiquette of meeting.
    • 煌遽令陈设 - Huang immediately ordered the preparations.
    • 对食毕，入成结褵之礼 - After eating together, they proceeded to the formal marriage ceremony.
    • 自是相欢之意，日愈殷勤 - From then on, their mutual affection grew ever more earnest.
    • 观其容容婉娩，言词闲雅 - Seeing her graceful and gentle demeanor, and her elegant speech.
    • 工容之妙，卓绝当时 - The beauty of her appearance was unparalleled at the time.
    • 信誓之诚，惟死而已 - The sincerity of her vows was such that nothing but death could change them.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 煌 - Likely refers to a person named Huang, possibly a noble or a significant figure.
    • 彭婆店 - Refers to Pengbo Inn, a specific location where the characters stayed.
    • 芝田别业 - Refers to Zhitian Villa, a place of residence or retreat.
    • 结褵之礼 - Refers to the formal marriage ceremony, a significant cultural practice in ancient China.
    • 信誓之诚 - Refers to the solemnity and sincerity of vows, a concept deeply rooted in traditional Chinese values.

    Proper Nouns

    • 煌 - The name of a person, likely a noble or significant figure.
    • 彭婆店 - The name of an inn or lodging place.
    • 芝田别业 - The name of a villa or residence.
    • 结褵之礼 - A formal term for the marriage ceremony.

    Huang summoned his attendant soldiers and traveled with him for more than ten miles, staying together at Pengbo Inn. He arranged a separate bed according to etiquette. Every time they heard Huang speak, they would weep uncontrollably, unable to help but treat him with courtesy. They arrived at the Zhitian Villa before dawn, in the main hall, they wept and said, "I am truly plain and clumsy, unworthy of the noble gentleman's attention. Now without a place to return to, I have been deeply thought of. Please prepare a banquet, to show the proper etiquette of meeting." Huang immediately ordered the preparations. After eating together, they proceeded to the formal marriage ceremony. From then on, their mutual affection grew ever more earnest. Seeing her graceful and gentle demeanor, and her elegant speech, the beauty of her appearance was unparalleled at the time. The sincerity of her vows was such that nothing but death could change them. [122]

    后数月，煌有故入洛。洛中有道士任玄言者，奇术之士也，素与煌善，见煌 颜色，大异之，曰：“郎何所偶，致形神如久耶？”煌笑曰：“纳一夫人耳。” 玄言曰：“所偶非夫人，乃威神之鬼也。令能速绝，尚可生全。更一二十日，生 路即断矣，玄言亦无能奉救也。”煌心不悦，以所谋之事未果，白不遗人请归〔 此句疑有脱文〕，其意尤切。缠绵之思，不可形状。
    　　
    　　更十余日，煌复入洛，遇玄言于南市，执其手而告曰：“郎之容色决死矣， 不信吾言，乃至如是，明日午时，其人当来，来即死矣。惜哉？惜哉？”因泣与 煌别，煌愈惑之。玄言曰：“郎不相信，请置符于怀中。明日午时，贤宠入门， 请以符投之，当见本形矣。”煌及取其符而怀之。既背去，玄言谓其仆曰：“明 日午时，芝田妖当来，汝郎必以符投之。汝可视其形状，非青面耐重鬼，即赤面 者也。入反坐汝郎，郎必死。死时视之，坐死耶？”其仆潜记之。 [123]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [123]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 后数月 - Indicates a period of time after a certain event, translating to 'several months later.'
    • 奇术之士 - Refers to someone skilled in extraordinary or magical arts, which can be translated as 'a sorcerer' or 'a magician.'
    • 素与煌善 - Means 'has always been friendly with Huang,' indicating a longstanding relationship.
    • 大异之 - Expresses a strong reaction, translating to 'was greatly surprised' or 'found it very strange.'
    • 纳一夫人耳 - Literally means 'has taken a wife,' which can be translated as 'has taken a wife.'
    • 威神之鬼 - Refers to a spirit with great power, translating to 'a powerful spirit.'
    • 令能速绝 - Means 'if you can quickly break off the relationship,' suggesting an urgent action.
    • 尚可生全 - Means 'you can still be saved,' indicating a chance for survival.
    • 生路即断矣 - Means 'the way to live will be cut off,' emphasizing the dire consequences.
    • 玄言亦无能奉救也 - Means 'Xuanyan will also be unable to save you,' indicating the limits of help.
    • 心不悦 - Means 'was not pleased,' expressing dissatisfaction.
    • 所谋之事未果 - Means 'the planned matter has not been successful,' indicating failure.
    • 白不遗人请归 - This phrase is ambiguous but likely means 'did not leave anyone behind to request a return,' suggesting a hurried departure.
    • 其意尤切 - Means 'his intention was even more urgent,' indicating a heightened sense of urgency.
    • 缠绵之思 - Refers to deep, lingering thoughts, translating to 'deep, lingering thoughts.'
    • 不可形状 - Means 'cannot be described,' indicating the complexity of the emotions.
    • 更十余日 - Means 'another ten or so days,' indicating a passage of time.
    • 执其手而告曰 - Means 'took his hand and said,' indicating a personal and direct communication.
    • 郎之容色决死矣 - Means 'Your appearance is that of someone who is about to die,' indicating a serious condition.
    • 不信吾言，乃至如是 - Means 'Not believing my words, you have come to this,' indicating regret over the lack of trust.
    • 贤宠入门 - Means 'your beloved enters the door,' indicating the arrival of the spirit.
    • 当见本形矣 - Means 'you will see its true form,' indicating a revelation.
    • 潜记之 - Means 'secretly remember it,' indicating a covert action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后数月 - Refers to a specific time period, which is common in historical narratives.
    • 奇术之士 - Refers to practitioners of ancient Chinese magic and sorcery, which were often associated with Daoist traditions.
    • 素与煌善 - Indicates a traditional Chinese value of friendship and loyalty.
    • 大异之 - Reflects the cultural importance of unexpected events and their impact on individuals.
    • 纳一夫人耳 - Refers to the traditional practice of marriage in ancient China.
    • 威神之鬼 - Refers to spirits and deities in Chinese folklore, often associated with supernatural powers.
    • 令能速绝 - Reflects the urgency and decisiveness required in dealing with dangerous situations.
    • 尚可生全 - Indicates the belief in the possibility of salvation or rescue, a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 生路即断矣 - Emphasizes the finality of certain outcomes, reflecting the fatalistic view in some Chinese philosophies.
    • 玄言亦无能奉救也 - Highlights the limitations of even powerful figures, a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 心不悦 - Reflects the emotional complexity and inner turmoil of characters in Chinese literature.
    • 所谋之事未果 - Indicates the theme of unfulfilled plans and the consequences of failure.
    • 白不遗人请归 - Suggests a hurried and possibly secretive departure, common in narratives of danger or urgency.
    • 其意尤切 - Emphasizes the intensity of emotions and intentions, a common literary device in Chinese literature.
    • 缠绵之思 - Reflects the deep, often unexpressed emotions characteristic of Chinese poetry and literature.
    • 不可形状 - Indicates the difficulty of expressing complex emotions, a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 更十余日 - Refers to the passage of time, a common element in narrative structure.
    • 执其手而告曰 - Indicates a personal and intimate moment, common in Chinese literature.
    • 郎之容色决死矣 - Reflects the theme of impending death and the physical signs of it, a common motif in Chinese literature.
    • 不信吾言，乃至如是 - Indicates the consequences of disbelief and the importance of trust, a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 贤宠入门 - Refers to the arrival of a significant figure, often a loved one or a spirit, a common narrative device.
    • 当见本形矣 - Indicates the revelation of true identity, a common plot point in Chinese literature.
    • 潜记之 - Reflects the theme of secrecy and hidden knowledge, common in Chinese literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 洛中 - Refers to the city of Luoyang, a significant location in Chinese history and culture.
    • 任玄言 - The name of a Taoist priest known for his magical abilities.
    • 煌 - The name of a character in the narrative.
    • 芝田 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location or region.
    • 赤面者 - Refers to a person with a red face, possibly a character or a spirit.

    Several months later, Huang returned to Luoyang for a reason. In Luoyang, there was a Taoist named Ren Xuanyan, a master of magical arts, who had always been friendly with Huang. Seeing Huang's appearance, Xuanyan was greatly surprised and asked, "What has happened to you, dear friend? Why do you look so worn out?" Huang smiled and replied, "I've taken a wife." Xuanyan said, "The one you've taken is not a human wife, but a powerful spirit. If you can quickly break off this relationship, you can still be saved. But in another ten or so days, your path to life will be cut off, and even I will be unable to save you." Huang was not pleased, as the planned matter had not been successful, and he had not left anyone behind to request a return. His intention was even more urgent. His deep, lingering thoughts could not be described.

    Another ten or so days passed, and Huang returned to Luoyang again. He met Xuanyan at the South Market, took his hand, and said, "Your appearance is that of someone who is about to die. Not believing my words, you have come to this. Tomorrow at noon, that person will come, and when they do, you will die. Alas! Alas!" He then wept and bid farewell to Huang, who became even more confused. Xuanyan said, "You don't believe me? Please keep this talisman in your chest. Tomorrow at noon, when your beloved enters the door, please throw the talisman at her, and you will see her true form." Huang took the talisman and kept it in his chest. After Xuanyan left, he told his servant, "Tomorrow at noon, the spirit from the Field of Cinnabar will come. Your master must throw the talisman at her. You can observe her shape; she will either be a blue-faced, heavy spirit or a red-faced one. When she enters and sits down, your master will die. When he dies, look at him—did he die sitting down?" The servant secretly remembered these words. [123]

    及时，煌坐堂中，芝田妖恨来，及门，煌以怀中符投之，立变面为耐重鬼。 鬼执煌，已死矣，问其仆曰：“如此，奈何取妖道士言，令吾形见！”反捽煌， 卧于床上，一踏而毙。日暮，玄言来候之，煌已死矣。问其仆曰：“何形？”仆 乃告之。玄言曰：“此乃北天王右脚下耐重也，例三千年一替，其鬼年满，自合 择替，故化形成人而取之。煌得坐死，满三千年亦当求替。今既卧亡，终天不复 得替矣。”前睹煌尸，脊骨已折。玄言泣之而去。此传之仆。 [124]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [124]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 及時 - Means 'at the right time' or 'immediately,' indicating a specific moment.
    • 坐堂中 - Literally 'sit in the hall,' suggesting a formal setting.
    • 芝田妖恨来 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'the evil spirit from Zhitiang came' or 'the evil spirit from the field of jade came.'
    • 立变面为耐重鬼 - Means 'immediately changed his face into a Nai Zhong ghost.'
    • 鬼执煌，已死矣 - Means 'the ghost seized Huang, who was already dead.'
    • 问其仆曰 - Means 'asked his servant.'
    • 如此，奈何取妖道士言，令吾形见 - Means 'like this, why did you take the words of the evil Taoist and let my form appear?'
    • 反捽煌 - Means 'instead, he grabbed Huang.'
    • 卧于床上，一踏而毙 - Means 'lay him on the bed and crushed him to death with one step.'
    • 日暮，玄言来候之 - Means 'at dusk, Xuan Yan came to visit him.'
    • 煌已死矣 - Means 'Huang was already dead.'
    • 仆乃告之 - Means 'the servant then told him.'
    • 此乃北天王右脚下耐重也 - Means 'this is the Nai Zhong under the right foot of the Northern Sky King.'
    • 例三千年一替 - Means 'it is customary for it to be replaced every three thousand years.'
    • 其鬼年满，自合择替 - Means 'when the ghost's term is up, it should naturally choose a replacement.'
    • 故化形成人而取之 - Means 'so it transforms into a human and takes it.'
    • 煌得坐死，满三千年亦当求替 - Means 'Huang died sitting down, and after three thousand years, he should also seek a replacement.'
    • 今既卧亡，终天不复得替矣 - Means 'now that he has died lying down, he will never get a replacement again.'
    • 前睹煌尸，脊骨已折 - Means 'before his eyes, Huang's body was lying with his spine broken.'
    • 玄言泣之而去 - Means 'Xuan Yan wept and left.'
    • 此传之仆 - Means 'this is the servant who told the story.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 芝田 - A place name or a metaphorical location, possibly associated with supernatural or mystical significance.
    • 妖恨 - Refers to an evil spirit or demon.
    • 耐重鬼 - A type of ghost, possibly a heavy or oppressive spirit.
    • 北天王 - Refers to the Northern Sky King, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 玄言 - A Taoist master or sage.
    • 三千年 - A significant period in Chinese mythology, often associated with the lifespan of deities or supernatural beings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 煌 - The main character, Huang.
    • 芝田 - A place name or a metaphorical location.
    • 妖恨 - An evil spirit or demon.
    • 耐重鬼 - A type of ghost.
    • 北天王 - The Northern Sky King, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 玄言 - A Taoist master or sage.
    • 仆 - The servant who tells the story.

    At the right time, Huang sat in the hall, and the evil spirit from Zhitiang came. When it reached the door, Huang threw a talisman from his chest, and the spirit immediately changed its face into a Nai Zhong ghost. The ghost seized Huang, who was already dead, and asked his servant, "Like this, why did you take the words of the evil Taoist and let my form appear?" Instead, the ghost grabbed Huang, laid him on the bed, and crushed him to death with one step. At dusk, Xuan Yan came to visit Huang, who was already dead. Xuan Yan asked the servant, "What form did it take?" The servant then told him. "This is the Nai Zhong under the right foot of the Northern Sky King," he said. "It is customary for it to be replaced every three thousand years. When the ghost's term is up, it should naturally choose a replacement, so it transforms into a human and takes it." "Huang died sitting down," he continued, "and after three thousand years, he should also seek a replacement. Now that he has died lying down, he will never get a replacement again." Before his eyes, Huang's body was lying with his spine broken. Xuan Yan wept and left. This is the servant who told the story. [124]

    ○岑曦
    　　
    　　进士郑知古，睿宗朝客于相国岑公门下，有日矣。一夕，寝于内厅。夜分， 远闻众闹祈哀之声。倾耳听之，声声渐近。既而分明闻其所救人曰：“岑氏寒微， 未达于天下，幸而生之。曦谬掌朝政，其心畏惧，未尝敢危人。设使妇人而持权 者，其心亦猛于曦也。即曦□□御物，生无怨人，死无怨鬼，何所触犯，而当此 戮？唯使者恕之。某等当使曦以阴钱百万奉谢。”泣告之声盈路。俄见大鬼丈余， 蓬头朱衣，执长剑逾墙而入，有丈夫、妇女、老者、少者亦随之入，或自投于墙 下遮拜，其辞恳切。大鬼不顾，又逾中门，众已纷纭而入。食顷，闻阖门大哭之 声，惊起听之，大鬼者执曦头仍出，门内哭声极哀，若有大祸。衙鼓将动，稍稍 似息。知古徨不知所为，行于廊下，以及鸣鼓。 [125]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [125]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 寝于内厅 - Means 'slept in the inner hall,' indicating a place within the household where guests might stay.
    • 夜分 - Refers to midnight or the middle of the night.
    • 倾耳听之 - Means 'leaned forward to listen.'
    • 声声渐近 - Means 'the sounds became increasingly closer.'
    • 分明闻其所救人曰 - Means 'clearly heard the person being saved saying.'
    • 岑氏寒微 - Means 'Cen's family is humble and lowly.'
    • 未达于天下 - Means 'not widely known or respected.'
    • 幸而生之 - Means 'lucky to be alive.'
    • 曦谬掌朝政 - Means 'Xi mistakenly holds the reins of government.'
    • 其心畏惧 - Means 'his heart is filled with fear.'
    • 未尝敢危人 - Means 'never dared to harm anyone.'
    • 设使妇人而持权者 - Means 'if a woman were to hold power.'
    • 其心亦猛于曦也 - Means 'her heart would be even more fierce than Xi's.'
    • 即曦□□御物 - The phrase is incomplete and unclear; it could mean 'even if Xi were to rule over things.'
    • 生无怨人，死无怨鬼 - Means 'no one will resent me when I am alive, and no ghost will resent me when I die.'
    • 何所触犯，而当此戮 - Means 'what have I done to deserve such punishment?'
    • 唯使者恕之 - Means 'only the messenger can forgive me.'
    • 某等当使曦以阴钱百万奉谢 - Means 'we will make Xi offer a million hidden coins as a token of gratitude.'
    • 泣告之声盈路 - Means 'cries of pleading filled the road.'
    • 俄见大鬼丈余 - Means 'soon saw a large ghost, more than a zhang tall.'
    • 蓬头朱衣 - Means 'with disheveled hair and red clothing.'
    • 执长剑逾墙而入 - Means 'holding a long sword and leaping over the wall.'
    • 有丈夫、妇女、老者、少者 - Means 'men, women, old people, and young people.'
    • 或自投于墙下遮拜 - Means 'some threw themselves at the wall and prostrated themselves.'
    • 其辞恳切 - Means 'their words were earnest.'
    • 大鬼不顾 - Means 'the large ghost paid no heed.'
    • 又逾中门 - Means 'then leapt over the middle gate.'
    • 众已纷纭而入 - Means 'the crowd entered in a disordered manner.'
    • 食顷 - Means 'after a meal's time' or 'a short while.'
    • 闻阖门大哭之声 - Means 'heard loud weeping from the entire house.'
    • 惊起听之 - Means 'woke up and listened.'
    • 大鬼者执曦头仍出 - Means 'the large ghost took Xi's head and left.'
    • 门内哭声极哀 - Means 'the crying inside the door was extremely sad.'
    • 若有大祸 - Means 'as if a great calamity had befallen.'
    • 衙鼓将动 - Means 'the drum of the government office was about to sound.'
    • 稍稍似息 - Means 'gradually seemed to subside.'
    • 知古徨不知所为 - Means 'Zhi Gu was in a state of confusion and did not know what to do.'
    • 行于廊下 - Means 'walked along the corridor.'
    • 以及鸣鼓 - Means 'and the drum sounded.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 进士郑知古 - Refers to a scholar-official named Zheng Zhiguo.
    • 睿宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Ruizong of Tang.
    • 相国岑公 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Cen Xi.
    • 大鬼丈余 - Refers to a large ghost, more than a zhang (approximately 3.3 meters) tall.
    • 蓬头朱衣 - Refers to a ghost with disheveled hair and red clothing.
    • 执长剑 - Refers to a ghost holding a long sword.
    • 逾墙而入 - Refers to the ghost leaping over the wall.
    • 丈夫、妇女、老者、少者 - Refers to a diverse group of people.
    • 自投于墙下遮拜 - Refers to people throwing themselves at the wall and prostrating themselves.
    • 阖门大哭 - Refers to loud weeping from the entire house.
    • 衙鼓 - Refers to the drum of the government office, signaling the start of official business.

    Proper Nouns

    • 进士郑知古 - Zheng Zhiguo, a scholar-official.
    • 睿宗 - Emperor Ruizong of Tang.
    • 相国岑公 - Cen Xi, a high-ranking official.
    • 曦 - Cen Xi, the same person as mentioned earlier.
    • 衙鼓 - The drum of the government office.

    A Scholar's Encounter with the Supernatural: The Tale of Cen Xi

    During the reign of Emperor Ruizong, the scholar Zheng Zhiguo, a guest in the household of the high-ranking official Cen Xi, stayed there for some time. One night, he slept in the inner hall. At midnight, he heard from afar the clamor and cries for mercy. He leaned forward to listen, and the sounds became increasingly closer. Soon, he clearly heard the person being saved say, "Cen's family is humble and lowly, not widely known or respected. I am lucky to be alive. Cen Xi mistakenly holds the reins of government. His heart is filled with fear, and he never dared to harm anyone. If a woman were to hold power, her heart would be even more fierce than Cen Xi's. Even if Cen Xi were to rule over things, no one will resent me when I am alive, and no ghost will resent me when I die. What have I done to deserve such punishment? Only the messenger can forgive me. We will make Cen Xi offer a million hidden coins as a token of gratitude." The cries of pleading filled the road. Soon, he saw a large ghost, more than a zhang tall, with disheveled hair and red clothing, holding a long sword and leaping over the wall. Men, women, old people, and young people also followed him in, some throwing themselves at the wall and prostrating themselves, their words earnest. The large ghost paid no heed and then leapt over the middle gate. The crowd entered in a disordered manner. After a meal's time, he heard loud weeping from the entire house. He woke up and listened, and the large ghost took Cen Xi's head and left. The crying inside the door was extremely sad, as if a great calamity had befallen. The drum of the government office was about to sound, and gradually, it seemed to subside. Zheng Zhiguo was in a state of confusion and did not know what to do. He walked along the corridor, and the drum sounded. [125]

    鼓发，中门大开，厩吏乃惊焉。导从之士，俨立于门下矣。知古微觇之，闻 曦起而腆矣。有顷，朝天时至，执炬者告之。曦簪笏而出，抚马欲上，忽扪其颈 曰：“吾夜半项痛，及此愈甚，如何！”急命书吏为简，请展前假小憩之。遂复 入，行数步，回曰：“今晨有事，须自对敭。”强投简而登马。知古所见中夜 之事小验，益忧。有顷，一骑奔归曰：“相国伏法□，家当籍没！”知古逾垣而 出，免焉，法司所诘。前拜泣而求恕者，盖岑氏之先也。
    　　
    　　仆常闻人之荣辱，皆禀自阴灵。惟此鬼吏，其何神速矣。乃知幽晦之内，其 可忽之乎！ [126]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [126]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鼓发 - Beating of drums, often signaling the start of an event or ceremony.
    • 中门大开 - The central gate was wide open, indicating a significant moment or transition.
    • 厩吏乃惊焉 - The stable official was startled, suggesting a moment of surprise or alarm.
    • 导从之士，俨立于门下矣 - The attendants stood solemnly at the gate, describing a formal or ceremonial scene.
    • 知古微觇之 - Zhi Gu secretly observed, indicating covert or discreet observation.
    • 曦起而腆矣 - Xi arose and was embarrassed, suggesting a moment of discomfort or hesitation.
    • 朝天时至 - The time for court audience arrived, referring to the moment when officials or subjects were to present themselves before the emperor.
    • 执炬者告之 - The torch-bearer informed him, indicating a messenger or signaler.
    • 曦簪笏而出 - Xi put on his ceremonial hat and board and went out, describing the act of dressing for an official occasion.
    • 抚马欲上 - He patted the horse and was about to mount, indicating preparation for departure.
    • 忽扪其颈 - Suddenly he touched his neck, suggesting a physical reaction or realization.
    • 吾夜半项痛，及此愈甚，如何 - I had a pain in my neck at midnight, and it has become worse since then, what should I do? indicating a medical or personal issue.
    • 急命书吏为简 - He urgently ordered the scribe to write a note, describing a hurried official action.
    • 展前假小憩之 - To extend the previous leave for a short rest, indicating a request for time off.
    • 遂复入 - He then went back in, describing a return to a previous location.
    • 今晨有事，须自对扬 - There is something to attend to this morning, and I must address it personally, indicating a personal or official matter.
    • 强投简而登马 - He forced himself to hand over the note and mounted the horse, describing a reluctant or hurried action.
    • 知古所见中夜之事小验 - Zhi Gu's observation of the midnight event was slightly confirmed, indicating a partial verification of a previous observation.
    • 益忧 - Became even more worried, indicating increasing anxiety or concern.
    • 一骑奔归曰 - A rider rushed back and said, describing a messenger or informant.
    • 相国伏法□ - The chancellor was punished, with the square bracket indicating a missing character, possibly '伏法' meaning 'punished according to law'.
    • 家当籍没 - His family's property was to be confiscated, indicating a severe penalty.
    • 知古逾垣而出 - Zhi Gu climbed over the wall and escaped, describing a desperate or covert exit.
    • 免焉，法司所诘 - He escaped, avoiding interrogation by the judicial authorities, indicating a narrow escape from legal consequences.
    • 前拜泣而求恕者，盖岑氏之先也 - The one who had previously knelt and wept to seek pardon was a descendant of the Cen family, indicating a historical or familial connection.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 鼓发 - The beating of drums was a common signal in ancient Chinese ceremonies and military operations.
    • 中门大开 - The central gate was a significant architectural feature in ancient Chinese palaces and temples, often symbolizing a transition or important moment.
    • 厩吏 - The stable official was responsible for managing the emperor's or noble's horses and stables, indicating a high-level administrative role.
    • 导从之士 - Attendants or followers who accompanied officials, often in ceremonial or official capacities.
    • 知古 - A person's name, possibly a historical figure or fictional character in the context of the story.
    • 曦 - A person's name, likely referring to an official or noble.
    • 朝天时至 - Refers to the time when officials or subjects were to present themselves before the emperor, a formal and significant event.
    • 簪笏 - Ceremonial hat and board, symbols of official rank and authority.
    • 相国 - The highest-ranking official in the government, equivalent to a prime minister or chancellor.
    • 法司 - The judicial authorities, responsible for enforcing the law and administering justice.
    • 岑氏 - The Cen family, a historical or fictional family mentioned in the context of the story.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sequence describes a series of events leading to the stable official's surprise, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes the attendants' formal stance, which must be read as a description of a scene.
    • This sequence describes Zhi Gu's observation and Xi's reaction, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This sequence describes the arrival of the court audience and the torch-bearer's announcement, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This sequence describes Xi's preparation for departure and his sudden realization, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes Xi's medical issue and his concern, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This sequence describes Xi's urgent request for a note and a short rest, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This sequence describes Xi's return and his subsequent action, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes Xi's personal or official matter, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes Xi's reluctant action, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes Zhi Gu's partial verification of a previous observation, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes Zhi Gu's increasing concern, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes the rider's return and his announcement, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes the chancellor's punishment and the confiscation of his family's property, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes Zhi Gu's escape, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes Zhi Gu's avoidance of interrogation, which must be read as a connected narrative.
    • This clause describes the previous act of seeking pardon and its connection to the Cen family, which must be read as a connected narrative.

    Proper Nouns

    • 知古 - A person's name, possibly a historical figure or fictional character.
    • 曦 - A person's name, likely referring to an official or noble.
    • 相国 - The highest-ranking official in the government.
    • 法司 - The judicial authorities.
    • 岑氏 - The Cen family, a historical or fictional family.

    Drums sounded, and the central gate was wide open, startling the stable official. The attendants stood solemnly at the gate. Zhi Gu secretly observed and heard that Xi arose and was embarrassed. Shortly after, the time for court audience arrived, and the torch-bearer informed him. Xi put on his ceremonial hat and board and went out, patting the horse and about to mount. Suddenly, he touched his neck and said, "I had a pain in my neck at midnight, and it has become worse since then, what should I do!" He urgently ordered the scribe to write a note, requesting an extension of his previous leave for a short rest. He then went back in, walked a few steps, and turned back, saying, "There is something to attend to this morning, and I must address it personally." Reluctantly, he handed over the note and mounted the horse. Zhi Gu's observation of the midnight event was slightly confirmed, and he became even more worried. Shortly after, a rider rushed back and said, "The chancellor was punished, and his family's property was to be confiscated!" Zhi Gu climbed over the wall and escaped, avoiding interrogation by the judicial authorities. The one who had previously knelt and wept to seek pardon was a descendant of the Cen family.

    I have often heard that a person's honor and disgrace are all determined by the unseen spirits. Only this ghost official, how swift and mysterious he is. Thus, I know that within the depths of the unseen, one cannot be careless! [126]

    ○李沈
    　　
    　　陇西李沈者，其父尝受朱泚恩，贼平伏法，沈乃逃而得免。既而逢赦，以家 产童仆悉施洛北惠林寺而寓生焉。读书弹琴，聊以度日。今荆南相公清河崔公群， 群弟进士于，皆执门人礼，即其所与扬者，不待言矣。常与处士李擢为刎颈交。
    　　
    　　元和十三年秋，擢因谓沈曰：“吾有故将适宋，回期末卜，兄能泛舟相送乎？” 沈闻其去，离思浩然，遂登舟。初约一程，程尽则曰：“兄之情，岂尽于此？” 及又行，言似有感，竟不能别，直抵濉阳。其暮，擢谢舟人而去，与沈乃下汴堤， 月中徐曰：“承念诚久，兄识擢何人也？”沈曰：“辩博之士也。”擢曰：“非 也。擢乃冥官，顷为洛州都督，故在洛多时。阴道公事，故不任昼，乃得与兄同 游。今去阴迁阳，托孕于亲已五载矣。所以步步邀兄者，意有所托。”沈曰： “何事？”曰：“擢之此身，艺难为疋，唯虑一舍此身，都醉前业，祈兄与醒之 耳。 [127]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [127]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 尝受 - Implies a past experience of receiving something.
    • 贼平伏法 - The rebels were pacified and punished according to the law.
    • 逃而得免 - He escaped and was spared.
    • 逢赦 - Encountered amnesty.
    • 以家产童仆悉施洛北惠林寺 - Gave all his family property and servants to the Huilin Temple in Luobei.
    • 寓生焉 - Lived there temporarily.
    • 读书弹琴，聊以度日 - Read books and played the qin, just to pass the time.
    • 执门人礼 - Observed the etiquette of a disciple.
    • 刎颈交 - A sworn brother.
    • 初约一程 - Initially agreed to travel one stage.
    • 程尽则曰 - When the stage was over, he said.
    • 离思浩然 - He felt a deep sense of separation.
    • 遂登舟 - He then boarded the boat.
    • 承念诚久 - Your affection has been sincere for a long time.
    • 辩博之士也 - A learned and eloquent person.
    • 冥官 - An official in the underworld.
    • 顷为洛州都督 - Recently served as the governor of Luozhou.
    • 阴道公事 - Concealed official duties.
    • 托孕于亲已五载矣 - Has been entrusted to carry a child for five years.
    • 步步邀兄者 - Staying step by step with you.
    • 意有所托 - Has something to entrust.
    • 艺难为疋 - Difficult to match in skill.
    • 都醉前业 - To be completely absorbed in past karma.
    • 祈兄与醒之耳 - I ask you to wake me up.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陇西李沈 - A person from Longxi, a region in present-day Gansu Province, China.
    • 朱泚 - A rebel leader during the An Lushan Rebellion (755-763) who declared himself emperor but was later defeated.
    • 元和 - The reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (799-820).
    • 荆南相公清河崔公群 - A high-ranking official named Cui Qun, from Qinghe, who served as the chancellor of Jingnan.
    • 群弟进士于 - Cui Qun's brother, who passed the imperial examinations and became a jinshi (advanced scholar).
    • 李擢 - A character in the story, likely a scholar or official who claims to be a '冥官' (official in the underworld).
    • 洛北惠林寺 - The Huilin Temple located in Luobei, a place where Li Shen donated his property and servants.
    • 宋 - The state of Song, one of the Five Dynasties during the late Tang period.
    • 阴迁阳 - A metaphorical phrase suggesting a transition from the underworld to the earthly realm, possibly referring to reincarnation or a change in status.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陇西李沈 - Li Shen from Longxi.
    • 朱泚 - Zhu Ci, a rebel leader.
    • 元和 - The reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 荆南相公清河崔公群 - Cui Qun, a high-ranking official from Qinghe.
    • 群弟进士于 - Cui Qun's brother, a jinshi (advanced scholar).
    • 李擢 - Li Zuo, a character in the story.
    • 洛北惠林寺 - Huilin Temple in Luobei.

    ○Li Shen
    　　
    Li Shen of Longxi was the son of a man who once received favors from Zhu Ci. After the rebels were pacified and punished according to the law, Li Shen escaped and was spared. Later, when amnesty was granted, he gave all his family property and servants to the Huilin Temple in Luobei and lived there temporarily. He read books and played the qin, just to pass the time. Today, Cui Qun, the chancellor of Jingnan and a high-ranking official from Qinghe, along with his brother, a jinshi (advanced scholar), both observed the etiquette of disciples toward Li Shen, and his reputation was well-known. He often had a close friendship with the reclusive scholar Li Zuo, as sworn brothers.

    　　
    In the autumn of the thirteenth year of the Yuanhe era, Li Zuo told Li Shen, "I have an old comrade who is heading to Song. I won't know when I'll return, so can you accompany me on a boat trip?" Upon hearing that Li Zuo was leaving, Li Shen felt a deep sense of separation, so he boarded the boat. They initially agreed to travel one stage, and when the journey was over, Li Zuo said, "Your affection hasn't reached its limit yet, has it?" As they continued, Li Zuo seemed to be moved by something, and they couldn't say goodbye, continuing straight to Suoyang. That evening, Li Zuo thanked the boatman and left, and he and Li Shen descended from the Bian River embankment. Under the moonlight, Li Zuo slowly said, "Your affection has been sincere for a long time. Do you know who I am, Li Shen?" Li Shen replied, "A learned and eloquent person." Li Zuo said, "That's not it. I am an official in the underworld, recently serving as the governor of Luozhou, which is why I stayed in Luoyang for a long time. My official duties are concealed, so I cannot be seen during the day, which is why I could travel with you at night. Now, I am transitioning from the underworld to the earthly realm and have been entrusted to carry a child for five years. That's why I stayed with you step by step, having something to entrust to you." Li Shen asked, "What is it?" Li Zuo said, "My body is difficult to match in skill. I only fear that if I let go of this body, I might be completely absorbed in my past karma. I ask you to wake me up." [127]

    然擢孕五载，寓亲腹中，其家以为不祥，祈神祝佛之法，竭货而为。擢尚未 往，神固何为。兄可往其家，朱书“产”字令吞之，擢即生矣。必奉兄绢素。兄 得且去，候擢三岁，宜复来视之，且曰：“主人孙久不产者，某以朱字吞之，生 儿奇惠，今三载矣，思宿以告之，故复来也。”可取儿抱卧，夜久，伺掌人闭户， 即抱于静处呼曰：“李擢记我否？”儿当啼，啼即掌之。再三问之，擢必微悟。 兄宜与擢言洛中居处及游宴之地，擢当大悟，悟后此生之业无孑遗矣。此事必醒 素以归，擢乃后荣盛，兄不可复得从容矣。兄声名籍甚，不久当有大谏之拜，慎 勿赴也，赴当非寿。此郡北三十里有胡村，村前有车门，即擢新身之居也。言讫， 泣拜而去。 [128]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [128]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 然擢孕五载 - Literally 'Yet Ruo was pregnant for five years,' where '然' can be translated as 'yet' or 'however' to introduce the unusual situation.
    • 寓亲腹中 - Literally 'in the womb of the mother,' but it is more idiomatic to say 'in her mother's womb.'
    • 其家以为不祥 - Literally 'his family considered it an omen,' where '不祥' can be translated as 'unlucky' or 'ominous.'
    • 祈神祝佛之法，竭货而为 - Literally 'praying to gods and worshipping Buddha, spending all their wealth,' where '竭货' can be translated as 'spending all their resources.'
    • 擢尚未往，神固何为 - Literally 'Ruo had not yet been born, what could the gods do?' This is a rhetorical question implying that the gods could not help.
    • 兄可往其家，朱书“产”字令吞之 - Literally 'Brother can go to their home, write the character '产' in red ink and make them swallow it,' where '朱书' means 'write in red ink' and '令吞之' means 'make them swallow it.'
    • 擢即生矣 - Literally 'Ruo will be born immediately,' where '即' can be translated as 'immediately' or 'right away.'
    • 必奉兄绢素 - Literally 'they will certainly present the brother with fine silk and white cloth,' where '绢素' refers to fine silk and white cloth, often used as gifts.
    • 兄得且去 - Literally 'the brother should leave,' where '得' can be translated as 'should' or 'must.'
    • 候擢三岁，宜复来视之 - Literally 'wait until Ruo is three years old, and then come to see him again,' where '候' means 'wait' and '宜' means 'should.'
    • 主人孙久不产者，某以朱字吞之，生儿奇惠，今三载矣，思宿以告之，故复来也 - Literally 'the master's grandson who has not been born for a long time, I made them swallow the red character, and the boy is very clever, now he is three years old, I think it is appropriate to tell him, so I came back,' where '主人孙' means 'master's grandson' and '奇惠' means 'very clever.'
    • 可取儿抱卧 - Literally 'can take the child and hold him,' where '抱卧' means 'hold and lay down.'
    • 夜久，伺掌人闭户 - Literally 'at night, when the caretakers have closed the door,' where '伺' means 'wait' and '掌人' means 'caretakers.'
    • 即抱于静处呼曰 - Literally 'then take him to a quiet place and call out,' where '静处' means 'quiet place.'
    • 李擢记我否 - Literally 'Li Ruo, do you remember me?' where '记我否' means 'do you remember me?'
    • 啼即掌之 - Literally 'if he cries, then pat him,' where '掌之' means 'pat him.'
    • 再三问之 - Literally 'ask him repeatedly,' where '再三' means 'repeatedly.'
    • 擢必微悟 - Literally 'Ruo will surely have a slight understanding,' where '微悟' means 'slight understanding.'
    • 兄宜与擢言洛中居处及游宴之地 - Literally 'the brother should talk to Ruo about the places he lives and plays in Luoyang,' where '居处' means 'living place' and '游宴之地' means 'places of play and feasting.'
    • 擢当大悟 - Literally 'Ruo will have a great understanding,' where '大悟' means 'great understanding.'
    • 悟后此生之业无孑遗矣 - Literally 'after understanding, his life's work will be without any remnants,' where '孑遗' means 'remnants.'
    • 此事必醒素以归 - Literally 'this matter must be clearly explained and returned,' where '醒素' means 'clearly explained' and '以归' means 'returned.'
    • 擢乃后荣盛，兄不可复得从容矣 - Literally 'Ruo will later become prosperous and honored, the brother cannot afford to be leisurely anymore,' where '荣盛' means 'prosperous and honored' and '从容' means 'leisurely.'
    • 兄声名籍甚，不久当有大谏之拜，慎勿赴也，赴当非寿 - Literally 'the brother has a great reputation, soon he will be appointed to a high office, be cautious not to accept it, accepting it will not be good for longevity,' where '声名籍甚' means 'great reputation,' '大谏之拜' means 'appointment to a high office,' and '非寿' means 'not good for longevity.'
    • 此郡北三十里有胡村，村前有车门，即擢新身之居也 - Literally 'north of this prefecture, thirty miles away, there is a village called Hu, in front of the village there is a carriage gate, which is where Ruo will live in the future,' where '胡村' means 'Hu village' and '车门' means 'carriage gate.'
    • 言讫，泣拜而去 - Literally 'after finishing speaking, he wept and bowed, then left,' where '泣拜' means 'wept and bowed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李擢 - A character named Li Ruo, whose birth is the focus of the text.
    • 祈神祝佛 - Refers to the practice of praying to gods and worshipping Buddha, common in Chinese religious and folk traditions.
    • 朱书“产”字 - Writing the character '产' in red ink, which is a traditional method believed to facilitate childbirth.
    • 绢素 - Fine silk and white cloth, often used as gifts in traditional Chinese culture.
    • 胡村 - A village named Hu, which is mentioned as the future residence of Li Ruo.
    • 车门 - A carriage gate, which is a traditional architectural feature in Chinese villages.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李擢 - The name of the character whose birth is the focus of the text.
    • 胡村 - The name of the village where Li Ruo will live in the future.
    • 车门 - The name of the carriage gate, which is a traditional architectural feature in Chinese villages.

    Yet Ruo was pregnant for five years, in her mother's womb, and her family considered it an omen. They prayed to gods and worshipped Buddha, spending all their resources. Ruo had not yet been born, what could the gods do? Brother can go to their home, write the character "产" in red ink and make them swallow it, Ruo will be born immediately. They will certainly present the brother with fine silk and white cloth. The brother should leave, wait until Ruo is three years old, and then come to see him again, and say, "The master's grandson who has not been born for a long time, I made them swallow the red character, and the boy is very clever, now he is three years old, I think it is appropriate to tell him, so I came back." Can take the child and hold him, at night, when the caretakers have closed the door, then take him to a quiet place and call out, "Li Ruo, do you remember me?" If he cries, then pat him. Ask him repeatedly, Ruo will surely have a slight understanding. The brother should talk to Ruo about the places he lives and plays in Luoyang, Ruo will have a great understanding, after understanding, his life's work will be without any remnants. This matter must be clearly explained and returned, Ruo will later become prosperous and honored, the brother cannot afford to be leisurely anymore. The brother has a great reputation, soon he will be appointed to a high office, be cautious not to accept it, accepting it will not be good for longevity. North of this prefecture, thirty miles away, there is a village called Hu, in front of the village there is a carriage gate, which is where Ruo will live in the future. After finishing speaking, he wept and bowed, then left. [128]

    迟明，沈策杖访之，果有胡村。叩门求憩，掌人翁年八下余，倚杖延入。既 命坐，似有忧色，沈问之，翁曰：“新妇孕五载矣，计穷术尽，略无少征。”沈 因曰：“沈道门留心，颇善咒术，不产之由，见之即辨。”遽令左右召新妇来， 沈诊其臂曰：“男也，甚明惠，有非常之才，故不拘常月耳。”于是令速具产所 帷帐床榻毕，沈执笔若祝者，朱书“产”字令吞之。入口，而男生焉。翁极喜， 奉绢三十疋，沈乃受焉，曰：“此儿不常也，三岁当复来为君相之。”言讫而去。 [129]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [129]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 遲明 - Classical expression meaning 'just before dawn' or 'early morning.'
    • 叩門求憩 - Means 'knock on the door to seek rest,' indicating a polite request for shelter.
    • 掌人翁 - Refers to the elderly man in charge of the household, often translated as 'head of the household' or 'master of the house.'
    • 倚杖延入 - Means 'to lean on a cane and invite in,' indicating a polite and welcoming gesture.
    • 計窮術盡 - Colloquial expression meaning 'all methods have been exhausted,' suggesting that all efforts have failed.
    • 略无少征 - Means 'there are no signs at all,' indicating a complete lack of progress.
    • 甚明惠 - Means 'very bright and intelligent,' describing the child's exceptional qualities.
    • 有非常之才 - Means 'has extraordinary talent,' emphasizing the child's exceptional abilities.
    • 不拘常月耳 - Means 'just not bound by the usual lunar cycles,' explaining why the pregnancy was unusual.
    • 遽令左右召新妇来 - Means 'immediately ordered the attendants to bring the new wife,' indicating a prompt action.
    • 沈诊其臂 - Means 'Shen diagnosed her arm,' referring to a medical examination.
    • 朱书“产”字令吞之 - Means 'wrote the character '生' (birth) in red ink and had her swallow it,' describing a magical ritual.
    • 奉绢三十疋 - Means 'presented thirty bolts of silk,' indicating a generous gift.
    • 三岁当复来为君相之 - Means 'will return in three years to advise you further,' indicating a promise to revisit the family.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沈策杖 - Refers to Shen, a person who is walking with a staff, likely a respected figure or scholar.
    • 胡村 - Refers to a village named Hu, which is a specific place in the story.
    • 新妇 - Refers to a newly married woman, often used in classical texts to denote a wife.
    • 咒术 - Refers to magical incantations or spells, which were common in traditional Chinese beliefs.
    • 帷帐床榻 - Refers to the preparation of a birthing room, including curtains, beds, and other furnishings.
    • 绢 - Refers to silk, a valuable material often used as a form of payment or gift in classical times.
    • 相之 - Refers to divination or fortune-telling, a practice where a person's future is predicted or interpreted.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沈 - The name of the character who performs the magical ritual.
    • 胡村 - The name of the village where the story takes place.
    • 新妇 - The name of the woman who is pregnant.

    Just before dawn, Shen, walking with a staff, visited and indeed found the village of Hu. He knocked on the door to seek rest, and the elderly man of the household, over eighty years old, leaned on a cane and invited him in. After seating him, the elderly man seemed worried, and Shen asked him about it. The elderly man replied, "The new wife has been pregnant for five years, all methods have been exhausted, and there are no signs at all." Shen said, "I have studied the Daoist arts and am skilled in spells. The reason for the lack of birth, I can discern immediately." He immediately ordered the attendants to bring the new wife, and after examining her arm, said, "It is a boy, very bright and intelligent, with extraordinary talent, hence not bound by the usual lunar cycles." Then he ordered the preparations for the birthing room to be made quickly, including curtains, beds, and other furnishings. Shen took a brush as if performing a ritual, wrote the character '生' (birth) in red ink, and had her swallow it. After she swallowed it, a boy was born. The elderly man was extremely happy and presented thirty bolts of silk. Shen accepted it and said, "This child is not ordinary; I will return in three years to advise you further." After saying this, he left. [129]

    及期再往，乃曰：“前所生子，今三岁矣，愿得之一宿占相之。”掌人喜而 许之。沈夜伺人静，抱之远处，呼曰：“李擢，今识我否？”儿惊啼，沈掌之， 曰：“李擢何见我不记耶？”又掌之，儿愈啼。而问之者三四，儿忽曰：“十六 兄果能来此耶？”沈因与言洛中事，遂大笑言若平生，曰：“擢一一悟矣。”乃 抱之归宿。及明朝，告其掌人曰：“此儿有重禄，乃成家之贵人也，宜保持之。” 胡氏喜，又赠绢五十疋，因取别。乃忆醒素之言，盖以三才五星隐其成数耳。
    　　
    　　以沈食禄而诛，不食而免，其命乎？足以警贪禄位而不知其命者也。
    　　
    　　
    　　
    　　辑佚
    　　
    　　◇杜巫
    　　
    　　杜巫尚书年少未达时，曾于长白山遇道士贻丹一丸，即令服讫，不欲食，容 色悦怿，轻健无疾。后任商州刺史，自以既登太守，班位已崇而不食，恐惊于众， 于是欲去其丹，遇客无不问其法。 [130]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [130]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 及期再往 - Means 'on the agreed-upon date, went again,' indicating a second visit after a prior arrangement.
    • 願得之一宿占相之 - Literally 'wishes to take him for one night to observe and divine,' referring to a request for a child to be examined by a diviner.
    • 沈夜伺人静 - Means 'at night, when people are quiet,' indicating a time of secrecy and stillness.
    • 擢一一悟矣 - Means 'Li Zuo has now understood everything,' indicating a moment of realization.
    • 宜保持之 - Means 'it is advisable to keep him well,' suggesting care and protection for the child.
    • 不食而免 - Means 'not eating and thus avoiding disaster,' implying that abstaining from food led to a fortunate outcome.
    • 足以警贪禄位而不知其命者也 - Means 'sufficient to warn those who covet official rank and wealth without understanding their fate,' serving as a moral lesson.
    • 遇客无不问其法 - Means 'when meeting guests, he would always ask about the method,' indicating a habit of seeking advice.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沈 - Refers to Shen, a person who performs divination.
    • 李擢 - Refers to Li Zuo, a character in the story.
    • 胡氏 - Refers to a woman named Hu, who is the child's mother or guardian.
    • 太守 - Refers to a prefect, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 三才五星 - Refers to the three celestial bodies (heaven, earth, and man) and five stars, often used in divination and astrology.
    • 辑佚 - Refers to the practice of collecting and compiling lost or scattered texts.
    • 杜巫 - Refers to Du Wu, a historical figure known for his experiences with immortality elixirs.
    • 长白山 - Refers to a mountain in China, often associated with spiritual or mystical experiences.
    • 丹 - Refers to an elixir of immortality, a common theme in Chinese alchemy and folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沈 - A person who performs divination.
    • 李擢 - A character in the story.
    • 胡氏 - A woman named Hu, who is the child's mother or guardian.
    • 太守 - A prefect, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 三才五星 - The three celestial bodies (heaven, earth, and man) and five stars, used in divination and astrology.
    • 辑佚 - The practice of collecting and compiling lost or scattered texts.
    • 杜巫 - A historical figure known for his experiences with immortality elixirs.
    • 长白山 - A mountain in China, often associated with spiritual or mystical experiences.
    • 丹 - An elixir of immortality, a common theme in Chinese alchemy and folklore.

    Translation:

    On the agreed-upon date, Shen returned, and said, "The child you had before is now three years old. I wish to take him for one night to observe and divine." The caretaker was pleased and agreed. At night, when people were quiet, Shen took the child to a distant place and called out, "Li Zuo, do you recognize me now?" The child cried in fright, and Shen held him, saying, "Li Zuo, why don’t you remember me?" He held him again, and the child cried even more. Shen asked him three or four times, and the child suddenly said, "Is it really true that Brother Li has come here?" Shen then spoke to him about matters in Luoyang, and the child laughed as if they were old friends, saying, "I have now understood everything." Shen then took him back and kept him overnight. In the morning, he told the caretaker, "This child will have a great official rank and will be a prosperous person in his family. It is advisable to keep him well." Hu was pleased and gave him fifty bolts of silk as a gift. Shen then left. He remembered the words of the Taoist priest, who had used the three celestial bodies and five stars to hint at the child's fortune.

    Because Shen was killed due to his official rank, but avoided disaster by not eating, it was indeed his fate. This story is sufficient to warn those who covet official rank and wealth without understanding their fate.

    Compilation of Lost Texts

    Du Wu

    When Du Wu was young and not yet successful, he once met a Taoist priest on Changbai Mountain who gave him a pill of elixir. He took it, and from then on, he did not want to eat. His face appeared joyful and healthy, and he was light and agile without illness. Later, he became the prefect of Shangzhou and thought that he had already reached a high position.
