He feared to startle the crowd, so he decided to remove the elixir from his body. Whenever he met guests, he would always ask about the method. [130]

    岁余，有道士至，甚年少。巫询之，道士教以食猪肉仍吃血。巫从之食吃， 道士命挲罗。须臾，巫吐痰涎至多，有一块物如栗。道士取之。甚坚固。道士剖 之，若新胶之未乾者，丹在中。道士取以洗之，置于手中，其色绿莹。巫曰： “将来，吾自收之，暮年服也。”道士不与，曰：“长白吾师曰：‘杜巫悔服吾 丹，今愿出之。汝可教之，收药归也。’今我奉师之命，欲去其神物。今既去矣， 而又拟留至耄年。纵收得，亦不能用也。自宜息心。”遂吞之而去。巫后五十余 年，罄产烧药，竟不成。
    　　
    　　○崔尚
    　　
    　　开元时，有崔尚者，著《无鬼论》，词甚有理。既成，将进之，忽有道士诣 门，求见其论。读竟，谓尚曰：“词理甚工。然天地之间，若云无鬼，此谬矣。” 尚谓“何以言之？”道士曰：“我则鬼也，岂可谓无？君若进本，当为诸鬼神所 杀，不若焚之。”因尔不见，竟失其本。 [131]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [131]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 岁余 - After a year or after some time, indicating a passage of time.
    • 甚年少 - Extremely young, describing the age of the Taoist.
    • 巫询之 - The witch asked him, where '询' is a formal word for 'ask'.
    • 道士教以食猪肉仍吃血 - The Taoist taught her to eat pork and drink blood, where '教以' is a formal way of saying 'taught to'.
    • 巫从之食吃 - The witch followed his instructions and ate, where '从之' means 'followed him' and '食吃' is a colloquial way of saying 'ate'.
    • 道士命挲罗 - The Taoist ordered her to spit, where '命' means 'ordered' and '挲罗' is a colloquial term for 'spit'.
    • 须臾 - In a moment or shortly, indicating a brief period of time.
    • 巫吐痰涎至多 - The witch spat out a lot of phlegm, where '痰涎' refers to phlegm or saliva.
    • 有一块物如栗 - There was a lump like a chestnut, where '栗' is a metaphor for a small, hard object.
    • 道士取之 - The Taoist took it, where '取之' is a formal way of saying 'took it'.
    • 甚坚固 - Very hard, describing the texture of the lump.
    • 道士剖之 - The Taoist cut it open, where '剖之' is a formal way of saying 'cut it open'.
    • 若新胶之未乾者 - Like newly made glue that is not yet dry, describing the consistency of the substance inside.
    • 丹在中 - The pill was inside, where '丹' refers to a magical or alchemical pill.
    • 道士取以洗之 - The Taoist took it and washed it, where '取以' means 'took it' and '洗之' means 'washed it'.
    • 置于手中 - Put it in his hand, where '置于' means 'put in' and '手中' means 'his hand'.
    • 其色绿莹 - Its color was green and lustrous, describing the appearance of the pill.
    • 巫曰 - The witch said, where '曰' is a formal word for 'said'.
    • 将来，吾自收之，暮年服也 - Give it to me, I will keep it for my old age, where '将来' means 'give it to me', '自收之' means 'keep it', and '暮年服也' means 'for my old age'.
    • 道士不与 - The Taoist did not give it, where '不与' means 'did not give'.
    • 长白吾师曰 - My master told me, where '长白' is a respectful way of saying 'my master' and '曰' means 'said'.
    • 杜巫悔服吾丹 - Witch Du regrets taking my pill, where '悔' means 'regret' and '服' means 'take'.
    • 今愿出之 - Now wants to give it back, where '愿' means 'wants' and '出之' means 'give it back'.
    • 汝可教之，收药归也 - You can teach her to take the medicine back, where '教之' means 'teach her' and '收药归也' means 'take the medicine back'.
    • 今我奉师之命 - Now I am following my master's command, where '奉' means 'follow' and '师之命' means 'my master's command'.
    • 欲去其神物 - Want to remove the magical object, where '去' means 'remove' and '神物' means 'magical object'.
    • 今既去矣 - Now it is gone, where '既去' means 'is gone'.
    • 而又拟留至耄年 - And wants to keep it until old age, where '拟' means 'wants' and '耄年' means 'old age'.
    • 纵收得 - Even if you keep it, where '纵' means 'even if' and '收得' means 'keep it'.
    • 亦不能用也 - You will not be able to use it, where '亦' means 'also' and '不能用也' means 'will not be able to use it'.
    • 自宜息心 - You should calm your mind, where '自宜' means 'should' and '息心' means 'calm your mind'.
    • 遂吞之而去 - And swallowed it and left, where '遂' means 'and then' and '吞之而去' means 'swallowed it and left'.
    • 巫后五十余年 - After more than fifty years, where '五十余年' means 'more than fifty years'.
    • 罄产烧药 - Spent all his wealth on burning medicine, where '罄产' means 'spent all his wealth' and '烧药' means 'burning medicine'.
    • 竟不成 - But it never succeeded, where '竟' means 'but' and '不成' means 'never succeeded'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist practitioner or alchemist, who in this context is likely a figure of authority in the realm of alchemy and magic.
    • 巫 - Refers to a witch or shaman, a figure who practices traditional rituals and possibly has access to supernatural knowledge.
    • 杜巫 - Refers to a specific witch named Du, who is the subject of the narrative.
    • 长白吾师 - Refers to the Taoist's master, who is a figure of authority in the Taoist tradition.
    • 丹 - Refers to an alchemical pill, which is often associated with immortality or spiritual transformation in Chinese alchemical and Taoist traditions.
    • 罄产 - Refers to spending all one's wealth, a common trope in stories of alchemy and pursuit of immortality.
    • 烧药 - Refers to the process of burning or refining medicine, which is part of alchemical practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 杜巫 - The name of the witch, Du, who is the main character in the narrative.
    • 长白吾师 - The name of the Taoist's master, who is a figure of authority in the Taoist tradition.

    After more than a year, a Taoist arrived, and he was extremely young. The witch asked him, and the Taoist taught her to eat pork and drink blood. The witch followed his instructions and ate, and the Taoist ordered her to spit. Shortly after, the witch spat out a large amount of phlegm, and there was a lump in it like a chestnut. The Taoist took it. It was very hard. The Taoist cut it open, and it was like newly made glue that was not yet dry, with a pill inside. The Taoist took it and washed it, putting it in his hand, and its color was green and lustrous. The witch said, "Give it to me, I will keep it for my old age." The Taoist did not give it, saying, "My master told me, 'Witch Du regrets taking my pill, now wants to give it back. You can teach her to take the medicine back.' Now I am following my master's command, wanting to remove the magical object. Now it is gone, and she wants to keep it until old age. Even if you keep it, you will not be able to use it. You should calm your mind." And he swallowed it and left. After more than fifty years, the witch spent all his wealth on burning medicine, but it never succeeded.

    ○Cui Shang

    During the Kaiyuan era, there was a man named Cui Shang, who wrote the Wu Gui Lun (Treatise on the Nonexistence of Ghosts), which was very well-reasoned. After it was completed, he was about to submit it, when suddenly a Taoist came to his door, asking to see the treatise. After reading it, the Taoist said to Cui Shang, "The reasoning in your treatise is very skillful. However, in the world, if one says there are no ghosts, this is a mistake." Cui Shang asked, "Why do you say so?" The Taoist replied, "I am a ghost, how can one say there are none? If you submit this treatise, you will be killed by the spirits. It would be better to burn it." And so, the Taoist disappeared, and Cui Shang eventually lost the manuscript. [131]

    ○郑望
    　　
    　　乾元中，有郑望者自都入京。夜投野狐泉店宿，未至五六里而昏黑。忽于道 侧见人家。试问门者，云是王将军，与其亡父有旧。望甚喜，乃通名参承。将军 出，与望相见，叙悲泣，人事备之。因尔留宿，为设馔饮。中夜酒酣，令呼蘧蒢 三娘唱歌送酒，少间三娘至，容色甚丽，尤工唱《阿鹊盐》。及晓别去，将军夫 人传语，令买锦裤及头髻花红朱粉等。
    　　
    　　后数月，东归过，送所求物，将军相见欢洽，留宿如初。望问何以不见蘧蒢 三娘。将军云：“已随其夫还京。”以明日辞去。出门不复见宅，但余丘陇。望 怃然，却回。至野狐泉，问居人，曰是王将军冢。冢边，伶人至店，其妻暴疾亡， 以苇席裹尸，葬将军坟侧，故呼曰蘧蒢三娘云。旬日前，伶官亦移其尸归葬长安 讫。 [132]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [132]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 乾元中 - Reign period of Emperor Suzong of Tang (758-760 AD), a time of political and social upheaval following the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 夜投野狐泉店宿 - At night, he checked into a guesthouse called Wild Fox Spring for lodging; a common phrase in travel literature.
    • 未至五六里而昏黑 - He was not yet five or six li away when it became dark; 'li' is an ancient Chinese unit of distance.
    • 忽于道侧见人家 - Suddenly, he saw a house on the side of the road; sets up a sudden encounter.
    • 通名参承 - Introduced himself and paid his respects; a formal way of greeting in ancient times.
    • 叙悲泣，人事备之 - They exchanged sad tears and discussed various matters; indicates a deep emotional connection.
    • 蘧蒢三娘 - A name that seems to be a fictional character, possibly a courtesan or performer.
    • 尤工唱《阿鹊盐》 - Especially skilled at singing 'The Salt of the Magpie'; '阿鹊盐' is likely a song title.
    • 容色甚丽 - Her appearance was very beautiful; a common descriptive phrase.
    • 尤工唱 - Especially skilled at singing; emphasizes her talent.
    • 及晓别去 - Until dawn, they parted ways; indicates a long stay.
    • 将军夫人传语 - The general's wife conveyed a message; a formal way of passing information.
    • 锦裤及头髻花红朱粉等 - Silk trousers, headband, red flowers, and rouge; describes the items requested.
    • 后数月，东归过 - A few months later, on his way back east; indicates a return journey.
    • 欢洽 - Pleased and harmonious; describes the mood.
    • 何以不见蘧蒢三娘 - Why don't I see Qu Chu San娘 anymore; a question about the absence of a person.
    • 已随其夫还京 - She has already returned to the capital with her husband; explains her absence.
    • 明日辞去 - The next day, he bid farewell and left; indicates a departure.
    • 但余丘陇 - Only hills and mounds remained; suggests a desolate scene.
    • 惘然 - Disheartened or lost in thought; describes the emotional state.
    • 却回 - Returned; indicates a change in direction.
    • 问居人 - Asked the locals; seeking information.
    • 王将军冢 - The tomb of General Wang; identifies the location.
    • 伶人至店 - A musician came to the inn; indicates a performer.
    • 其妻暴疾亡 - His wife suddenly fell ill and died; describes a tragic event.
    • 以苇席裹尸 - Wrapped her body in a reed mat; a common practice for burial.
    • 葬将军坟侧 - Buried her beside the general's grave; indicates a specific location.
    • 故呼曰蘧蒢三娘云 - Thus she was called Qu Chu San娘; explains the origin of the name.
    • 旬日前，伶官亦移其尸归葬长安讫 - A few days ago, the musician also moved her body to be buried in Chang'an; indicates a recent event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 乾元中 - Reign period of Emperor Suzong of Tang, a time of significant historical events.
    • 野狐泉 - A specific location, likely a well-known inn or resting place.
    • 王将军 - A historical or fictional figure, likely a military leader.
    • 蘧蒢三娘 - A fictional character, possibly a courtesan or performer, whose name and story add cultural depth.
    • 阿鹊盐 - A song title, possibly a popular or traditional piece of music.
    • 锦裤及头髻花红朱粉等 - Describes items that were fashionable or significant in the period, reflecting the cultural context.
    • 王将军冢 - The tomb of a historical figure, adding a layer of historical significance.
    • 伶人 - Refers to musicians or performers, a common social class in ancient China.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Tang dynasty, a center of political and cultural activity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 乾元 - The reign period of Emperor Suzong of Tang.
    • 野狐泉 - A specific location, likely a well-known inn or resting place.
    • 王将军 - A historical or fictional figure, likely a military leader.
    • 蘧蒢三娘 - A fictional character, possibly a courtesan or performer.
    • 阿鹊盐 - A song title, possibly a popular or traditional piece of music.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Tang dynasty.

    In the middle of the Qianyuan era, there was a man named Zheng Wang who traveled from the capital to the imperial city. At night, he checked into a guesthouse called Wild Fox Spring for lodging, but he was not yet five or six li away when it became dark. Suddenly, he saw a house on the side of the road. He asked the gatekeeper, who told him it was the residence of General Wang, and that his father had been friends with the general. Zheng Wang was very pleased and introduced himself, paying his respects. The general came out and greeted him, exchanging sad tears and discussing various matters. They decided to stay overnight and had a meal together.

    In the middle of the night, after drinking to their heart's content, the general asked for a musician to sing and entertain them. Soon, a young woman named Qu Chu San娘 appeared, her appearance very beautiful, and she was especially skilled at singing "The Salt of the Magpie." Until dawn, they parted ways. The general's wife conveyed a message, instructing Zheng Wang to buy silk trousers, a headband, red flowers, and rouge.

    A few months later, on his way back east, Zheng Wang stopped by to deliver the requested items. The general greeted him warmly and invited him to stay overnight, as before. Zheng Wang asked why he didn't see Qu Chu San娘 anymore. The general replied, "She has already returned to the capital with her husband." The next day, Zheng Wang bid farewell and left. As he stepped out, he saw no more signs of the house, only hills and mounds remained. Zheng Wang felt disheartened and turned back. When he reached Wild Fox Spring, he asked the locals, who told him it was the tomb of General Wang. Near the tomb, a musician came to the inn. The musician's wife had suddenly fallen ill and died. She was wrapped in a reed mat and buried beside the general's grave, hence she was called Qu Chu San娘. A few days ago, the musician had also moved her body to be buried in Chang'an. [132]

    ○元载
    　　
    　　大历九年春，中书侍郎平章事元载，早入朝。有献文章者，命左右收之。此 人若欲载读，载云：“候至中书，当为看。”人言：“若不能读，请自诵一首。” 诵毕不见，方知非人耳。诗曰：
    　　
    　　城东城西旧居处，城里飞花乱如絮。
    　　
    　　海燕衔泥欲下来，屋里无人却飞去。
    　　
    　　载后竟破家，妻子被杀云。
    　　
    　　○魏朋
    　　
    　　建州刺史魏朋，辞满后，客居南昌。素无诗思，后遇病，迷惑失心，如有人 相引接。忽索笔抄诗言：
    　　
    　　孤坟临清江，每睹白日晚。
    　　
    　　松影摇长风，蟾光落岩甸。
    　　
    　　故乡千里余，亲戚罕相见。
    　　
    　　望望空云山，哀哀泪如霰。
    　　
    　　恨为泉台客，复此异乡县。 [133]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [133]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 早入朝 - Literally 'early entered court,' meaning arriving at the court early in the morning.
    • 候至中书，当为看 - Wait until I am in the cabinet, then I will read it, indicating a delay in reading the submission.
    • 若不能读，请自诵一首 - If you cannot read it, please recite one yourself, suggesting the possibility of recitation if reading is not possible.
    • 海燕衔泥欲下来 - The swallow carries mud and wants to descend, a metaphorical expression that may be open to interpretation.
    • 屋里无人却飞去 - The house is empty, yet it flies away, possibly a metaphor for something that was expected but did not materialize.
    • 恨为泉台客 - Regret being a guest of the underworld, a reference to death and the afterlife.
    • 复此异乡县 - Again in this foreign county, indicating a sense of displacement or being far from home.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元载 - Yuan Zai, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his political influence and eventual downfall.
    • 大历九年春 - The ninth year of the Dali era (774 AD) during the reign of Emperor Daizong of Tang.
    • 建州刺史魏朋 - Wei Peng, a governor of Jianzhou, who later became a guest in Nanchang after his term ended.
    • 建州 - A prefecture in Fujian province during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南昌 - The modern city of Nanchang, Jiangxi province, historically known as Gan Prefecture.
    • 泉台 - A term for the underworld or the realm of the dead, often used in classical Chinese literature to refer to the afterlife.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '有献文章者，命左右收之' is elliptical, with '命左右收之' (ordered the attendants to collect it) being implied but not explicitly stated.
    • The sentence '诵毕不见，方知非人耳' is ambiguous about the timing and nature of the disappearance of the person who recited the poem.
    • The sentence '恨为泉台客，复此异乡县' is elliptical, with '复此异乡县' (again in this foreign county) being a metaphorical expression that may be open to interpretation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元载 - Yuan Zai, a historical figure.
    • 魏朋 - Wei Peng, a historical figure.
    • 建州 - Jianzhou, a prefecture in Fujian province.
    • 南昌 - Nanchang, a city in Jiangxi province.

    Yuan Zai

    In the spring of the ninth year of the Dali era, Yuan Zai, the Chancellor of the Secretariat, arrived at court early in the morning. Someone presented an article, and he ordered the attendants to collect it. If this person wanted Yuan Zai to read it, Yuan Zai said, "Wait until I am in the cabinet, then I will read it." The person said, "If you cannot read it, please recite one yourself." After reciting, the person disappeared, and it was only then that Yuan Zai realized it was not a human being. The poem said:

    In the old residence to the east and west of the city, the flying flowers in the city are as chaotic as fluff.
    The swallow carries mud and wants to descend, but the house is empty, yet it flies away.

    Later, Yuan Zai's family was broken, and his wife and children were killed.

    Wei Peng

    Wei Peng, the governor of Jianzhou, after his term ended, became a guest in Nanchang. He had never had a thought for poetry, but later, after falling ill, he became confused and lost his mind, as if someone was leading him. Suddenly, he picked up a pen and wrote a poem:

    A lonely grave by the clear river, every day I see the late white sun.
    The shadow of pines sways in the long wind, the moonlight falls on the rocky fields.
    My hometown is thousands of miles away, relatives seldom meet.
    Looking at the empty clouds and mountains, tears fall like snowflakes.
    Regret being a guest of the underworld, again in this foreign county. [133]

    愿言敦畴昔，勿以弃疵贱。
    　　
    　　诗意如其亡妻以赠朋也。后十余日，朋卒。
    　　
    　　○岑顺
    　　
    　　汝南岑顺字孝伯，少好学有文，老大尤精武略。旅于陕州，贫无第宅。其外 族吕氏有山宅，将废之，顺请居焉。人有劝者，顺曰：“天命有常，何所惧耳！” 卒居之。
    　　
    　　后岁余，顺常独坐书阁下，虽家人莫得入。夜中闻鼓鼙之声，不知所来。及 出户，则无闻，而独喜，自负之，以为石勒之祥也。祝之曰：“此必阴兵助我， 若然，当示我以富贵期。”数夕后，梦一人被甲胄前报曰：“金象将军使我语岑 君，军城夜警，有喧诤者，蒙君见嘉，敢不敬命。君甚有厚禄，幸自爱也。既负 壮志，能猥顾小国乎？今敌国犯垒，侧席委贤，钦味芳声，愿执旌钺。”顺谢曰： “将军天质英明，师真以律，猥烦德音，屈顾疵贱。然犬马之志，惟欲用之。” 使者复命。顺忽然而寤，恍若自失，坐而思梦之征。 [134]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [134]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 愿言敦畴昔 - Formal expression meaning 'I wish to cherish the past,' where '愿言' is a polite way of expressing a wish, '敦' means to cherish or value, and '畴昔' refers to the past.
    • 勿以弃疵贱 - Means 'do not abandon me because of my flaws or lowly status,' where '勿以' means 'do not because of,' '弃' means to abandon, '疵' means flaws or blemishes, and '贱' means lowly or humble.
    • 诗意如其亡妻以赠朋也 - Literary expression meaning 'the poem is like the late wife's gift to a friend,' where '诗意' refers to the poetic meaning or essence, '如' means like or as, '其亡妻' means her late wife, '以' means to give or present, and '赠朋' means to give as a gift to a friend.
    • 后十余日，朋卒 - Means 'after more than ten days, the friend died,' where '后' means after, '十余日' means more than ten days, '朋' means friend, and '卒' means died.
    • 汝南岑顺字孝伯 - Means 'Cen Shun from Runan, courtesy name Xiao Bo,' where '汝南' is a place name, '岑顺' is the surname and given name, '字' means courtesy name, and '孝伯' is the courtesy name.
    • 少好学有文，老大尤精武略 - Means 'he was fond of learning and had literary talent when young, and especially skilled in military strategy when older,' where '少' means young, '好学' means fond of learning, '有文' means had literary talent, '老大' means when older, and '尤精武略' means especially skilled in military strategy.
    • 旅于陕州，贫无第宅 - Means 'he lived in Shan Prefecture, poor and without a home,' where '旅' means to live or reside, '陕州' is the place name Shan Prefecture, '贫' means poor, and '无第宅' means without a home.
    • 其外族吕氏有山宅，将废之，顺请居焉 - Means 'his cousin, the Lü family, had a mountain house that was about to be abandoned, and Cen Shun requested to live there,' where '其外族' means his cousin, '吕氏' means the Lü family, '有山宅' means had a mountain house, '将废之' means was about to be abandoned, and '顺请居焉' means Cen Shun requested to live there.
    • 天命有常，何所惧耳 - Means 'fate is constant, so what is there to fear,' where '天命' means fate, '有常' means is constant, '何所惧' means what is there to fear, and '耳' is a particle indicating a rhetorical question.
    • 顺常独坐书阁下，虽家人莫得入 - Means 'Cen Shun often sat alone in the study, and even family members could not enter,' where '顺常' means Cen Shun often, '独坐' means sat alone, '书阁下' means in the study, '虽家人' means even family members, and '莫得入' means could not enter.
    • 夜中闻鼓鼙之声，不知所来 - Means 'at night he heard the sound of drums, not knowing where it came from,' where '夜中' means at night, '闻' means heard, '鼓鼙之声' means the sound of drums, and '不知所来' means not knowing where it came from.
    • 及出户，则无闻，而独喜，自负之，以为石勒之祥也 - Means 'when he went out the door, he heard nothing, but he was alone in his joy, believing it to be an omen of Shi Le,' where '及出户' means when he went out the door, '则无闻' means he heard nothing, '而独喜' means but he was alone in his joy, '自负之' means he believed it, and '以为石勒之祥也' means he thought it was an omen of Shi Le.
    • 祝之曰 - Means 'he prayed, saying,' where '祝之曰' means he prayed, saying.
    • 金象将军使我语岑君，军城夜警，有喧诤者，蒙君见嘉，敢不敬命 - Means 'the Golden Elephant General sent me to tell Cen Shun, the city is on alert at night, there are noisy disturbances, I am honored by your favor, how dare I not follow your command,' where '金象将军' is the name of the general, '使我语岑君' means sent me to tell Cen Shun, '军城夜警' means the city is on alert at night, '有喧诤者' means there are noisy disturbances, '蒙君见嘉' means I am honored by your favor, and '敢不敬命' means how dare I not follow your command.
    • 君甚有厚禄，幸自爱也 - Means 'you will have a generous salary, please take care of yourself,' where '君甚有厚禄' means you will have a generous salary, and '幸自爱也' means please take care of yourself.
    • 既负壮志，能猥顾小国乎 - Means 'having great ambitions, can you condescend to visit a small country,' where '既负壮志' means having great ambitions, '能猥顾' means can you condescend to visit, and '小国' means a small country.
    • 今敌国犯垒，侧席委贤，钦味芳声，愿执旌钺 - Means 'now the enemy country is attacking, we are eager to entrust the wise, we admire your good reputation, and wish to hold the banner,' where '今敌国犯垒' means now the enemy country is attacking, '侧席委贤' means we are eager to entrust the wise, '钦味芳声' means we admire your good reputation, and '愿执旌钺' means wish to hold the banner.
    • 顺谢曰 - Means 'Cen Shun replied, saying,' where '顺谢曰' means Cen Shun replied, saying.
    • 将军天质英明，师真以律，猥烦德音，屈顾疵贱 - Means 'General, your natural qualities are brilliant, you are a true teacher, I am honored by your words, and you have condescended to visit me,' where '将军天质英明' means General, your natural qualities are brilliant, '师真以律' means you are a true teacher, '猥烦德音' means I am honored by your words, and '屈顾疵贱' means you have condescended to visit me.
    • 然犬马之志，惟欲用之 - Means 'however, my loyalty is to serve you,' where '然' means however, '犬马之志' means my loyalty, and '惟欲用之' means is to serve you.
    • 使者复命 - Means 'the messenger returned with his message,' where '使者' means messenger, and '复命' means returned with his message.
    • 顺忽然而寤，恍若自失，坐而思梦之征 - Means 'Cen Shun suddenly woke up, feeling as if he had lost himself, sitting and thinking about the meaning of the dream,' where '顺忽然而寤' means Cen Shun suddenly woke up, '恍若自失' means feeling as if he had lost himself, '坐而思' means sitting and thinking, and '梦之征' means the meaning of the dream.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 愿言敦畴昔，勿以弃疵贱 - This line reflects the classical sentiment of cherishing past relationships and not abandoning someone due to flaws or low status.
    • 诗意如其亡妻以赠朋也 - This line suggests that the poem is a gift from a deceased wife to a friend, reflecting the cultural practice of literary gifts and the emotional significance of such gestures.
    • 后十余日，朋卒 - This line indicates a tragic event, the death of a friend, which is a common theme in classical literature.
    • 汝南岑顺字孝伯 - Cen Shun is a historical figure from Runan, and his courtesy name '孝伯' (Xiao Bo) is a traditional Chinese naming convention.
    • 少好学有文，老大尤精武略 - This line describes Cen Shun's multifaceted talents, combining literary and military skills, which was highly valued in classical Chinese society.
    • 旅于陕州，贫无第宅 - This line describes Cen Shun's humble living conditions, reflecting the social and economic realities of the time.
    • 其外族吕氏有山宅，将废之，顺请居焉 - This line describes a practical solution to Cen Shun's living situation, involving a family connection and a shared resource.
    • 天命有常，何所惧耳 - This line reflects the classical belief in the constancy of fate and the acceptance of one's destiny.

    I wish to cherish the past, do not abandon me because of my flaws or lowly status.

    The poem is like the late wife's gift to a friend. After more than ten days, the friend died.

    ○ Cen Shun

    Cen Shun from Runan, courtesy name Xiao Bo, was fond of learning and had literary talent when young, and especially skilled in military strategy when older. He lived in Shan Prefecture, poor and without a home. His cousin, the Lü family, had a mountain house that was about to be abandoned, and Cen Shun requested to live there. When someone advised him against it, Cen Shun said, "Fate is constant, so what is there to fear!" and eventually moved in.

    After more than a year, Cen Shun often sat alone in the study, and even family members could not enter. At night, he heard the sound of drums, not knowing where it came from. When he went out the door, he heard nothing, but he was alone in his joy, believing it to be an omen of Shi Le. He prayed, saying, "This must be the aid of hidden soldiers. If so, please show me the promise of wealth and status." A few nights later, he dreamed of a man in armor and armor reporting to him, saying, "The Golden Elephant General sent me to tell Cen Shun, the city is on alert at night, there are noisy disturbances, I am honored by your favor, how dare I not follow your command. You will have a generous salary, please take care of yourself. Having great ambitions, can you condescend to visit a small country? Now the enemy country is attacking, we are eager to entrust the wise, we admire your good reputation, and wish to hold the banner." Cen Shun replied, saying, "General, your natural qualities are brilliant, you are a true teacher, I am honored by your words, and you have condescended to visit me. However, my loyalty is to serve you." The messenger returned with his message. Cen Shun suddenly woke up, feeling as if he had lost himself, sitting and thinking about the meaning of the dream. [134]

    俄然鼓角四起，声愈振厉。顺整巾下床，再拜祝之。须臾，户牖风生，帷帘 飞扬，灯下忽有数百铁骑，飞驰左右，悉高数寸，而被坚执锐，星散遍地。倏闪 之间，云阵四合。顺惊骇，定神气以观之。须臾，有卒赍书云：“将军传檄。” 顺受之，云：
    　　
    　　地连獯虏，戎马不息，向数十年。将老兵穷，姿霜卧甲，天设劲敌，势不可 止。明公养素畜德，进业及时，屡承嘉音，愿托神契。然明公阳官，固当享大禄 于圣世，今小国安敢望之。缘天那国北山贼合从，克日会战，事图子夜，否灭未 期，良用惶骇。
    　　
    　　顺谢之，室中益烛，坐观其变。夜半后，鼓角四发。先是东面壁下有鼠穴， 化为城门，垒敌崔嵬，三奏金革，四门出兵，连旗万计，风驰云走，两皆列阵。 其东壁下是天那军，西壁下金象军。部后各定，军师进曰： [135]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [135]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 俄然 - Means 'suddenly' or 'all of a sudden,' used to describe the abrupt onset of events.
    • 鼓角四起 - Literally 'drums and horns rise from all sides,' used to describe the sudden and intense sound of military alarms.
    • 声愈振厉 - Means 'the sound grows more resounding and fierce,' emphasizing the increasing intensity of the noise.
    • 順整巾下床，再拜祝之 - Describes the action of 'Shun' tidying his headgear, getting out of bed, and bowing twice to make a wish or prayer.
    • 須臾，戶牖風生，帷帘飛揚 - Means 'in a moment, the wind blows through the doors and windows, and the curtains fly up,' describing the sudden and strong gust of wind.
    • 燈下忽有數百鐵騎，飛驰左右，悉高數寸 - Describes the sudden appearance of hundreds of iron-clad cavalry under the lamp, flying around, all about an inch tall.
    • 被堅執銳，星散遍地 - Means 'wearing armor and holding sharp weapons, scattered like stars across the ground,' describing the appearance and arrangement of the cavalry.
    • 倏閃之間，云陣四合 - Means 'in the blink of an eye, the cloud formations gather from all sides,' describing the rapid and dramatic change in the scene.
    • 順驚駭，定神氣以觀之 - Describes 'Shun' being startled but then calming down to observe the scene.
    • 須臾，有卒赍书云：“将军传檄。” - Means 'in a moment, a soldier arrives with a message saying, 'The general sends a summons.'
    • 地連獯虏，戎馬不息，向数十年 - Means 'the land is connected to the Xiongnu, with continuous warfare for several decades,' describing the long-standing conflict.
    • 将老兵穷，姿霜卧甲，天设劲敌，势不可止 - Means 'old generals and soldiers are exhausted, lying in their armor like frost, facing a powerful enemy that cannot be stopped,' describing the dire situation.
    • 明公养素畜德，进业及时，屡承嘉音，愿托神契 - Means 'Your Excellency has nurtured virtue and pursued your career at the right time, often receiving good news, and I wish to entrust our fate to a divine pact,' expressing admiration and a desire for alliance.
    • 然明公阳官，固当享大禄于圣世，今小国安敢望之 - Means 'However, Your Excellency holds a high office and should enjoy great fortune in this sacred era, so how dare I, a small state, hope for such things,' expressing humility and respect.
    • 缘天那国北山贼合从，克日会战，事图子夜，否灭未期，良用惶骇 - Means 'Because the bandits from the northern mountains of Tianya State have united, they plan to attack at midnight, and the outcome is uncertain, causing great fear,' describing the impending danger.
    • 顺谢之，室中益烛，坐观其变 - Means 'Shun thanked him, lit more candles in the room, and sat to observe the changes,' describing Shun's reaction.
    • 夜半后，鼓角四发 - Means 'After midnight, drums and horns sounded from all sides,' describing the continuation of the military alarms.
    • 先是东面壁下有鼠穴，化为城门，垒敌崔嵬，三奏金革，四门出兵，连旗万计，风驰云走，两皆列阵 - Means 'Earlier, there was a mouse hole on the eastern wall, which transformed into a city gate, with enemy fortifications towering up, three times the sound of metal and leather, four gates sending out troops, thousands of banners, rushing like wind and clouds, both sides arrayed in formation,' describing the transformation and battle preparations.
    • 其东壁下是天那军，西壁下金象军 - Means 'On the eastern wall is the Tianya Army, and on the western wall is the Jinxiang Army,' identifying the opposing forces.
    • 部后各定，军师进曰 - Means 'After the formations were set, the military advisor said,' introducing the next speaker.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 俄然鼓角四起 - Refers to the sudden and intense sound of military alarms, a common occurrence in times of war.
    • 順整巾下床，再拜祝之 - Describes a traditional Chinese ritual of bowing and making a wish, reflecting the cultural importance of such actions.
    • 須臾，戶牖風生，帷帘飛揚 - Describes a sudden and strong gust of wind, which is often associated with supernatural or divine intervention in classical literature.
    • 燈下忽有數百鐵騎，飛驰左右，悉高數寸 - Describes the appearance of miniature iron-clad cavalry, which is a fantastical element often found in classical Chinese literature.
    • 被堅執銳，星散遍地 - Describes the appearance and arrangement of the cavalry, reflecting the military imagery and tactics of the time.
    • 倏閃之間，云陣四合 - Describes the rapid and dramatic change in the scene, which is often used to convey the power and unpredictability of the supernatural.
    • 順驚駭，定神氣以觀之 - Describes the emotional response of the protagonist, reflecting the human reaction to extraordinary events.
    • 須臾，有卒赍书云：“将军传檄。” - Describes the arrival of a message from a general, reflecting the hierarchical structure and communication methods of the time.
    • 地连獯虏，戎马不息，向数十年 - Refers to the long-standing conflict with the Xiongnu, a historical enemy of the Chinese states.
    • 将老兵穷，姿霜卧甲，天设劲敌，势不可止 - Describes the dire situation faced by the generals and soldiers, reflecting the harsh realities of prolonged warfare.
    • 明公养素畜德，进业及时，屡承嘉音，愿托神契 - Expresses admiration and a desire for alliance, reflecting the diplomatic and political strategies of the time.
    • 然明公阳官，固当享大禄于圣世，今小国安敢望之 - Expresses humility and respect, reflecting the social and political hierarchy.
    • 缘天那国北山贼合从，克日会战，事图子夜，否灭未期，良用惶骇 - Describes the impending danger, reflecting the strategic and tactical considerations of the time.
    • 顺谢之，室中益烛，坐观其变 - Describes the protagonist's reaction, reflecting the calm and composed demeanor expected of a leader.
    • 夜半后，鼓角四发 - Describes the continuation of the military alarms, reflecting the ongoing tension and alertness.
    • 先是东面壁下有鼠穴，化为城门，垒敌崔嵬，三奏金革，四门出兵，连旗万计，风驰云走，两皆列阵 - Describes the transformation and battle preparations, reflecting the fantastical and military elements of the story.
    • 其东壁下是天那军，西壁下金象军 - Identifies the opposing forces, reflecting the political and military alliances of the time.
    • 部后各定，军师进曰 - Introduces the next speaker, reflecting the hierarchical structure of the military.

    Suddenly, drums and horns sounded from all sides, and the sound grew more resounding and fierce. Shun tidied his headgear, got out of bed, and bowed twice to make a wish. In a moment, the wind blew through the doors and windows, and the curtains flew up. Under the lamp, hundreds of iron-clad cavalry suddenly appeared, flying around, all about an inch tall, wearing armor and holding sharp weapons, scattered like stars across the ground. In the blink of an eye, the cloud formations gathered from all sides. Shun was startled but then calmed down to observe the scene. In a moment, a soldier arrived with a message saying, "The general sends a summons." Shun received it, and it read:

    The land is connected to the Xiongnu, with continuous warfare for several decades. Old generals and soldiers are exhausted, lying in their armor like frost, facing a powerful enemy that cannot be stopped. Your Excellency has nurtured virtue and pursued your career at the right time, often receiving good news, and I wish to entrust our fate to a divine pact. However, Your Excellency holds a high office and should enjoy great fortune in this sacred era, so how dare I, a small state, hope for such things. Because the bandits from the northern mountains of Tianya State have united, they plan to attack at midnight, and the outcome is uncertain, causing great fear.

    Shun thanked him, lit more candles in the room, and sat to observe the changes. After midnight, drums and horns sounded from all sides. Earlier, there was a mouse hole on the eastern wall, which transformed into a city gate, with enemy fortifications towering up, three times the sound of metal and leather, four gates sending out troops, thousands of banners, rushing like wind and clouds, both sides arrayed in formation. On the eastern wall is the Tianya Army, and on the western wall is the Jinxiang Army. After the formations were set, the military advisor said: [135]

    天马斜飞度三止，上将横行系四方。
    　　
    　　辎车直入无回翔，六甲次第不乖行。
    　　
    　　王曰：“善。”于是鼓之，两军俱有一马，斜去三尺，止。又鼓之，各有一 步卒，横行一尺。又鼓之，车进。如是鼓渐急而各出，物包矢石乱交。须臾之间， 天那军大败奔溃，杀伤涂地。王单马南驰，数百人投西南隅，仅而免焉。先是西 南有药臼，王栖臼中，化为城堡。金象军大振，收其甲卒，舆尸横地。顺俯伏观 之，于时一骑至禁，颁曰：“阴阳有厝，得之者昌。亭亭天威，风驱连激，一阵 而胜，明公以为何如？”顺曰：“将军英贯白日，乘天用时，窃窥神化灵文，不 胜庆快。”如是数日会战，胜败不常。王神貌伟然，雄姿罕俦。宴馔珍筵，与顺 致宝贝明珠珠玑无限。顺遂荣于其中，所欲皆备焉。后遂与亲朋稍绝，闲间不出。 [136]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [136]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天馬斜飛度三止 - Metaphorical description of a horse's movement, where '斜飛' means 'diagonally flying' or 'slanting,' and '度三止' means 'crossing three stops' or 'stopping three times.'
    • 上將橫行系四方 - Metaphorical description of a general's movement, where '橫行' means 'moving horizontally' or 'advancing broadly,' and '系四方' means 'binding all directions' or 'controlling all sides.'
    • 辎车直入无回翔 - Metaphorical description of a carriage's movement, where '直入' means 'directly entering' or 'advancing straight,' and '无回翔' means 'without circling' or 'without turning back.'
    • 六甲次第不乖行 - Reference to the arrangement of troops, where '六甲' refers to the six divisions of the army, '次第' means 'order' or 'sequence,' and '不乖行' means 'not deviating from the line' or 'not straying from the path.'
    • 王曰：“善。” - Direct quote of the king's approval, where '善' means 'good' or 'excellent.'
    • 于是鼓之，两军俱有一马，斜去三尺，止。 - Description of the battle, where '鼓之' means 'to beat the drum' or 'to signal,' '两军俱有一马' means 'both armies had one horse,' '斜去三尺' means 'slanted away three feet,' and '止' means 'stopped.'
    • 又鼓之，各有一步卒，横行一尺。 - Another description of the battle, where '又鼓之' means 'again beat the drum,' '各有一步卒' means 'each had one foot soldier,' and '横行一尺' means 'moved horizontally one foot.'
    • 又鼓之，车进。 - Another description of the battle, where '又鼓之' means 'again beat the drum,' and '车进' means 'the carriage advanced.'
    • 如是鼓渐急而各出，物包矢石乱交。 - Description of the battle, where '如是' means 'like this' or 'thus,' '鼓渐急' means 'the drumming became more urgent,' '各出' means 'each came out,' and '物包矢石乱交' means 'objects, arrows, and stones were mixed together in chaos.'
    • 须臾之间，天那军大败奔溃，杀伤涂地。 - Description of the battle, where '须臾之间' means 'in a moment' or 'in a short time,' '天那军大败奔溃' means 'the heavenly army was defeated and scattered,' and '杀伤涂地' means 'killed and wounded were scattered everywhere.'
    • 王单马南驰，数百人投西南隅，仅而免焉。 - Description of the king's escape, where '王单马南驰' means 'the king rode alone to the south,' '数百人投西南隅' means 'hundreds of people fled to the southwest corner,' and '仅而免焉' means 'barely escaped.'
    • 先是西南有药臼，王栖臼中，化为城堡。 - Description of the king's refuge, where '先是' means 'before that,' '西南有药臼' means 'there was a medicine mortar in the southwest,' '王栖臼中' means 'the king stayed in the mortar,' and '化为城堡' means 'turned into a castle.'
    • 金象军大振，收其甲卒，舆尸横地。 - Description of the victorious army, where '金象军大振' means 'the golden elephant army was greatly invigorated,' '收其甲卒' means 'gathered their armored soldiers,' and '舆尸横地' means 'carried bodies across the ground.'
    • 顺俯伏观之，于时一骑至禁，颁曰：“阴阳有厝，得之者昌。亭亭天威，风驱连激，一阵而胜，明公以为何如？” - Description of the scene, where '顺俯伏观之' means 'Shun bowed down to observe,' '于时一骑至禁' means 'at that time, a rider arrived at the palace,' '颁曰' means 'declared,' '阴阳有厝' means 'the yin and yang have their place,' '得之者昌' means 'those who obtain it will flourish,' '亭亭天威' means 'the majestic power of heaven,' '风驱连激' means 'wind-driven and continuous,' '一阵而胜' means 'won in one battle,' and '明公以为何如' means 'what do you think, distinguished sir?'
    • 顺曰：“将军英贯白日，乘天用时，窃窥神化灵文，不胜庆快。” - Response from Shun, where '将军英贯白日' means 'the general's brilliance shines through the day,' '乘天用时' means 'riding the heavens and using the time,' '窃窥神化灵文' means 'secretly observing the divine and spiritual writings,' and '不胜庆快' means 'cannot help but feel joy and relief.'
    • 如是数日会战，胜败不常。 - Description of the ongoing battles, where '如是数日会战' means 'like this, battles continued for several days,' and '胜败不常' means 'victories and defeats were not constant.'
    • 王神貌伟然，雄姿罕俦。 - Description of the king, where '王神貌伟然' means 'the king's divine appearance was magnificent,' and '雄姿罕俦' means 'his heroic appearance was rare.'
    • 宴馔珍筵，与顺致宝贝明珠珠玑无限。 - Description of the feast, where '宴馔珍筵' means 'a feast of fine dishes,' '与顺致' means 'together with Shun, presented,' and '宝贝明珠珠玑无限' means 'innumerable treasures, pearls, and gemstones.'
    • 顺遂荣于其中，所欲皆备焉。 - Description of Shun's status, where '顺遂荣于其中' means 'Shun was honored among them,' and '所欲皆备焉' means 'all his desires were fulfilled.'
    • 后遂与亲朋稍绝，闲间不出。 - Description of Shun's later life, where '后遂与亲朋稍绝' means 'later, he gradually distanced himself from relatives and friends,' and '闲间不出' means 'did not go out.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天马 - Refers to a mythical or exceptionally fast horse, often used in classical Chinese literature to symbolize speed and agility.
    • 上将 - Refers to a high-ranking military commander or general.
    • 辎车 - Refers to a carriage or vehicle used for transportation, often associated with military or ceremonial purposes.
    • 六甲 - Refers to the six divisions of the army, a common military organization in ancient China.
    • 王 - Refers to the king or ruler.
    • 顺 - Refers to a person named Shun, likely a general or advisor.
    • 金象军 - Refers to a specific military unit, possibly named after a golden elephant, symbolizing strength and power.
    • 阴阳 - Refers to the concept of yin and yang, representing the balance of opposing forces in Chinese philosophy.
    • 亭亭天威 - Refers to the majestic and awe-inspiring power of heaven, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe divine or supernatural forces.
    • 神化灵文 - Refers to divine and spiritual writings, often associated with religious or mystical texts.

    A Swift Horse and a Mighty General

    A swift horse, slanting its flight across three stops, and a mighty general, advancing broadly to bind all directions.

    A carriage, directly entering without circling, and the six divisions of the army, proceeding in order without deviating from the path.

    The king said, "Good." Then, the drum was beaten, and both armies had one horse, which slanted away three feet and stopped. Again, the drum was beaten, and each had one foot soldier, who moved horizontally one foot. Again, the drum was beaten, and the carriage advanced. Thus, as the drumming became more urgent, each emerged, and objects, arrows, and stones were mixed together in chaos. In a moment, the heavenly army was defeated and scattered, with killed and wounded scattered everywhere. The king rode alone to the south, and hundreds of people fled to the southwest corner, barely escaping. Before that, in the southwest, there was a medicine mortar, and the king stayed in the mortar, which turned into a castle. The golden elephant army was greatly invigorated, gathered their armored soldiers, and carried bodies across the ground. Shun bowed down to observe, and at that time, a rider arrived at the palace, declaring, "The yin and yang have their place; those who obtain it will flourish. The majestic power of heaven, wind-driven and continuous, won in one battle. What do you think, distinguished sir?" Shun replied, "The general's brilliance shines through the day, riding the heavens and using the time, secretly observing the divine and spiritual writings, cannot help but feel joy and relief." Like this, battles continued for several days, with victories and defeats not constant. The king's divine appearance was magnificent, and his heroic appearance was rare. A feast of fine dishes, together with Shun, presented innumerable treasures, pearls, and gemstones. Shun was honored among them, and all his desires were fulfilled. Later, he gradually distanced himself from relatives and friends, did not go out. [136]

    家人异之，莫究其由。而顺颜色憔悴，为鬼气所中。亲戚共意有异，诘之不 言。因饮以醇醪，醉而究，泄之。其亲入潜备锹锸，因顺如厕而隔之。荷锸乱作， 以掘室内八、九尺，忽坎陷，是古墓也。墓有砖堂，其盟器悉多，甲胄数百，前 有金床戏局，列马满枰，皆金铜成形，其干戈之事备矣。乃悟军师之词，乃象戏 行马之势也。既而焚之，遂平其地。多得宝贝，皆墓内所畜者。顺阅之，恍然而 醒，乃大吐。自此充悦，宅亦不复凶矣。时宝应元年也。 [137]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [137]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 家人異之，莫究其由 - The family found him strange, but none could fathom the reason. '異之' means to find someone strange or unusual, and '莫究其由' means unable to investigate or understand the cause.
    • 順顏色憔悴，為鬼氣所中 - Shun's complexion became gaunt, as if he had been afflicted by a ghostly presence. '顏色憔悴' means pale and emaciated appearance, and '為鬼氣所中' means to be affected or possessed by a ghostly or supernatural force.
    • 親戚共意有異，詰之不言 - Relatives all felt something was amiss and interrogated him, but he remained silent. '共意有異' means all had a sense that something was strange or different, and '詰之不言' means questioned him, but he did not respond.
    • 因飲以醇醪，醉而究，泄之 - They gave him strong liquor to drink, and when he was drunk, they questioned him, and he revealed the truth. '醇醪' means strong, pure liquor, '醉而究' means questioned him while he was intoxicated, and '泄之' means revealed or confessed the truth.
    • 荷锸亂作，以掘室內八、九尺 - They carried shovels and began digging frantically, digging eight or nine feet into the room. '荷锸' means carrying shovels, '亂作' means acting frantically or in a disorderly manner, and '掘室內' means digging into the room.
    • 忽坎陷，是古墓也 - Suddenly, a hole collapsed, revealing an ancient tomb. '坎陷' means a hole or pit that collapses, and '是古墓也' means this is an ancient tomb.
    • 墓有磚堂，其盟器悉多 - The tomb had a brick chamber, and it was filled with many ritual objects. '磚堂' means a chamber made of bricks, '盟器' means ritual objects or artifacts, and '悉多' means all were abundant or numerous.
    • 甲胄數百，前有金床戲局，列馬滿枰，皆金銅成形，其干戈之事備矣 - There were hundreds of armor and weapons, with a gold bed in front of a chessboard, lined with horses on the board, all made of gold and bronze, and all the implements of warfare were present. '甲胄數百' means hundreds of armor and weapons, '金床戲局' means a gold bed with a chessboard, '列馬滿枰' means horses lined up on the chessboard, '金銅成形' means made of gold and bronze, and '干戈之事備矣' means all the implements of warfare were present.
    • 乃悟軍師之詞，乃象戲行馬之势也 - They then realized that the general's words referred to the formation of the chess pieces. '悟軍師之詞' means realized the meaning of the general's words, and '象戲行馬之势' means the formation of the chess pieces in the game of Xiangqi (Chinese chess).
    • 既而焚之，遂平其地 - Afterward, they burned it and leveled the ground. '焚之' means burned it, and '平其地' means leveled the ground.
    • 多得寶貝，皆墓內所畜者 - They found many treasures, all of which had been stored in the tomb. '寶貝' means treasures, and '畜者' means stored or accumulated.
    • 順閱之，恍然而醒，乃大吐 - Shun examined them, and suddenly he woke up, vomiting profusely. '閱之' means examined them, '恍然而醒' means suddenly woke up, and '大吐' means vomited profusely.
    • 自此充悅，宅亦不復凶矣 - From then on, he felt refreshed, and the house no longer seemed ominous. '充悅' means felt refreshed or satisfied, and '不復凶矣' means no longer seemed ominous.
    • 時寶應元年也 - This was during the first year of the Baoying era. '寶應元年' means the first year of the Baoying era (762 CE).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 順 - The name 'Shun' likely refers to a historical or fictional figure, though the exact identity is unclear.
    • 軍師 - A general or military strategist, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.
    • 象戲 - Refers to Xiangqi (Chinese chess), a traditional Chinese board game.
    • 干戈 - Metaphorically refers to warfare or conflict.
    • 寶應元年 - The first year of the Baoying era, which was the era name of Emperor Suzong of Tang (762 CE).

    Proper Nouns

    • 順 - Shun (name of the character).
    • 軍師 - General or military strategist (title or role).
    • 象戲 - Xiangqi (Chinese chess).
    • 干戈 - Warfare or conflict (metaphorical term).
    • 寶應元年 - The first year of the Baoying era (762 CE).

    The family found Shun strange, but none could fathom the reason. Shun's complexion became gaunt, as if he had been afflicted by a ghostly presence. Relatives all felt something was amiss and interrogated him, but he remained silent. They gave him strong liquor to drink, and when he was drunk, they questioned him, and he revealed the truth. His relatives secretly prepared shovels and, while Shun was in the bathroom, they separated him. They carried shovels and began digging frantically, digging eight or nine feet into the room. Suddenly, a hole collapsed, revealing an ancient tomb. The tomb had a brick chamber, and it was filled with many ritual objects. There were hundreds of armor and weapons, with a gold bed in front of a chessboard, lined with horses on the board, all made of gold and bronze, and all the implements of warfare were present. They then realized that the general's words referred to the formation of the chess pieces. Afterward, they burned it and leveled the ground. They found many treasures, all of which had been stored in the tomb. Shun examined them, and suddenly he woke up, vomiting profusely. From then on, he felt refreshed, and the house no longer seemed ominous. This was during the first year of the Baoying era. [137]

    ○韦协律兄
    　　
    　　太常协律韦生，有兄甚凶，自云平生无惧惮耳。闻有凶宅，必往独宿之。其 弟话于同官，同官有试之者，且闻延康东北角有马镇西宅，常多怪物，因领送其 宅，具与酒肉，夜则皆去，独留之于大池之西孤亭中宿。韦生以饮酒且热，袒衣 而寝。
    　　
    　　夜半方寤，乃见一小儿，长可尺余，身短脚长，其色颇黑，自池中而出，冉 冉前来，循阶而上，以至生前。生不为之动，乃言曰：“卧者恶物，直又顾我耶？” 乃绕床而行。须臾，生回枕仰卧，乃觉其物上床，生亦不动。逡巡，觉有两个小 脚缘于生脚上，冷如水铁，上彻于心，行步甚迟。生不动，候其渐行，上及于肚， 生乃遽以手摸之，则一古铁鼎子，已欠一脚矣。遂以衣带系之于床脚。明旦，众 看之，具白其事。乃以杵碎其鼎，染染有血色。自是人皆信韦生之凶而能绝宅之 妖也。 [138]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [138]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 協律 - A term referring to a musical official or a person skilled in music, particularly in the context of the imperial court.
    • 凶宅 - A house believed to be haunted or cursed, often associated with supernatural occurrences.
    • 獨宿 - To stay alone, often implying a sense of bravery or challenge.
    • 夜半方寤 - To wake up in the middle of the night, indicating a sudden awareness or disturbance.
    • 直又顧我耶 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Do you think you're so important that you can look down on me?' or 'Are you so arrogant to even glance at me?'
    • 循階而上 - To ascend the stairs, describing the movement of the creature.
    • 繞床而行 - To walk around the bed, indicating the creature's movement.
    • 逡巡 - To hesitate or delay, often with a sense of caution.
    • 欠一脚 - Missing one leg, describing the physical condition of the object.
    • 系之于床脚 - To tie it to the bedpost, indicating the action taken by the protagonist.
    • 明旦 - The next morning, indicating the passage of time.
    • 具白其事 - To fully explain or report the incident, indicating the recounting of events.
    • 杵碎其鼎 - To smash the pot with a pestle, indicating the action taken to destroy the object.
    • 染染有血色 - The color of blood, indicating the appearance of the object after being destroyed.
    • 人皆信韋生之凶而能絕宅之妖也 - People believe that Wei Sheng is so fearsome that he can banish the evil from the haunted house.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太常協律韋生 - Wei Sheng, a musical official of the Imperial Household (太常). The term '太常' refers to the highest-ranking official in charge of religious and ceremonial affairs.
    • 凶宅 - A house believed to be haunted or cursed, often associated with supernatural occurrences. This concept is deeply rooted in Chinese folklore and superstition.
    • 怪物 - Monsters or supernatural beings, often associated with haunted places or cursed objects.
    • 古鐵鼎子 - An ancient iron pot, likely a significant or valuable object in the context of the story.
    • 人皆信韋生之凶而能絕宅之妖也 - This phrase reflects the belief in the power of certain individuals to overcome supernatural threats, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太常協律韋生 - Wei Sheng, a musical official of the Imperial Household.
    • 凶宅 - A haunted house, a term used in Chinese folklore and literature.
    • 怪物 - Monsters or supernatural beings, often associated with haunted places or cursed objects.
    • 古鐵鼎子 - An ancient iron pot, a significant object in the story.

    The Brother of Wei Sheng, the Imperial Music Official

    Wei Sheng, an official of the Imperial Household known as the Imperial Music Official, had a brother who was exceedingly fearsome. He claimed that he had never been afraid of anything. Whenever he heard of a haunted house, he would go there alone to spend the night. His brother told this to their colleagues, and one of them decided to test him. They learned that there was a house belonging to Ma Zhenxi in the northeast corner of Yan Kang, which was often plagued by strange creatures. So, they led Wei Sheng’s brother to this house, providing him with wine and food, and then left him alone in a solitary pavilion west of a large pond to spend the night. Wei Sheng’s brother drank until he was slightly warm and then undressed and lay down to sleep.

    In the middle of the night, he suddenly woke up and saw a small child, about a foot tall, with a short body and long legs, and a rather dark complexion. The child emerged from the pond, slowly moving forward, climbing the steps, and approaching Wei Sheng’s brother. Wei Sheng’s brother did not move, and the child said, "You’re a vile creature, do you think you’re so important that you can look down on me?" Then the child began to walk around the bed. After a while, Wei Sheng’s brother turned over and lay on his back, and he felt the creature climb onto the bed. He remained still. After a moment, he felt two small feet climbing up his own feet, cold as water and iron, reaching up to his heart, and moving very slowly. Wei Sheng’s brother did not move, waiting for the creature to continue moving until it reached his belly. He then quickly reached out and touched it, discovering it was an ancient iron pot missing one leg. He tied it to the bedpost with his belt. The next morning, the others came to see what had happened, and he fully explained the incident. They smashed the pot with a pestle, and it turned a bloody color. From then on, people believed that Wei Sheng’s brother was so fearsome that he could banish the evil spirits from the haunted house. [138]

    ○苏履霜
    　　
    　　太原节度马侍中燧小将苏履霜者，顷事前节度使鲍防，从行营日，并将伐回 纥。时防临阵，指一旗刘明远，以不进锋，命履霜斩之。履霜受命，然数日明远 遽进，得脱丧元之祸。后十余年卒。履霜亦游于冥间，见明远，乃谓履霜曰： “曩日蒙君以生成之故，无因酬德，今日当展素愿。”遂指一路，路多榛棘，云： “但趋此途，必遇舍利王。王平生会为侍中之部将也，见而诉之，必获免。”告 之命去，履霜遂行一二十里间，果逢舍利王弋猎。舍利索识履霜，惊问曰：“何 因至此？”答曰：“为冥司所召。”乃曰：“公不合来，宜速反！”遂命判官王 凤翔，令早放回，兼附信耳。谓履霜曰：“为余告侍中，自此二年，当罢节，一 年之内，先须去入赴朝廷。郎君早弃人世，慎勿泄之。”凤翔检籍放归。至一关 门，逢平生饮酒之友数人，谓履霜曰：“公独行归，余曹企慕所不及也。” [139]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [139]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 游於冥間 - Refers to wandering in the underworld.
    • 展素願 - Means fulfilling a long-held wish.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太原節度馬侍中燧 - Refers to Ma Sui, a prominent military leader during the Tang dynasty.
    • 回 纇 - Refers to the Uyghurs, a nomadic people often at odds with the Tang dynasty.
    • 侍中 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty, often serving as a close advisor to the emperor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蘇履霜 - The name of a historical figure.
    • 鮑防 - The name of a previous military governor.
    • 劉明遠 - The name of a soldier.
    • 舍利王 - The name of a king in the underworld.
    • 判官王鳳翔 - The name of a judge or official.

    Su Lushuang, a subordinate under Ma Sui, the military governor of Taiyuan, once served under Bo Fang, a previous military governor. During a campaign against the Uyghurs, Bo Fang, on the battlefield, pointed to a flag of Liu Mingyuan and ordered Su Lushuang to execute him for not advancing in battle. Su Lushuang accepted the order, but a few days later, Liu Mingyuan suddenly advanced and managed to escape the death penalty. Many years later, Su Lushuang died. He also wandered in the underworld, where he met Liu Mingyuan. Liu Mingyuan said to Su Lushuang, "In the past, I was saved by you, and I had no way to repay your kindness. Today, I will fulfill my long-held wish." He then pointed out a path, which was filled with thorny bushes, and said, "Just follow this path, and you will meet Sheli Wang. Sheli Wang was a subordinate of the Chancellor in life. If you see him and plead with him, you will surely be spared." He told Su Lushuang to leave quickly, and Su Lushuang walked about twenty miles, eventually meeting Sheli Wang, who was hunting. Sheli Wang recognized Su Lushuang and asked, "Why are you here?" Su Lushuang replied, "I was summoned by the underworld authorities." Sheli Wang said, "You should not have come here; you should return quickly!" He then ordered the judge Wang Fengxiang to release Su Lushuang early and to deliver a message. He told Su Lushuang, "Tell the Chancellor that two years from now, he will be dismissed from his position. Within a year, he must first go to court. Your son died young; be careful not to reveal this." Wang Fengxiang released Su Lushuang. When Su Lushuang reached a gate, he met several friends he had drunk with before, who said to him, "You have returned alone; we could not even hope to accompany you." [139]

    生五六日，遂造凤翔。凤翔逆已知之，问云：“舍利何词？”曰：“有之， 不令告他人也。”凤翔曰：“余亦知之，汝且归，余侯隙当白侍中。”旬日，遂 与履霜白之。侍中召履霜讯之，履霜亦具所见。凤翔陈告，后所验一如履霜所言， 盖凤翔生自司冥局，隐而莫有知之者，因履霜还生而泄也。
    　　
    　　○景生
    　　
    　　景生者，河中猗氏人也，素精于经籍，授胄子数十人。岁暮将归，途中偶逢 故相吕潭，以旧相识，遂以后乘载之而去。群胄子乃散，报景生之家。而景生到 家，身已卒讫，数日乃苏，云：“冥中见黄门侍郎严武、朔方节度张或然。” [140]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [140]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 遂造凤翔 - Means 'then arrived at Fengxiang.'
    • 逆已知之 - Means 'already anticipated or knew it.'
    • 舍利何词 - Asks 'what words or statements were made about Sarira.'
    • 余亦知之 - Means 'I also knew it.'
    • 汝且归 - Instructs 'you to return temporarily.'
    • 余侯隙当白侍中 - Means 'I will report to the Attendant General when the opportunity arises.'
    • 遂与履霜白之 - Means 'then reported something with the person named Lushuang.'
    • 侍中召履霜讯之 - Means 'the Attendant General summoned Lushuang to interrogate him/her.'
    • 盖凤翔生自司冥局 - Suggests 'Fengxiang was presumably born from the Department of the Underworld.'
    • 隐而莫有知之者 - Means 'the fact was hidden and unknown to others.'
    • 因履霜还生而泄也 - Suggests 'the fact was revealed because Lushuang returned to life.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 凤翔 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure or a person of significance.
    • 侍中 - The title 'Attendant General,' a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 司冥局 - The 'Department of the Underworld,' a bureaucratic or administrative body associated with the afterlife or underworld.
    • 景生 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure or a person of significance.
    • 河中猗氏 - A place name, referring to a specific region in China.
    • 故相吕潭 - '故相' means 'former chancellor,' and '吕潭' is a proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 黄门侍郎严武 - '黄门侍郎' is a high-ranking official, and '严武' is a proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 朔方节度张或然 - '朔方节度' refers to the military governor of the northern frontier, and '张或然' is a proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 凤翔 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure or a person of significance.
    • 侍中 - The title 'Attendant General,' a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 司冥局 - The 'Department of the Underworld,' a bureaucratic or administrative body associated with the afterlife or underworld.
    • 景生 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure or a person of significance.
    • 河中猗氏 - A place name, referring to a specific region in China.
    • 故相吕潭 - '故相' means 'former chancellor,' and '吕潭' is a proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 黄门侍郎严武 - '黄门侍郎' is a high-ranking official, and '严武' is a proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 朔方节度张或然 - '朔方节度' refers to the military governor of the northern frontier, and '张或然' is a proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.

    A person was born and lived for five or six days, then arrived at Fengxiang. Fengxiang had already anticipated and knew about something, and asked, "What words were spoken about Sarira?" The person replied, "There were words, but I was not allowed to tell others." Fengxiang said, "I also know about it. You may return for now; I will report to the Attendant General when the opportunity arises." After ten days, the person reported it with Lushuang. The Attendant General summoned Lushuang to interrogate him, and Lushuang also detailed what he had seen. Fengxiang then reported, and what was later verified matched exactly what Lushuang had said. It is said that Fengxiang was born from the Department of the Underworld, and the fact was hidden and unknown to others, but it was revealed because Lushuang returned to life.

    Jing Sheng, a man from Yishi in Hedong, was always highly skilled in the classics and taught dozens of students. At the end of the year, he was about to return home. On the way, he happened to meet the former chancellor Lü Tan, an old acquaintance, and was taken away by him. The students then scattered and reported to Jing Sheng's family. However, when Jing Sheng arrived home, he had already died. After several days, he woke up and said, "In the underworld, I saw the Yellow Gate Attendant General Yan Wu and the Military Governor of the Northern Frontier Zhang Huo." [140]

    景生善《周易》，早岁兼与吕相讲授，未终秩，遇吕相薨，乃命景生，请终 余秩。时严、张俱为左右台郎，顾吕而怒曰：“景生未合来，固非冥间之所勾留， 奈何私欲而有所害？”共请放回。吕遂然之。张尚书乃引景生，嘱：“两男，一 名曾子，一名夫子，闰正月三日当起比屋，妨曾子新妇，为报止之。令速罢，当 脱大祸。”及景苏数日，而后报其家，屋已立，其妻已亡矣。又说：“曾子当终 刺史，夫子亦为刺史，而不正拜。”后果如其言。
    　　
    　　○卢顼表姨
    　　
    　　洺州刺史卢顼表姨常畜一猧子，名花子，每加念焉。一旦而失，为人所毙。 后数月，卢氏忽亡。冥间见判官姓李，乃谓曰：“夫人天命将尽，有人切论，当 得重生一十二年。”拜谢而出。 [141]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [141]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 善《周易》 - Proficient in *The Book of Changes*; a key text in Confucianism.
    • 未终秩，遇吕相薨 - Before completing his term, he encountered the death of Lord Lv; *秩* refers to a term of office, and *薨* is a respectful term for the death of a high-ranking official.
    • 顾吕而怒曰 - He looked at Lord Lv and angrily said; *顾* means 'to look at' or 'to regard.'
    • 固非冥间之所勾留 - It is certainly not something that can be detained in the afterlife; *固* means 'certainly' or 'definitely,' and *冥间* refers to the afterlife.
    • 奈何私欲而有所害 - Why should private desires harm him? *奈何* means 'why' or 'how can,' and *私欲* refers to personal desires.
    • 共请放回 - They jointly requested to let him return; *共* means 'jointly' or 'together.'
    • 张尚书乃引景生 - The Minister Zhang then called Jing Sheng; *引* means 'to call' or 'to lead.'
    • 嘱：“两男，一名曾子，一名夫子，闰正月三日当起比屋，妨曾子新妇，为报止之。” - He instructed: 'Two sons, one named Zengzi, the other named Fuzi, on the third day of the leap month of the first month, they will start building next to each other's houses, which will harm Zengzi's new wife. Please report and stop it.' *嘱* means 'to instruct' or 'to advise.'
    • 令速罢，当脱大祸 - Let them stop quickly, and he will escape a great disaster; *令* means 'let' or 'order,' and *脱* means 'escape.'
    • 及景苏数日 - After Jing Sheng regained consciousness for several days; *苏* means 'to regain consciousness.'
    • 忽亡 - Suddenly died; *忽* means 'suddenly.'
    • 冥间见判官姓李 - In the afterlife, he saw a judge surnamed Li; *判官* refers to a judge or official in the afterlife.
    • 夫人天命将尽 - The woman's fate is nearly over; *天命* refers to destiny or fate.
    • 有人切论 - Someone strongly argued; *切论* means 'strongly argued' or 'made a firm argument.'
    • 当得重生一十二年 - He will be given another twelve years of life; *当* means 'will' or 'should,' and *重生* means 'reborn' or 'given new life.'
    • 拜谢而出 - He bowed in thanks and left; *拜谢* means 'to bow in thanks.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《周易》 - One of the Five Classics of Confucianism; a foundational text in Chinese philosophy.
    • 吕相 - Refers to Lord Lv, a high-ranking official.
    • 严、张 - Refers to two officials, likely of high rank, who are involved in the story.
    • 曾子 - A historical figure, Zengzi, a disciple of Confucius.
    • 夫子 - A respectful term for a teacher or scholar, often used to refer to Confucius.
    • 闰正月三日 - Refers to the third day of the leap month of the first month in the Chinese lunar calendar.
    • 冥间 - The afterlife, a concept in Chinese folk religion and Taoism.
    • 判官 - A judge or official in the afterlife, often associated with the underworld in Chinese mythology.
    • 天命 - Destiny or fate, a concept in Chinese philosophy and religion.
    • 卢顼 - A historical figure, Lu Xū, whose aunt is mentioned in the story.

    Proper Nouns

    • 景生 - Jing Sheng, a historical figure.
    • 吕相 - Lord Lv, a high-ranking official.
    • 严、张 - Officials Yan and Zhang.
    • 曾子 - Zengzi, a disciple of Confucius.
    • 夫子 - Fuzi, a respectful term for a teacher or scholar.
    • 卢顼 - Lu Xū, a historical figure.
    • 花子 - Huāzi, the name of the dog.

    Jing Sheng was proficient in The Book of Changes. In his early years, he also taught alongside Lord Lv, but before he could complete his term, Lord Lv passed away. Lord Lv then appointed Jing Sheng to complete the remaining term. At that time, Yan and Zhang were both officials in the imperial court. They looked at Lord Lv and angrily said, "Jing Sheng should not be here; it is certainly not something that can be detained in the afterlife. Why should private desires harm him?" They jointly requested to let Jing Sheng return. Lord Lv agreed. Minister Zhang then called Jing Sheng and instructed him: "Two sons, one named Zengzi, the other named Fuzi, on the third day of the leap month of the first month, they will start building next to each other's houses, which will harm Zengzi's new wife. Please report and stop it. Let them stop quickly, and he will escape a great disaster." After Jing Sheng regained consciousness for several days, he reported this to his family. By then, the houses had already been built, and his wife had already passed away. Minister Zhang also said, "Zengzi will eventually become a prefect, and Fuzi will also become a prefect, but not through proper appointment." Indeed, as predicted, the events unfolded as he had said.

    Lu Xū's aunt, who was the aunt of the Prefect of Mingzhou, often kept a small dog named Huāzi, and she would always think of it fondly. One day, the dog went missing and was killed by someone. Several months later, Lu Xū's aunt suddenly died. In the afterlife, she saw a judge surnamed Li, who told her, "Your fate is nearly over, but someone strongly argued, and you will be given another twelve years of life." She bowed in thanks and left. [141]

    行长衢中，逢大宅。有丽人，侍婢十余人，将游门屏，使人呼夫人入，谓曰： “夫人相识耶？”曰：“不省也。”丽人曰：“某即花子也。平生蒙不以兽畜之 贱，常加育养。某今为李判官别室。昨所嘱夫人者，即某也。冥司不广其请，只 加一纪。某潜以改十二年为二十，以报存育之恩。有顷李至，伏愿白之本名，无 为夫人之号，恳将力祈。”李逡巡而至，至别坐语笑。丽人首以图乙改年白李。 李将让之，对曰：“妾平生受恩，以此申报，万不获一，料必无难之。”李欣然 谓曰：“事则匪易。”感言请之切，遂许之。临将别，谓夫人曰：“请收余骸， 为瘗埋之。骸在履信坊街之北墙委粪之中。”夫人既苏，验而果在。遂以子礼葬 之。后申谢于梦寐之间。后二十年，夫人乃亡也。 [142]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [142]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 行长衢中 - Walking along the main street or traversing the main thoroughfare.
    • 逢大宅 - Encountered a large mansion or came across a grand house.
    • 将游门屏 - About to go through the gate or preparing to pass through the entrance.
    • 使人呼夫人入 - Sent someone to call the lady in or had someone summon the lady inside.
    • 不省也 - Does not recognize or does not recall.
    • 平生蒙不以兽畜之贱 - Throughout my life, I was not treated as a lowly animal or all my life, I was not considered a mere beast.
    • 常加育养 - Always took care of me or often nurtured me.
    • 冥司不广其请 - The underworld did not expand the request or the underworld did not extend the petition.
    • 只加一纪 - Only added twelve years or only granted a twelve-year extension.
    • 潜以改十二年为二十 - Secretly changed twelve years to twenty or quietly altered the twelve years to twenty.
    • 以报存育之恩 - To repay the kindness of nurturing or to express gratitude for being raised.
    • 李逡巡而至 - Li hesitantly arrived or Li came with some delay.
    • 首以图乙改年白李 - First, she presented the petition to change the year to Li or she first showed the petition to Li.
    • 将让之 - Was about to refuse or was on the verge of declining.
    • 万不获一 - Hardly ever succeeds or rarely succeeds.
    • 料必无难之 - Believes it will not be difficult or thinks it will be easy.
    • 欣然谓曰 - Happily said or cheerfully replied.
    • 事则匪易 - The matter is not easy or the task is not simple.
    • 感言请之切 - Moved by the earnest request or touched by the sincere plea.
    • 遂许之 - So he agreed or thus he consented.
    • 临将别 - As they were about to part or just before parting.
    • 请收余骸 - Please collect my remains or please gather my bones.
    • 为瘗埋之 - For burial or for interment.
    • 骸在履信坊街之北墙委粪之中 - The remains are in the northern wall of the street in the Lu Xin Ward, among the refuse or the bones are in the northern wall of the street in the Lu Xin Ward, in the garbage.
    • 夫人既苏 - The lady then revived or the lady then came to.
    • 验而果在 - Checked and found it to be true or verified and confirmed it.
    • 遂以子礼葬之 - So she buried him with the rites of a son or thus she buried him according to the rites for a son.
    • 后申谢于梦寐之间 - Later, she expressed gratitude in her dreams or later, she thanked him in her dreams.
    • 后二十年 - Twenty years later or after twenty years.
    • 夫人乃亡也 - The lady then passed away or the lady then died.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 夫人 - Refers to a lady or noblewoman, possibly a wife or concubine.
    • 冥司 - Refers to the underworld or the realm of the dead, where petitions for favors are made.
    • 李判官 - Refers to Li, a judge or official, possibly a magistrate or a high-ranking official.
    • 履信坊街 - Refers to a specific street in a ward named Lu Xin, indicating a location in a city.
    • 子礼 - Refers to the rites of a son, indicating a high level of respect and honor in burial.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李判官 - Li, a judge or official.
    • 花子 - The lady's name or title.
    • 履信坊街 - A specific street in a ward named Lu Xin.

    Walking along the main street, he encountered a grand mansion. Inside, there was a beautiful woman accompanied by more than ten attendants, who were about to pass through the gate. Someone was sent to call the lady inside, and she was asked, "Do you recognize this lady?" The lady replied, "I do not recall her." The beautiful woman said, "I am Flower Girl. Throughout my life, I was not treated as a lowly animal, but was always nurtured. I am now the concubine of Judge Li. The request I made to you yesterday was indeed me. The underworld did not expand the petition, only granting a twelve-year extension. I secretly changed the twelve years to twenty to repay the kindness of being raised. Soon after, Judge Li arrived, and I humbly ask that you present my true name, not the title of 'Lady,' and I earnestly pray for your help." Judge Li hesitated and arrived. The beautiful woman first presented the petition to change the year to Judge Li. Judge Li was about to refuse, but she replied, "I have received much kindness in my life, and I am using this opportunity to report it. It is rare to succeed, but I believe it will not be difficult." Judge Li happily said, "The matter is not easy." Moved by the earnest request, he agreed. As they were about to part, the beautiful woman said to the lady, "Please collect my remains for burial. They are in the northern wall of the street in the Lu Xin Ward, among the refuse." The lady then revived, verified the location, and indeed found the remains. She buried him with the rites of a son. Later, she expressed gratitude in her dreams. Twenty years later, the lady passed away. [142]

    ○狐诵通天经
    　　
    　　裴仲元家鄠北，因逐兔入大冢，有狐凭棺读书。元仲搏之不中，取书以归， 字不可认识。忽有胡秀才请见，曰行周，仍凭棺读书者。裴曰：“何书也？”曰： “《通天经》，非人间所习。足下诚无所用，愿奉百金赎之。”裴不应。又曰： “千镒。”又不应。客怒，拂衣而起。裴内兄韦端士，已死，忽逢之，曰：“闻 逐兔得书，吾识其字。”乃出示之。韦云：“为胡秀才取尔。”遂失不见。裴亦 寻卒。 [143]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [143]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 狐诵通天经 - Literal meaning 'the fox reciting the *Tongtian Jing*,' where *Tongtian Jing* is a mysterious or esoteric text.
    • 凭棺读书 - Literal meaning 'sitting on a coffin and reading,' which is an unusual and symbolic act.
    • 字不可认识 - Literal meaning 'the characters cannot be recognized,' indicating that the text is difficult or unknown.
    • 足下诚无所用 - Literal meaning 'you truly have no use for it,' a polite way of saying that the text is not useful to the speaker.
    • 愿奉百金赎之 - Literal meaning 'I wish to offer a hundred gold pieces to redeem it,' indicating a high offer for the text.
    • 遂失不见 - Literal meaning 'he then vanished and was no longer seen,' indicating a sudden disappearance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 裴仲元 - A name of a person, likely a scholar or official from the Tang or Song dynasty.
    • 鄠北 - Refers to a location in the northern part of Huxian (鄠县), a county in Shaanxi province.
    • 通天经 - A mysterious or esoteric text, possibly a reference to a lost or legendary work.
    • 胡秀才 - A scholar of the Hu ethnicity, which could imply foreign or non-Han Chinese origins, reflecting the multicultural context of the time.
    • 韦端士 - A deceased person, likely a scholar or official, whose knowledge is invoked to interpret the text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 裴仲元 - A name of a person, likely a scholar or official from the Tang or Song dynasty.
    • 鄠北 - A location in the northern part of Huxian (鄠县), a county in Shaanxi province.
    • 通天经 - A mysterious or esoteric text, possibly a reference to a lost or legendary work.
    • 胡秀才 - A scholar of the Hu ethnicity, which could imply foreign or non-Han Chinese origins.
    • 行周 - The name of the scholar, *Xingzhou*.
    • 韦端士 - The name of the deceased brother-in-law, *Wei Danshi*.

    A Fox Reciting the Tongtian Jing

    Péi Zhongyuan lived in the northern part of Huxian, and one day, while chasing a rabbit into a large tomb, he saw a fox sitting on a coffin and reading. Zhongyuan tried to catch the fox but failed, and instead, he took the book home. The characters on the book were unreadable. Suddenly, a scholar of the Hu ethnicity appeared and asked to be seen. He introduced himself as Xingzhou, the same fox who had been reading the book on the coffin. Péi asked, "What book is this?" The scholar replied, "It is the Tongtian Jing, a text not studied by ordinary people. You truly have no use for it, but I wish to offer a hundred gold pieces to redeem it." Péi refused. The scholar then offered a thousand yì (an ancient unit of weight for gold or silver), but Péi still refused. Angered, the scholar waved his robe and rose to leave. Péi’s brother-in-law, Wei Danshi, who had passed away, suddenly appeared and said, "I heard you caught a rabbit and found a book. I can recognize its characters." He then showed the book to Péi. Wei said, "It belongs to the scholar Xingzhou." He then vanished and was no longer seen. Péi also died shortly after. [143]

    ○狐诵通天经
    　　
    　　裴仲元家鄠北，因逐兔入大冢，有狐凭棺读书。元仲搏之不中，取书以归， 字不可认识。忽有胡秀才请见，曰行周，仍凭棺读书者。裴曰：“何书也？”曰： “《通天经》，非人间所习。足下诚无所用，愿奉百金赎之。”裴不应。又曰： “千镒。”又不应。客怒，拂衣而起。裴内兄韦端士，已死，忽逢之，曰：“闻 逐兔得书，吾识其字。”乃出示之。韦云：“为胡秀才取尔。”遂失不见。裴亦 寻卒。

    A Fox Reciting the Tongtian Jing

    Péi Zhongyuan lived in the northern part of Huxian, and one day, while chasing a rabbit into a large tomb, he saw a fox sitting on a coffin and reading. Zhongyuan tried to catch the fox but failed, and instead, he took the book home. The characters on the book were unreadable. Suddenly, a scholar of the Hu ethnicity appeared and asked to be seen. He introduced himself as Xingzhou, the same fox who had been reading the book on the coffin. Péi asked, "What book is this?" The scholar replied, "It is the Tongtian Jing, a text not studied by ordinary people. You truly have no use for it, but I wish to offer a hundred gold pieces to redeem it." Péi refused. The scholar then offered a thousand yì (an ancient unit of weight for gold or silver), but Péi still refused. Angered, the scholar waved his robe and rose to leave. Péi’s brother-in-law, Wei Danshi, who had passed away, suddenly appeared and said, "I heard you caught a rabbit and found a book. I can recognize its characters." He then showed the book to Péi. Wei said, "It belongs to the scholar Xingzhou." He then vanished and was no longer seen. Péi also died shortly after.
